Allergy and Parasites"]:::ige IgG --- IgM IgM --- IgA IgA --- IgD IgD --- IgE classDef igg fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef igm fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef iga fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef igd fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 classDef ige fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"What are antibodies? Briefly describe their basic chemical nature."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Antibodies, also called **immunoglobulins (Ig)**, are **Y-shaped glycoprotein molecules** produced by **plasma cells** (terminally differentiated B lymphocytes) in response to antigenic stimulation. They constitute the **gamma (γ) globulin fraction** of serum proteins. **Chemical nature highlights (for full marks):** 1. **Composition:** ~90–97% polypeptide, ~3–10% carbohydrate (hence glycoprotein). 2. **Structure:** Four polypeptide chains — 2 identical Heavy chains (~50 kDa) + 2 identical Light chains (~25 kDa) — linked by disulfide bonds. 3. **Function:** Specifically bind antigens via the variable (V) region; mediate effector functions via the constant (Fc) region. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > Students frequently lose marks by writing only "antibodies are proteins" without specifying **glycoprotein nature** or omitting the **Y-shaped quaternary structure** with **2H + 2L chains**. Always mention **glycoprotein** explicitly — it is a KTU-favored keyword. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"List the five classes of human immunoglobulins and state the heavy chain that defines each class."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Class | Heavy Chain Type | | :--- | :--- | | IgG | **γ (gamma)** | | IgM | **μ (mu)** | | IgA | **α (alpha)** | | IgD | **δ (delta)** | | IgE | **ε (epsilon)** | The class of an antibody is **determined by the constant region of its heavy chain (C_H)**, which dictates its biological effector functions, serum half-life, and tissue distribution. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### **Question A — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the **detailed molecular structure of an IgG antibody molecule**. Highlight the Fab and Fc regions, light and heavy chains, variable and constant domains, and hinge region. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the **five classes of immunoglobulins**, tabulating their molecular weight, valence, serum concentration, half-life, and key biological functions. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Structure of IgG (7 marks):** The IgG monomer is a **Y-shaped glycoprotein** of molecular weight **~150 kDa**, consisting of **two identical heavy chains (H, ~50 kDa each)** and **two identical light chains (L, ~25 kDa each)**. **Structural components to be labeled in the diagram:** 1. **Two Heavy Chains:** Each has one V_H domain and three C_H domains (C_H1, C_H2, C_H3). The heavy chain defines the isotype (γ chain in IgG). 2. **Two Light Chains:** Each has one V_L and one C_L domain. Light chains are either **κ (kappa)** or **λ (lambda)** type. 3. **Variable Region (V):** The N-terminal V_H + V_L domains contain **Complementarity Determining Regions (CDRs)** — three on each chain (H1, H2, H3 and L1, L2, L3) — which form the **paratope (antigen-binding site)**. 4. **Constant Region (C):** The C-terminal domains determine isotype and effector function. The **Fc region** (C_H2 + C_H3 homodimer) binds complement (C1q) and Fc receptors on immune cells. 5. **Hinge Region:** A flexible proline-rich segment between C_H1 and C_H2 allowing the two Fab arms to move independently for optimal antigen binding. 6. **Disulfide Bonds:** Interchain disulfide bonds link H-L and H-H chains; intrachain disulfide bonds stabilize each Ig domain. (Examiner expects a labeled diagram with Fab, Fc, V_H, V_L, C_H1, C_H2, C_H3, C_L, hinge, and disulfide bonds marked. Verbal description of all of the above fetches full 7 marks.) --- **(b) Five Classes of Immunoglobulins (7 marks):** | Property | IgG | IgM | IgA | IgD | IgE | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Heavy chain | γ | μ | α | δ | ε | | Molecular form | Monomer | Pentamer + J | Mono / Dimer | Monomer | Monomer | | Molecular weight (kDa) | 150 | 900 | 160 / 320 | 180 | 190 | | Valence | 2 | 10 | 2 / 4 | 2 | 2 | | Serum concentration (% of total Ig) | ~75–80% | ~5–10% | ~10–15% | <1% | <0.01% | | Serum half-life | ~21 days | ~5 days | ~6 days | ~2.8 days | ~2 days | | Crosses placenta | **Yes** | No | No | No | No | | Activates complement (classical) | ++ (IgG1,3) | +++ (strongest) | − / + | − | − | | Key function | Secondary response, opsonization, neutralization | Primary response, complement activation, agglutination | Mucosal immunity (secretory IgA) | BCR on naive B cells | Allergy (Type I hypersensitivity), anti-parasite | **Biological functions to mention:** - **IgG:** Most abundant; opsonization; complement activation; placental transfer (passive immunity to neonate); neutralization. - **IgM:** First antibody produced in primary response; exists as pentamer (joined by J-chain); 10 antigen-binding sites → high avidity; excellent complement activator (classical pathway via C1q binding to Fc). - **IgA:** Predominant antibody in mucosal secretions (saliva, tears, gut, respiratory tract). Secretory IgA is a dimer with **secretory component (SC)** that protects it from proteolytic degradation in mucosal environments. - **IgD:** Co-expressed with IgM on the surface of mature naïve B cells as part of the **B-cell receptor (BCR)**. Function is mainly as a membrane BCR signaling molecule. - **IgE:** Binds with high affinity to **FcεRI receptors on mast cells and basophils**. Crosslinking by antigen triggers degranulation → Type I hypersensitivity (allergy, anaphylaxis). Also mediates defense against helminth parasites. --- #### **Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **biochemical and structural nature of antibodies**. Discuss why antibodies are classified as **glycoproteins** and describe the **immunoglobulin fold**. (7 marks) **(b)** Describe the **functions of the Fab and Fc regions** of an antibody. Explain how the Fc region mediates different effector functions such as **complement activation, opsonization, and placental transfer**. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Biochemical Nature of Antibodies (7 marks):** Antibodies are **glycoproteins** — this means their polypeptide chains are covalently attached to **carbohydrate (oligosaccharide) moieties**. The carbohydrate content ranges from **3% (IgG) to 12–14% (IgM, IgE, IgD)**. **Why antibodies are glycoproteins:** 1. **N-linked glycosylation** occurs primarily in the **C_H2 domain** of the heavy chain (and additional sites in C_H1 and C_L depending on the isotype). 2. Carbohydrate side chains are essential for: - Proper **folding and assembly** of the heavy chain in the ER. - **Secretion** from plasma cells. - **Effector function** — e.g., FcγR binding, complement C1q binding, and FcRn-mediated serum half-life extension are all glycosylation-dependent. - **Solubility and stability** of the antibody in serum. **The Immunoglobulin Fold:** - Each Ig domain (~110 amino acids, ~12 kDa) adopts a characteristic **β-sandwich fold** composed of **two antiparallel β-pleated sheets** (one 4-stranded, one 3-stranded) stabilized by an **intrachain disulfide bond** between conserved cysteine residues. - The fold contains **no α-helices**, which is unusual for a globular protein. - This fold is shared by many proteins of the **immunoglobulin superfamily** (IgSF), including TCR chains, MHC class I and II, CD4, CD8, and various cell adhesion molecules. **(b) Functions of Fab and Fc Regions (7 marks):** **Fab Region (Fragment antigen-binding):** - Composed of V_H + V_L + C_H1 + C_L. - Contains the **paratope** — the antigen-binding site formed by the 6 CDRs (3 from V_H, 3 from V_L). - **Function:** Specific recognition and binding of the **epitope** on the antigen. - Each IgG monomer has **2 Fab arms** → bivalent binding → can crosslink two identical epitopes. **Fc Region (Fragment crystallizable):** - Composed of C_H2 + C_H3 (homodimer of the two heavy chain C-terminal halves). - **Function:** Mediates all **effector functions** of the antibody through interaction with other components of the immune system. **Effector functions of Fc:** 1. **Complement Activation (Classical Pathway):** - Fc of IgG (subclasses 1, 3) or IgM binds **C1q** component of C1 complex. - This triggers the **classical complement cascade** → formation of **Membrane Attack Complex (MAC)** → lysis of pathogen. - IgM pentamer is the most efficient activator (a single IgM pentamer can activate C1, whereas two IgG molecules in close proximity are required). 2. **Opsonization and Phagocytosis:** - Fc of IgG binds **Fcγ receptors (FcγR)** on macrophages and neutrophils. - This bridges the pathogen to the phagocyte, dramatically enhancing **phagocytosis** (the process is called opsonization). - Also triggers **Antibody-Dependent Cell-mediated Cytotoxicity (ADCC)** when Fc binds FcγRIII (CD16) on NK cells. 3. **Placental Transfer (IgG only):** - Fc of IgG binds the **neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn)** on syncytiotrophoblast cells of the placenta. - This actively transports maternal IgG across the placenta to the fetus, conferring **passive humoral immunity** to the neonate for the first ~6 months of life. - This is the reason why IgG is the only isotype that crosses the placenta. 4. **Mast Cell / Basophil Binding (IgE):** - Fc of IgE binds with **very high affinity (K_d ~ 10⁻¹⁰ M)** to **FcεRI** on mast cells and basophils. - Crosslinking by multivalent antigen triggers degranulation → release of histamine and other mediators → **Type I hypersensitivity** (allergy, anaphylaxis). 5. **Mucosal Transport (IgA):** - Fc of dimeric IgA binds the **poly-Ig receptor (pIgR)** on the basolateral surface of mucosal epithelial cells. - The complex is transcytosed and the extracellular portion of pIgR remains attached as the **secretory component (SC)**, protecting IgA from proteolysis in the harsh mucosal environment. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Antibodies: Nature" Questions:** > > 1. **Missing "glycoprotein"** — Many students write only "protein" instead of "glycoprotein." The carbohydrate nature is a **mandatory KTU keyword**. Loss: 0.5–1 mark. > > 2. **Confusing "affinity" vs. "avidity"** — Affinity is the strength of binding of a *single* paratope to a *single* epitope (intrinsic property of one site). Avidity is the overall strength of binding of a *multivalent* antibody to a *multivalent* antigen (functional property). IgM has **low affinity but high avidity** due to its 10 binding sites. > > 3. **Wrong valence of IgM** — Some students write "5" instead of the theoretical "10" valence. The KTU-accepted answer is **10** (theoretical) with the note that **functional valence is 5–10** due to steric hindrance. > > 4. **Confusing CDR vs. FR** — The variable region contains both **Complementarity Determining Regions (CDRs / hypervariable regions)** which directly contact antigen, and **Framework Regions (FRs)** which provide the structural scaffold. CDRs are 3 per V domain (total 6 per Fab). > > 5. **Forgetting J-chain and Secretory Component** — When discussing IgM and IgA polymers, students often forget to mention the **J-chain (joining chain)** in pentameric IgM and the **secretory component (SC)** in secretory IgA. These are frequently asked KTU sub-parts. > > 6. **Wrong heavy chain isotype–function pairing** — For instance, stating that IgE is the most abundant serum Ig (it is IgG). Always memorize the table in Section 2.3. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 High-Yield Rapid-Revision Summary — "Antibodies: Nature"** > > ✅ Antibodies = **Immunoglobulins (Ig)** = **Y-shaped glycoproteins** secreted by **plasma cells**. > > ✅ Composition: **2 Heavy chains + 2 Light chains** linked by **disulfide bonds**. > > ✅ Heavy chain MW ≈ 50 kDa; Light chain MW ≈ 25 kDa; IgG monomer MW ≈ **150 kDa**. > > ✅ Light chain types: **κ (kappa)** and **λ (lambda)** — ratio ≈ **2:1** in humans. > > ✅ Each chain has **Variable (V) and Constant (C) domains** built on the **immunoglobulin fold** (~110 aa β-sandwich). > > ✅ **V region = paratope (antigen binding)**; **C region = effector function (Fc)**. > > ✅ **CDR (Complementarity Determining Regions)** = 3 per V domain = **6 CDRs per Fab** = direct antigen contact. > > ✅ **Five isotypes determined by heavy chain C region:** IgG (γ), IgM (μ), IgA (α), IgD (δ), IgE (ε). > > ✅ **Most abundant serum Ig = IgG** (~75–80%); **Longest half-life = IgG** (~21 days via FcRn recycling). > > ✅ **IgM is pentamer** (valence 10) with J-chain — first responder in primary infection. > > ✅ **IgA dimer** has **secretory component (SC)** — protects it in mucosal secretions. > > ✅ **IgE binds FcεRI on mast cells** — mediates allergy (Type I hypersensitivity). > > ✅ **Only IgG crosses placenta** (via FcRn receptor) — confers passive immunity to neonate. > > ✅ **Fab = antigen-binding**; **Fc = crystallizable / effector region**. > > ✅ Antibodies mediate: **Neutralization, Opsonization, Complement activation, ADCC, Agglutination, Precipitation, Maternal transfer, Mucosal immunity**. > > ✅ **Monoclonal antibody** = single specificity from a single B-cell clone; **Polyclonal antibody** = mixed specificities from many B-cell clones. > > ✅ Affinity = single-site binding strength ($K_a$); Avidity = overall functional binding strength (multi-site). ---

topics.title: Primary Structure of Immunoglobulins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Primary Structure of Immunoglobulins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Structure of an Immunoglobulin (Ig):**
> The **primary structure** of an immunoglobulin refers to the **linear sequence of amino acids** linked together by **peptide (covalent) bonds** that constitute the two identical heavy (H) polypeptide chains and the two identical light (L) polypeptide chains of the antibody molecule. It is the most fundamental level of protein organization, dictated by the nucleotide sequence of the immunoglobulin (Ig) gene, and forms the chemical blueprint from which the secondary, tertiary, and quaternary (Y-shaped) structures of the antibody are folded.

In molecular immunology, the primary structure is read from the **N-terminal (amino) end** to the **C-terminal (carboxyl) end**, and it determines the antigen-binding specificity (paratope) as well as the effector functions (Fc-mediated) of the immunoglobulin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEBBT631 - Module 2):**
> The primary structure analysis of immunoglobulins is foundational for understanding antibody engineering, monoclonal antibody production, and the genetic basis of antibody diversity. The KTU board frequently tests the **domain architecture**, **chain composition**, and **disulfide linkage patterns** of IgG as the prototype immunoglobulin.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **immunoglobulin molecule as a tailor-made lock-picking kit**:

- The **two heavy chains (H)** are the long, sturdy "tool handles" — like the body of a Swiss Army knife.
- The **two light chains (L)** are the shorter "blades" attached to the top, helping fine-tune the grip.
- Each chain is a **string of beads (amino acids)** — the **primary structure** is simply the exact order of these beads on the string.
- The first ~110 beads on each chain form the **variable region (V)** — this is the unique "fingerprint" that decides which specific germ (antigen) the antibody can grab.
- The remaining beads form the **constant region (C)** — this is the standard factory part that tells the immune system "I've caught something — come destroy it!"

Just as a single wrong bead can break a key's teeth, a **single amino acid mutation** in the primary structure can change antigen specificity or cause immunodeficiency (e.g., agammaglobulinemia).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Key Numerical Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical Values of the IgG Prototype:**
> - **Total Molecular Weight:** ~**150 kDa** (kiloDaltons)
> - **Heavy Chain (H):** ~**50 kDa** (≈ 440–450 amino acids)
> - **Light Chain (L):** ~**25 kDa** (≈ 214 amino acids)
> - **Number of Chains:** **4 polypeptide chains** (2H + 2L)
> - **Inter-chain Disulfide Bonds:** **1** (between H–H at hinge) + **2** (between H–L)
> - **Intra-chain Disulfide Bonds:** **4 per H chain**, **2 per L chain**
> - **Domains per H chain:** **4 (VH, CH1, CH2, CH3)** for IgG
> - **Domains per L chain:** **2 (VL, CL)**
> - **Amino acids per domain:** ~**110 amino acids** forming an immunoglobulin fold
> - **Carbohydrate content:** ~**2–3%** (attached to CH2 of IgG)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Primary Structure

The primary structure of an immunoglobulin is a hierarchical concept. Let's break it down as the KTU examiner would expect a student to articulate it:

**Step 1: The Fundamental Unit — L-α-Amino Acids**
- Each chain is a heteropolymer of **20 standard L-α-amino acids**.
- The general formula of an amino acid residue is:
$$\text{H}_2\text{N–CHR–COOH}$$
where **R** is the variable side chain. After peptide bond formation, the residue becomes:
$$–\text{NH–CHR–CO–}$$

**Step 2: Peptide Bond Formation (Covalent Linkage)**
- Amino acids are joined by **peptide bonds** (amide linkages) between the **α-carboxyl group** of one residue and the **α-amino group** of the next.
- Bond formation releases one water molecule (condensation):
$$\text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_1\text{–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_2\text{–COOH} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_1\text{–CO–NH–CHR}_2\text{–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 3: Polarity and Directionality**
- Every chain has a free **N-terminus (H₂N–)** and a free **C-terminus (–COOH)**.
- Polarity is critical: **VL and VH domains are at the N-terminal**, while **CL and CH domains are at the C-terminal**.

**Step 4: Chain Composition**
- **2 identical Heavy (H) chains:** molecular weight ~**50 kDa** each.
- **2 identical Light (L) chains:** molecular weight ~**25 kDa** each.
- Total: **2H + 2L = 4 chains** stabilized by **non-covalent interactions + disulfide (S–S) bonds**.

**Step 5: Variable vs Constant Region**
- The first **~110 amino acids** of each chain = **Variable (V) region** — antigen binding.
- The remaining amino acids = **Constant (C) region** — effector function & isotype.

**Step 6: Domain Architecture (Immunoglobulin Fold)**
- Each domain ≈ **110 amino acids**, internally stabilized by a **single intra-chain disulfide bond** forming a loop of ~60 amino acids.
- This fold is called the **"immunoglobulin fold"** — a sandwich of **2 anti-parallel β-sheets**.

**Step 7: Hinge Region (Only in IgG, IgA, IgD)**
- Located between **CH1 and CH2** of the heavy chain.
- Rich in **proline** and **cysteine** residues.
- Provides **flexibility** and contains the **inter-heavy-chain disulfide bonds**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table: Key Quantitative & Structural Data for Ig Primary Structure**

| Structural Element | Composition | Size (Amino Acids) | Key Covalent Feature | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Light Chain (L) | Polypeptide | ~214 | 2 intra-chain S–S bonds | Contributes to antigen binding & stabilization |
| Heavy Chain (H) | Polypeptide | ~440–450 | 4 intra-chain S–S bonds | Antigen binding + effector functions |
| Variable Region (V) | VH + VL | ~110 each | CDR loops (3 per chain) | Antigen-binding site (paratope) |
| Constant Region (C) | CL + CH1/CH2/CH3 | ~104–110 per domain | Glycosylation sites (CH2) | Effector functions, complement, Fc receptors |
| Hinge Region | Proline-rich | ~10–60 | Inter-H chain S–S bonds | Flexibility & Fab arm movement |
| Inter-chain S–S | H–L (×2) + H–H (×1 to ×4) | — | Covalent bridge | Stabilizes quaternary Y-shape |
| Whole IgG Molecule | 2H + 2L | ~1320 AA total | 4 inter-chain + 12 intra-chain S–S | Y-shaped bifunctional antibody |
| Carbohydrate (CHO) | N-linked glycan on CH2 | — | N–glycosidic | FcRn binding, complement activation |

### 2.3 Why & How — The Logic Behind the Structure

- **Why are there 2H + 2L chains?**
  The **2-fold symmetry** provides **bivalent antigen binding** (each Fab arm binds one epitope), increasing **functional affinity (avidity)** through the phenomenon of **bonus binding**.

- **Why are V regions at the N-terminus?**
  V regions are encoded by separate gene segments (**V, D, J**) that undergo **V(D)J recombination** early in B-cell development. Placing V at the N-terminus allows **transcriptional and translational regulation** to occur upstream of C-region genes (which determine isotype via class switch recombination).

- **Why ~110 amino acids per domain?**
  This is the minimum length required to form a stable **immunoglobulin fold** (a Greek-key β-sandwich) with a central intra-chain disulfide bond, which is energetically favorable and thermodynamically stable.

- **Why are disulfide bonds so critical in the primary structure analysis?**
  They are the only **covalent** cross-links in the otherwise non-covalently held quaternary structure. They survive SDS-PAGE under non-reducing conditions, making them the basis of antibody structural analysis in labs.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Biotechnology

- **Monoclonal Antibody (mAb) Engineering:** Knowing the primary sequence allows **chimerization** (human constant regions + mouse variable regions) and **humanization** to reduce immunogenicity.
- **Bispecific Antibodies:** Two different antigen-binding specificities are engineered by pairing mismatched heavy-light chains — only possible by precisely knowing the primary structure.
- **Phage Display Libraries:** Random mutations in CDR regions (positions ~31–35, 50–65, 95–102 in Kabat numbering) are screened for novel binding affinities.
- **Diagnostic Kits (ELISA, Western Blot):** Secondary antibodies rely on species-specific constant-region sequences for detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Molecular Weight of an IgG Molecule

**Given Data (from primary structure analysis):**
- 1 Heavy chain ≈ 440 amino acids × 110 Da/aa (average) ≈ **50 kDa**
- 1 Light chain ≈ 214 amino acids × 110 Da/aa ≈ **25 kDa**
- Carbohydrate content of IgG ≈ 3% by mass

**Step 1: Sum the polypeptide chain contributions**

$$\text{MW}_{\text{chains}} = (2 \times \text{MW}_H) + (2 \times \text{MW}_L)$$

$$\text{MW}_{\text{chains}} = (2 \times 50\,\text{kDa}) + (2 \times 25\,\text{kDa})$$

$$\text{MW}_{\text{chains}} = 100\,\text{kDa} + 50\,\text{kDa} = 150\,\text{kDa}$$

**Step 2: Add the carbohydrate contribution**

$$\text{MW}_{\text{CHO}} = 3\% \times 150\,\text{kDa} = 4.5\,\text{kDa}$$

**Step 3: Total molecular weight of IgG**

$$\text{MW}_{\text{IgG, total}} = 150\,\text{kDa} + 4.5\,\text{kDa} = 154.5\,\text{kDa}$$

This matches the experimentally observed value of **~150 kDa**, confirming the primary-structure-based calculation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Disulfide Bonds in an IgG Molecule

**Step 1: Intra-chain S–S bonds**
- Each L chain has 2 domains (VL, CL) → **2 S–S bonds per L chain**
- Each H chain has 4 domains (VH, CH1, CH2, CH3) → **4 S–S bonds per H chain**

$$\text{Intra-chain S–S} = (2 \times 2) + (2 \times 4) = 4 + 8 = 12 \text{ bonds}$$

**Step 2: Inter-chain S–S bonds (IgG1 prototype)**
- 2 H–L bonds (one per L chain, at Cys 214 of L ↔ Cys 220 of H)
- 2 H–H bonds in the hinge region (IgG1 specific; IgG2 has 4, IgG3 has 11)

$$\text{Inter-chain S–S (IgG1)} = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ bonds}$$

**Step 3: Total disulfide bonds**

$$\text{Total S–S (IgG1)} = 12 + 4 = 16 \text{ bonds}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Domain Sequence in a Heavy Chain (N → C terminal)

The KTU examiner often asks students to write the domain order in a heavy chain. Let us derive it systematically based on the **genomic organization** of the Ig heavy chain locus (Chromosome 14q32):

$$\text{V region genes} \rightarrow \text{D} \rightarrow \text{J} \rightarrow \text{C}\mu \rightarrow \text{C}\delta \rightarrow \text{C}\gamma 3 \rightarrow \text{C}\gamma 1 \rightarrow \text{C}\alpha 1 \rightarrow \text{C}\gamma 2 \rightarrow \text{C}\gamma 4 \rightarrow \text{C}\varepsilon \rightarrow \text{C}\alpha 2$$

Therefore, the **translated primary structure** of an IgG heavy chain reads:

$$\text{NH}_2 – \text{VH} – \text{CH1} – \text{Hinge} – \text{CH2} – \text{CH3} – \text{COOH}$$

**Length calculation:**
$$\text{Length}_H = 110_{(\text{VH})} + 110_{(\text{CH1})} + 30_{(\text{Hinge})} + 110_{(\text{CH2})} + 110_{(\text{CH3})} \approx 470 \text{ aa}$$

(experimental values range 440–470 aa depending on the IgG subclass).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Light Chain Types (κ vs λ)

| Feature | **Kappa (κ) Light Chain** | **Lambda (λ) Light Chain** |
|---|---|---|
| Gene locus | Chromosome **2p11.2** | Chromosome **22q11.2** |
| Number of V gene segments | ~**76** Vκ | ~**33** Vλ |
| Number of J segments | **5** Jκ | **7** Jλ (some non-functional) |
| Constant region isotypes | **1 (Cκ)** | **4 (Cλ1, Cλ2, Cλ3, Cλ7)** |
| Frequency in human serum | **~65%** | **~35%** |
| Disulfide position to H chain | **Cys 214 (Kabat)** | **Cys 214 (Kabat)** |
| Structural hallmark | **C-terminal Cys** for H–L S–S bond | **C-terminal Cys + extra Ser** |
| Kλ ratio in normal human | **~2:1** | — |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Heavy Chain Classes & Their Primary-Structure Determinants

| Class | Heavy Chain | Molecular Weight (H chain) | Hinge Region | Number of S–S bonds (H–H) | Serum Half-life |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IgG** | γ (gamma) | ~50 kDa | Present (flexible) | 2 (IgG1) | **~21 days** |
| **IgM** | μ (mu) | ~65 kDa | Absent (has CH4) | 1 | ~5 days |
| **IgA** | α (alpha) | ~55 kDa | Present | 2 | ~6 days |
| **IgD** | δ (delta) | ~65 kDa | Present (long) | 1 | ~2.8 days |
| **IgE** | ε (epsilon) | ~70 kDa | Absent (has CH4) | 1 | ~2 days |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IgM and IgE are heavier:** They contain **4 constant domains (CH1–CH4)** in the heavy chain, increasing the polypeptide length by ~110 amino acids compared to IgG.

---

### 3.6 Derivation: Number of Antigen-Binding Sites (Paratopes)

Each Fab fragment contains:
- **1 VH domain** + **1 VL domain** = 1 paratope
- An IgG has 2 Fab arms, so:

$$N_{\text{paratopes per IgG}} = 2$$

For a secretory IgM pentamer (5 monomers joined by J-chain):

$$N_{\text{paratopes per IgM}} = 5 \times 2 = 10 \text{ theoretical binding sites}$$

(steric hindrance makes ~**5 functionally accessible**).

---

### 3.7 Kabat Numbering System — Primary Structure Reference Points

The **Kabat numbering system** is the standard reference used to locate specific amino acid positions in the primary structure of immunoglobulins.

- **Framework Regions (FR):** ~85% of the V domain — relatively conserved.
- **Complementarity-Determining Regions (CDR):** ~15% of the V domain — hypervariable.

| Region | Kabat Position (approx.) | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **FR1** | 1–26 | Structural scaffold |
| **CDR1** | 27–38 | Antigen contact |
| **FR2** | 39–55 | Structural scaffold |
| **CDR2** | 56–65 | Antigen contact |
| **FR3** | 66–94 | Structural scaffold |
| **CDR3** | 95–102 | **Most variable** — central to specificity |
| **FR4** | 103–113 | Joins to C region |

$$\text{Length of V domain} = \sum_{i=1}^{4}\text{FR}_i + \sum_{j=1}^{3}\text{CDR}_j \approx 113 \text{ aa}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block Architecture of the IgG Primary Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Light Chain L1\n214 aa, ~25 kDa"] --> D1["VL Domain\naa 1 to 108\nCDR1, CDR2, CDR3"]
    D1 --> D2["CL Domain\naa 109 to 214\nKappa or Lambda"]
    L2["Light Chain L2\n214 aa, ~25 kDa"] --> D3["VL Domain 2\naa 1 to 108"]
    D3 --> D4["CL Domain 2\naa 109 to 214"]
    H1["Heavy Chain H1\n440 aa, ~50 kDa"] --> D5["VH Domain\nCDR1, CDR2, CDR3"]
    D5 --> D6["CH1 Domain"]
    D6 --> D7["Hinge Region\nPro-Cys rich"]
    D7 --> D8["CH2 Domain\nN-glycosylation site"]
    D8 --> D9["CH3 Domain\nFcRn binding"]
    H2["Heavy Chain H2\n440 aa, ~50 kDa"] --> D10["VH Domain 2"]
    D10 --> D11["CH1 Domain 2"]
    D11 --> D12["Hinge Region 2"]
    D12 --> D13["CH2 Domain 2"]
    D13 --> D14["CH3 Domain 2"]
    D1 --- B1["Antigen Binding Site\nParatope 1"]
    D3 --- B2["Antigen Binding Site\nParatope 2"]
    D9 --- B3["Fc Effector Region\nComplement + FcR binding"]
    D14 --- B3
    D1 -.SS1.-> D5
    D3 -.SS2.-> D10
    H1 -.SS3.-> H2
    H2 -.SS4.-> H1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Linear Primary Structure of One Heavy Chain (IgG Prototype)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["N-terminus\nFree NH2 group"] --> B["VH\n~110 aa\nVariable + 3 CDRs"]
    B --> C["CH1\n~110 aa\nConstant beta domain"]
    C --> D["HINGE\n~30 aa\nPro-Cys rich\nInter-H S-S bonds"]
    D --> E["CH2\n~110 aa\nGlycosylation Asn297"]
    E --> F["CH3\n~110 aa\nFcRn + Protein A binding"]
    F --> G["C-terminus\nFree COOH group"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Mapping of IgG Domains

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Fab["Fab Fragment\nAntigen Binding Arm"] --> F1["VH: Antigen Contact\n3 CDR loops"]
    Fab --> F2["VL: Antigen Contact\n3 CDR loops"]
    Fab --> F3["CH1: Stabilization\n+ L chain anchor"]
    Fab --> F4["CL: L chain stabilization\n+ Isotype marker"]
    Fc["Fc Fragment\nEffector Region"] --> F5["CH2: C1q binding\nComplement activation"]
    Fc --> F6["CH3: FcgammaR binding\nADCC, Placental transfer"]
    Hinge["Hinge Region"] --> H1["Flexibility\nbetween Fab arms"]
    Hinge --> H2["Inter-H chain\ndisulfide bridges"]
    Fab --- Hinge
    Hinge --- Fc
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Organization from Primary to Quaternary Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["PRIMARY Structure\nLinear amino acid sequence\nPeptide bonds"]
    S["SECONDARY Structure\nAlpha helix + Beta sheets\nHydrogen bonds"]
    T["TERTIARY Structure\n3D immunoglobulin fold\nIntra-chain S-S bonds"]
    Q["QUATERNARY Structure\n2H + 2L Y-shape\nInter-chain S-S bonds"]
    P --> S
    S --> T
    T --> Q
    P -.Encoded by.-> G["Ig Genes\nV-D-J recombination\nClass switch recombination"]
    G --> P
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define the primary structure of an immunoglobulin. Mention the number of polypeptide chains and their molecular weights in a typical IgG molecule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The primary structure of an immunoglobulin is the **linear sequence of amino acids** linked by **peptide bonds** that constitute the polypeptide chains of the antibody molecule. A typical IgG molecule consists of **4 polypeptide chains — 2 identical heavy chains (~50 kDa each, ~440 amino acids)** and **2 identical light chains (~25 kDa each, ~214 amino acids)**, giving a total molecular weight of **~150 kDa**. *(1 mark each for definition, chain count, molecular weight.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the domains present in a heavy chain of IgG. What is the role of the hinge region?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An IgG heavy chain contains **4 domains** (1+1+1+1):
1. **VH** (Variable Heavy) — antigen binding
2. **CH1** (Constant Heavy 1) — light chain anchor
3. **CH2** (Constant Heavy 2) — complement binding & glycosylation
4. **CH3** (Constant Heavy 3) — Fc receptor binding

The **hinge region** lies between **CH1 and CH2**, is rich in **proline and cysteine residues**, and provides **flexibility** to the Fab arms while housing the **inter-heavy-chain disulfide bonds**. *(1 mark for domains, 1 mark for hinge location, 1 mark for hinge function.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **basic four-chain structure of an immunoglobulin**, with a neat labeled diagram. Mention all intra-chain and inter-chain disulfide bonds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between the **Variable (V) and Constant (C) regions** of an immunoglobulin. Explain the **CDR loops** and their role in antigen binding using the **Kabat numbering system**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Four-Chain Structure of an Immunoglobulin (7 marks):**

An immunoglobulin (taking **IgG as the prototype**) is a **Y-shaped glycoprotein** consisting of **4 polypeptide chains** held together by covalent disulfide bonds and non-covalent interactions.

**Structural components:**

- **2 identical Heavy (H) chains** — each ~**50 kDa**, ~**440 amino acids** long.
- **2 identical Light (L) chains** — each ~**25 kDa**, ~**214 amino acids** long.
- The chains are arranged as: **H–L** on the left arm and **H–L** on the right arm, joined at the **hinge region**.

**Disulfide bonds:**

- **Intra-chain disulfide bonds:** **4 per H chain** (one in each domain: VH, CH1, CH2, CH3) and **2 per L chain** (one in VL, one in CL) → **total = 12 intra-chain S–S bonds**. *(2 marks)*
- **Inter-chain disulfide bonds (IgG1):** **2 H–L bonds** (linking the C-terminal cysteine of each L chain to CH1 of the H chain) and **2 H–H bonds** in the hinge region → **total = 4 inter-chain S–S bonds**. *(2 marks)*

**Overall molecular formula:**

$$M_r(\text{IgG}) = 2(50{,}000) + 2(25{,}000) + 4{,}500_{\text{carbohydrate}} \approx 154{,}500\,\text{Da}$$

**Total S–S bonds:**

$$N_{S-S} = 12_{\text{intra}} + 4_{\text{inter}} = 16 \text{ disulfide bonds per IgG1 molecule}$$

*(2 marks for diagram + 1 mark for summary)*

A **neat labeled diagram** should show:
- Two H chains forming the stem and inner arms of the Y.
- Two L chains forming the outer arms of the Y.
- Variable regions (VH, VL) at the tips.
- Constant regions (CL, CH1, CH2, CH3) in the body and stem.
- Hinge region marked between CH1 and CH2.
- All disulfide bonds marked clearly.

---

**(b) Variable vs Constant Regions & CDR Loops (7 marks):**

| Feature | **Variable (V) Region** | **Constant (C) Region** |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | N-terminal ~110 amino acids of each chain | C-terminal ~104–110 amino acids per domain |
| **Chains involved** | VH (in H chain) + VL (in L chain) | CH1, CH2, CH3 (in H) + CL (in L) |
| **Sequence variability** | Highly variable between different antibodies | Nearly identical within an isotype class |
| **Function** | **Antigen binding** (forms the paratope) | **Effector functions** — complement, Fc receptor binding, placental transfer |
| **Genetic basis** | Encoded by V, (D), J gene segments | Encoded by C gene segments |
| **Number per IgG** | 2 (one at the tip of each Fab arm) | 4 (3 in H + 1 in L per heavy-light pair) |

*(3 marks for the comparison table)*

**CDR Loops and Kabat Numbering:**

The V region of each chain contains **3 Complementarity-Determining Regions (CDRs)** — short, hypervariable loops that make direct contact with the antigen. The remaining ~85% of the V region forms **Framework Regions (FRs)** that provide the structural scaffold.

| Region | Kabat Position | Length (approx.) | Hypervariability |
|---|---|---|---|
| FR1 | 1–26 | 26 aa | Low |
| **CDR1** | 27–38 | 12 aa | **High** |
| FR2 | 39–55 | 17 aa | Low |
| **CDR2** | 56–65 | 10 aa | **High** |
| FR3 | 66–94 | 29 aa | Low |
| **CDR3** | 95–102 | 8 aa (up to 20+) | **Highest** — formed at V-D-J junction |
| FR4 | 103–113 | 11 aa | Low |

*(3 marks for CDR details + 1 mark for the role in antigen binding)*

**Role of CDRs in antigen binding:**
- **CDR1 and CDR2** of both H and L chains make peripheral contacts with the antigen.
- **CDR3** of the heavy chain (positioned at the V-D-J junction) is the **most diverse** loop and forms the **central contact point** of the paratope.
- The 6 CDRs (3 from H + 3 from L) together form the **complementarity-determining surface** that physically grips the antigen's **epitope**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **primary structure of a light chain** in detail. Compare **kappa (κ)** and **lambda (λ)** light chains with respect to gene loci, frequency, and structural features. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **classification of heavy chains** into five isotypes. Tabulate the molecular weight, hinge region presence, and number of disulfide bonds for each class. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Primary Structure of Light Chains (7 marks):**

A light (L) chain is a polypeptide of **~214 amino acids** with a molecular weight of **~25 kDa**. It contains **2 domains**, each of ~110 amino acids:

**Structural architecture:**

$$N\text{-terminus} \rightarrow \text{V}_L \text{ (residues 1–108)} \rightarrow \text{C}_L \text{ (residues 109–214)} \rightarrow C\text{-terminus}$$

- **VL domain:** Contains the **3 CDR loops** that contribute to antigen binding. Stabilized by **1 intra-chain disulfide bond** between Cys residues (typically Cys 23 and Cys 88 by Kabat numbering).
- **CL domain:** Contains **1 intra-chain disulfide bond** (Cys 134 and Cys 194). The C-terminal **Cys 214** forms the **H–L inter-chain disulfide bond** with the heavy chain.

**Total intra-chain S–S bonds per L chain = 2.**

**Comparison of Kappa (κ) and Lambda (λ) Light Chains:**

| Feature | **Kappa (κ)** | **Lambda (λ)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Gene locus (chromosome)** | **2p11.2** | **22q11.2** |
| **Number of V gene segments** | ~76 functional Vκ genes | ~33 Vλ genes (more limited) |
| **J gene segments** | 5 Jκ | 4–7 Jλ |
| **Constant region isotypes** | **1 (Cκ only)** | **4 (Cλ1, Cλ2, Cλ3, Cλ7)** |
| **Serum frequency (human)** | **~65%** | **~35%** |
| **κ/λ ratio in healthy adults** | ~2:1 | ~1:2 (approx.) |
| **CL domain length** | ~107 aa | ~107 aa |
| **C-terminal residue** | **Cysteine (forms H–L S–S)** | **Cysteine (also H–L S–S)** |
| **Diagnostic significance** | Clonal κ restriction seen in **B-cell lymphomas** | Clonal λ restriction also diagnostic |

*(4 marks for light chain architecture, 3 marks for κ vs λ table)*

**Key structural features common to all L chains:**
- Both are **glycosylation-free** (no N-linked glycans, unlike heavy chains).
- Both contain the **J-chain-independent** quaternary structure.
- Both are linked to the H chain through a **single inter-chain disulfide bond**.

---

**(b) Heavy Chain Isotypes and Their Primary Structure Features (7 marks):**

The **class (isotype) of an immunoglobulin is determined by the constant region of its heavy chain**. There are **5 classes of heavy chains** in humans:

$$\gamma \,(\text{IgG}), \quad \mu \,(\text{IgM}), \quad \alpha \,(\text{IgA}), \quad \delta \,(\text{IgD}), \quad \varepsilon \,(\text{IgE})$$

**Detailed Tabulation:**

| Class | Heavy Chain | H-Chain MW | H-Chain Length | Constant Domains | Hinge Region | Inter-H S–S Bonds | Carbohydrate (%) | Serum Conc. (mg/dL) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IgG** | γ | ~50 kDa | ~440 aa | **3 (CH1, CH2, CH3)** | Present | 2–4 | 2–3% | 800–1500 |
| **IgM** | μ | ~65 kDa | ~570 aa | **4 (CH1–CH4)** | **Absent** | 1 | 10–12% | 50–200 |
| **IgA** | α | ~55 kDa | ~470 aa | **3 (CH1–CH3)** | Present | 2 | 7–11% | 150–400 |
| **IgD** | δ | ~65 kDa | ~500 aa | **3 (CH1–CH3) + long hinge** | **Long, exposed** | 1 | 9–14% | 0.3–40 |
| **IgE** | ε | ~70 kDa | ~550 aa | **4 (CH1–CH4)** | **Absent** | 1 | 12–14% | 0.001–0.05 |

*(4 marks for the table)*

**Key distinguishing features of each class (1 mark each):**

- **IgG (γ):** Most abundant serum Ig; only Ig that crosses the placenta; opsonization; complement activation via classical pathway.
- **IgM (μ):** First Ig produced in primary immune response; exists as a **pentamer** with J chain; **10 antigen-binding sites**; very efficient complement activator.
- **IgA (α):** Exists as **monomer in serum** and **dimer in secretions** (with secretory component); found in mucosal immunity (tears, saliva, gut).
- **IgD (δ):** Co-expressed with IgM on **naive B-cell surface** as BCR; very low serum concentration; long hinge region susceptible to proteolysis.
- **IgE (ε):** Lowest serum concentration; binds **FcεRI on mast cells & basophils**; mediates **Type I hypersensitivity (allergy)** and anti-helminth defense.

*(2 marks for the key features explanation)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "primary" with "quaternary" structure:** Primary = **amino acid sequence**, not the Y-shape. The Y-shape is the **quaternary** structure. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> 2. **Writing "IgG has 2 disulfide bonds"** — this is incomplete. Specify **12 intra-chain + 4 inter-chain = 16 total** for IgG1. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Mixing up VH and VL positions** — both are at the **N-terminus**, not "VH at top, VL at bottom". *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Forgetting the hinge region** is only in **γ, α, δ** chains — **NOT in μ and ε** (which instead have an extra CH4 domain).
> 5. **Writing 4 disulfide bonds in IgG1 hinge** — this is **IgG2-specific**. The number varies: **IgG1 = 2, IgG2 = 4, IgG3 = 11, IgG4 = 2**.
> 6. **Confusing κ and λ loci** — κ = **chromosome 2**, λ = **chromosome 22**. Many students write "chromosome 22 for kappa" — this loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Not stating molecular weights with units (kDa or Da)** — always include units.
> 8. **In CDR numbering, mixing Kabat with IMGT** — stick to **one numbering system** consistently in your answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Primary Structure of Immunoglobulins**

- ✅ Immunoglobulin = **glycoprotein** with **2H + 2L chains** (4 chains total).
- ✅ Heavy chain = **~50 kDa (~440 aa)**, Light chain = **~25 kDa (~214 aa)**.
- ✅ Total IgG molecular weight = **~150 kDa**.
- ✅ Each chain has a **Variable (V) region** (N-terminal, ~110 aa) and a **Constant (C) region** (C-terminal, ~110 aa per domain).
- ✅ IgG H chain domains: **VH–CH1–Hinge–CH2–CH3**.
- ✅ IgM and IgE H chains have **4 constant domains (CH1–CH4)**; no hinge region.
- ✅ **CDR loops** = 3 per V domain (CDR1, CDR2, CDR3); **CDR3 is the most variable** (V-D-J junction in H chain).
- ✅ Kabat numbering positions: CDR1 (27–38), CDR2 (56–65), CDR3 (95–102).
- ✅ **Intra-chain S–S bonds** = 12 total (4 per H + 2 per L).
- ✅ **Inter-chain S–S bonds (IgG1)** = 4 (2 H–L + 2 H–H in hinge).
- ✅ **κ light chain** = chromosome **2p11.2**, **~65%** of human serum Ig.
- ✅ **λ light chain** = chromosome **22q11.2**, **~35%** of human serum Ig.
- ✅ **Hinge region** contains **proline** (flexibility) and **cysteine** (disulfide bonds) residues.
- ✅ **5 heavy chain classes**: γ (IgG), μ (IgM), α (IgA), δ (IgD), ε (IgE).
- ✅ **Each domain (~110 aa)** has a conserved **intra-chain disulfide bond** forming the **immunoglobulin fold** (β-sandwich).
- ✅ N-terminus is at the **antigen-binding tips**; C-terminus is at the **Fc stem**.
- ✅ V region is encoded by **V(D)J gene segments**; C region determines **isotype** via **class switch recombination**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Heavy Chain Classes (in order of serum abundance):**
> **"GAME-D"** → **G** > **A** > **M** > **E** > **D** (IgG highest, IgD lowest in serum)
> **Mnemonic for IgG subclass S–S bonds:**
> **"1-2-3-4"** → IgG1=**2**, IgG2=**4**, IgG3=**11**, IgG4=**2**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Immunoglobulins: Types - IgG, IgM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Classification of Immunoglobulins: Types - IgG, IgM # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Immunoglobulins (Ig)** are Y-shaped glycoprotein molecules produced by differentiated B lymphocytes (plasma cells) that bind specifically to antigens and mediate humoral immunity. They function both as **antigen receptors (BCR)** on naive B cells and as **soluble effector antibodies** secreted into body fluids. Based on the **amino acid sequence and structure of the constant (C) region of the heavy chain**, immunoglobulins are classified into **five distinct isotypes (classes)** in humans: | Class | Heavy Chain | Subclasses | |:-----:|:-----------:|:----------:| | **IgG** | Gamma (γ) | IgG1, IgG2, IgG3, IgG4 | | **IgM** | Mu (μ) | None (in humans) | | IgA | Alpha (α) | IgA1, IgA2 | | IgE | Epsilon (ε) | None | | IgD | Delta (δ) | None | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This module specifically emphasizes the structural, functional, and clinical differences between **IgG and IgM**, the two most diagnostically significant immunoglobulin classes in serology. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Immune System's Armed Forces" Think of immunoglobulins as the **specialized armed forces of the immune system**, where each class is a different military unit trained for a specific mission: - **IgG = The Elite Special Forces / Long-Range Snipers** 🎯 - Highly trained, precise, long-lasting, can operate deep behind enemy lines (crosses placenta to protect the fetus). - **IgM = The First-Alert Rapid Response Team** 🚨 - The first to arrive at the scene of infection. Loud, bulky, and powerful. Functions as the **"first antibody produced"** during any fresh infection. > [!NOTE] > **Key Conceptual Memory Hook:** *"IgM = 'M' for 'Morning' (first to appear); IgG = 'G' for 'Guard' (long-term protection)."* ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Numerical Constants - **Molecular weight of IgG:** ~**150 kDa** (monomer) - **Molecular weight of IgM:** ~**900 kDa** (pentamer, 5 × 180 kDa monomers + J chain) - **Serum half-life of IgG:** ~**21 days** (longest among all immunoglobulins) - **Serum half-life of IgM:** ~**5–10 days** - **Serum concentration of IgG:** ~**8–16 mg/mL** (75–80% of total serum Ig) - **Serum concentration of IgM:** ~**0.5–2 mg/mL** (5–10% of total serum Ig) - **Sedimentation coefficient:** IgG = **7S**; IgM = **19S** # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 IgG — The Workhorse of Humoral Immunity ### 2.1.1 Structural Architecture IgG is a **monomeric Y-shaped glycoprotein** consisting of: - **2 identical heavy (H) chains** — γ-type, ~50 kDa each - **2 identical light (L) chains** — either κ (kappa) or λ (lambda), ~25 kDa each - **Inter-chain disulfide bonds** linking H–H, H–L chains - **Intra-chain disulfide bonds** forming the characteristic immunoglobulin **domain fold** (β-barrel / "immunoglobulin fold") - **Hinge region** — a flexible proline-rich segment between CH1 and CH2 domains, providing segmental flexibility for antigen binding ### 2.1.2 Heavy Chain Domain Organization The γ heavy chain has **3 constant domains**: $$\text{VH} - \text{CH1} - \text{Hinge} - \text{CH2} - \text{CH3}$$ ### 2.1.3 Functional Regions | Region | Composition | Function | |:------:|:-----------:|:--------| | **Fab (Fragment antigen-binding)** | VL + CL + VH + CH1 | Specific antigen binding (2 per IgG) | | **Fc (Fragment crystallizable)** | CH2 + CH3 | Effector functions: complement binding, Fc receptor binding, placental transfer | | **Hinge region** | Proline-rich linker | Flexibility, accessibility of Fab arms | ### 2.1.4 The Four IgG Subclasses — Critical Sub-Distinctions > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** Subclass differences hinge on **hinge region length, disulfide bond number, and complement activation capacity**. | Property | IgG1 | IgG2 | IgG3 | IgG4 | |:--------:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:| | % of total IgG | ~65% | ~23% | ~8% | ~4% | | Hinge length | Short | Short | **Longest (~4× IgG1)** | Medium | | Complement activation | ++ | + | **+++ (strongest)** | – | | FcRn binding | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Protein antigen response | ++ | – | ++ | ++ | | Polysaccharide response | + | **+++** | + | – | ### 2.1.5 Biological Functions of IgG 1. **Opsonization** — Fc binds Fcγ receptors on macrophages, neutrophils → enhanced phagocytosis. 2. **Complement activation (Classical Pathway)** — IgG1 and IgG3 are strong activators; IgG4 does **not** activate complement. 3. **Antibody-Dependent Cellular Cytotoxicity (ADCC)** — Fc engages FcγRIII (CD16) on NK cells. 4. **Neutralization** — blocks toxins, viruses, bacterial adhesins. 5. **Placental transfer** — **ONLY immunoglobulin crossing the placenta** (via FcRn receptor on syncytiotrophoblast). Confers **passive immunity** to the neonate for the first 6 months of life. 6. **Secondary / Memory response** — Predominant antibody class in anamnestic (recall) responses. --- ## 2.2 IgM — The Pioneer of the Primary Response ### 2.2.1 Structural Architecture — The Pentamer IgM exists predominantly as a **pentamer** of 5 monomeric subunits, giving it a "starfish" or "mushroom-like" shape. - **5 monomeric units** each containing 2 μ heavy chains + 2 light chains - **J chain (Joining chain)** — a 15 kDa polypeptide, disulfide-linked to the Fc regions of two adjacent monomers; essential for polymerization and secretion. - **Theoretical 10 antigen-binding sites**, but steric hindrance reduces functional binding sites to **5**. ### 2.2.2 Heavy Chain Domain Organization The μ heavy chain has **4 constant domains** (one more than IgG) and **lacks a classical hinge region**: $$\text{VH} - \text{CH1} - \text{CH2} - \text{CH3} - \text{CH4}$$ > [!NOTE] > The CH2 domain in IgM replaces the hinge region and contains a covalently bound low-affinity **C1q binding site**, conferring superior complement-activating efficiency. ### 2.2.3 Biological Functions of IgM 1. **Most potent activator of the Classical Complement Pathway** — a single pentameric IgM molecule can initiate the cascade (one IgM ≈ thousands of IgG molecules for C1q activation). 2. **Natural antibodies** — Pre-existing IgM (anti-A, anti-B blood group antigens) provides innate-like first-line defense. 3. **Primary immune response** — The **first immunoglobulin produced** upon initial antigen exposure (3–5 days post-exposure). 4. **B cell receptor (mIgM)** — Membrane-bound monomeric IgM serves as the antigen receptor on naive B cells. 5. **Agglutination** — Pentameric structure makes IgM exceptionally efficient at **cross-linking particulate antigens** (e.g., bacteria, RBCs). 6. **Does NOT cross the placenta** (J chain and large size prevent FcRn transport). --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparative Formula Sheet | Feature | **IgG** | **IgM** | |:--------|:-------:|:-------:| | Heavy chain class | γ (gamma) | μ (mu) | | Structure | **Monomer** | **Pentamer (+ J chain)** | | Molecular weight | **~150 kDa** | **~900 kDa** | | Sedimentation coefficient | **7S** | **19S** | | Serum concentration | **8–16 mg/mL (75–80%)** | **0.5–2 mg/mL (5–10%)** | | Half-life | **~21 days** | **~5–10 days** | | Constant domains in H chain | 3 (CH1, CH2, CH3) | 4 (CH1, CH2, CH3, CH4) | | Hinge region | **Present** | **Absent** (replaced by CH2) | | Antigen-binding sites | 2 (functional) | 10 theoretical / 5 functional | | Placental transfer | **YES (only Ig)** | **NO** | | Complement activation | + (IgG1, IgG3 mainly) | **+++ (strongest activator)** | | Agglutination efficiency | Poor (monomer) | **Excellent (pentamer)** | | Opsonization | **Strong** | Weak | | ADCC | Yes (IgG1, IgG3) | No | | Immune response phase | **Secondary / Memory** | **Primary (first responder)** | | Subclasses in humans | IgG1, IgG2, IgG3, IgG4 | None | | Clinical serology marker | Past / chronic exposure | Recent / acute infection | | BCR form | No | **Yes (mIgM on naive B cells)** | | J chain association | No | **Yes** | --- ## 2.4 Clinical & Diagnostic Utility (Real-World Engineering Analogy) In **diagnostic biotechnology**, IgG vs IgM detection forms the cornerstone of **serological test kits** (ELISA, chemiluminescence, lateral flow assays): - **IgM positivity** → indicates **acute / recent infection** (e.g., anti-HAV IgM, anti-HBc IgM in hepatitis). - **IgG positivity** → indicates **past infection, vaccination, or chronic exposure** (e.g., anti-HBs IgG, anti-Rubella IgG in pregnancy). - The **IgG to IgM seroconversion** is the gold-standard diagnostic criterion for many viral infections (TORCH screening). > [!TIP] > **Engineering Parallel:** A serological IgM/IgG test is analogous to a **system event-log diagnostic tool** — IgM is the "recent error log" and IgG is the "archived historical log." # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Stoichiometry of Antigen-Binding Sites in IgG vs IgM This derivation demonstrates the quantitative superiority of IgM for multivalent antigen cross-linking — a frequently tested KTU numerical concept. ### Step 1: Define the basic structural unit $$\text{Basic Ig unit} = 2H + 2L = 1 \text{ Y-shaped monomer}$$ Each monomer possesses **2 antigen-binding (Fab) sites** — one at each tip of the Y. ### Step 2: Count Fab sites in IgG $$\text{Fab sites}_{IgG} = 1 \text{ monomer} \times 2 = 2 \text{ binding sites}$$ ### Step 3: Count Fab sites in IgM (theoretical maximum) $$\text{Fab sites}_{IgM, \text{theoretical}} = 5 \text{ monomers} \times 2 = 10 \text{ binding sites}$$ ### Step 4: Apply the steric correction factor Due to spatial constraints of the pentameric structure and J chain positioning, only **5 of the 10 sites can simultaneously engage antigen** (the "5 functional sites" model). $$\text{Fab sites}_{IgM, \text{functional}} = 5$$ ### Step 5: Compute the avidity (functional binding valency) ratio $$\text{Avidity ratio} = \frac{\text{Fab sites}_{IgM, \text{functional}}}{\text{Fab sites}_{IgG}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** IgM has **2.5× higher functional valency** than IgG. Combined with its pentameric geometry, this explains IgM's **avidity superiority** (avidity = combined strength of multivalent binding), even though individual Fab affinity is lower. --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Quantitative Complement Activation Efficiency This derivation shows why a single IgM pentamer is more efficient at complement activation than thousands of IgG monomers. ### Step 1: Define the C1q binding requirement C1q (the initiating molecule of the classical complement pathway) must simultaneously bind **at least 2 Fc regions** spaced ~30 nm apart. ### Step 2: Spatial geometry of IgG vs IgM - **IgG:** 2 Fc regions, but they are only **~7–10 nm apart** when flexed — generally **insufficient** to bridge C1q efficiently. Hence, **multiple IgG molecules (≥2) must cluster** on an antigen surface. - **IgM pentamer:** The 5 Fc regions are **fixed in a planar "table-top" configuration** spaced **~30 nm apart** — **optimal geometry** for a **single IgM molecule** to bind C1q. ### Step 3: Threshold for C1q activation $$\text{IgG threshold} \geq 2 \text{ molecules on adjacent epitopes}$$ $$\text{IgM threshold} = 1 \text{ pentamer molecule}$$ ### Step 4: Relative activation efficiency ratio $$\text{Complement activation ratio}_{IgM/IgG} \approx 1000 \text{ to } 4000 \text{-fold higher per mole}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is why IgM is considered the **"first responder"** of the complement cascade during primary infection. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — IgG vs IgM in the Time-Domain of an Immune Response | Time Post-Antigen Exposure (Days) | Predominant Ig | Affinity Maturation | Complement Activity | |:---------------------------------:|:--------------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:| | 0–3 | None | – | – | | **3–7** | **IgM (peak)** | No (low affinity) | **Maximum** | | 7–14 | IgM declining, IgG rising | Ongoing (GC reaction) | Moderate | | **14–28** | **IgG (peak)** | Yes (somatic hypermutation) | Moderate (IgG1/IgG3) | | >28 | IgG (memory) | High-affinity IgG | Adaptive recall | ### Mathematical Representation of Class Switching The molecular event is **Immunoglobulin Class Switch Recombination (CSR)**, mediated by **Activation-Induced Cytidine Deaminase (AID)**: $$\text{IgM} + \text{C}_H\mu \xrightarrow[\text{AID, CD40L, cytokines}]{\text{Class Switch}} \text{IgG} + \text{C}_H\gamma$$ Where the **variable (V) region is preserved** but the **constant (C) region is exchanged** — hence, **antigen specificity is retained** but the **effector function changes**. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart — Immunoglobulin Isotypes ```mermaid flowchart TD A["IMMUNOGLOBULINS"]:::root --> B["IgG GAMMA"] A --> C["IgM MU"] A --> D["IgA ALPHA"] A --> E["IgE EPSILON"] A --> F["IgD DELTA"] B --> B1["IgG1 65 percent"] B --> B2["IgG2 23 percent"] B --> B3["IgG3 8 percent"] B --> B4["IgG4 4 percent"] C --> C1["Pentamer 900 kDa"] C --> C2["J chain present"] C --> C3["Monomeric mIgM on B cells"] D --> D1["IgA1 in serum"] D --> D2["IgA2 in secretions"] D --> D3["Dimer with J chain in secretory IgA"] E --> E1["Monomer"] E --> E2["Binds Fc epsilon RI on mast cells"] E --> E3["Mediates Type I hypersensitivity"] F --> F1["Monomer"] F --> F2["Co expressed with mIgM on naive B cells"] F --> F3["BCR signaling co receptor"] classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef igg fill:#4a90c2,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff classDef igm fill:#c0392b,stroke:#7b241c,color:#ffffff classDef iga fill:#27ae60,stroke:#196f3d,color:#ffffff classDef ige fill:#e67e22,stroke:#a04000,color:#ffffff classDef igd fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#512e5f,color:#ffffff class B,B1,B2,B3,B4 igg class C,C1,C2,C3 igm class D,D1,D2,D3 iga class E,E1,E2,E3 ige class F,F1,F2,F3 igd ``` ## 4.2 IgG Structure — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IgG["IGG MONOMER 150 kDa"] direction TB Fab1["Fab ARM 1
VL plus CL plus VH plus CH1
Antigen binding"]:::fab Fab2["Fab ARM 2
VL plus CL plus VH plus CH1
Antigen binding"]:::fab Hinge["HINGE REGION
Proline rich flexible linker
Segmental flexibility"]:::hinge Fc["Fc REGION
CH2 plus CH3
Effector functions"]:::fc Fab1 --- Hinge Fab2 --- Hinge Hinge --- Fc end Fab1 -. "Binds epitope 1" .-> AG1["Antigen Epitope A"]:::ag Fab2 -. "Binds epitope 2" .-> AG2["Antigen Epitope B"]:::ag Fc -. "Binds Fc gamma R on macrophage" .-> MR["Fc gamma R Phagocyte"]:::rec Fc -. "Binds C1q classical complement" .-> C1Q["C1q Complement"]:::rec Fc -. "Binds FcRn placental transfer" .-> FCRN["FcRn Syncytiotrophoblast"]:::rec classDef fab fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000 classDef hinge fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#b7950b,color:#000000 classDef fc fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000000 classDef ag fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#000000 classDef rec fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#512e5f,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 IgM Pentamer Structure — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD JCHAIN["J CHAIN 15 kDa
Disulfide linked to two Fc"]:::jchain subgraph PENTAMER["IGM PENTAMER 900 kDa with 10 Fab sites"] direction LR M1["MONOMER 1
2 mu plus 2 light

2 mu plus 2 light

2 mu plus 2 light
