"Agglutinates particulate antigen" .-> AGGL["Multivalent Antigen Crosslinking"]:::aggl classDef mono fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000000 classDef jchain fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000000 classDef compl fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000 classDef aggl fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.4 IgG vs IgM Time-Domain Response Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PHASE1["PRIMARY RESPONSE Day 0 to 14"] P1["Antigen exposure"] P2["IgM surge Day 5 to 7 peak"]:::igmBox P3["Low affinity No class switch yet"]:::igmBox end subgraph PHASE2["SECONDARY RESPONSE Day 14 onwards"] S1["Memory B cell reactivation"] S2["IgG surge Day 7 to 10 peak"]:::iggBox S3["High affinity Class switched"]:::iggBox end P1 --> P2 --> P3 S1 --> S2 --> S3 classDef igmBox fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef iggBox fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between the heavy chain compositions of IgG and IgM. Why is this difference functionally significant?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) IgG has γ (gamma) heavy chain; IgM has μ (mu) heavy chain. - (1 mark) γ chain possesses **3 constant domains (CH1, CH2, CH3)**; μ chain possesses **4 constant domains (CH1, CH2, CH3, CH4)**. - (1 mark) Functional significance: The extra CH4 domain in μ and the replacement of a classical hinge with a rigid CH2 region in IgM provides the **planar geometry required for high-avidity C1q binding**, making IgM the most potent complement activator. IgG's hinge region, in contrast, allows **flexibility needed for opsonization and Fc receptor engagement**. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Why is IgM considered the "first responder" of humoral immunity, and what structural feature enables this role?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) IgM is the **first immunoglobulin secreted** during the primary immune response (appears 3–5 days post-antigen exposure). - (1 mark) Its **pentameric structure** (5 monomers + J chain, 900 kDa) provides **10 antigen-binding sites**, enabling efficient **agglutination** of particulate antigens. - (1 mark) The **planar arrangement of Fc regions** in the pentamer provides the optimal 30 nm spacing for **C1q binding**, making IgM the **most efficient activator of the classical complement pathway**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 14 Marks]` **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the detailed structure of IgG, including its heavy and light chain composition, domain organization, and the four human IgG subclasses. Highlight the functional differences between the subclasses. **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the mechanism by which IgG is selectively transported across the placenta and discuss the clinical significance of this transfer. --- #### Model Solution for (a): **(i) Basic monomeric structure of IgG [2 marks]:** - IgG is a **Y-shaped monomer** (~150 kDa, 7S sedimentation coefficient). - Composed of **2 identical heavy chains (γ, ~50 kDa each)** and **2 identical light chains (κ or λ, ~25 kDa each)**. - Chains are held together by **inter-chain disulfide bonds** (H-L and H-H) and stabilized by non-covalent interactions. - The light chain has 1 variable (VL) and 1 constant (CL) domain; the heavy chain has 1 variable (VH) and 3 constant (CH1, CH2, CH3) domains. **(ii) Functional regions [2 marks]:** - **2 Fab regions** — each composed of VL + CL + VH + CH1; contains the **complementarity-determining regions (CDRs)** responsible for antigen specificity. Each Fab binds **one epitope**, giving IgG **2 antigen-binding sites** (bivalent). - **1 Fc region** — composed of paired CH2 and CH3 domains; mediates **effector functions** (complement binding, Fc receptor binding, placental transport). - **Hinge region** — proline-rich segment between CH1 and CH2; provides **segmental flexibility** and houses inter-heavy chain disulfide bonds. **(iii) The four human IgG subclasses [3 marks]:** | Subclass | Hinge Length | Complement Activation | Key Functional Role | |:--------:|:------------:|:---------------------:|:-------------------:| | **IgG1** | Short | ++ | Major subclass; protein antigens; viral neutralization | | **IgG2** | Short | + | **Polysaccharide antigens** (e.g., bacterial capsules) | | **IgG3** | **Longest (4× IgG1)** | **+++ (strongest)** | Most potent complement activator; protein antigens; HIV, CMV responses | | **IgG4** | Medium | **– (none)** | Non-inflammatory; anti-allergic; Fab-arm exchange | All four subclasses **cross the placenta** (FcRn-mediated). --- #### Model Solution for (b): **(i) Mechanism of placental transfer [4 marks]:** The selective transport of IgG across the placental syncytiotrophoblast is mediated by the **neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn)** — a β2-microglobulin-associated MHC class I-like molecule. - **Step 1:** Maternal IgG is internalized into endosomes of the syncytiotrophoblast via **fluid-phase pinocytosis**. - **Step 2:** At the **acidic pH (~6.0)** of the endosome, IgG binds with **high affinity** to FcRn (CH2–CH3 interface interaction). - **Step 3:** FcRn-IgG complexes are **transcytosed** to the basolateral (fetal) surface. - **Step 4:** At **physiological pH (~7.4)** of the fetal circulation, IgG **dissociates** from FcRn and is released into the fetal bloodstream. - **Step 5:** Unbound FcRn is **recycled** back to the maternal surface. - **Note:** All four IgG subclasses cross the placenta, with **IgG1 being transferred most efficiently**, followed by IgG4, IgG3, IgG2. **(ii) Clinical significance [3 marks]:** - **Passive immunity:** Full-term neonates receive ~**100% of maternal IgG levels at birth**, conferring protection against pathogens the mother has encountered (e.g., measles, rubella, tetanus) for the **first 6 months of life**. - **Maternal IgG in autoimmune disease:** In **neonatal thrombocytopenia, neonatal lupus, and neonatal myasthenia gravis**, pathogenic maternal IgG autoantibodies cross the placenta and cause transient disease in the newborn. - **Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN):** Maternal **anti-RhD IgG** crosses the placenta and destroys fetal Rh+ RBCs (prevented by Rh immunoglobulin / RhoGAM prophylaxis at 28 weeks). - **IgG subclass deficiencies:** IgG2 deficiency → recurrent **encapsulated bacterial infections**; IgG3 deficiency → recurrent **respiratory infections**. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 14 Marks]` (Internal Choice Alternative) **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the pentameric structure of IgM in detail. Explain the role of the J chain and the absence of a hinge region. Discuss why IgM is the most efficient activator of the classical complement pathway. **(b) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast the temporal kinetics, effector functions, and clinical diagnostic significance of IgM and IgG during an immune response. --- #### Model Solution for (a): **(i) Pentameric structure of IgM [3 marks]:** - IgM circulates as a **pentamer** (~900 kDa, 19S sedimentation coefficient) of 5 monomeric Y-shaped subunits. - Each monomer has 2 μ heavy chains and 2 light chains (κ or λ) — identical to the basic Ig unit. - Each monomer contains 2 Fab arms and 1 Fc stem. - The 5 monomers are arranged with their Fc regions pointing inward and Fab arms radiating outward, forming a **"star" or "mushroom"** shape. **(ii) Role of the J chain [2 marks]:** - The **J chain (Joining chain)** is a **~15 kDa** polypeptide, rich in acidic residues, produced by plasma cells. - It is **disulfide-bonded to the Fc regions of two adjacent μ heavy chains** (cysteine residues in the CH3/CH4 region). - Functions: - **Initiates polymerization** of the 5 monomeric units. - Essential for **efficient secretion** of the pentamer from the plasma cell. - Directs **transcytosis** of polymeric Ig (IgA and IgM) across mucosal epithelia via the **poly-Ig receptor (pIgR)**. - Without J chain, only hexamers (6 monomers) form, which bind C1q but **do not transcytose** to mucosal surfaces. **(iii) Absence of hinge region & complement activation [2 marks]:** - The μ heavy chain has **4 constant domains (CH1, CH2, CH3, CH4)** instead of the usual 3. - The CH2 domain functionally replaces the hinge, providing **rigidity** rather than flexibility. - The Fc regions of the pentamer are held in a **fixed planar geometry** with the **C1q-binding site always exposed**. - The **5 Fc regions are spaced ~30 nm apart** — the **optimal distance for simultaneous binding of the 6 globular heads of C1q**. - A **single IgM pentamer can initiate the classical complement cascade**, whereas C1q activation by IgG requires **≥2 adjacent IgG molecules** (random collision). - Result: IgM is **~1000–4000× more efficient** than IgG at complement activation on a per-molecule basis. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **(i) Temporal kinetics [2 marks]:** | Phase | Antibody | Peak (Days) | Duration | Class Switch | |:-----:|:--------:|:-----------:|:--------:|:------------:| | Primary | **IgM** | 5–7 | Short | Absent | | Secondary | **IgG** | 7–10 | Long-lived (memory) | Completed | - **IgM** is secreted first; it is **low affinity** (no somatic hypermutation yet) but **high avidity** (pentameric). - **IgG** appears later, after class switch recombination; it is **high affinity** (somatic hypermutation completed) and long-lived. **(ii) Effector functions [2 marks]:** | Function | IgM | IgG | |:--------:|:---:|:---:| | Opsonization | Weak | **Strong** | | Complement activation | **Strongest** | Moderate | | ADCC | Absent | Yes (IgG1, IgG3) | | Agglutination | **Strongest** | Weak | | Placental transfer | None | **Yes (only Ig)** | | Toxin/virus neutralization | Moderate | **Strong** | **(iii) Clinical diagnostic significance [3 marks]:** - **IgM serology** = **acute / recent infection marker**. - Examples: anti-HAV IgM, anti-HBc IgM, anti-Dengue IgM, anti-Toxoplasma IgM. - Persistence of IgM in some infections (e.g., Hepatitis B core IgM) indicates **active viral replication**. - **IgG serology** = **past exposure / immunity / chronicity marker**. - Examples: anti-HBs IgG (vaccination), anti-Rubella IgG (pre-conception screening). - **Seroconversion** (IgM⁻ → IgM⁺ → IgG⁺) = **gold standard for confirming recent primary infection**. - **IgG avidity testing** — Low-avidity IgG indicates **recent primary infection**; high-avidity IgG indicates **past infection** (used in TORCH screening and CMV diagnosis). - **IgM in ABO blood groups** — Naturally occurring **anti-A and anti-B IgM antibodies** cause acute hemolytic transfusion reactions in ABO-incompatible transfusions. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "affinity" (single binding site strength) with "avidity" (overall multivalent strength).** IgM has LOW affinity but HIGH avidity. Many students lose 2–3 marks on this distinction. > 2. **Do NOT claim IgM crosses the placenta.** It does NOT — only IgG does, via the FcRn receptor. > 3. **Do NOT state that all IgG subclasses activate complement equally.** IgG4 does NOT activate complement; IgG3 is the STRONGEST activator. > 4. **Always specify the heavy chain isotype** (γ, μ, α, ε, δ) when asked about immunoglobulin structure — saying "heavy chain" alone is incomplete and costs marks. > 5. **The J chain is NOT an immunoglobulin domain** — it is a separate small polypeptide; do not confuse it with the "J gene segment" that encodes part of the variable region. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — IgG & IgM** - 🔹 **IgG = Monomer (150 kDa, 7S)** | **IgM = Pentamer (900 kDa, 19S) + J chain** - 🔹 Heavy chain: IgG = **γ** (3 CH domains) | IgM = **μ** (4 CH domains) - 🔹 **IgG is the only immunoglobulin that crosses the placenta** (via FcRn) → passive neonatal immunity - 🔹 **IgM is the first antibody produced** in the primary immune response (day 5–7) - 🔹 **IgG dominates the secondary / memory response** - 🔹 **IgM is the most efficient complement activator** (1 pentamer ≫ thousands of IgG monomers for C1q) - 🔹 **IgG is the best opsonin** (especially IgG1 and IgG3) - 🔹 **4 IgG subclasses** (G1, G2, G3, G4); **IgG3 has the longest hinge** and is the strongest complement activator; **IgG4 does not fix complement** - 🔹 **Membrane-bound mIgM** serves as the **B cell receptor (BCR)** on naive B cells (co-expressed with mIgD) - 🔹 **Class switch recombination (CSR)** is mediated by **AID** and switches the constant region from Cμ to Cγ (IgM → IgG) while preserving antigen specificity - 🔹 **J chain** is essential for IgM polymerization and mucosal transcytosis (via pIgR) - 🔹 **Diagnostic rule:** *IgM = acute; IgG = past/immunity; seroconversion = confirmed primary infection*

topics.title: IgD and IgE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IgD and IgE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Immunoglobulin D (IgD)

**Formal Academic Definition:**
Immunoglobulin D (IgD) is a monomeric immunoglobulin isotype characterized by a heavy chain of the **δ (delta)** class. It exists primarily as a membrane-bound B cell receptor (BCR) co-expressed with IgM on the surface of mature, naïve B lymphocytes, where it functions as an antigen receptor that signals B cell activation. A small fraction of IgD is secreted into the serum, where it constitutes only **0.25%** of the total serum immunoglobulins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** IgD is co-expressed with IgM on the surface of mature B cells. It acts as a **phenotypic marker** of mature naïve B lymphocytes and serves as a **BCR** for antigen recognition.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of IgD as the **"junior partner"** sitting next to IgM on the B cell surface. If IgM is the loud megaphone that broadcasts a B cell's readiness to act, IgD is the silent walkie-talkie — it picks up fine-tuned environmental signals about antigens and helps the B cell decide whether to activate, stay quiet, or become tolerant. Because it is mostly tethered to the membrane, its job is local surveillance, not long-distance blood patrols.

**Standard Structural Metrics of IgD (in bold):**
- **Molecular Weight: ~185 kDa**
- **Heavy chain class: δ (delta)**
- **Serum concentration: 0.03–0.4 mg/dL** (trace amount)
- **Half-life in serum: ~2.8 days**
- **Hinge region: Extended but highly O-glycosylated and susceptible to proteolysis**

---

### 1.2 Immunoglobulin E (IgE)

**Formal Academic Definition:**
Immunoglobulin E (IgE) is a monomeric immunoglobulin isotype with a **ε (epsilon)** heavy chain. It is present in the lowest concentration in normal serum but has the **highest affinity** for its Fc receptor (FcεRI). IgE is the principal mediator of **Type I hypersensitivity (allergic reactions)** and plays a critical protective role in immunity against **helminth (parasitic worm) infections**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** IgE binds with extremely high affinity (Kd ≈ **10⁻¹⁰ M**) to FcεRI receptors on mast cells and basophils, "arming" them. Upon re-exposure to antigen, cross-linking of these IgE molecules triggers immediate degranulation, releasing histamine and other mediators.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine IgE as a **loaded mousetrap glued onto a guard dog's (mast cell's) back**. The trap (IgE) sits patiently — it has no mouse in it yet, but it's ready. The moment a specific antigen (the cheese/mouse) touches and cross-links two traps at once, the trap snaps shut, and the guard dog goes wild — releasing histamine, sneezing, itching, swelling, and going into full alarm mode. This is the molecular essence of an allergy attack.

**Standard Metrics of IgE (in bold):**
- **Molecular Weight: ~190 kDa**
- **Heavy chain class: ε (epsilon)**
- **Serum concentration: 0.0001–0.0002 mg/dL** (lowest among all isotypes)
- **Half-life in serum: ~2 days**, but **tissue half-life bound to mast cells: weeks to months**
- **Receptor affinity (FcεRI): 10⁻¹⁰ M (highest of any Ig–Fc receptor pair)**

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic Anchor:** **"IgE = E for Emergency"** — even in tiny amounts, it triggers emergency allergic and anti-parasitic responses.

---

### 1.3 Comparison Snapshot: IgD vs IgE

| Property | IgD | IgE |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy chain | δ (delta) | ε (epsilon) |
| Molecular weight | **~185 kDa** | **~190 kDa** |
| Serum concentration | **0.25%** of total Ig | Lowest of all isotypes |
| Primary form | Membrane-bound on B cells | Soluble in serum |
| Key receptor | None high-affinity classical FcR | FcεRI (very high affinity) |
| Principal function | B cell maturation / BCR signaling | Type I hypersensitivity / Anti-helminth |
| Heat stability | Highly heat-labile | Heat-labile |
| Crosses placenta | No | No |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Data Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Architecture of IgD

**Step-by-step structural logic:**
- **Heavy Chain Composition:** Two identical δ heavy chains (~64 kDa each), each comprising one variable domain (V_H) and **three** constant domains (C_δ1, C_δ2, C_δ3). IgD lacks a C_H4 domain, distinguishing it from IgM and IgE.
- **Light Chains:** Either κ or λ paired with each δ heavy chain via disulfide bonds.
- **Hinge Region:** IgD has a long, exposed hinge region (~64 amino acids) that is heavily **O-glycosylated**. This makes the molecule highly flexible but extremely susceptible to proteolytic cleavage.
- **Membrane vs Secreted Forms:** Membrane-bound IgD (mIgD) possesses a **transmembrane domain and a short cytoplasmic tail** of only ~3 amino acids. The secreted form (sIgD) lacks the transmembrane anchor and is released as a soluble monomer.
- **Disulfide Bonding:** Unlike IgG, IgD δ chains are **not** disulfide-linked to each other across the hinge. They are joined only by light-chain disulfides, contributing to its fragile nature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is IgD fragile?** The hinge region of IgD is highly susceptible to proteases like plasmin, and because the two δ chains are not strongly cross-linked, the molecule readily dissociates. This is why IgD has a very short serum half-life.

---

### 2.2 Functional Roles of IgD

1. **BCR Signaling Component:**
   On mature naïve B cells, mIgD co-exists with mIgM as part of the **B cell receptor complex** (with Ig-α/Ig-β heterodimers for signal transduction). Both isotypes share the **same V region** and therefore recognize the **same antigen**.

2. **Tolerance and Anergy Induction:**
   IgD signaling without strong co-stimulation tends to promote **B cell anergy** (a state of unresponsiveness), preventing autoimmunity against self-antigens.

3. **Mucosal Immunity and Respiratory Defenses:**
   Secreted IgD is found in upper respiratory tract secretions and may play a role in mucosal defense against respiratory pathogens.

4. **Interaction with Innate Immune Cells:**
   IgD can bind to **basophils** and **mast cells** (a less-characterized, low-affinity interaction compared to IgE) and may amplify inflammatory responses by inducing cytokine release.

---

### 2.3 Structural Architecture of IgE

**Step-by-step structural logic:**
- **Heavy Chain Composition:** Two identical ε heavy chains (~72 kDa each), each with one V_H domain and **four** constant domains (C_ε1, C_ε2, C_ε3, C_ε4). The presence of C_ε4 is structurally analogous to IgM and distinguishes IgE from IgG/IgD.
- **No Hinge Region:** IgE **lacks a classical hinge region**. Instead, the C_ε2 domains fold back, providing conformational flexibility.
- **Light Chains:** κ or λ light chains disulfide-bonded to each ε heavy chain.
- **Fc Region Interactions:** The C_ε3 and C_ε4 domains of IgE are responsible for high-affinity binding to the **α-chain of FcεRI**.

---

### 2.4 Functional Roles of IgE

1. **Type I Hypersensitivity (Allergy):**
   - IgE is the central effector molecule of immediate hypersensitivity reactions.
   - **Sensitization phase:** Allergen-specific IgE produced by plasma cells binds FcεRI on tissue mast cells and circulating basophils.
   - **Effector phase:** Re-exposure to the allergen cross-links adjacent IgE molecules, triggering mast cell/basophil **degranulation** — release of histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins, and cytokines.

2. **Defense Against Helminths:**
   - IgE binds to FcεRI on **eosinophils** (via a related receptor, FcεRII/CD23 in some contexts).
   - Cross-linking on eosinophils triggers **antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC)** against large parasites that cannot be phagocytosed.

3. **FcεRI vs FcεRII (CD23):**
   - **FcεRI:** High-affinity (Kd = 10⁻¹⁰ M) receptor on mast cells, basophils, and activated eosinophils.
   - **FcεRII (CD23):** Low-affinity receptor on B cells, macrophages, and eosinophils; involved in IgE regulation and antigen presentation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** **"Epsilon-RI is E-xciting and E-xtremely tight"** — high affinity, mast cells, allergy.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Data Sheet (Formula / Parameter Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The parameters below are critical numerical values expected in KTU examinations. Memorize the affinity constant, half-lives, and serum concentrations.

| Parameter | IgD | IgE |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy chain class | δ | ε |
| Molecular weight | **185 kDa** | **190 kDa** |
| Serum concentration | 0.03–0.4 mg/dL | 0.0001–0.0002 mg/dL |
| Serum half-life | **2.8 days** | **2 days** |
| Tissue-bound half-life | — | **Weeks to months** |
| Affinity for Fc receptor | None classical | **Kd ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ M** (highest) |
| Hinge region | Long, O-glycosylated | Absent (replaced by C_ε2) |
| Number of C_H domains | 3 | 4 |
| Crosses placenta? | No | No |
| Classical complement activation | No | No |
| Principal effector role | BCR / B cell maturation | Allergy / anti-helminth |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Biomedical Utility

- **Allergy Diagnostics:** ELISA and ImmunoCAP assays measure serum IgE levels to diagnose atopic diseases. **Engineered recombinant allergen chips** for IgE profiling are a direct biotech application.
- **Monoclonal Anti-IgE Therapy:** **Omalizumab** is a humanized monoclonal antibody that binds the C_ε3 domain of circulating IgE, blocking its interaction with FcεRI. It is used clinically for severe asthma and chronic spontaneous urticaria.
- **Immunodeficiency Screening:** Elevated IgE is a hallmark of **Hyper-IgE Syndrome (Job syndrome)**, often associated with STAT3 mutations. Measuring IgD and IgE panels aids diagnosis of rare immunodeficiencies.
- **Vaccine Adjuvant Design:** IgE-inducing adjuvants are being explored to enhance mucosal immunity against helminths.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Reasoning & Logical Derivations

### 3.1 Class Switch Recombination (CSR) Leading to IgD or IgE Production

**Logical derivation of how a B cell commits to IgD or IgE secretion:**

**Step 1 — Germline Configuration:**
Every B cell initially expresses IgM (and IgD) through alternative splicing of a single V-D-J-C transcript.
The heavy chain locus on chromosome 14 contains: 5'-V-D-J — C_μ — C_δ — C_γ3 — C_γ1 — C_α1 — C_γ2 — C_γ4 — C_ε — C_α2 — 3'.

**Step 2 — Cytokine & CD40L Signals Determine Switch Direction:**

| T-helper signal | Cytokines involved | Switch outcome toward |
|---|---|---|
| Th2 response | **IL-4** | IgE (and IgG4 in humans) |
| TGF-β dominant | TGF-β | IgA |
| IFN-γ dominant | IFN-γ | IgG |

**Step 3 — Switch Recombination Mechanism:**
Activation-induced cytidine deaminase (**AID**) initiates double-strand breaks in switch (S) regions upstream of the target C_H gene. Loop excision deletes intervening C_H genes, and the V-D-J is permanently joined to the new C_H gene.

**Mathematical Note on Affinity (for IgE):**
The exceptionally high affinity of IgE for FcεRI is critical because IgE is present at extremely low concentrations. The affinity constant is given by:

$$
K_a = \frac{1}{K_d}
$$

With K_d ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ M:

$$
K_a = \frac{1}{10^{-10}} = 10^{10} \text{ M}^{-1}
$$

This ultra-high affinity ensures that **virtually every IgE molecule** secreted is rapidly captured and immobilized on tissue mast cells — making IgE essentially a tissue-resident sentinel antibody.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Receptor Occupancy on Mast Cells

**Logical model for how many IgE molecules occupy a single mast cell:**

Let:
- N_total = total number of FcεRI receptors on one mast cell ≈ **10⁵ to 10⁶**
- f = fraction of receptors occupied by IgE at serum equilibrium

Given K_d = 10⁻¹⁰ M and typical free serum [IgE] ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ M:

$$
f = \frac{[IgE]}{K_d + [IgE]} = \frac{10^{-10}}{10^{-10} + 10^{-10}} = 0.5
$$

At physiological IgE, roughly **50%** of FcεRI sites on circulating basophils are occupied. This is why even tiny antigenic triggers can produce a vigorous allergic response — the cells are **pre-loaded** like loaded springs.

---

### 3.3 Logical Pathway of Mast Cell Degranulation by IgE

**Step 1 — Sensitization:**
Plasma cells secrete **allergen-specific IgE**, which diffuses into tissues and binds FcεRI on mast cell surfaces.

**Step 2 — Receptor Cross-linking:**
Upon re-exposure, a multivalent allergen binds **two adjacent IgE molecules**, cross-linking their FcεRI receptors.

**Step 3 — Signal Transduction Initiation:**
Cross-linking activates the **Lyn and Syk tyrosine kinases**, leading to phosphorylation of ITAMs on the FcεRI β and γ chains.

**Step 4 — Calcium Influx and Degranulation:**
Phospholipase C γ (PLCγ) activation generates **IP3**, releasing intracellular Ca²⁺ stores:

$$
[Ca^{2+}]_i \uparrow \uparrow \rightarrow \text{degranulation}
$$

**Step 5 — Mediator Release:**
Mast cells release:
- **Preformed mediators:** histamine, heparin, tryptase, chymase
- **Newly synthesized mediators:** leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4), prostaglandin D2 (PGD2), cytokines (TNF-α, IL-4, IL-5)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The two-signal requirement (allergen + IgE + cross-link) is a classic **biological threshold detection** mechanism — analogous to a Schmitt trigger in electronics, where noise cannot fire the output unless a sufficient input crosses a defined threshold.

---

### 3.4 Why IgD Does Not Have a Classical Fc Receptor

The δ heavy chain C-terminal constant domains (C_δ1, C_δ2, C_δ3) lack the structural motifs required for high-affinity Fc receptor binding. Furthermore, the **membrane-bound IgD** is structurally anchored; its cytoplasmic tail is only ~3 amino acids long, far too short for autonomous signal transduction. Therefore, IgD **relies on the Ig-α/Ig-β heterodimer** (CD79a/CD79b) for signal propagation. This is in contrast to IgE, which uses the long cytoplasmic ITAM-bearing γ chain of FcεRI.

This structural fact is summarized in the equation of signal competence:

$$
\text{Signal output} = f(\text{ITAM phosphorylation}) \times g(\text{co-receptor engagement})
$$

For IgD, the **ITAM contribution comes from Ig-α/Ig-β**, while for IgE the ITAMs reside in FcεRI itself.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
