# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** Define immune tolerance. Differentiate between **central** and **peripheral** tolerance with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Immune tolerance** is the acquired, antigen-specific failure of the immune system to respond to a particular antigen, most importantly self-antigens, while retaining the ability to respond to foreign antigens.
>
> - **Central tolerance** occurs in the primary lymphoid organs during lymphocyte maturation. Example: Clonal deletion of self-reactive T cells in the thymic medulla by AIRE-driven negative selection.
> - **Peripheral tolerance** occurs in secondary lymphoid organs and tissues after lymphocyte maturation. Example: Induction of T-cell anergy due to lack of B7–CD28 co-stimulation by non-professional APCs.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for central, 1 mark for peripheral with example)*

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** What is the role of the **AIRE protein** in immune tolerance? Mention the disease caused by its mutation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **AIRE (Autoimmune Regulator)** protein is a transcription factor expressed in **medullary thymic epithelial cells (mTECs)**. It drives *promiscuous gene expression* of peripheral tissue-specific antigens (e.g., insulin, thyroglobulin) within the thymus, allowing negative selection of T cells that would otherwise react against these self-antigens.
>
> Mutations in the AIRE gene cause **APECED syndrome (Autoimmune Polyendocrinopathy–Candidiasis–Ectodermal Dystrophy)**, also called *autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 1 (APS-1)*, characterized by multi-organ autoimmunity.
>
> *(1 mark AIRE function, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark disease)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE, Part B questions of Module 2 carry **14 marks** with an internal choice between two questions (typically Or option). Two complete alternative questions are provided below.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **mechanisms of central immune tolerance** in T lymphocytes. Describe the role of the thymus, AIRE protein, and the two-stage selection process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **peripheral tolerance mechanisms** with a focus on **anergy, clonal ignorance, and activation-induced cell death (AICD)**. Add a note on **regulatory T cells (Tregs)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Question A

**Part (a) — Central Tolerance in T Lymphocytes (7 marks):**

T cell central tolerance occurs in the **thymus** and consists of two sequential selection stages — **positive selection** (cortex) followed by **negative selection** (medulla).

1. **Origin and Migration (1 mark):** Hematopoietic stem cell-derived lymphoid progenitors migrate from the bone marrow to the thymic cortex as *double-negative (CD4⁻CD8⁻)* thymocytes.

2. **TCR Rearrangement (1 mark):** The RAG1/RAG2 enzyme complex mediates V(D)J recombination, generating a diverse TCR repertoire.

3. **Double-Positive Stage:** Successful TCRβ expression leads to *CD4⁺CD8⁺ double-positive* thymocytes.

4. **Positive Selection in the Cortex (1 mark):** cTECs present self-peptides on MHC-I and MHC-II. Thymocytes with TCRs that can recognize self-MHC receive survival signals (Bcl-2 upregulation) and progress. Those that fail to bind undergo **death by neglect** (~95% of thymocytes die here). This establishes **MHC restriction**.

5. **Lineage Commitment:** MHC-I binding → CD8⁺ cytotoxic lineage; MHC-II binding → CD4⁺ helper lineage.

6. **Negative Selection in the Medulla (2 marks):** mTECs and dendritic cells present a broad array of self-peptides. The **AIRE transcription factor** drives *promiscuous gene expression* of tissue-specific antigens in mTECs, exposing developing thymocytes to antigens they would otherwise only encounter in peripheral organs (e.g., insulin, myelin basic protein). Thymocytes with high-affinity TCR–self-pMHC interactions undergo **clonal deletion (apoptosis)**.

7. **Outcome (1 mark):** Only **1–5%** of thymocytes survive as **mature, naive, self-tolerant, MHC-restricted** T cells, which exit to the peripheral circulation. Failure of this process due to **AIRE mutations** causes **APECED syndrome**.

---

**Part (b) — Peripheral Tolerance Mechanisms (7 marks):**

Peripheral tolerance acts on mature lymphocytes that *escape* central tolerance. Four principal mechanisms operate:

1. **Anergy — The Two-Signal Hypothesis (2 marks):** T cell activation requires two signals: **Signal 1 (TCR–pMHC)** and **Signal 2 (B7–CD28 co-stimulation)**. If Signal 1 occurs *without* Signal 2 (e.g., antigen presented by a non-professional APC lacking B7), the T cell becomes **anergic** — it fails to produce IL-2, upregulates E3 ubiquitin ligases (Cbl-b, GRAIL), and is rendered functionally unresponsive. The inhibitory receptor **CTLA-4** further reinforces anergy by outcompeting CD28 for B7.

2. **Clonal Ignorance (1.5 marks):** Some self-antigens are physically sequestered in **immune-privileged sites** (anterior chamber of the eye, brain, testis, placenta). Lymphocytes never encounter them under normal conditions. Breach of these barriers (e.g., trauma, surgery) can release these antigens and trigger autoimmunity — the classic example being **sympathetic ophthalmia**, where injury to one eye leads to autoimmune attack on the contralateral eye.

3. **Activation-Induced Cell Death — AICD (1.5 marks):** Repeated stimulation of self-reactive T cells leads to upregulation of **Fas (CD95)** on the T cell surface and **FasL (CD95L)** on the same or neighboring cells. Fas–FasL engagement triggers the **caspase-8 → caspase-3 apoptotic cascade**, eliminating chronically stimulated T cells. Defects in this pathway cause **ALPS (Autoimmune Lymphoproliferative Syndrome)**.

4. **Regulatory T Cells — Tregs (2 marks):** **CD4⁺CD25⁺FoxP3⁺ Tregs** are the master regulators of peripheral tolerance. They suppress effector T cells via:
   - **Cell-contact-dependent mechanisms** (CTLA-4–B7 trans-endocytosis).
   - **Anti-inflammatory cytokines** (IL-10, TGF-β, IL-35).
   - **Metabolic disruption** (CD25-mediated IL-2 depletion, CD39/CD73-mediated adenosine production).
   - **FoxP3** is the master transcription factor; **FoxP3 mutations** cause **IPEX syndrome**, a fatal early-onset autoimmune disorder in humans.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **mechanisms of B cell tolerance** in the bone marrow. Explain **receptor editing** and **clonal deletion** with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a detailed note on the **clinical implications of immune tolerance failure**. Discuss any **three autoimmune diseases** with their underlying tolerance defects. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Question B

**Part (a) — B Cell Tolerance (7 marks):**

B cell maturation occurs in the **bone marrow**. Immature B cells express surface **IgM (BCR)** and encounter self-antigens in the bone marrow microenvironment. Three principal mechanisms establish B cell central tolerance:

1. **Receptor Editing (3 marks):** When an immature B cell's BCR binds a self-antigen with **high affinity**, the cell receives a *second chance*. The RAG1/RAG2 recombinase genes are *reactivated*, and **V(D)J recombination** of the light chain locus is re-initiated, producing a new κ or λ light chain. The newly assembled BCR is re-tested; if it is no longer self-reactive, the B cell survives. This is the **dominant** mechanism of B cell central tolerance — studies suggest that **25–50%** of self-reactive B cells are rescued by receptor editing.

2. **Clonal Deletion (2 marks):** If receptor editing fails to eliminate self-reactivity (or in cases of strong, polyvalent self-antigen binding), the B cell undergoes **mitochondrial apoptosis** (Bcl-2 family-mediated). This removes the self-reactive clone from the repertoire.

3. **Anergy (2 marks):** B cells that bind **soluble self-antigens** with moderate affinity become **anergic**. Anergic B cells exit to the periphery as **mature naive B cells** with downregulated surface IgM but retained IgD. They have a **short lifespan**, fail to respond to antigenic stimulation, and cannot enter germinal centers. They are often found in the **splenic marginal zone** and represent a *permissive* form of tolerance.

> **Clinical correlation:** Defects in B cell tolerance contribute to **Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)**, where anti-nuclear antibodies are produced due to failed deletion of anti-self B cell clones.

---

**Part (b) — Clinical Implications: Three Autoimmune Diseases (7 marks):**

1. **Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1D) — Tolerance Defect: Peripheral T cell–mediated (2.5 marks):**
   - Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic **β-cells** by autoreactive CD8⁺ T cells and CD4⁺ Th1 cells.
   - Tolerance defect: Failure of peripheral T cell tolerance to insulin and GAD65 (glutamic acid decarboxylase).
   - Genetic association: **HLA-DR3/DR4** haplotypes; **CTLA-4 polymorphisms**.
   - Autoantibodies: Anti-insulin, anti-GAD65, anti-ZnT8 antibodies are diagnostic markers.

2. **Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) — Tolerance Defect: Defective B cell tolerance and apoptotic clearance (2.5 marks):**
   - Loss of B cell tolerance leads to production of **anti-nuclear antibodies (ANA)**, **anti-dsDNA**, and **anti-Smith** antibodies.
   - Tolerance defect: Defective clearance of apoptotic cell debris exposes nuclear self-antigens; failure of B cell anergy and receptor editing.
   - Genetic association: **C1q deficiency** (classical complement pathway failure → impaired clearance of apoptotic bodies).
   - Affects skin, kidneys (lupus nephritis), joints, and CNS.

3. **Multiple Sclerosis (MS) — Tolerance Defect: Failure of Treg function (2 marks):**
   - Autoimmune demyelination of CNS neurons by autoreactive T cells recognizing **myelin basic protein (MBP)** and **myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG)**.
   - Tolerance defect: Reduced number and function of **FoxP3⁺ Tregs** in the CNS; failure of peripheral anergy against myelin antigens.
   - Genetic association: **HLA-DR2** haplotype.
   - Animal model: **Experimental Autoimmune Encephalomyelitis (EAE)** — induced by immunization with MBP in CFA.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN IMMUNE TOLERANCE QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "anergy" with "deletion."** Anergy = *reversible, functional* unresponsiveness; deletion = *irreversible* apoptosis. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the two-signal requirement.** When discussing peripheral anergy, *always* explicitly state **Signal 1 alone ≠ activation; needs Signal 2 (B7–CD28)**. Many students omit this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not linking AIRE → promiscuous gene expression → APECED.** This is a *triad* often asked. Stating only "AIRE is in the thymus" without explaining its function and disease consequence yields partial credit only.
> 4. **Confusing FoxP3 with CD25.** Both are Treg markers, but FoxP3 is the *lineage-defining transcription factor* (intracellular), while CD25 is a *surface activation marker*. Examiners expect precise terminology.
> 5. **Ignoring B cell tolerance.** Many students write only about T cell tolerance. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly includes B cell mechanisms — receptor editing, anergy, deletion. Deducting 2–3 marks is common.
> 6. **Writing clinical correlations without molecular basis.** In 14-mark answers, simply naming diseases is *insufficient* — explicitly connect the disease to the **specific tolerance defect** (e.g., "IPEX = FoxP3 mutation → absent Tregs → multi-organ autoimmunity").
> 7. **Skipping diagrams in 14-mark answers.** The KTU valuation key awards **1–2 marks** for a clearly labeled diagram. Always include one (use a Mermaid flow or simple labeled sketch in the exam).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 2 (Immune Tolerance):**

- ✅ **Immune tolerance** = antigen-specific unresponsiveness, *not* general immunosuppression.
- ✅ Two broad classes: **Central (thymus/bone marrow)** and **Peripheral (tissues/lymph nodes)**.
- ✅ T cell central tolerance = **positive selection (cortex) → negative selection (medulla)**.
- ✅ **AIRE protein** drives promiscuous gene expression in mTECs → enables negative selection against peripheral tissue antigens.
- ✅ **AIRE mutation** → APECED syndrome.
- ✅ B cell central tolerance has **3 mechanisms**: receptor editing (RAG reactivation) → clonal deletion (apoptosis) → anergy.
- ✅ **Receptor editing** is the *most common* B cell tolerance mechanism.
- ✅ Peripheral anergy follows the **two-signal hypothesis**: Signal 1 (TCR–pMHC) + Signal 2 (B7–CD28) = activation; Signal 1 *without* Signal 2 = anergy.
- ✅ **CTLA-4** outcompetes CD28 for B7 → reinforces anergy and Treg suppression.
- ✅ **Tregs** are CD4⁺CD25⁺FoxP3⁺; suppress via IL-10, TGF-β, IL-35, IL-2 deprivation, and adenosine.
- ✅ **FoxP3 mutation** → IPEX syndrome.
- ✅ **AICD (Fas–FasL)** eliminates chronically stimulated T cells; **Fas mutation** → ALPS.
- ✅ **Clonal ignorance** = physical sequestration (immune privilege: eye, brain, testis, placenta).
- ✅ Breach of immune privilege → **sympathetic ophthalmia**.
- ✅ Tolerance failure → autoimmunity; key diseases: **T1D, SLE, MS, RA, APECED, IPEX, ALPS**.
- ✅ **Therapeutic exploitation:** Abatacept (CTLA-4-Ig fusion) induces anergy in RA; CAR-Treg cell therapy is in clinical trials for transplant tolerance.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for tolerance mechanisms:** **"D-RAID"** — **D**eletion, **R**eceptor editing, **A**nergy, **I**gnorance, **D**eletion (AICD).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MHC: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MHC: Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC)** is a tightly linked cluster of genes encoding cell-surface glycoproteins that are essential for the adaptive immune system's ability to recognize "self" versus "non-self." In humans, this gene complex is located on the **short arm of chromosome 6 (6p21.3)** and is designated as the **Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA)** system.

The MHC molecules are classified into **three principal classes** based on their structure, tissue distribution, and immunological function:

| Class | Alternate Name | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **MHC Class I** | HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C | Presentation of **endogenous (intracellular)** peptides to **CD8+ cytotoxic T cells** |
| **MHC Class II** | HLA-DP, HLA-DQ, HLA-DR | Presentation of **exogenous (extracellular)** peptides to **CD4+ helper T cells** |
| **MHC Class III** | Non-HLA complement/cytokine genes | Encode complement proteins, cytokines, heat shock proteins, and other immune-related molecules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme specifically emphasizes the structural differences, chromosomal loci, peptide-binding groove characteristics, and antigen-presentation pathways of Class I and Class II molecules. Class III is treated as supplementary context.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "ID Card Display Board" Model

Imagine a **secure government office building** where every employee (cell) is required to display their **identity card on a board outside their cubicle**:

- **MHC Class I** = A small personal display board found on **EVERY employee's desk** (all nucleated cells). It shows what the employee is currently working on (intracellular proteins). If the employee has been **hacked (viral infection)** or is **malfunctioning (cancer)**, the board reveals suspicious activity to **security guards (CD8+ T cells)** who immediately eliminate that employee.

- **MHC Class II** = A specialized display board found only in the **reception area**, used by **front-desk staff (Antigen Presenting Cells — APCs)**. The receptionist **brings in external visitors (engulfed pathogens)**, processes their documents, and displays summaries on the board for **senior management (CD4+ T helper cells)** who then issue directives to the entire security team.

- **MHC Class III** = The **building's support infrastructure** — fire alarms (complement system), intercoms (cytokines), and emergency response systems that don't display ID cards but are critical for coordinated immunity.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Total HLA gene region size:** Approximately **3.6 megabases (Mb)** of DNA
- **Number of identified HLA genes:** Over **200** functional genes
- **HLA Class I peptide-binding groove length:** Typically accommodates peptides of **8–10 amino acids**
- **HLA Class II peptide-binding groove length:** Accommodates peptides of **13–25 amino acids**
- **Chromosomal location:** 6p21.3 (short arm, region 21, band 3)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "**One** for all nucleated cells; **Two** for **T**eam coordinators (APCs only) who call the **T**hinkers (CD4)" → Class I → CD8, Class II → CD4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of MHC Class I

### 2.1.1 Structural Architecture

MHC Class I molecules are heterodimers composed of:

1. **A heavy α-chain (44 kDa)** — encoded on chromosome 6, comprising three extracellular domains:
   - **α1 domain** — contributes to one-half of the peptide-binding groove
   - **α2 domain** — contributes to the other half of the peptide-binding groove
   - **α3 domain** — conserved Ig-fold that binds **CD8 co-receptor**
2. **β2-microglobulin (12 kDa)** — a light chain encoded on chromosome 15, non-covalently associated with the α3 domain; **does not span the membrane**.

The peptide-binding groove is formed by the **α1 and α2 domains**, creating a platform of **8 anti-parallel β-sheets** flanked by **two α-helices**. The groove is **closed at both ends**, restricting peptide length to roughly **9 amino acids**.

> [!NOTE]
> The α3 domain interacts with CD8, while the peptide-β2m-α chain complex is recognized by the T cell receptor (TCR) of CD8+ T cells.

### 2.1.2 Tissue Distribution

- **All nucleated somatic cells** in the body
- **Platelets** (anucleate but derived from megakaryocytes)
- **NOT expressed on mature red blood cells** (anucleate, no nucleus)

### 2.1.3 Functional Logic Flow

1. Cytosolic proteins undergo **ubiquitination**.
2. Ubiquitinated proteins are degraded by the **proteasome (26S)** into 8–10 residue peptides.
3. Peptides are transported into the **Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER)** via the **TAP (Transporter Associated with Antigen Processing)** heterodimer.
4. Peptides bind nascent MHC Class I molecules assisted by chaperones (**calnexin, calreticulin, tapasin**).
5. Stable trimeric complex (peptide + α chain + β2m) is exported via the **Golgi apparatus** to the cell surface.

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown of MHC Class II

### 2.2.1 Structural Architecture

MHC Class II molecules are heterodimers composed of two **non-covalently associated transmembrane glycoproteins**:

1. **α-chain (33–35 kDa)** — contains α1 and α2 extracellular domains
2. **β-chain (28–30 kDa)** — contains β1 and β2 extracellular domains

The peptide-binding groove is formed by the **α1 and β1 domains**, generating an **open-ended groove** that can accommodate longer peptides (13–25 residues). The **β2 domain** contains the **CD4 binding site**.

### 2.2.2 Tissue Distribution

- **Professional Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs):**
  - **Dendritic cells** (highest expression)
  - **Macrophages**
  - **B lymphocytes**
- **Thymic epithelial cells** (for T cell education/positive selection)

### 2.2.3 Functional Logic Flow

1. Extracellular antigens are internalized by **phagocytosis, endocytosis, or receptor-mediated endocytosis**.
2. Antigens are processed in **endosomal/lysosomal compartments (MIIC)**.
3. MHC Class II αβ heterodimers are synthesized in the ER and bind the **Invariant chain (Ii/CD74)** to prevent premature peptide loading.
4. The invariant chain guides MHC II to the **MIIC compartment**, where it is degraded, leaving only **CLIP** (Class II-associated Invariant chain Peptide).
5. **HLA-DM** catalyzes the exchange of CLIP for antigenic peptides.
6. Peptide-loaded MHC II is transported to the cell surface for CD4+ T cell recognition.

## 2.3 MHC Class III — Brief Overview

Located **centromeric** to Class I and Class II regions. **NOT** involved in antigen presentation. Encodes:

- **Complement components:** C2, C4, Factor B
- **Cytokines:** TNF-α, lymphotoxin
- **Heat shock proteins:** HSP70
- **21-hydroxylase (CYP21)**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of the pipe character `|` in table cells to avoid breaking markdown syntax.

| Parameter | MHC Class I | MHC Class II |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoding chromosome (heavy chain)** | Chromosome 6 | Chromosome 6 |
| **Encoding chromosome (light chain)** | Chromosome 15 ($\beta$2-microglobulin) | Chromosome 6 (both $\alpha$ and $\beta$) |
| **Chain composition** | $\alpha$ heavy chain (3 domains) + $\beta$2m | $\alpha$ chain + $\beta$ chain |
| **Domains forming groove** | $\alpha$1 + $\alpha$2 | $\alpha$1 + $\beta$1 |
| **Peptide length** | $8$–$10$ amino acids | $13$–$25$ amino acids |
| **Groove ends** | Closed | Open |
| **Co-receptor binding domain** | $\alpha$3 (binds CD8) | $\beta$2 (binds CD4) |
| **Presenting cell type** | All nucleated cells | Professional APCs only |
| **Antigen source** | Endogenous (cytosolic) | Exogenous (extracellular) |
| **Processing compartment** | Cytosol $\rightarrow$ ER | Endosome/lysosome (MIIC) |
| **Transporter** | TAP | Invariant chain |
| **Activates T cell subset** | CD8$^+$ cytotoxic T cells | CD4$^+$ helper T cells |
| **HLA loci (human)** | HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C | HLA-DP, HLA-DQ, HLA-DR |
| **Surface density** | $\sim 10^{5}$–$10^{6}$ molecules per cell | Variable; upregulated upon APC activation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Biomedical Utility

- **Transplantation Medicine:** HLA Class I and II mismatching between donor and recipient triggers **graft rejection**. HLA typing is mandatory before kidney, bone-marrow, and stem-cell transplantation.
- **Forensic Science:** HLA-DR and HLA-DQ hypervariable regions are used in **DNA fingerprinting**.
- **Disease Association:** Specific HLA alleles correlate with autoimmune disease susceptibility (e.g., HLA-B27 $\rightarrow$ ankylosing spondylitis; HLA-DR3/DR4 $\rightarrow$ Type 1 diabetes).
- **Vaccine Design:** Peptide epitope prediction algorithms target MHC-binding grooves for **rational vaccine engineering**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** All antigen processing and presentation pathway steps are explicitly derived below with mathematical and logical rigor.

## 3.1 Quantitative Estimation of MHC Class I Surface Density

The steady-state surface density of MHC Class I molecules on a nucleated cell can be modeled using a first-order kinetic equilibrium. Let:

- $S$ = surface MHC Class I density (molecules/$\mu$m$^2$)
- $k_{\text{syn}}$ = synthesis rate constant (molecules $\cdot$ cell$^{-1}$ $\cdot$ min$^{-1}$)
- $k_{\text{deg}}$ = degradation/internalization rate constant (min$^{-1}$)
- $k_{\text{load}}$ = peptide-loading efficiency (dimensionless fraction, $0 \le k_{\text{load}} \le 1$)

At steady-state ($dS/dt = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dt} &= k_{\text{syn}} \cdot k_{\text{load}} - k_{\text{deg}} \cdot S = 0 \\[6pt]
\therefore S_{\text{steady}} &= \frac{k_{\text{syn}} \cdot k_{\text{load}}}{k_{\text{deg}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Surface density is directly proportional to synthesis rate and peptide-loading efficiency, and inversely proportional to degradation rate. A defect in TAP (e.g., in Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome Type I) reduces $k_{\text{load}} \to 0$, leading to $S \to 0$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Peptide-Binding Affinity for MHC Groove

The binding affinity of an antigenic peptide to an MHC groove can be approximated using the **anchored-residue model**. The total binding free energy $\Delta G_{\text{bind}}$ is the sum of contributions from anchor residues and auxiliary contacts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G_{\text{bind}} &= \Delta G_{\text{anchor}} + \Delta G_{\text{aux}} + \Delta G_{\text{backbone}} \\[4pt]
\Delta G_{\text{bind}} &= \sum_{i \in \text{anchor}} \Delta G_i^{\text{anchor}} + \sum_{j \in \text{aux}} \Delta G_j^{\text{aux}} + N \cdot \Delta G_{\text{bb}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = total number of amino acids in the peptide
- $\Delta G_{\text{bb}}$ = free energy contribution per backbone hydrogen bond ($\approx -0.5$ kcal/mol)

For MHC Class I, the dissociation constant $K_D$ is related to $\Delta G_{\text{bind}}$ by:

$$
\Delta G_{\text{bind}} = -RT \ln \left(\frac{1}{K_D}\right) = RT \ln(K_D)
$$

Where $R = 1.987 \times 10^{-3}$ kcal $\cdot$ mol$^{-1}$ $\cdot$ K$^{-1}$ and $T = 298$ K.

Typical $K_D$ values for high-affinity binders:

$$
K_D \approx 10^{-9} \text{ to } 10^{-7} \text{ M}
$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Endogenous Antigen Presentation (Class I Pathway)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Cytosolic protein} \xrightarrow{\text{Ubiquitin ligase (E3)}} \text{Ubiquitinated protein} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Ubiquitinated protein} \xrightarrow{\text{26S Proteasome}} \text{Peptide fragments (8-10 aa)} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Peptides} \xrightarrow{\text{TAP1/TAP2 (ATP-dependent)}} \text{ER lumen} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \alpha\text{-chain} + \beta 2m + \text{peptide} \xrightarrow{\text{Calnexin, Calreticulin, Tapasin}} \text{Stable trimer} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Stable trimer} \xrightarrow{\text{Golgi apparatus}} \text{Cell surface} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Cell surface MHC I} \xrightarrow{\text{TCR recognition}} \text{CD8}^+ \text{ T cell activation}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Exogenous Antigen Presentation (Class II Pathway)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Extracellular pathogen} \xrightarrow{\text{Phagocytosis/Endocytosis}} \text{Endosome} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Endosome} \xrightarrow{\text{Acidification + Proteases}} \text{Antigenic peptides (13-25 aa)} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{MHC II (ER)} + \text{Invariant chain (Ii)} \xrightarrow{\text{Golgi}} \text{MIIC} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Ii degradation} \rightarrow \text{CLIP} \xrightarrow{\text{HLA-DM}} \text{Peptide exchange} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{MHC II + Peptide} \rightarrow \text{Cell surface} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Cell surface MHC II} \xrightarrow{\text{TCR + CD4 binding}} \text{CD4}^+ \text{ T cell activation}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Cross-Presentation Derivation

In **cross-presentation**, exogenous antigens (e.g., from a phagocytosed virus-infected apoptotic cell) are processed via the **cytosolic pathway** and presented on **MHC Class I** to CD8$^+$ T cells. This allows dendritic cells to "cross-prime" cytotoxic responses against intracellular pathogens that did not directly infect the dendritic cell itself.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exogenous antigen} \xrightarrow{\text{Phagocytosis by DC}} \text{Phagosome} \xrightarrow{\text{ER-membrane fusion}} \text{Cytosol release} \xrightarrow{\text{Proteasome}} \text{Peptides} \\[4pt]
\xrightarrow{\text{TAP}} \text{ER} \xrightarrow{\text{MHC I loading}} \text{Cell surface} \xrightarrow{\text{CD8}^+ \text{ activation}}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between MHC Class I and MHC Class II molecules based on their distribution and function.**

**Model Answer:**

MHC Class I molecules are expressed on **all nucleated cells** and **platelets**, whereas MHC Class II molecules are expressed only on **professional antigen-presenting cells** such as **dendritic cells, macrophages, and B lymphocytes**. Functionally, MHC Class I presents **endogenous antigens (e.g., viral proteins, tumor antigens)** derived from cytosolic processing to **CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes**, whereas MHC Class II presents **exogenous antigens (e.g., bacterial proteins)** internalized from the extracellular environment to **CD4+ helper T lymphocytes**. Additionally, the peptide-binding groove of Class I is closed-ended and binds peptides of 8–10 amino acids, while Class II has an open-ended groove accommodating peptides of 13–25 amino acids.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of TAP in MHC Class I antigen presentation?**

**Model Answer:**

**TAP (Transporter Associated with Antigen Processing)** is a heterodimeric ATP-binding cassette transporter consisting of **TAP1** and **TAP2** subunits, embedded in the **endoplasmic reticulum (ER) membrane**. Its primary role is to **translocate cytosolic peptide fragments (8–10 amino acids)** generated by the proteasome into the ER lumen. Inside the ER, TAP transfers these peptides to **nascent MHC Class I molecules** with the assistance of chaperones such as **tapasin, calnexin, and calreticulin**. Without functional TAP (as seen in **Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome Type I**), MHC Class I molecules cannot be properly loaded with peptides, resulting in **unstable MHC I retention in the ER** and severely impaired CD8+ T cell activation.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common pitfall — students often confuse the **directionality of antigen processing** (Class I = endogenous/cytosolic vs. Class II = exogenous/extracellular). Marks are frequently lost when students incorrectly assign TAP to the Class II pathway or state that Class I is restricted to APCs only.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the structure of MHC Class I molecules and explain the endogenous antigen presentation pathway with a suitable diagram.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare the genetic organization and chromosomal locations of the three classes of MHC molecules.**

#### Model Solution:

**(a)** **Structure of MHC Class I Molecule:**

MHC Class I is a heterodimer composed of:
- A **44 kDa α heavy chain** with three extracellular domains (α1, α2, α3), a transmembrane region, and a cytoplasmic tail. The **α1 and α2 domains** form the peptide-binding groove — a platform of eight antiparallel β-strings topped by two α-helices, with **closed ends** that restrict peptide length to **8–10 amino acids**. The **α3 domain** is a conserved immunoglobulin-fold that interacts with the **CD8 co-receptor**.
- A non-covalently associated **12 kDa β2-microglobulin** (encoded on chromosome 15), which stabilizes the α chain.

**Endogenous Antigen Presentation Pathway:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} & \quad \text{Intracellular proteins} \xrightarrow{\text{Ubiquitination (E1, E2, E3)}} \text{Polyubiquitinated protein} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} & \quad \text{Polyubiquitinated protein} \xrightarrow{\text{26S Proteasome}} \text{Peptides (8-10 residues)} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} & \quad \text{Peptides} \xrightarrow{\text{TAP1/TAP2 (ATP-dependent)}} \text{ER lumen} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} & \quad \alpha\text{-chain + } \beta 2m + \text{peptide} \xrightarrow{\text{Calnexin, Calreticulin, Tapasin, ERP57}} \text{Stable pMHC-I} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5:} & \quad \text{pMHC-I} \xrightarrow{\text{Golgi + Exocytosis}} \text{Plasma membrane} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6:} & \quad \text{CD8}^+ \text{ T cell} \xrightarrow{\text{TCR + CD8 engagement}} \text{Cytotoxic response}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** **Genetic Organization and Chromosomal Locations:**

The MHC (HLA in humans) is located on the **short arm of chromosome 6 (6p21.3)** and spans approximately **3.6 Mb**, making it the most gene-dense region of the human genome.

- **Class I region (telomeric end):** Contains classical (HLA-A, -B, -C) and non-classical (HLA-E, -F, -G) genes. Encodes only the α chain; β2-microglobulin is encoded on chromosome 15.
- **Class III region (central):** Contains genes for **complement components (C2, C4, Factor B)**, **cytokines (TNF-α, lymphotoxin-α)**, **heat shock proteins (HSP70)**, and **21-hydroxylase**. These genes do NOT encode antigen-presenting molecules.
- **Class II region (centromeric end):** Contains classical (HLA-DP, -DQ, -DR) and non-classical (HLA-DM, -DO) genes. Both α and β chains are encoded here.

The three regions differ in gene density, polymorphism, and function but are inherited as a **haplotype** with low recombination frequency.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the structure of MHC Class II molecules and describe the exogenous antigen presentation pathway.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of HLA-DM and the invariant chain in MHC Class II antigen presentation. What happens when these molecules are defective?**

#### Model Solution:

**(a)** **Structure of MHC Class II Molecule:**

MHC Class II is a heterodimer of two non-covalently associated transmembrane glycoproteins:
- An **α chain (~34 kDa)** with α1 and α2 extracellular domains
- A **β chain (~29 kDa)** with β1 and β2 extracellular domains

The **α1 and β1 domains** together form the **peptide-binding groove**. Unlike Class I, this groove is **open at both ends**, accommodating longer peptides of **13–25 amino acids**. The **β2 domain** contains the conserved **CD4 binding site**.

**Exogenous Antigen Presentation Pathway:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} & \quad \text{Extracellular antigen} \xrightarrow{\text{Phagocytosis/Endocytosis (APC)}} \text{Endocytic vesicle} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} & \quad \text{Endocytic vesicle} \xrightarrow{\text{Acidification + Proteases (cathepsins)}} \text{Antigenic peptides (13-25 aa)} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} & \quad \text{MHC II } \alpha\beta \text{ (ER)} + \text{Invariant chain (Ii/CD74)} \xrightarrow{\text{Golgi}} \text{MIIC} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} & \quad \text{Ii degradation} \rightarrow \text{CLIP remnant} \xrightarrow{\text{HLA-DM}} \text{CLIP release} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5:} & \quad \text{MHC II + Antigenic peptide} \rightarrow \text{Cell surface} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6:} & \quad \text{CD4}^+ \text{ T cell} \xrightarrow{\text{TCR + CD4 engagement}} \text{Helper T cell activation}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** **Role of HLA-DM and Invariant Chain:**

- **Invariant Chain (Ii, CD74):** Synthesized in the ER, Ii binds the MHC Class II αβ heterodimer to (i) **block premature peptide loading** by occupying the groove, (ii) **chaperone MHC II from ER to the MIIC compartment** via cytoplasmic sorting signals, and (iii) **prevent binding of ER-resident peptides** that would otherwise misdirect the immune response.
- **HLA-DM:** A non-classical MHC Class II molecule that resides in the MIIC compartment. It catalyzes (i) **release of CLIP** from the MHC II groove and (ii) **editing of the peptide repertoire** by removing weakly bound peptides in favor of high-affinity antigenic peptides.

**Consequences of Defects:**

- **Invariant chain deficiency:** MHC Class II molecules fail to reach the MIIC compartment; peptides cannot be loaded properly; **Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome Type II** results, characterized by severe CD4+ T cell immunodeficiency.
- **HLA-DM deficiency:** CLIP remains bound to MHC II; **CLIP-loaded MHC II** traffics to the surface but fails to present antigenic peptides, again causing CD4+ T cell immunodeficiency.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **MHC Class I** = **all nucleated cells** + **CD8+ T cells** + **endogenous antigens** + **8–10 aa peptides** + **closed groove** + **α1/α2 groove** + **α3 binds CD8**.
- ⭐ **MHC Class II** = **professional APCs only** + **CD4+ T cells** + **exogenous antigens** + **13–25 aa peptides** + **open groove** + **α1/β1 groove** + **β2 binds CD4**.
- ⭐ **MHC Class III** = **non-antigen presenting** — encodes complement, cytokines, HSPs.
- ⭐ **TAP** transports peptides **into the ER** (Class I pathway) — ATP-dependent.
- ⭐ **Invariant chain (Ii)** blocks MHC II groove in ER; degraded to **CLIP**.
- ⭐ **HLA-DM** releases CLIP and edits peptide repertoire in **MIIC** compartment.
- ⭐ **Cross-presentation**: Exogenous antigens presented on Class I by dendritic cells.
- ⭐ **Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome Type I** = TAP defect → unstable MHC I.
- ⭐ **Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome Type II** = MHC II transcription factor (CIITA) defect.
- ⭐ HLA genes are the **most polymorphic** in the human genome — critical for transplant matching and disease association studies.
- ⭐ Chromosomal mnemonic: **"I for Inverted chromosome 15"** (β2m on chr 15); **everything else MHC is on chromosome 6**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MHC: Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** MHC: Structure # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC)** is a tightly linked cluster of genes encoding cell-surface glycoproteins that bind peptide fragments derived from intracellular or extracellular pathogens and present them to **T lymphocytes**, thereby initiating the adaptive (specific) immune response. In humans, this gene complex is termed the **Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA)** system, located on the **short arm of chromosome 6 (6p21.3)**. The MHC was originally discovered through studies of **tissue graft rejection** in inbred mouse strains (George Snell, 1930s–1940s). The word *histo* means *tissue*, indicating its role in tissue compatibility. MHC molecules are also known as **MHC antigens** because they were first identified as alloantigens responsible for graft rejection. ### Two Principal Classes of MHC Molecules | Feature | **MHC Class I** | **MHC Class II** | |---|---|---| | Alternate Name | Class Ia / Classical | Class IIa / Classical | | Genetic Loci (Human) | **HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C** | **HLA-DP, HLA-DQ, HLA-DR** | | Chain Composition | α chain (heavy) + β2-microglobulin (light) | α chain + β chain (both transmembrane) | | Peptide Source | Endogenous (cytosolic proteins) | Exogenous (phagocytosed/extracellular) | | Presenting Cells | **All nucleated cells** + Platelets | **Professional APCs** (Dendritic cells, Macrophages, B cells) | | T-cell Partner | **CD8+ Cytotoxic T-cells (Tc)** | **CD4+ Helper T-cells (Th)** | | Peptide Length | **8–10 amino acids** | **13–25 amino acids** | | Expression | Constitutive | Inducible (by IFN-γ) | > [!NOTE] > **Class III MHC region** is technically part of the MHC locus but encodes **non-antigen-presenting molecules** such as complement components (C2, C4, factor B), cytokines (TNF-α, lymphotoxin), and heat-shock proteins. It is **NOT** involved in peptide presentation to T-cells. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine MHC molecules as "biological display windows" on the surface of your body's cells.** Every nucleated cell in your body operates like a citizen of a country. To prove identity and health status, each cell wears a uniform — these uniforms are **MHC Class I molecules**. Inside every cell, proteins are continuously broken down into small peptide fragments (much like shredding old documents). A tiny piece of every internal protein is "pinned" onto the MHC Class I window, allowing patrolling **CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells** to peek at the display. If a cell is healthy, the peptides shown are normal "self" peptides, and the immune system leaves it alone. If the cell is infected by a virus or has become cancerous, *foreign* or *mutated self* peptides are displayed — the CD8+ T-cell recognizes these abnormal fragments and orders the cell to self-destruct (apoptosis). For **MHC Class II**, only specialized security guards — the **professional Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs)** — wear these uniforms. They are stationed at body entry points (skin, mucosa, lymph nodes) and "sample" the external environment by engulfing bacteria, toxins, and debris. After digestion, they display peptide fragments on MHC Class II windows to the **CD4+ helper T-cells**, which then rally the rest of the immune system (B-cells, macrophages, cytotoxic T-cells) into action. **Key constants / metrics to remember:** - Human MHC occupies approximately **4 Mb** of DNA on chromosome **6p21.3**. - Class I heavy chain: ~**45 kDa**; β2-microglobulin: ~**12 kDa**. - Class II α chain: ~**33 kDa**; β chain: ~**28 kDa**. - There are approximately **200+ HLA genes**, making it the **most gene-dense and polymorphic region of the human genome**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Architecture of MHC Molecules Both MHC Class I and Class II molecules belong to the **Immunoglobulin (Ig) superfamily** because their extracellular domains possess the characteristic Ig-fold (a β-sandwich stabilized by an internal disulfide bond). This structural homology means they share evolutionary ancestry with antibodies and T-cell receptors. ### 2.1.1 MHC Class I — Detailed Structure The Class I molecule is a heterodimer composed of: 1. **Heavy α chain (44–47 kDa)** — encoded by *HLA-A, -B, or -C* genes on chromosome 6. - Contains **three extracellular domains**: α1, α2, α3 (~90 amino acids each). - Has a **transmembrane hydrophobic segment** (~25 hydrophobic residues). - Has a short **cytoplasmic tail** (~30 amino acids) with phosphorylation sites for signaling. - **Non-covalently associated with β2-microglobulin (12 kDa)**, encoded on **chromosome 15**. 2. **β2-microglobulin (β2m)** — a soluble, non-transmembrane, non-covalently bound light chain. It is essential for proper folding, peptide loading, and surface expression of the heavy chain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Peptide-Binding Groove of Class I:** > - Formed by the **α1 and α2 domains**. > - Has a **closed-ended groove** (blocked at both ends by conserved residues: **Tyr84, Tyr123, Trp143, Tyr171**). > - This is why it accommodates only **short peptides (8–10 aa)**. > - The floor is formed by **8 antiparallel β-strands**. > - The walls are formed by **two α-helices** running along the groove. > - Six pockets (**A–F**) within the groove anchor peptide side chains via anchor residues. ### 2.1.2 MHC Class II — Detailed Structure The Class II molecule is also a heterodimer, but **both chains are transmembrane** and both are encoded within the MHC locus on chromosome 6. 1. **α chain (~33 kDa)** — contains α1 and α2 extracellular domains. 2. **β chain (~28 kDa)** — contains β1 and β2 extracellular domains. 3. The **α2 and β2 domains** are Ig-fold domains that interact with **CD4** co-receptor. 4. The **transmembrane and cytoplasmic regions** of both chains anchor the molecule to the APC membrane. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Peptide-Binding Groove of Class II:** > - Formed by the **α1 and β1 domains**. > - Has an **open-ended groove** (open at both ends), accommodating **longer peptides (13–25 aa)**. > - Pockets in Class II are more flexible than in Class I, allowing binding of a wider variety of peptide sequences. > - Critical anchor residues: a hydrophobic/aromatic residue at **P1** (deep pocket) and a small/hydrophobic residue at **P9** of the core 9-mer. ### 2.1.3 Gene Organization of the Human MHC (HLA Complex) The HLA complex spans ~4 Mb and is conventionally divided into three regions: | Region | Class | Key Genes | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **Telomeric** | Class I | HLA-A, -B, -C (classical); HLA-E, -F, -G (non-classical) | Peptide presentation to CD8+ T-cells / NK regulation | | **Centromeric** | Class II | HLA-DP, -DQ, -DR (classical); HLA-DM, -DO (non-classical) | Peptide presentation to CD4+ T-cells | | **Central** | Class III | C2, C4, Bf, TNF, HSP70 | Complement, cytokines, inflammation | > [!NOTE] > **Non-classical MHC** molecules (e.g., HLA-E, HLA-G, HLA-DM, CD1) have specialized functions. **HLA-DM** catalyzes peptide exchange in Class II loading; **CD1** presents lipid/glycolipid antigens (e.g., mycobacterial cell wall lipids) to NKT cells. ## 2.2 Polymorphism, Polygeny & Linkage Disequilibrium These three concepts define the uniqueness of MHC and are extremely high-yield for KTU exams. ### A. Polygeny The presence of **multiple genes** encoding MHC molecules of similar function within the genome. For example, an individual has *HLA-A, -B,* and *-C* genes for Class I, and *HLA-DP, -DQ,* and *-DR* genes for Class II — each gene product presents a distinct repertoire of peptides. ### B. Polymorphism Within a population, each MHC locus has **many alleles** (e.g., *HLA-B* has over **3,500 known alleles**). Polymorphism is concentrated in the **exons encoding the peptide-binding groove (α1 and α2 of Class I; β1 of Class II)**. This ensures that different individuals can present different sets of pathogen-derived peptides. ### C. Linkage Disequilibrium (LD) Certain HLA alleles occur together on the same haplotype **far more frequently** than predicted by random assortment. For example, the haplotype **A1-B8-DR3** is commonly inherited together in Caucasians. ### D. Mendelian (Codominant) Expression Because humans are diploid, every individual expresses **both maternal and paternal MHC alleles** simultaneously on every nucleated cell. This is known as **codominant expression**, and it dramatically expands the peptide repertoire presented to T-cells. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Hook — The "4 P's" of MHC:** > 1. **P**olygenic (multiple genes per locus) > 2. **P**olymorphic (many alleles per gene) > 3. **P**resentation (peptide presentation to T-cells) > 4. **P**athway linkage (Class I → CD8; Class II → CD4) ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Immunology has relatively few quantitative formulas, but the following relations are frequently tested in KTU examinations, particularly in population-genetics contexts. | Concept / Formula | Description | Variables / Units | |---|---|---| | $H = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i^2$ | Hardy-Weinberg MHC homozygosity | $p_i$ = allele frequency | | $h = 1 - H = \sum p_i^2$ | Probability that two alleles at a locus are identical (homozygous) | — | | $P_{\text{match}} = \prod_{j=1}^{k} (1 - p_j^2)$ | Probability of being homozygous at $k$ MHC loci | $p_j$ = allele frequency at locus $j$ | | $N_{\text{peptides}} = 2 \times n_{\text{loci}} \times m_{\text{alleles}}$ | Total distinct MHC molecules expressed per cell (codominant) | $n_{\text{loci}}$ = number of MHC loci; $m_{\text{alleles}}$ = number of alleles per haplotype | | $L_{\text{Class I}} = 8$ to $10$ aa | Length of peptides bound in Class I groove | amino acids | | $L_{\text{Class II}} = 13$ to $25$ aa | Length of peptides bound in Class II groove | amino acids | | $\Delta G_{\text{binding}} \approx -8$ to $-12$ kcal/mol | Free energy of peptide-MHC binding | kcal/mol | | $t_{1/2}^{\text{Class I}} \approx 24 \text{ h}$ | Half-life of MHC Class I on cell surface | hours | | $M_{\alpha}^{\text{I}} \approx 45$ kDa | Molecular mass of Class I heavy chain | kilodaltons | | $M_{\beta 2m} \approx 12$ kDa | Molecular mass of β2-microglobulin | kilodaltons | | $M_{\alpha}^{\text{II}} \approx 33$ kDa | Molecular mass of Class II α chain | kilodaltons | | $M_{\beta}^{\text{II}} \approx 28$ kDa | Molecular mass of Class II β chain | kilodaltons | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world utility in clinical / engineering fields:** > - **Organ transplantation**: MHC matching reduces graft rejection. > - **Forensic medicine & paternity testing**: HLA typing via PCR-SSO/SSP/SBT. > - **Disease association**: HLA-B27 → ankylosing spondylitis; HLA-DR3/DR4 → Type 1 diabetes; HLA-DQ2/DQ8 → celiac disease. > - **Vaccine design**: Predictions of peptide-MHC binding affinities drive **epitope-based vaccine design** (e.g., neoantigen cancer vaccines). > - **Bioinformatics & computational immunology**: Tools like **NetMHCpan**, **SYFPEITHI**, and **IEDB** predict peptide-MHC binding for personalized immunotherapy. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Quantitative Estimation of MHC Diversity in an Individual This derivation is a **favourite KTU numerical/analytical question** because it tests conceptual understanding of **codominant expression** combined with **polymorphism** and **polygeny**. ### Problem Statement A heterozygous human expresses the following Class I HLA alleles on chromosome 6: - *HLA-A\*02:01* and *HLA-A\*24:02* - *HLA-B\*07:02* and *HLA-B\*35:01* - *HLA-C\*04:01* and *HLA-C\*07:02* **Calculate the total number of distinct MHC Class I molecules expressed on the surface of a nucleated cell of this individual.** Assume each allele produces a functional heavy chain that pairs with β2-microglobulin. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the relevant genetic loci.** The Class I classical loci in humans are: - *HLA-A* - *HLA-B* - *HLA-C* Thus, $n_{\text{loci}} = 3$. **Step 2 — Apply the principle of codominant expression.** Because the individual is heterozygous at every locus, both maternal and paternal alleles are expressed. Each allele encodes a distinct heavy α chain that folds with β2-microglobulin to yield a unique Class I molecule. **Step 3 — Count the alleles per haplotype.** For the paternal haplotype: $$A_{\text{paternal}} = \{A^{*}02{:}01,\ B^{*}07{:}02,\ C^{*}04{:}01\}$$ $$|A_{\text{paternal}}| = 3$$ For the maternal haplotype: $$A_{\text{maternal}} = \{A^{*}24{:}02,\ B^{*}35{:}01,\ C^{*}07{:}02\}$$ $$|A_{\text{maternal}}| = 3$$ **Step 4 — Compute the total number of distinct heavy chains.** Since each allele is unique (different sequences → different peptide-binding specificities), all 6 alleles yield 6 distinct heavy chains: $$N_{\text{heavy}} = |A_{\text{paternal}}| + |A_{\text{maternal}}| = 3 + 3 = 6$$ **Step 5 — Pair each heavy chain with β2-microglobulin.** Because β2-microglobulin is **monomorphic** (essentially invariant across individuals) and a single gene on chromosome 15 produces it, every heavy chain pairs with the same β2-microglobulin. The pairing does **NOT** generate new diversity: $$N_{\text{Class I}} = N_{\text{heavy}} \times 1 = 6 \times 1 = 6$$ **Step 6 — Generalization formula.** Using the notation from Section 2: $$N_{\text{Class I}}^{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n_{\text{loci}}} m_j$$ where $m_j$ is the number of distinct alleles at locus $j$ in the diploid genotype. For the Class II analysis (assuming *HLA-DR\*01:01 / DR\*04:04*, *HLA-DQ\*02:01 / DQ\*05:02*, *HLA-DP\*04:01 / DP\*02:01*): $$\begin{aligned} N_{\text{Class II}} &= \sum_{j=1}^{n_{\text{loci II}}} m_j \\ &= m_{DR} + m_{DQ} + m_{DP} \\ &= 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 \text{ distinct } (\alpha\beta) \text{ heterodimers} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Unlike Class I, **each Class II molecule is an αβ heterodimer**. Both chains are polymorphic, so pairing one α chain with one β chain creates a unique antigen-presenting molecule. If you have $a$ α-chain alleles and $b$ β-chain alleles at the same locus, the theoretical maximum heterodimers = $a \times b$ (assuming random pairing). However, within the *DR* locus, the α chain is largely monomorphic, so diversity comes predominantly from β chain polymorphism. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Class I vs. Class II — Comprehensive Comparison This matrix consolidates every KTU-exam-relevant parameter and is **directly reproducible** in answer scripts. | Parameter | **MHC Class I** | **MHC Class II** | |---|---|---| | Chains | α (heavy, 45 kDa) + β2m (12 kDa) | α (33 kDa) + β (28 kDa) | | Chromosomal location of genes | α on **chr 6**; β2m on **chr 15** | Both on **chr 6** | | Domain organization | α1, α2, α3 + β2m | α1, α2 + β1, β2 | | Peptide-binding groove | α1 + α2 | α1 + β1 | | Groove geometry | Closed ends | Open ends | | Peptide length | 8–10 aa | 13–25 aa | | Peptide origin | Cytosolic (endogenous) | Extracellular (exogenous) | | Loading compartment | Endoplasmic reticulum (TAP-dependent) | MIIC / Endosome (invariant chain pathway) | | Chaperones in loading | Calnexin, calreticulin, tapasin | Invariant chain (Ii/CD74), HLA-DM | | TAP dependency | **Yes** | **No** | | Expression pattern | All nucleated cells | Professional APCs only | | Cytokine upregulation | IFN-α/β, IFN-γ (mild) | **IFN-γ (strong inducer)** | | T-cell partner | CD8+ Tc | CD4+ Th | | Co-receptor binding site | α3 domain binds CD8 | β2 domain binds CD4 | | Surface half-life | ~24 h | ~few hours to days | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Hardy-Weinberg Homozygosity at HLA-DRB1 **Problem:** In a population, the frequency of *HLA-DRB1\*04:01* allele is $p = 0.18$ and *HLA-DRB1\*04:04* allele frequency is $q = 0.12$. Assuming Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, calculate: (a) The frequency of homozygotes carrying the *04:01/04:01* genotype. (b) The frequency of *04:01/04:04* heterozygotes. (c) The homozygosity at this single locus. **Solution:** (a) Homozygous *04:01/04:01* frequency: $$\begin{aligned} f_{04{:}01/04{:}01} &= p^2 \\ &= (0.18)^2 \\ &= 0.0324 \end{aligned}$$ (b) Heterozygous *04:01/04:04* frequency: $$\begin{aligned} f_{04{:}01/04{:}04} &= 2pq \\ &= 2 \times 0.18 \times 0.12 \\ &= 0.0432 \end{aligned}$$ (c) Homozygosity at this locus (assuming only these two alleles exist): $$\begin{aligned} H &= p^2 + q^2 \\ &= 0.0324 + (0.12)^2 \\ &= 0.0324 + 0.0144 \\ &= 0.0468 \end{aligned}$$ Heterozygosity: $$h = 1 - H = 1 - 0.0468 = 0.9532$$ > [!NOTE] > A high heterozygosity indicates that MHC loci in the population carry enormous diversity, ensuring survival against rapidly evolving pathogens. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MHC Class I vs. Class II The following Mermaid diagram represents the **molecular architecture** and **functional interactions** of MHC Class I and Class II molecules. Briefly describe their structure with a neat diagram. **Model Answer:** MHC Class I molecules are cell-surface glycoproteins that present **endogenous peptide fragments (8–10 amino acids)** to **CD8+ cytotoxic T-lymphocytes**. They are expressed on **all nucleated cells** and platelets. **Structure:** - **Heavy α chain (~45 kDa)** with three extracellular domains (α1, α2, α3), a transmembrane region, and a cytoplasmic tail. Encoded by *HLA-A, -B,* or *-C* genes on chromosome 6. - **β2-microglobulin (~12 kDa)**: a non-covalently associated, monomorphic light chain encoded on chromosome 15. - **Peptide-binding groove** formed by α1 and α2 domains, with **closed ends** that constrain peptide length. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]** Differentiate between MHC Class I and MHC Class II molecules based on their chain composition, peptide source, and T-cell partner. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | **MHC Class I** | **MHC Class II** | |---|---|---| | Chain composition | α chain + β2-microglobulin | α chain + β chain | | Peptide source | Endogenous (cytosolic) | Exogenous (extracellular) | | Peptide length | 8–10 aa | 13–25 aa | | Presenting cells | All nucleated cells | Professional APCs | | T-cell partner | CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells | CD4+ helper T-cells | | Groove | Closed ends (α1 + α2) | Open ends (α1 + β1) | --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > *Note: KTU ESE Module 2 conventionally offers internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Both alternatives are provided below.* ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester]** **(a)** Describe in detail the structural organization of MHC Class I molecules, including their chain composition, domains, peptide-binding groove architecture, and chromosomal location of encoding genes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concepts of polymorphism, polygeny, and codominant expression in MHC. How do these properties enhance the immune response at the population level? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a)** [7 marks — structure of MHC Class I] MHC Class I molecules are heterodimers comprising: **1. Heavy α chain (45 kDa):** - Encoded by classical Class I genes (*HLA-A, -B, -C*) located on chromosome 6p21.3. - Contains three extracellular domains — **α1 (N-terminal, ~90 aa), α2 (~90 aa), α3 (membrane-proximal, ~90 aa)**. - Has a hydrophobic transmembrane segment (~25 residues) anchoring it to the plasma membrane. - Has a short cytoplasmic tail (~30 aa) with phosphorylation and endocytosis motifs. **2. β2-microglobulin (12 kDa):** - Encoded by a single gene on chromosome 15q21-q22. - Soluble, non-covalently associated light chain. - Has a single Ig-like domain. - Essential for proper folding, intracellular trafficking, and surface stability of the heavy chain. **3. Peptide-binding groove:** - Formed jointly by the **α1 and α2 domains**. - Floor: 8-stranded antiparallel β-pleated sheet. - Walls: two long α-helices running along the groove. - Contains **6 pockets (A–F)** that engage peptide side chains at anchor positions (P2 and P9 most conserved). - **Closed at both ends** by conserved residues (Tyr7, Tyr84, Tyr123, Trp143, Tyr171), limiting peptides to **8–10 aa**. **4. α3 domain:** - Ig-fold domain responsible for non-covalent interaction with **CD8 co-receptor** on cytotoxic T-cells. **(b)** [7 marks — polymorphism, polygeny, codominant expression] **Polymorphism:** Within a population, each MHC locus exists in many allelic forms. *HLA-B* alone has over **3,500 known alleles**. Polymorphic residues are clustered in the **exons encoding the peptide-binding groove (α1/α2 for Class I; β1 for Class II)**. Different alleles bind different peptide repertoires. **Polygeny:** Each individual carries multiple MHC genes encoding similar but non-identical molecules (e.g., HLA-A, -B, -C for Class I; HLA-DP, -DQ, -DR for Class II). This expands the peptide repertoire presented by a single individual. **Codominant expression:** Both maternal and paternal alleles at every MHC locus are co-expressed on the cell surface. For example, a heterozygous individual expresses **6 distinct Class I heavy chains** (2 × 3 loci) simultaneously. **Population-level advantage:** - A population with diverse MHC alleles ensures that at least some members can present peptides from any emerging pathogen. - This is the basis of **heterozygote advantage** at MHC loci — individuals heterozygous at MHC loci present a broader peptide repertoire and resist a wider variety of pathogens than homozygotes. - Pathogens that mutate to evade one MHC allele still face immune recognition via other MHC molecules in heterozygous or polygenic individuals. - This is also why MHC is implicated in **mate-selection preferences** (e.g., MHC-disassortative mating in mice and humans), maintaining population diversity. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End Semester]** **(a)** Describe the detailed structure of MHC Class II molecules and compare it with MHC Class I structure using a comparative table. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the genomic organization of the HLA complex. Add a note on the role of non-classical MHC molecules. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a)** [7 marks — structure of MHC Class II] MHC Class II molecules are heterodimers of two non-covalently associated transmembrane glycoproteins: **α chain (33 kDa):** Encoded by *HLA-DP, -DQ,* or *-DR* α genes on chromosome 6. Contains α1 and α2 extracellular domains, a transmembrane segment, and a cytoplasmic tail. **β chain (28 kDa):** Encoded by *HLA-DP, -DQ,* or *-DR* β genes on chromosome 6. Contains β1 and β2 extracellular domains, a transmembrane segment, and a cytoplasmic tail. **Peptide-binding groove:** Formed by **α1 and β1** domains. **Open at both ends**, accommodating longer peptides (13–25 aa). The groove has greater flexibility than Class I, allowing binding of a wider peptide repertoire. **CD4 binding site:** The β2 domain (membrane-proximal Ig-fold) binds **CD4 co-receptor** on helper T-cells. | Feature | **MHC Class I** | **MHC Class II** | |---|---|---| | Chains | α + β2-microglobulin | α + β | | Domain layout | α1, α2, α3 + β2m | α1, α2 + β1, β2 | | Groove-forming domains | α1 + α2 | α1 + β1 | | Groove geometry | Closed ends | Open ends | | Co-receptor site | α3 (binds CD8) | β2 (binds CD4) | | Chromosomal location of α gene | Chr 6 | Chr 6 | | Chromosomal location of partner gene | Chr 15 (β2m) | Chr 6 (β) | **(b)** [7 marks — HLA genomic organization & non-classical MHC] **Genomic Organization of the HLA Complex:** The HLA complex spans approximately **4 megabases** on the **short arm of chromosome 6 (6p21.3)** and is one of the most gene-dense regions of the human genome. It is divided into three regions: 1. **Class I region (telomeric):** Contains classical (*HLA-A, -B, -C*) and non-classical (*HLA-E, -F, -G*) Class I genes. Class I genes are also present on other chromosomes as pseudogenes. 2. **Class III region (central):** Located between Class I and Class II. Contains genes for **complement components (C2, C4A, C4B, factor B)**, **cytokines (TNF-α, lymphotoxin-α, lymphotoxin-β)**, **heat-shock proteins (HSP70)**, and **steroid hydroxylases (CYP21)**. Class III products are **NOT** MHC molecules in the classical sense — they do not present antigens. 3. **Class II region (centromeric):** Contains classical Class II genes (*HLA-DP, -DQ, -DR*) and non-classical genes (*HLA-DM, -DO*). **Non-classical MHC molecules:** - **HLA-E, HLA-F, HLA-G:** Class Ib molecules with limited polymorphism. HLA-E presents leader peptides derived from other Class I molecules and regulates **NK cell activity** via CD94/NKG2 receptors. HLA-G is expressed on placental trophoblasts and mediates **maternal-fetal immune tolerance**. - **HLA-DM:** A Class II-like chaperone that catalyzes **peptide exchange** in the MIIC compartment, removing the invariant chain (CD74) fragment CLIP and loading high-affinity antigenic peptides onto classical Class II molecules. - **HLA-DO:** A Class II-like molecule that modulates HLA-DM function in B cells. - **CD1 family (CD1a–e):** Although encoded outside the MHC locus on chromosome 1, CD1 molecules structurally resemble MHC Class I (associated with β2m) but present **lipid and glycolipid antigens** (e.g., mycobacterial cell wall lipids) to **NKT cells**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (where students lose marks):** > 1. **Confusing α chains with β chains:** Many students mix up the chain compositions. **Memorize:** Class I = α + β2m; Class II = α + β. > 2. **Peptide length confusion:** Class I binds **short** peptides (8–10 aa); Class II binds **longer** peptides (13–25 aa). Closed groove vs. open groove is the structural reason. > 3. **Groove-forming domains confusion:** Class I groove = α1 + α2; Class II groove = α1 + β1 (NOT α2 + β2 — those are the Ig-fold domains). > 4. **Forgetting CD4 vs. CD8 binding sites:** CD4 binds β2 of Class II; CD8 binds α3 of Class I. > 5. **Misclassifying Class III as MHC:** Class III encodes complement and cytokines — **NOT** antigen-presenting molecules. > 6. **Codominance oversimplification:** Saying "all MHC genes are expressed" is too vague. Specify that **both parental alleles** at **every MHC locus** are co-expressed. > 7. **Polymorphism location:** Students often say "MHC is polymorphic" without specifying that polymorphism is concentrated in the **peptide-binding groove** exons. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — MHC Structure** - **MHC = HLA in humans; H-2 in mice.** Encoded on **chromosome 6p21.3**; spans **~4 Mb**; most gene-dense and polymorphic region in the human genome. - **Three regions:** Class I (telomeric), Class III (central), Class II (centromeric). - **Class I:** α chain (45 kDa, chr 6) + β2-microglobulin (12 kDa, chr 15). α1 + α2 form groove; α3 binds CD8. Closed-ended groove; peptides **8–10 aa**. - **Class II:** α (33 kDa) + β (28 kDa); both on chr 6. α1 + β1 form groove; β2 binds CD4. Open-ended groove; peptides **13–25 aa**. - **Class III:** Complement (C2, C4, Bf), cytokines (TNF-α, LT), HSP70. **Not antigen-presenting.** - **Polygeny:** multiple MHC genes per individual. - **Polymorphism:** thousands of alleles per locus; concentrated in peptide-binding groove exons. - **Codominant expression:** both parental alleles co-expressed. - **Peptide loading Class I:** ER, TAP-dependent, tapasin/calnexin/calreticulin. - **Peptide loading Class II:** MIIC endosome, invariant chain (CD74), HLA-DM. - **Class I → CD8+ Tc (endogenous antigens: viruses, tumors).** - **Class II → CD4+ Th (exogenous antigens: bacteria, toxins).** - **Non-classical MHC:** HLA-E (NK regulation), HLA-G (placental tolerance), HLA-DM (peptide exchange), CD1 (lipid antigens). - **HLA-disease associations:** B27 (ankylosing spondylitis), DR3/DR4 (T1DM), DQ2/DQ8 (celiac disease). - **Hardy-Weinberg homozygosity formula:** $H = \sum p_i^2$. Heterozygosity $h = 1 - H$. - **Diversity formula:** $N_{\text{MHC}} = \sum_{j} m_j$ (sum of alleles at each locus). - **Clinical applications:** transplantation matching, paternity testing, vaccine epitope design, autoimmune disease diagnosis.

topics.title: MHC: Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** MHC: Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC)** is a tightly linked cluster of genes located on the short arm of chromosome 6 in humans (HLA complex) that encode cell-surface glycoproteins essential for the regulation of immune responses. The MHC molecules perform three principal biological functions:

1. **Antigen Presentation** — Displaying peptide fragments derived from intracellular (endogenous) and extracellular (exogenous) protein sources on the cell surface for surveillance by T lymphocytes.
2. **Self vs. Non-Self Discrimination** — Providing the molecular basis by which the immune system distinguishes infected/abnormal cells from healthy host cells.
3. **T-Cell Receptor (TCR) Engagement** — Delivering the **first signal** of T-cell activation through the trimolecular interaction of **MHC–Peptide–TCR**, supported by co-stimulatory signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The functions of MHC molecules are central to understanding **adaptive cellular immunity**. KTU examiners frequently test antigen presentation pathways (Class I vs. Class II) and the distinction between **MHC restriction** and **co-stimulation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Exhibition Hall" Model

Imagine the human body as a **massive museum**, and every nucleated cell is a **gallery room** inside it:

- Each cell has two **exhibition windows** (MHC Class I and MHC Class II).
- Inside each room, **security cameras** constantly photograph protein fragments (peptides) being made or broken down.
- These photographs are **mounted on the windows** so that roaming **security inspectors** (T-cells) can look at them.
- If the photos show **routine household items** (self-peptides), the inspector walks past. If the photos show **suspicious intruder fingerprints** (foreign/viral peptides), the inspector **calls for backup** (immune activation).

> **Class I windows are present on EVERY nucleated cell** (every gallery has one), while **Class II windows are present only on "Professional Exhibition Halls"** — the Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs) such as dendritic cells, macrophages, and B-cells.

---

## 1.3 Critical Quantitative Markers

| Parameter | MHC Class I | MHC Class II |
|---|---|---|
| Genetic locus | HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C | HLA-DP, HLA-DQ, HLA-DR |
| Structure | α-chain + β₂-microglobulin | α-chain + β-chain (both MHC-encoded) |
| Expression | All nucleated cells | Professional APCs (constitutive), others (induced) |
| Polypeptide size | ~45 kDa (α) + 12 kDa (β₂m) | ~33 kDa (α) + ~28 kDa (β) |
| Peptide length | **8–10 amino acids** | **13–25 amino acids** |
| T-cell partner | **CD8⁺ cytotoxic T-cells** | **CD4⁺ helper T-cells** |
| Antigen source | Endogenous (cytosolic) | Exogenous (phagocytosed/endocytosed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MHC RESTRICTION (Zinkernagel–Doherty phenomenon, 1974):**
> A T-cell recognizes a peptide **ONLY** when it is presented by the **SAME MHC allele** that the T-cell encountered during thymic selection. This is the foundational rule governing all T-cell responses and is a guaranteed high-yield topic in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Functions of MHC — Expanded

### Function 1: Antigen Presentation

MHC molecules are **peptide display platforms**. They do **not** generate antibodies, do not kill pathogens, and do not signal directly. Their exclusive role is to **bind peptide fragments and present them extracellularly** for inspection.

**Mechanistic logic:**
1. Proteins inside the cell are constantly turned over.
2. Short peptide fragments (8–25 aa) are generated.
3. These peptides are loaded onto MHC molecules in intracellular compartments (ER for Class I; MIIC/endo-lysosomes for Class II).
4. The peptide–MHC complex is transported to the cell surface.
5. The complex is scanned by T-cells bearing matching TCRs.

### Function 2: Self / Non-Self Discrimination

During T-cell development in the **thymus**, thymocytes are subjected to a two-step quality check:

- **Positive selection** → T-cells that can weakly recognize self-MHC survive (MHC restriction imposed here).
- **Negative selection** → T-cells that strongly recognize self-peptide + self-MHC are eliminated (central tolerance).

This dual selection ensures that, in the periphery, surviving T-cells will respond ONLY when:
- They see a peptide in the groove of a self-MHC molecule, AND
- The peptide is foreign/abnormal (i.e., altered self).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Analogy (KTU Style):**
> This is analogous to a **firewall with a whitelist**. Only packets carrying the correct IP signature (self-MHC) AND approved payload structure (non-self peptide) are allowed to trigger downstream responses.

### Function 3: Initiation of Adaptive Immune Responses

MHC-mediated antigen presentation is the **bridge between innate and adaptive immunity**. The full activation of a naïve T-cell requires:

- **Signal 1:** TCR engagement with peptide–MHC complex.
- **Signal 2 (Co-stimulation):** B7 (CD80/86) on APC binding CD28 on T-cell.
- **Signal 3 (Cytokine):** IL-12, IL-4, or other cytokines that direct differentiation.

> **Without MHC presentation, NO Signal 1 can ever be generated**, and the adaptive immune response cannot initiate. This is why MHC-deficient individuals suffer severe combined immunodeficiency.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet (Formula/Cheat-Sheet Style)

| Concept | Key Feature | KTU Exam-Relevant Distinction |
|---|---|---|
| MHC Class I pathway | Endogenous antigens; proteasome → TAP transporter → ER → cell surface | CD8⁺ T-cells; **all nucleated cells** |
| MHC Class II pathway | Exogenous antigens; phagolysosome → MIIC → cell surface | CD4⁺ T-cells; **only professional APCs** |
| TAP transporter | ATP-dependent peptide pump (TAP1/TAP2) | Required for Class I peptide loading |
| Invariant chain (Ii) | Blocks Class II groove in ER, directs Class II to endosomes | Prevents premature self-peptide binding |
| HLA-DM | Catalyst for peptide exchange in endosomes | Removes CLIP from Class II groove |
| Cross-presentation | Dendritic cells load Class I from exogenous antigens | Activates CD8⁺ T-cells against extracellular pathogens/tumors |
| MHC polymorphism | Multiple alleles per locus in population | Basis of transplant rejection and disease association |
| MHC restriction | TCR sees peptide **only in context of self-MHC** | Nobel Prize 1996 (Zinkernagel & Doherty) |
| Alloreactivity | ~1–10% of T-cells recognize foreign MHC directly | Causes graft rejection |
| Superantigens | Bypass peptide groove; bind outside MHC II to TCR Vβ | Mass polyclonal activation (e.g., TSST-1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Rule of Thumb (often tested):**
> $$\text{Polymorphic residues in MHC groove} \approx 5\text{–}10 \text{ aa positions out of ~180 total}$$
> $$\Rightarrow \text{A single allele presents thousands of distinct peptides}$$

---

## 2.3 Engineering / Real-World Utility

The principle of MHC antigen presentation has been translated into multiple **biomedical engineering applications**, all examinable in the KTU Biotechnology syllabus:

1. **Recombinant vaccine design** — Engineering peptide epitopes that bind human HLA alleles with high affinity (e.g., HPV L1, SARS-CoV-2 spike vaccines).
2. **Tumor immunotherapy** — Chimeric Antigen Receptor (CAR) constructs partially mimic MHC–peptide–TCR signaling.
3. **Transplant matching** — Tissue typing laboratories use serological and DNA-based HLA typing to match donors and recipients.
4. **HLA-disease association studies** — Identifying HLA-B27 (ankylosing spondylitis), HLA-DR3/DR4 (Type 1 diabetes) for diagnostics.
5. **Peptide-MHC multimer technology** — Used in flow cytometry to track antigen-specific T-cells (clinical monitoring).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Pathways & Comparison Matrices

## 3.1 Analytical Pathway A — Endogenous (Class I) Antigen Presentation

We will exhaustively walk through the **cytosolic pathway**, since KTU frequently asks students to "trace the fate of an intracellular viral protein."

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting material:** A viral protein synthesized on a ribosome in the cytosol of an infected cell.
> **End goal:** Peptide displayed on the cell surface via MHC Class I, recognized by a CD8⁺ cytotoxic T-lymphocyte.

**Step 1 — Ubiquitin tagging:**
$$\text{Viral protein} + \text{Ubiquitin (Ub)} \xrightarrow{\text{E1, E2, E3 ligases}} \text{Ubiquitinated protein}$$

**Step 2 — Proteasomal degradation:**
$$\text{Ubiquitinated protein} \xrightarrow{\text{26S Proteasome}} \text{Peptides (8–10 aa)}$$

The proteasome is a **multi-subunit cylindrical protease** with chymotrypsin-, trypsin-, and caspase-like activities.

**Step 3 — TAP-mediated transport into ER:**
$$\text{Peptide}_{\text{cytosol}} + \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{TAP1/TAP2}} \text{Peptide}_{\text{ER lumen}}$$

TAP = **Transporter Associated with Antigen Processing**, an ABC-family transporter.

**Step 4 — MHC Class I assembly in ER:**
$$\alpha\text{-chain} + \beta_2\text{-microglobulin} + \text{peptide} \xrightarrow{\text{Erp57, calnexin, calreticulin}} \text{Stable trimer}$$

> **Critical step:** The MHC Class I molecule is **unstable without a peptide**. Empty Class I molecules are retained in the ER by **calreticulin** and **tapasin** until a high-affinity peptide is loaded.

**Step 5 — Exocytosis to cell surface:**
$$\text{pMHC I}_{\text{ER}} \xrightarrow{\text{Golgi vesicles}} \text{Plasma membrane}$$

**Step 6 — TCR recognition:**
$$\text{TCR (CD8⁺)} + \text{pMHC I} \rightarrow \text{Signal 1} \rightarrow \text{CTL activation}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Pathway B — Exogenous (Class II) Antigen Presentation

> **Starting material:** A bacterial protein internalized by phagocytosis in a dendritic cell.
> **End goal:** Peptide displayed on the cell surface via MHC Class II, recognized by a CD4⁺ helper T-cell.

**Step 1 — Antigen uptake:**
$$\text{Bacterium} \xrightarrow{\text{Phagocytosis / Endocytosis}} \text{Phagosome} \xrightarrow{\text{Fusion with lysosome}} \text{Phagolysosome}$$

**Step 2 — Proteolytic cleavage:**
$$\text{Bacterial protein} \xrightarrow{\text{Cathepsins (B, D, L, S)}} \text{Peptides (13–25 aa)}$$

**Step 3 — MHC Class II synthesis and trafficking:**
$$\alpha\beta\text{-chain (ER)} + \text{Invariant Chain (Ii)} \rightarrow \alpha\beta\text{-Ii}_3 \text{ nonamer}$$

The invariant chain (Ii) performs **three jobs** simultaneously:
- **Blocks the peptide-binding groove** (preventing ER peptides from binding prematurely).
- **Targets the complex to endosomes** via a di-leucine motif in its cytoplasmic tail.
- **Provides CLIP** (Class II-associated Invariant chain Peptide) that occupies the groove during transit.

**Step 4 — Peptide loading in MIIC compartment:**
$$\alpha\beta\text{-CLIP} + \text{Exogenous peptide} \xrightarrow{\text{HLA-DM}} \alpha\beta\text{-peptide (CLIP released)}$$

HLA-DM acts as a **peptide editor** — it accelerates CLIP release and stabilizes the empty groove until a high-affinity peptide binds.

**Step 5 — Surface expression and recognition:**
$$\text{pMHC II}_{\text{MIIC}} \xrightarrow{\text{Vesicular trafficking}} \text{Plasma membrane}$$
$$\text{TCR (CD4⁺)} + \text{pMHC II} \rightarrow \text{Signal 1} \rightarrow \text{T}_H \text{ activation}$$

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Class I vs. Class II Pathways

| Feature | MHC Class I Pathway | MHC Class II Pathway |
|---|---|---|
| **Antigen origin** | Endogenous (cytosolic) | Exogenous (extracellular) |
| **Site of proteolysis** | Cytosolic proteasome | Endosomal/lysosomal proteases |
| **Transporter** | TAP (ER membrane) | Not required — vesicle fusion |
| **Loading compartment** | Endoplasmic Reticulum | MIIC (late endosome/lysosome) |
| **Chaperones involved** | Calnexin, calreticulin, tapasin, Erp57 | Invariant chain, HLA-DM |
| **Peptide length** | **8–10 aa** | **13–25 aa** |
| **T-cell partner** | CD8⁺ cytotoxic T-cell | CD4⁺ helper T-cell |
| **Co-receptor function** | CD8 binds α3 domain | CD4 binds β2 domain |
| **Cellular expression** | **All nucleated cells** + platelets | **Professional APCs**: DC, Mφ, B-cells; (induced: endothelial, epithelial) |
| **Effector outcome** | Killing of infected cell (perforin/granzyme) | Cytokine release → B-cell help, Mφ activation |
| **Cross-presentation exception** | Exogenous antigens → Class I (in DCs only) | None in classical sense |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Reasoning — T-Cell Activation Threshold

The strength of Signal 1 is governed by a biophysical parameter known as the **TCR-pMHC dwell time** (τ, in seconds).

$$\text{Signal 1 strength} \propto \tau \times n_{\text{pMHC}} \times \text{Affinity}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = dwell time of TCR on pMHC complex (typical range: **1–100 seconds**)
- $n_{\text{pMHC}}$ = number of pMHC complexes per cell (typically **>100 copies** needed for activation)
- Affinity is characterized by the equilibrium dissociation constant $K_D \approx 1\text{–}100 \ \mu M$ for TCR-pMHC interactions.

> **Engineering interpretation:** T-cell activation behaves like a **threshold comparator circuit**. Only when **Signal 1 + Signal 2 + Signal 3** all exceed their respective thresholds does the T-cell commit to clonal expansion.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — MHC Class I Antigen Presentation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Viral protein in cytosol] --> B[Ubiquitination E1-E2-E3]
    B --> C[26S Proteasome]
    C --> D[Peptide fragments 8-10 aa]
    D --> E[TAP1 TAP2 transporter ER membrane]
    E --> F[ER lumen peptide loading]
```
