```mermaid
    F --> G[MHC Class I alpha chain plus beta2m]
    G --> H[Chaperone complex calnexin calreticulin tapasin]
    H --> I[Stable pMHC Class I trimer]
    I --> J[Golgi apparatus]
    J --> K[Cell surface plasma membrane]
    K --> L[CD8 positive T-cell scan]
    L --> M{Peptide recognized as foreign?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Signal 1 delivered CTL activation]
    M -->|No| O[No response tolerance maintained]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — MHC Class II Antigen Presentation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Extracellular bacterium] --> B1[Phagocytosis by dendritic cell]
    B1 --> C1[Phagosome fuses with lysosome]
    C1 --> D1[Phagolysosome acidic environment]
    D1 --> E1[Cathepsins B D L S cleave bacterial protein]
    E1 --> F1[Peptide fragments 13-25 aa]
    F2[MHC Class II alpha plus beta chains in ER] --> G1[Invariant chain Ii binds groove]
    G1 --> H1[Nonamer alpha beta Ii complex]
    H1 --> I1[Trafficked via Golgi to MIIC endosome]
    I1 --> J1[HLA-DM removes CLIP peptide]
    J1 --> K1[Exogenous peptide loaded into groove]
    K1 --> L1[Stable pMHC Class II complex]
    L1 --> M1[Vesicle fuses with plasma membrane]
    M1 --> N1[CD4 positive T-cell scan]
    N1 --> O1{Peptide recognized as foreign?}
    O1 -->|Yes| P1[Signal 1 plus Signal 2 helper T activation]
    O1 -->|No| Q1[No response tolerance maintained]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MHC Functions in the Immune System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER Antigen Source"]
        I1[Endogenous proteins]
        I2[Exogenous pathogens]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING["PROCESSING LAYER Antigen Processing"]
        P1[Proteasome TAP ER]
        P2[Phagolysosome Cathepsins MIIC]
    end

    subgraph PRESENTATION["PRESENTATION LAYER MHC Display"]
        PR1[MHC Class I on all nucleated cells]
        PR2[MHC Class II on professional APCs]
    end

    subgraph RECOGNITION["RECOGNITION LAYER T-cell Surveillance"]
        R1[CD8 positive cytotoxic T-cells]
        R2[CD4 positive helper T-cells]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER Effector Response"]
        O1[Target cell apoptosis]
        O2[Antibody class switching M1 polarization]
    end

    I1 --> P1 --> PR1 --> R1 --> O1
    I2 --> P2 --> PR2 --> R2 --> O2
    PR1 -. Cross-presentation .-> R1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Trimolecular Interaction MHC-Peptide-TCR

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRIMOLECULAR_COMPLEX["Trimolecular Interaction Zone"]
        MHC[MHC molecule on APC surface]
        PEP[Peptide fragment in groove]
        TCR[T-cell receptor]
        CD[Co-receptor CD8 or CD4]
    end

    MHC --- PEP
    PEP --- TCR
    TCR --- CD
    MHC -. Stabilizes .-> TCR

    S1[Signal 1 TCR-pMHC]:::signal
    S2[Signal 2 B7-CD28 costimulation]:::signal
    S3[Signal 3 Cytokine polarization]:::signal

    S1 --> ACTIVATION[T-cell activation and clonal expansion]
    S2 --> ACTIVATION
    S3 --> ACTIVATION

    classDef signal fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Define MHC restriction. Who discovered this phenomenon and what was its significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MHC restriction** is the principle that a T-lymphocyte can recognize a peptide antigen **only when it is presented on the surface of an antigen-presenting cell (APC) by the SAME MHC allele** that the T-cell encountered during its development in the thymus.

- **Discovered by:** Peter C. Doherty and Rolf M. Zinkernagel (1974, Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1996).
- **Experimental basis:** They showed that virus-specific cytotoxic T-cells from infected mice would kill only virus-infected target cells sharing the same H-2 (MHC) haplotype, not those from a different haplotype — even when both were infected with the same virus.
- **Significance:** Established that TCR recognition is **dual-specific** — it requires simultaneous recognition of both the foreign peptide AND the self-MHC molecule. This laid the foundation for modern cellular immunology and vaccine design.

*(Distribution: Definition – 1 mark; Discovery – 1 mark; Significance – 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Distinguish between MHC Class I and MHC Class II molecules in terms of (i) cellular expression and (ii) T-cell partner.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | MHC Class I | MHC Class II |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Cellular expression** | Expressed on **all nucleated cells** and platelets. | Expressed **constitutively only on professional APCs** (dendritic cells, macrophages, B-cells); induced on others by IFN-γ. |
| **(ii) T-cell partner** | Recognized by **CD8⁺ cytotoxic T-lymphocytes (CTLs)**; co-receptor CD8 binds the α3 domain of MHC I. | Recognized by **CD4⁺ helper T-lymphocytes (Th)**; co-receptor CD4 binds the β2 domain of MHC II. |

*(Distribution: Each feature correctly stated with co-receptor detail – 1.5 marks each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **endogenous (MHC Class I) antigen presentation pathway**, starting from the synthesis of a viral protein to its display on the cell surface.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain **MHC polymorphism and its biological significance** in transplant rejection and population-level disease resistance.

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Antigen generation (1 mark):**
A viral protein is synthesized on cytosolic ribosomes in an infected cell. It is recognized as misfolded or "nonself" and is tagged with **ubiquitin** by E1, E2, and E3 ligases.

**Step 2 — Proteasomal degradation (1 mark):**
The ubiquitinated protein is delivered to the **26S proteasome**, a multi-catalytic protease complex located in the cytosol. The proteasome cleaves the protein into short peptides of **8–10 amino acids** with hydrophobic C-terminal residues preferred.

**Step 3 — Transport into ER via TAP (1 mark):**
Cytosolic peptides are translocated into the lumen of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) by the **TAP transporter** (TAP1/TAP2), an ATP-binding cassette (ABC) family transporter. Peptides with N-terminal basic residues or hydrophobic C-termini are preferred substrates.

**Step 4 — MHC Class I assembly and peptide loading (1.5 marks):**
The newly synthesized **MHC Class I α-chain** (with α1, α2, α3 domains) is co-translationally inserted into the ER membrane. It associates with **β2-microglobulin** (a non-covalently bound invariant chain encoded on chromosome 15). The partially assembled molecule is stabilized by chaperones **calnexin, calreticulin, and Erp57**, and bridged to TAP by **tapasin**. Peptide loading then occurs — the empty Class I molecule is unstable and retained in the ER until a suitable peptide binds.

**Step 5 — Surface expression (1 mark):**
Once a stable peptide–MHC I trimer (α-chain + β2m + peptide) is formed, it exits the ER through the **Golgi apparatus** and is delivered to the **plasma membrane** via constitutive exocytosis, where it is available for inspection by CD8⁺ T-cells.

**Step 6 — T-cell recognition (1.5 marks):**
CD8⁺ cytotoxic T-lymphocytes scan the surface. If the TCR recognizes the peptide–MHC I complex with sufficient affinity AND receives co-stimulatory signals (B7–CD28), the CTL becomes activated, proliferates, and can kill the infected cell via **perforin/granzyme release** or **Fas–FasL interactions**.

> **Schematic representation requirement (draw a labelled diagram of the pathway for full marks).**

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
**MHC polymorphism** refers to the presence of multiple alleles at each MHC locus within a population, with each allele encoding a protein with slightly different amino acid sequences, particularly in the peptide-binding groove.

**Molecular basis (2 marks):**
- The HLA complex on chromosome 6p21.3 is the **most polymorphic gene cluster in the human genome**, with hundreds of alleles identified for HLA-A, -B, -C, -DR, -DQ, and -DP loci.
- Polymorphic residues are concentrated in the **α1 and α2 domains** of Class I (and α1, β1 domains of Class II), precisely the regions that form the peptide-binding groove.
- This polymorphism is generated and maintained by **gene conversion, point mutation, and balancing selection** driven by diverse pathogen pressures over evolutionary time.

**Significance in transplant rejection (2 marks):**
- Because TCRs are MHC-restricted to self, they can also **directly recognize foreign MHC molecules** (a phenomenon called **direct alloreactivity**).
- Approximately **1–10% of a recipient's T-cell repertoire** can directly recognize donor MHC.
- This causes **graft rejection** unless donor and recipient HLA alleles are matched as closely as possible. Clinical transplantation uses **HLA-A, -B, and -DR typing** for donor selection.

**Significance in disease resistance (2 marks):**
- Heterozygous individuals expressing multiple MHC alleles can present a broader range of pathogen-derived peptides, conferring **heterozygote advantage**.
- Specific HLA alleles are associated with resistance or susceptibility to diseases — e.g., **HLA-B57** protects against HIV progression, **HLA-B27** strongly associates with ankylosing spondylitis, **HLA-DR3/DR4** predispose to Type 1 diabetes.
- This explains why some populations show differential susceptibility to infectious and autoimmune diseases.

---

### Question B [14 Marks, Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, describe the **exogenous (MHC Class II) antigen presentation pathway**. Mention the role of the **invariant chain** and **HLA-DM**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a detailed note on the **structure of MHC Class I molecule** and explain how it presents peptide antigens to CD8⁺ T-cells.

---

### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Antigen uptake (1 mark):**
Professional APCs (dendritic cells, macrophages, B-cells) internalize extracellular pathogens or antigens via **phagocytosis, receptor-mediated endocytosis, or pinocytosis**, generating vesicles that fuse with lysosomes to form **phagolysosomes**.

**Step 2 — Antigen processing (1 mark):**
Within the acidic phagolysosomal environment (pH ~4.5–5.0), lysosomal proteases — particularly **cathepsins B, D, L, and S** — degrade the engulfed proteins into peptides of **13–25 amino acids**.

**Step 3 — MHC Class II biosynthesis and invariant chain function (2 marks):**
MHC Class II α and β chains are synthesized in the ER and assemble into a heterodimer. The **invariant chain (Ii, CD74)** binds the peptide-binding groove of the αβ dimer in a way that:
- **Physically blocks** the groove to prevent binding of endogenous ER peptides.
- Provides a **targeting signal** (di-leucine motif in the cytoplasmic tail) that directs the αβ-Ii complex from the Golgi to the **MIIC** (MHC Class II compartment), a late endosomal/lysosomal organelle.
- A portion of Ii, called **CLIP** (Class II-associated Invariant chain Peptide), remains lodged in the groove during transit.

**Step 4 — HLA-DM-mediated peptide exchange (1.5 marks):**
In the MIIC compartment, the **HLA-DM molecule** (a non-classical MHC Class II protein) catalyzes the **release of CLIP** from the groove and stabilizes the empty MHC Class II molecule, enabling high-affinity exogenous peptides to bind. HLA-DM also acts as a **peptide editor**, removing weakly bound peptides to ensure only stable complexes reach the surface.

**Step 5 — Surface display and T-cell recognition (1.5 marks):**
The peptide–MHC II complex is transported to the plasma membrane, where it is recognized by **CD4⁺ helper T-cells**. The CD4 co-receptor binds the β2 domain, enhancing TCR sensitivity. Upon recognition of a foreign peptide, the T-helper cell becomes activated and secretes cytokines (IL-2, IFN-γ, IL-4) to orchestrate the adaptive immune response.

> **Note:** Draw a labelled diagram showing: Extracellular antigen → Phagocytosis → Phagolysosome → MIIC → Invariant chain pathway → HLA-DM action → Cell surface → CD4⁺ T-cell contact. (1 mark reserved for diagram.)

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Domain architecture (3 marks):**
An MHC Class I molecule consists of:
- A **heavy α-chain (~45 kDa)** encoded by HLA-A, HLA-B, or HLA-C genes on chromosome 6p21.3. It contains three extracellular domains — **α1, α2, and α3** — a transmembrane region, and a short cytoplasmic tail.
- A non-covalently associated **β2-microglobulin (~12 kDa)** encoded on chromosome 15, which is essential for surface expression and stability.
- The **α1 and α2 domains** fold together to form the **peptide-binding groove**, consisting of a β-pleated sheet floor flanked by two α-helical walls.
- The **α3 domain** is an immunoglobulin-like fold that binds the **CD8 co-receptor** of cytotoxic T-cells.

**Peptide-binding features (2 marks):**
- The groove accommodates peptides of **8–10 amino acids** in an extended conformation.
- **Anchor residues** at the peptide's N- and C-termini bind into specific pockets (A–F) within the groove; the middle of the peptide arches upward and is exposed for TCR contact.
- Class I molecules preferentially bind peptides with **hydrophobic C-terminal anchor residues** (because TAP translocator selects such peptides).

**Presentation to CD8⁺ T-cells (2 marks):**
- The peptide–MHC I complex is displayed on the cell surface as a **trimolecular interface**: TCR contacts both the exposed peptide residues AND the α-helices of the MHC groove.
- **CD8 co-receptor** binds the conserved α3 domain, recruiting **Lck tyrosine kinase** to the TCR–CD3 complex and amplifying Signal 1.
- A successful encounter (foreign peptide + co-stimulation) triggers CTL activation, clonal expansion, and target cell killing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Confusing the Class I and Class II pathways** is the single most common cause of mark loss. Always write the **cellular location** explicitly: proteasome/cytosol for Class I; endosome/MIIC for Class II.
> 2. **Forgetting the invariant chain and HLA-DM** in Class II answers costs 2–3 marks. Both are non-negotiable components of any complete KTU model answer.
> 3. **MHC restriction ≠ MHC polymorphism.** These are distinct concepts. Restriction = TCR sees peptide only on self-MHC (Zinkernagel-Doherty). Polymorphism = multiple alleles per locus in a population. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Do NOT write that MHC Class I "presents extracellular antigens"** — this is false in classical presentation. The exception is **cross-presentation** in dendritic cells, which you may mention as an advanced point only.
> 5. **Always mention co-stimulation (Signal 2)** in any answer about T-cell activation. Writing only Signal 1 is incomplete and will lose at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for MHC: Function**

- ☐ **MHC = Major Histocompatibility Complex** = HLA in humans, located on **chromosome 6p21.3**.
- ☐ **Three core functions:** Antigen presentation, self/non-self discrimination, initiation of adaptive immunity.
- ☐ **Class I** → endogenous antigens → **CD8⁺ CTLs** → all nucleated cells → peptides **8–10 aa** → pathway uses **proteasome + TAP + ER chaperones (calnexin, calreticulin, tapasin, Erp57)**.
- ☐ **Class II** → exogenous antigens → **CD4⁺ helper T-cells** → professional APCs only → peptides **13–25 aa** → pathway uses **phagolysosome + cathepsins + invariant chain + HLA-DM in MIIC**.
- ☐ **MHC restriction** = TCR dual-recognition of self-MHC + foreign peptide; discovered by **Zinkernagel & Doherty (1974)**.
- ☐ **MHC polymorphism** = multiple alleles per locus; concentrated in peptide-binding groove residues.
- ☐ **Cross-presentation** = dendritic cells load exogenous antigens onto Class I to prime CD8⁺ responses (important for tumors, intracellular pathogens).
- ☐ **Three signals for T-cell activation:** (1) TCR–pMHC, (2) B7–CD28 co-stimulation, (3) cytokine polarization signal.
- ☐ **TAP** = ATP-dependent peptide transporter for Class I loading; deficiency causes bare lymphocyte syndrome type I.
- ☐ **Invariant chain** blocks Class II groove in ER; **HLA-DM** is the peptide editor in endosomes.
- ☐ **Superantigens** (e.g., TSST-1, SPE-A) bypass the peptide groove and cause **mass polyclonal T-cell activation** — associated with toxic shock syndrome.
- ☐ **Direct alloreactivity** in transplants is driven by ~1–10% of recipient T-cells reacting against foreign MHC.
- ☐ **Numerical anchors:** Class I peptide ≈ **8–10 aa**; Class II peptide ≈ **13–25 aa**; activation threshold ≈ **>100 pMHC complexes per cell**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Complement Pathways - Types and Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Complement Pathways - Types and Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **complement system** is a tightly regulated network of **~30 soluble plasma proteins** and cell-surface receptors, produced mainly by the **liver (hepatocytes)** and circulating in inactive **zymogen (proenzyme)** form. Upon activation via one of three distinct enzymatic cascades, complement proteins cleave and assemble into effector molecules that mediate **innate immune defense**, **antibody-dependent clearance**, and **inflammatory signalling**.

The three activation pathways — **Classical, Lectin (MBL), and Alternative** — all converge on the central cleavage of **C3** into **C3a** and **C3b**, ultimately producing a pore-forming **Membrane Attack Complex (MAC; C5b–C9)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to *describe the three complement activation pathways*, *distinguish their initiators*, and *enumerate the biological effector functions* of the resulting cleavage fragments. The convergence at C3 is the most frequently tested concept.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Security Alarm" Model

Imagine a shopping mall with three different alarm triggers:

- **Glass-break sensor** (Classical pathway) → triggered when a *specific alarm pad* (antibody:IgG/IgM bound to antigen) is pressed.
- **Motion sensor** (Lectin pathway) → triggered when *security guards* (Mannose-Binding Lectin / MBL) spot a *suspicious pattern* (mannose-rich microbial surfaces).
- **Pressure plate** (Alternative pathway) → triggered *spontaneously* when a *foreign object* (microbe lacking protective regulators like DAF) steps on it.

All three sensors (pathways) sound the **same central bell (C3)** and eventually deploy the same **security response team (C5–C9 = MAC, plus C3a/C5a as crowd-control alarms)**. The "circuit breakers" (regulators like **C1-INH, Factor H, DAF, CD59**) prevent the alarm from ringing inside the mall's own offices (host cells).

### 1.3 Key Physical & Numerical Parameters

- **Total complement proteins:** ~30 (numbered C1–C9, plus factors B, D, H, I, P, MBL, MASP-1/2).
- **Normal plasma C3 concentration:** **0.9–1.8 g/L** (the most abundant and clinically measured complement component).
- **Normal plasma C4 concentration:** **0.15–0.45 g/L**.
- **Molecular mass of C3:** **~185 kDa**.
- **MAC pore diameter:** **~10 nm**, allowing osmotic influx → cell lysis.
- **Number of C9 molecules polymerized per MAC:** **10–18 copies** (single ring polymer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters clinically:** A deficiency in C1-INH causes **hereditary angioedema**, while a deficiency in the terminal components (C5–C9) causes recurrent **Neisseria infections** — both are KTU-favorite short-answer associations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Activation Cascades — Structured Logic

#### **A. Classical Pathway (Antibody-Dependent)**

**Trigger:** Antigen–antibody complex; minimum **two Fc regions of IgG (CH2 domain)** OR **one IgM pentamer** must be clustered to bind **C1q**.

1. **C1q** binds Fc → activates **C1r** → activates **C1s** (a serine protease).
2. **C1s** cleaves **C4 → C4a + C4b**; C4b binds covalently to the surface.
3. **C1s** cleaves **C2 → C2a + C2b**; **C2a** joins **C4b** → forms **C4b2a = Classical C3 Convertase**.
4. C3 convertase cleaves **C3 → C3a + C3b** (amplification step).
5. Additional C3b binds the convertase → **C4b2a3b = Classical C5 Convertase**.
6. C5 convertase cleaves **C5 → C5a + C5b** → initiates MAC assembly (C5b + C6 + C7 + C8 + C9ₙ).

#### **B. Lectin (MBL) Pathway (Antibody-Independent)**

**Trigger:** Microbial surfaces rich in **terminal mannose, fucose, or N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc)** residues (mammalian cells lack these patterns on healthy surfaces).

1. **MBL (or Ficolins)** binds microbial sugars → activates associated **MASP-1 and MASP-2** (MBL-Associated Serine Proteases).
2. **MASP-2** cleaves **C4 → C4a + C4b** and **C2 → C2a + C2b** (MASP-1 assists by activating MASP-2).
3. Formation proceeds identically to the Classical pathway from step 3 onwards: **C4b2a (C3 convertase) → C4b2a3b (C5 convertase) → MAC**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** The Lectin pathway is essentially the **Classical pathway with a different ignition key (MBL/MASP instead of C1q/C1r/C1s)**. Both produce the *same* C3/C5 convertases: **C4b2a** and **C4b2a3b**.

#### **C. Alternative Pathway (Spontaneous / Antibody-Independent)**

**Trigger:** Spontaneous low-rate hydrolysis of C3 in plasma (**"C3 tickover"** — C3 → **C3(H₂O)**), OR direct recognition of foreign surfaces lacking host regulators.

1. **C3(H₂O)** binds **Factor B** → **Factor D** cleaves B → **C3(H₂O)Bb = Fluid-phase C3 convertase**.
2. C3b deposited on a foreign surface binds **Factor B**; **Factor D** cleaves B → **C3bBb = Surface-bound C3 convertase**, stabilized by **Properdin (Factor P)**.
3. C3bBb cleaves more C3 → **massive amplification loop** (a single C3b can generate thousands more C3b).
4. Additional C3b joins → **C3bBb3b = Alternative C5 Convertase** → C5 → C5a + C5b → MAC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Host Protection — "Self vs. Non-Self" Discrimination:** Host cells express **DAF (CD55)** which displaces Bb, and **MCP (CD46)** plus **Factor I** which inactivates C3b → **iC3b**. Pathogens lack these regulators, so C3bBb persists on their surfaces.

### 2.2 Convergence Point and Terminal Pathway

All three pathways converge at:
- **C3 cleavage** (C3 → C3a + C3b)
- **C5 cleavage** (C5 → C5a + C5b)
- **MAC assembly** (C5b-C6-C7-C8-C9ₙ)

$$\text{C5b} + \text{C6} \rightarrow \text{C5b-6} \xrightarrow{+C7} \text{C5b-7} \xrightarrow{+C8} \text{C5b-8} \xrightarrow{+C9_n} \text{MAC (C5b-9)}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Quick-Reference Table)

| Feature | Classical | Lectin (MBL) | Alternative |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initiator molecule** | C1q (binds Fc of IgG/IgM) | MBL / Ficolins (bind mannose) | Spontaneous C3 hydrolysis |
| **Activating protease** | C1r, C1s | MASP-1, MASP-2 | Factor D |
| **Antibody required?** | Yes (IgG/IgM) | No | No |
| **C3 Convertase** | **C4b2a** | **C4b2a** | **C3bBb** (stabilized by Properdin) |
| **C5 Convertase** | **C4b2a3b** | **C4b2a3b** | **C3bBb3b** |
| **Mg²⁺ / Ca²⁺ requirement** | Ca²⁺ (for C1q); Mg²⁺ (for convertase) | Ca²⁺ (for MBL) | Mg²⁺ (for Factor B binding) |
| **% of total complement activation** (typical) | ~10% (adaptive) | ~10% (innate) | ~80% (innate amplification) |

### 2.4 Key Effector Functions of Complement Fragments

| Fragment | Function | Type |
|---|---|---|
| **C3a, C4a, C5a** | Anaphylatoxins — mast cell degranulation, vasodilation, smooth muscle contraction (C5a is most potent) | Inflammatory |
| **C3b / iC3b** | Opsonization — marks pathogens for phagocytosis via **CR1 (CD35)** and **CR3 (CD11b/CD18)** | Clearance |
| **C3b (clusters)** | Immune adherence → clearance of immune complexes by RBCs (via CR1) | Homeostatic |
| **C5b–C9 (MAC)** | Forms transmembrane pore → osmotic lysis (esp. *Neisseria*) | Cytolytic |
| **C3d (from iC3b)** | Binds CR2 (CD21) on B cells → **enhances B-cell activation / lowers activation threshold** | Adaptive bridge |
| **C5a** | Powerful **chemotactic factor** for neutrophils | Inflammatory |
| **C1q** | Direct opsonin + apoptotic cell clearance | Clearance |

### 2.5 Soluble and Membrane-Bound Regulators (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Regulator | Target | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **C1-INH** | C1r, C1s, MASP-1/2 | Serine protease inhibitor (suicide substrate) |
| **Factor I** (+ cofactors) | C3b, C4b | Proteolytic cleavage to iC3b / iC4b |
| **Factor H** | C3b | Cofactor for Factor I; competes with Factor B |
| **DAF (CD55)** | C3bBb, C4b2a | Dissociates convertases on host cells |
| **MCP (CD46)** | C3b, C4b | Cofactor for Factor I on host cells |
| **CD59 (Protectin)** | C8, C9 | Prevents MAC formation on host cells |
| **Properdin (Factor P)** | C3bBb | **Stabilizes** alternative C3 convertase (the only positive regulator) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering/Biomedical Utility

- **CH50 / AH50 assays:** Measure functional classical / alternative pathway activity in patient serum — critical for diagnosing complement deficiencies.
- **Eculizumab:** A monoclonal antibody against **C5** that prevents C5a/C5b generation; used clinically in **Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH)** and **Atypical Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (aHUS)**.
- **Recombinant C1-INH (C1 esterase inhibitor):** Therapeutic for hereditary angioedema.
- **Vaccine adjuvant research:** Engineered complement-targeting nanoparticles amplify C3d–CR2 signaling for stronger humoral responses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Proteolytic Cascades & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Proteolytic Cascade — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis

We will explicitly trace each cleavage, naming the protease, the substrate, the products, and the cumulative effect. This is the **core analytical matrix** KTU examiners expect students to reproduce verbatim in long-answer questions.

---

#### **Step Block 1 — Initiation of the Classical Pathway**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \quad & \text{Ag--Ab (IgG/IgM)} + \text{C1q} \rightarrow [\text{Ag--Ab--C1q}] \\
& \text{C1q is a hexameric bouquet; binding requires } \geq 2 \text{ Fc regions} \\
\text{(ii)} \quad & [\text{C1q}] \rightarrow \text{activates C1r (auto-cleavage)} \rightarrow \text{C1r}^*\ (\text{active serine protease}) \\
\text{(iii)} \quad & \text{C1r}^* \rightarrow \text{cleaves C1s} \rightarrow \text{C1s}^*\ (\text{active serine protease}) \\
\text{(iv)} \quad & \text{C1s}^* \text{ cleaves C4} \rightarrow \text{C4a (small, fluid-phase)} + \text{C4b (large, surface-anchored via thioester)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** C1q is the recognition subunit; C1r and C1s are the catalytic subunits of the **C1 complex (C1qr²s²)**. The internal thioester bond in C4 (and C3) is the chemical "anchor" that allows C4b to **covalently attach to nearby –OH or –NH₂ groups** on the pathogen surface — a critical concept for KTU.

---

#### **Step Block 2 — Formation of the Classical C3 Convertase**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(v)} \quad & \text{C1s}^* \text{ cleaves C2} \rightarrow \text{C2a (catalytic)} + \text{C2b (small)} \\
\text{(vi)} \quad & \text{C4b (surface)} + \text{C2a (catalytic)} \rightarrow \text{C4b2a = Classical C3 Convertase} \\
& \text{C4b = regulatory/substrate-binding subunit} \\
& \text{C2a = catalytic serine protease subunit}
\end{aligned}$$

**Stoichiometric note:** A single **C1s** can cleave **>100 C4 molecules** before being inactivated — a built-in amplification mechanism.

---

#### **Step Block 3 — C3 Cleavage and the Amplification Bottleneck**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(vii)} \quad & \text{C4b2a} + \text{C3} \rightarrow \text{C3a (anaphylatoxin)} + \text{C3b (opsonin)} \\
& \text{This step is the convergence point of all three pathways.} \\
\text{(viii)} \quad & \text{C3b amplification:} \\
& \text{One C3 convertase generates } \sim 200 \text{ C3b molecules} \\
& \text{Each C3b can join the convertase to form a C5 convertase, OR opsonize, OR feed the alternative pathway amplification loop.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Quantitative amplification logic:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Amplification factor} & = \frac{\text{Number of C3b produced}}{\text{Number of C3 convertase molecules}} \\
& \approx \frac{200}{1} = 200\text{-fold per convertase complex}
\end{aligned}$$

This is why the alternative pathway, once initiated on a foreign surface, rapidly dominates total complement flux.

---

#### **Step Block 4 — C5 Convertase Formation and Terminal Pathway**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(ix)} \quad & \text{C4b2a} + \text{C3b} \rightarrow \text{C4b2a3b = Classical C5 Convertase} \\
\text{(x)} \quad & \text{C4b2a3b} + \text{C5} \rightarrow \text{C3a} + \text{C5a (anaphylatoxin)} + \text{C5b} \\
\text{(xi)} \quad & \text{C5b} + \text{C6} \rightarrow \text{C5b6 (hydrophilic, fluid-phase)} \\
\text{(xii)} \quad & \text{C5b6} + \text{C7} \rightarrow \text{C5b67 (amphipathic, exposes hydrophobic regions)} \\
\text{(xiii)} \quad & \text{C5b67} + \text{C8} \rightarrow \text{C5b678 (inserts into lipid bilayer, partial pore)} \\
\text{(xiv)} \quad & \text{C5b678} + \text{C9}_{n=10\text{--}18} \rightarrow \text{C5b--C9}_{n} = \text{MAC (full transmembrane pore)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering analog:** The MAC behaves like a **self-assembling molecular syringe barrel** — C5b-7 is the membrane-anchoring base, C8 initiates the pore, and C9 polymerizes to form the β-barrel lumen of ~10 nm diameter.

---

#### **Step Block 5 — Alternative Pathway "C3 Tickover" Mechanism**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(xv)} \quad & \text{C3} \xrightarrow{\text{spontaneous hydrolysis}} \text{C3(H}_2\text{O)} \\
\text{(xvi)} \quad & \text{C3(H}_2\text{O)} + \text{Factor B} \rightarrow \text{C3(H}_2\text{O)B} \\
\text{(xvii)} \quad & \text{C3(H}_2\text{O)B} \xrightarrow{\text{Factor D}} \text{C3(H}_2\text{O)Bb + Ba} \\
\text{(xviii)} \quad & \text{C3(H}_2\text{O)Bb} + \text{C3} \rightarrow \text{C3a} + \text{C3b} \\
\text{(xix)} \quad & \text{C3b (on foreign surface)} + \text{Factor B} \xrightarrow{\text{Factor D}} \text{C3bBb} \\
\text{(xx)} \quad & \text{C3bBb} + \text{Properdin (P)} \rightarrow \text{C3bBbP (stabilized)} \\
\text{(xxi)} \quad & \text{C3bBbP} + \text{C3b} \rightarrow \text{C3bBb3b = Alternative C5 Convertase}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic for self vs. non-self:** On **host cells**, Factor H outcompetes Factor B for C3b binding, and DAF accelerates Bb dissociation → no amplification. On **microbial cells**, Factor H binds poorly (lacks sialic acid/polyanions), so Factor B wins → amplification proceeds.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Fragment-By-Fragment Functional Map

| Step | Protease / Trigger | Substrate | Fragment A (small/fluid) | Fragment B (large/surface) | KTU Function Tested |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | C1s (Classical) | C4 | **C4a** (anaphylatoxin, weak) | **C4b** (convertase subunit) | ✓ |
| 2 | C1s (Classical) | C2 | **C2b** | **C2a** (catalytic subunit) | ✓ |
| 3 | C4b2a | C3 | **C3a** (anaphylatoxin) | **C3b** (opsonin + convertase) | ✓✓✓ |
| 4 | C3b + Factor I | C3b | — | **iC3b → C3d** (CR2 ligand) | ✓ |
| 5 | C4b2a3b / C3bBb3b | C5 | **C5a** (most potent anaphylatoxin, chemotaxin) | **C5b** (MAC nucleation) | ✓✓✓ |
| 6 | C5b + C6+C7+C8+C9 | — | — | **C5b–C9 = MAC** | ✓✓✓ |
| 7 | Spontaneous / Factor D | C3 / Factor B | **C3a / Ba** | **C3(H₂O)Bb / C3bBb** | ✓✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** The "small fragment = fluid phase, large fragment = surface-bound" rule is *almost* always true (except C2, where C2a is the catalytic, large subunit and C2b is small). KTU examiners love testing this exception.

### 3.3 Convergence Logic — Why C3 is the "Master Switch"

The fact that all three pathways converge on **C3 cleavage** is a beautiful example of **convergent biological engineering**:

$$\text{Classical C3 convertase} = \text{C4b2a}$$
$$\text{Lectin C3 convertase} = \text{C4b2a}$$
$$\text{Alternative C3 convertase} = \text{C3bBb}$$

The body has evolved **two structurally distinct C3 convertases** to provide redundancy: if an organism evolves resistance to the classical pathway (e.g., IgM/IgG-independent evasion), the lectin and alternative pathways still operate, and vice versa. The downstream effector functions (C3a, C3b, C5a, C5b–9) are **identical regardless of trigger**, ensuring the immune response is unified.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Convergence of the Three Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["ANTIGEN-ANTIBODY COMPLEX\n(IgG or IgM bound to pathogen)"] --> B1["C1q recognition\n(binds Fc region)"]
    A2["MICROBIAL SURFACE\nwith mannose / fucose / GlcNAc"] --> B2["MBL or Ficolin recognition\n(lectin domain)"]
    A3["FOREIGN SURFACE\nlacks host regulators\n+ spontaneous C3 tickover"] --> B3["C3 hydrolysis to C3-H2O\n+ Factor B + Factor D"]

    B1 --> C1["C1r and C1s activation\n(serine proteases)"]
    B2 --> C2["MASP-1 and MASP-2 activation\n(serine proteases)"]
    B3 --> C3["C3bBb formation\n+ Properdin stabilization"]

    C1 --> D1["C4 cleaved to C4a + C4b"]
    C2 --> D2["C4 cleaved to C4a + C4b"]
    C3 --> D3["C3b deposited on surface\n(amplification loop)"]

    D1 --> E1["C2 cleaved to C2a + C2b"]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E3["C3bBb = Alternative C3 convertase"]

    E1 --> F1["C4b2a formed\n= CLASSICAL and LECTIN C3 convertase"]
    E3 --> F3["Amplification of C3b pool"]

    F1 --> G["CONVERGENCE NODE\nC3 cleaved to C3a + C3b"]
    F3 --> G

    G --> H1["C3a = anaphylatoxin\nmast cell degranulation"]
    G --> H2["C3b = opsonin\nbinds CR1 on phagocytes"]
    G --> H3["Additional C3b binds convertase\nto form C5 convertase"]

    H3 --> I["C5 cleaved to C5a + C5b"]
    I --> J1["C5a = potent chemotactic factor\nfor neutrophils"]
    I --> J2["C5b + C6 + C7 + C8 + C9n\n= MEMBRANE ATTACK COMPLEX MAC"]
    I --> J3["C5b67 + C8 + C9\nforms transmembrane pore\n~10 nm diameter"]

    H2 --> K["OPSONIZATION AND PHAGOCYTOSIS\nvia CR1, CR3, CR4 receptors"]

    style A1 fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style A2 fill:#e5f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style A3 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#009933
    style F1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style E3 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style G fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#ff6600,stroke-width:3px
    style J2 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style J3 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 MAC Assembly Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["C5b\n(initiator, exposes binding site)"] --> S2["C5b-6 complex\n(fluid phase, hydrophilic)"]
    S2 --> S3["C5b-7 complex\n(amphipathic, hydrophobic\ndomain exposed)"]
    S3 --> S4["C5b-8 complex\n(partial membrane insertion,\nforms small lesion)"]
    S4 --> S5["C5b-9n = MAC\n(10-18 C9 molecules polymerize\nto form transmembrane pore)"]

    S5 --> OUT["OSMOTIC LYSIS\nNa+ and H2O influx\nCell death"]

    style S1 fill:#ffcccc
    style S5 fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Complement Regulation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["C3bBb or C4b2a\nC3 CONVERTASE"] --> R1["Factor H competes with Factor B\n(alternative pathway)"]
    P --> R2["DAF CD55\ndisplaces Bb from C3b"]
    P --> R3["MCP CD46\ncofactor for Factor I"]
    P --> R4["C1-INH\ninactivates C1r, C1s, MASP"]

    R3 --> R5["Factor I cleaves C3b to iC3b\nFactor I cleaves C4b to iC4b"]

    M["C5b67 inserting into membrane"] --> M1["CD59 Protectin\nblocks C8 and C9 recruitment\non HOST cells"]

    P --> POS["Properdin Factor P\nSTABILIZES C3bBb\n(only positive regulator)"]
    POS -.->|amplifies| P

    style POS fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600
    style R1 fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099
    style R2 fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099
    style R3 fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099
    style R4 fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099
    style M1 fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099
```

### 4.4 Effector Functions Overview — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C3C["C3 CENTRAL NODE\nC3a, C3b, iC3b, C3d"] --> F1["INFLAMMATION\nC3a, C5a act as anaphylatoxins\nvasodilation, mast cell degranulation"]
    C3C --> F2["OPSONIZATION\nC3b and iC3b bind CR1 CR3 CR4\nenhanced phagocytosis"]
    C3C --> F3["IMMUNE CLEARANCE\nC3b-mediated immune complex\nsolubilization via RBC CR1"]
    C3C --> F4["ADAPTIVE BRIDGE\nC3d binds CR2 CD21 on B cells\nlowers B cell activation threshold"]

    C5C["C5 CENTRAL NODE\nC5a, C5b"] --> F5["CHEMOTAXIS\nC5a recruits neutrophils\nvia C5aR receptor"]
    C5C --> F6["CYTOLYSIS\nC5b-C9 MAC forms pore\n10 nm diameter osmotic lysis"]

    style C3C fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:3px
    style C5C fill:#ff9933,stroke:#cc3300,stroke-width:3px
    style F1 fill:#ffe5e5
    style F2 fill:#e5f5ff
    style F3 fill:#e5ffe5
    style F4 fill:#fff4cc
    style F5 fill:#ffe5e5
    style F6 fill:#ffcccc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*List the three complement activation pathways and state the specific recognition molecule that initiates each pathway.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Pathway | Recognition Molecule |
|---|---|
| Classical pathway | **C1q** — binds Fc region of IgG (CH2 domain) or pentameric IgM in immune complexes |
| Lectin (MBL) pathway | **Mannose-Binding Lectin (MBL)** or **Ficolins** — bind terminal mannose, fucose, or GlcNAc on microbial surfaces |
| Alternative pathway | **Spontaneous hydrolysis of C3** (C3 tickover) producing C3(H₂O), with subsequent amplification on foreign surfaces |

*All three pathways converge on the cleavage of C3 into C3a and C3b.* (1 mark for the table + 2 marks for accurate recognition molecules)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is an anaphylatoxin? Name the three complement fragments that function as anaphylatoxins and rank them by potency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Anaphylatoxins are small (~9 kDa) complement cleavage fragments released into the fluid phase that trigger **mast cell and basophil degranulation**, smooth muscle contraction, and increased vascular permeability. The three anaphylatoxins, ranked by potency, are:

1. **C5a** (most potent, also chemotactic for neutrophils) — ~11 kDa
2. **C3a** (intermediate potency) — ~9 kDa
3. **C4a** (least potent) — ~9 kDa

All three are inactivated by **carboxypeptidase N** (and B), which cleaves their C-terminal arginine residue to form **C5a-desArg, C3a-desArg, C4a-desArg**, abrogating their activity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 2 Model]**

**(a) Describe in detail the Classical Pathway of complement activation. Add a note on the C1 complex structure.** (7 marks)

**(b) Compare the three complement activation pathways in a comparative table and explain how the alternative pathway amplifies the cascade. Why are host cells protected from complement-mediated damage?** (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part A (a):**

**Structure of the C1 Complex (1 mark):**
C1 is a **calcium-dependent** pentameric complex composed of:
- **One C1q molecule** — a hexameric "bouquet" of 6 globular heads connected by collagen-like stalks (made of 18 polypeptide chains: 6 A, 6 B, 6 C).
- **Two C1r molecules** — serine proenzymes.
- **Two C1s molecules** — serine proenzymes.
- C1q is a recognition subunit; C1r and C1s are catalytic subunits.

**Step-by-step activation (6 marks):**

1. **Trigger (1 mark):** When **two or more Fc regions of IgG** (or **one pentameric IgM**) cluster on a pathogen surface, **C1q** binds the Fc regions via its globular heads. The binding of multiple heads generates a conformational change.

2. **C1r autoactivation (1 mark):** This conformational change induces **C1r** to auto-cleave and become **C1r\*** (active serine protease).

3. **C1s activation (1 mark):** **C1r\*** cleaves **C1s** to **C1s\*** (active serine protease).

4. **C4 cleavage (1 mark):** **C1s\*** cleaves plasma **C4** → **C4a** (small, fluid-phase, weak anaphylatoxin) + **C4b** (large, surface-anchored via its internal thioester bond reacting with –OH or –NH₂ groups on the pathogen).

5. **C2 cleavage and C3 convertase formation (1 mark):** **C1s\*** also cleaves **C2** → **C2a** (catalytic subunit) + **C2b**. **C2a** binds surface-anchored **C4b** → **C4b2a = Classical C3 Convertase**.

6. **C3 cleavage (1 mark):** C4b2a cleaves **C3** → **C3a** (anaphylatoxin) + **C3b** (opsonin). C3b can either opsonize the pathogen or join C4b2a to form **C4b2a3b = C5 Convertase**, which initiates the terminal pathway and MAC formation.

---

**Model Solution — Part A (b):**

**Comparative table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Classical | Lectin | Alternative |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initiator | C1q | MBL / Ficolins | C3 tickover |
| Activating protease | C1r, C1s | MASP-1, MASP-2 | Factor D |
| Antibody required | Yes (IgG/IgM) | No | No |
| C3 Convertase | C4b2a | C4b2a | C3bBb |
| C5 Convertase | C4b2a3b | C4b2a3b | C3bBb3b |
| Evolutionary origin | Adaptive | Innate | Innate |

**Alternative pathway amplification (2 marks):**
The alternative pathway generates a **positive feedback amplification loop**:
- C3bBb (stabilized by **Properdin / Factor P**) cleaves additional C3 → more C3b.
- Each new C3b can form additional C3bBb complexes, exponentially amplifying the signal.
- A single initial C3b can generate **thousands of C3b molecules within minutes**, making the alternative pathway the dominant source of total complement activation in vivo (~80% of total complement flux).
- C3b deposited on the pathogen also feeds the **terminal pathway** by forming C3bBb3b (C5 convertase).

**Host cell protection (1 mark):**
Host cells express membrane-bound regulators on their surfaces:
- **DAF (CD55)** — accelerates dissociation of Bb from C3b, dismantling the convertase.
- **MCP (CD46)** — cofactor for **Factor I**-mediated cleavage of C3b → **iC3b** (inactive).
- **CD59 (Protectin)** — prevents C8 and C9 from polymerizing, blocking MAC formation.

Additionally, soluble **Factor H** competes with Factor B for C3b binding on host surfaces (which are rich in sialic acid that enhances Factor H affinity). Pathogens lack sialic acid, so Factor B wins, and amplification proceeds.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Explain the Lectin (MBL) pathway of complement activation. How does it differ from the Classical pathway? (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the structure and formation of the Membrane Attack Complex (MAC). What biological consequences does MAC formation produce, and which pathogens are most susceptible? (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part B (a):**

**Lectin (MBL) Pathway (5 marks):**

1. **Recognition (1 mark):** **MBL (Mannose-Binding Lectin)**, a large oligomeric plasma protein synthesized by the liver, circulates in complex with **MASP-1 (MBL-Associated Serine Protease 1)** and **MASP-2**. MBL binds via its carbohydrate recognition domains (CRDs) to **terminal mannose, fucose, or N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc)** residues that are abundantly present on microbial surfaces but rare on mammalian cells (which terminate their glycans in sialic acid or galactose).

2. **MASP activation (1 mark):** MBL binding to a microbial surface induces a conformational change that activates **MASP-1** (which auto-cleaves) and then **MASP-1** activates **MASP-2** (or MASP-2 auto-activates).

3. **C4 and C2 cleavage (2 marks):** **MASP-2** cleaves **C4** → **C4a + C4b**, and cleaves **C2** → **C2a + C2b**. These are the same substrates as in the classical pathway. **MASP-1** also contributes by cleaving C2 (and weakly C4).

4. **C3 and C5 convertase formation (1 mark):** **C4b + C2a → C4b2a (C3 convertase)**, identical to the classical pathway. C4b2a cleaves C3 → C3a + C3b; additional C3b forms **C4b2a3b (C5 convertase)** → C5 → C5a + C5b → MAC.

**Differences from Classical Pathway (2 marks):**

| Feature | Classical | Lectin (MBL) |
|---|---|---|
| Recognition molecule | C1q | MBL / Ficolins |
| Activating proteases | C1r, C1s | MASP-1, MASP-2 |
| Trigger | Antibody (IgG/IgM) – antigen complex | Microbial surface sugars (mannose, fucose) |
| Antibody required? | Yes | No |
| Evolutionary origin | Adaptive immune (evolved later) | Innate immune (ancient) |
| Time scale | 7–10 days (primary response) | Immediate (innate, present at birth) |

---

**Model Solution — Part B (b):**

**MAC Structure (3 marks):**
The Membrane Attack Complex is a **transmembrane pore** assembled from the terminal complement components:
- **C5b** — nucleation site; C5 is cleaved by C5 convertase to release C5a and C5b.
- **C6** — binds C5b → **C5b-6** (hydrophilic, fluid-phase complex).
- **C7** — binds C5b-6 → **C5b-7** (amphipathic; hydrophobic domains exposed).
- **C8** — binds C5b-7 → **C5b-8** (inserts into lipid bilayer, forms a small initial lesion).
- **C9** (n = **10–18 molecules**) — polymerizes on C5b-8 to form a **β-barrel transmembrane pore** of **~10 nm diameter**.

**MAC Formation Steps (2 marks):**
- C5b + C6 → C5b6 (fluid-phase)
- C5b6 + C7 → C5b67 (membrane-anchoring)
- C5b67 + C8 → C5b678 (partial pore; lipid bilayer insertion)
- C5b678 + C9n (10–18) → C5b–C9n = **MAC (full transmembrane pore)**

**Biological Consequences (1 mark):**
- **Osmotic lysis:** Na⁺, Ca²⁺, and H₂O rush in; the cell swells and ruptures.
- **Sub-lytic MAC** (in nucleated cells) can trigger **cell activation, proliferation, or inflammatory signalling** rather than lysis.
- **Most susceptible pathogens:** *Neisseria* species (**N. meningitidis, N. gonorrhoeae**) — patients with terminal complement deficiencies (C5–C9) suffer recurrent Neisseria infections, a KTU-favorite clinical correlation.

**Pathogen Evasion (1 mark):**
Many pathogens evade MAC:
- *CD59-like molecules* on viral envelopes.
- Long LPS O-antigens on Gram-negative bacteria (sterically hinder C5b-7 membrane insertion).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **C2a vs C2b confusion:** A common mistake is to label the *large* fragment of C2 as C2b. The correct convention is: **C2a = large/catalytic; C2b = small**. Same naming logic as C3a/C3b and C4a/C4b.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of Properdin:** Students often list Factor B and Factor D but omit **Properdin (Factor P)**, the **only positive regulator** of complement — it stabilizes the alternative C3 convertase. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss in 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Confusing the lectin pathway's MASP enzymes with the classical C1r/C1s:** They are *analogous* but *not identical*. Don't write "MASP = C1r/C1s" — they are evolutionarily related but encoded by separate genes.
> 4. **Neglecting to mention that the alternative pathway is *spontaneous*:** Examiners want to see the phrase **"C3 tickover"** explicitly. Without it, your answer appears to be missing the initiation step.
> 5. **Not connecting C3d to B-cell activation:** The bridge from innate complement to **adaptive B-cell immunity** via **C3d–CR2 (CD21) interaction** is a high-yield integrative concept — omitting it forfeits marks in long-answer questions.
> 6. **Forgetting the role of Mg²⁺ and Ca²⁺:** C1q-C1r-C1s assembly requires **Ca²⁺**, while C3 convertase function requires **Mg²⁺**. Chelation with EDTA abolishes all complement activity; this is the basis of clinical CH50 sample handling.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and review before every KTU exam attempt on this module.**

- [ ] The complement system comprises **~30 plasma proteins**, mostly produced by the **liver** as inactive **zymogens**.
- [ ] **Three pathways:** Classical (C1q), Lectin (MBL), Alternative (C3 tickover).
- [ ] **Classical and Lectin pathways share the same C3 convertase: C4b2a.**
- [ ] **Alternative pathway C3 convertase: C3bBb**, stabilized by **Properdin (Factor P)**.
- [ ] **All three converge on C3 cleavage** → C3a + C3b.
- [ ] **C5 convertase** formed by adding another C3b: C4b2a3b (Classical/Lectin) or C3bBb3b (Alternative).
- [ ] **C5 cleavage** releases **C5a** (most potent anaphylatoxin + chemotaxin) and **C5b** (MAC nucleator).
- [ ] **MAC = C5b-C6-C7-C8-C9ₙ**, a ~10 nm transmembrane pore causing **osmotic lysis**.
- [ ] **Three anaphylatoxins ranked:** C5a > C3a > C4a; all inactivated by **carboxypeptidase N/B**.
- [ ] **C3b / iC3b** = opsonins, recognized by **CR1 (CD35)** and **CR3 (CD11b/CD18)**.
- [ ] **C3d** binds **CR2 (CD21)** on B cells → lowers B-cell activation threshold (innate-to-adaptive bridge).
- [ ] **Host regulators:** **C1-INH, Factor H, Factor I, DAF (CD55), MCP (CD46), CD59**.
- [ ] **C1-INH deficiency** → **Hereditary Angioedema (HAE)**.
- [ ] **C5–C9 deficiency** → recurrent **Neisseria** infections.
- [ ] **Eculizumab** (anti-C5 mAb) is the FDA-approved therapy for **PNH** and **aHUS**.
- [ ] **Ca²⁺** is required for C1q-C1r-C1s assembly; **Mg²⁺** is required for convertase function; **EDTA** abolishes all complement activity (key for clinical CH50 sample collection).
- [ ] **"Small = fluid phase, Large = surface-bound"** is the rule (with the **C2 exception** where C2a is the large/catalytic subunit).
- [ ] **Properdin (Factor P) is the ONLY positive regulator** — all others are inhibitory.
- [ ] The alternative pathway contributes **~80% of total complement activation** due to the positive-feedback amplification loop.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Type I: Allergies and Anaphylaxis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Type I: Allergies and Anaphylaxis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type I Hypersensitivity (Immediate Hypersensitivity / Anaphylactic Hypersensitivity):**
> A rapid-onset, **IgE-mediated** immunological reaction triggered by the cross-linking of IgE antibodies bound to high-affinity **FcεRI receptors** on the surface of mast cells and basophils, resulting in the immediate release of preformed and newly synthesized inflammatory mediators. Reactions typically occur within **seconds to minutes** (usually < 30 min) following re-exposure to a previously encountered antigen (allergen).

According to the **Coombs and Gell classification (1963)**, Type I hypersensitivity is the only reaction that can be fatal within minutes (anaphylaxis), distinguishing it clinically from Types II, III, and IV.

### Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Allergen** | A normally harmless environmental antigen (e.g., pollen, dust mite protein) that elicits a Type I response in sensitized individuals. |
| **Atopy** | The genetic predisposition to produce IgE antibodies against common environmental allergens. |
| **Sensitization** | The initial asymptomatic priming event in which IgE is produced and bound to mast cells. |
| **Effector Phase** | The symptomatic re-exposure event in which mast cell degranulation occurs. |
| **Anaphylaxis** | A severe, systemic, life-threatening Type I reaction involving two or more organ systems or a sudden drop in blood pressure. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Booby-Trapped Guard Dog" Analogy:**
> Imagine your immune system as a neighborhood security system. During **sensitization** (first exposure), harmless pollen enters the body. The immune system mistakenly identifies it as a deadly threat and trains "guard dogs" (mast cells) by attaching **"wanted posters" (IgE antibodies)** to their collars. The dogs are now *primed* but silent — the neighborhood looks peaceful. Upon **re-exposure**, the same pollen appears again. The moment it brushes against a wanted poster, the mast cell "barks violently" (degranulates), releasing histamine and other alarm chemicals. If only one guard dog barks, you get a sneeze (local allergy). If *every* guard dog in the neighborhood barks at once, the entire town is thrown into chaos — that's **anaphylaxis**.

### Clinical Manifestations Overview

- **Localized:** Allergic rhinitis (hay fever), allergic asthma, urticaria (hives), atopic dermatitis, food allergy (oral).
- **Systemic:** Anaphylaxis (laryngeal edema, bronchospasm, hypotension, cardiovascular collapse).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Approximately **30–40%** of the global population is atopic, and the prevalence of allergic diseases has risen dramatically in industrialized nations (the "hygiene hypothesis" context). **Anaphylaxis incidence is ~50–2000 episodes per 100,000 person-years**, with a case fatality rate of **< 1%** when treated promptly with epinephrine.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Phase Mechanism of Type I Hypersensitivity

### Phase 1 — Sensitization (Priming Phase, Days 1–14)

1. **Antigen uptake:** Allergen is captured by **Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs)** — dendritic cells in mucosal/skin tissues.
2. **T-helper polarization:** Allergen presented on **MHC class II** to naïve CD4⁺ T cells. In atopic individuals, this drives differentiation into **Th2 cells** (rather than Th1).
3. **Th2 cytokine secretion:** Th2 cells release the hallmark cytokine triad:
   - **IL-4** → B-cell class switching to **IgE** (isotype switching signal)
   - **IL-5** → Eosinophil recruitment, proliferation, activation
   - **IL-13** → Reinforces IgE class switching, goblet cell hyperplasia, mucus production
4. **IgE binding:** Secreted IgE binds with extremely high affinity (**Kd ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ M**) to **FcεRI receptors** on tissue mast cells and circulating basophils. This stable binding lasts **weeks to months**, providing long-term memory.

### Phase 2 — Early & Late Phase Responses (Effector Phase)

**Early Phase (within 5–30 minutes):**
1. **Re-exposure** to the same (multivalent) allergen.
2. **Cross-linking** of at least two adjacent IgE molecules on the mast cell surface → receptor aggregation.
3. **Degranulation** releases **preformed mediators** from cytoplasmic granules within seconds.
4. **Arachidonic acid cascade** generates **newly synthesized lipid mediators** within minutes.

**Late Phase (2–48 hours later, in ~50% of patients):**
- Recruited **eosinophils, basophils, neutrophils, and Th2 cells** infiltrate the tissue.
- Release of **major basic protein (MBP)**, eosinophil cationic protein, and additional cytokines.
- Causes chronic inflammation and tissue remodeling (e.g., in chronic asthma).

## 2.2 The Two Waves of Mast Cell Mediators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preformed Mediators (Released from Granules, < 30 sec):**
> - **Histamine** — Vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, smooth muscle contraction, pruritus.
> - **TNF-α** — Pro-inflammatory, activates endothelium.
> - **Heparin** — Anticoagulant, mast cell-specific marker.
> - **Tryptase** — Serum marker of mast cell activation (used clinically in anaphylaxis diagnosis).
> - **Proteases** (chymase, carboxypeptidase) — Tissue remodeling.

**Newly Synthesized Lipid Mediators (from Arachidonic Acid, minutes):**
- **LTC4, LTD4, LTE4 (Cysteinyl leukotrienes)** — 1000× more potent than histamine; bronchoconstriction, mucus secretion.
- **PGD2** — Vasodilation, neutrophil chemotaxis.
- **PAF (Platelet Activating Factor)** — Bronchoconstriction, vasodilation, platelet aggregation.

**Cytokines & Chemokines (hours):**
- IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, IL-13, TNF-α, CCL2, CCL3 — Sustain the late-phase response.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> Since Type I hypersensitivity is largely qualitative, the mathematical tools used are prevalence, affinity, and pharmacokinetic. Use the table below as a **cheat sheet** for any numerical component in KTU examinations.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| IgE–FcεRI Affinity | $K_a = \dfrac{[IgE \cdot Fc\varepsilon RI]}{[IgE][Fc\varepsilon RI]}$ | $K_a \approx 10^{10}\ M^{-1}$ (very high) |
| IgE half-life (free) | $t_{1/2} \approx 2\text{–}3\ \text{days}$ | Bound form: weeks–months |
| Histamine receptor occupancy (response) | $\dfrac{[H]}{[H] + K_d}$ | Hill coefficient ≈ 1 (single site) |
| Epinephrine dose (anaphylaxis) | $0.01\ \text{mg/kg IM}$ (max **0.5 mg adult / 0.3 mg child**) | Repeat every 5–15 min |
| Allergen sensitization rate (population) | $P = \dfrac{\text{Number sensitized}}{\text{Total exposed}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as % prevalence |
| Receiver Operating Characteristic (diagnostic) | $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$, $\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | Unitless ratio ∈ [0,1] |
| Wheal-and-Flare area (skin test) | $A = \pi r^2$ | $r$ = radius in mm; positive if $A \geq 7\ \text{mm}^2$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility & Clinical Relevance

- **Diagnostic:** Skin-prick testing and serum **specific IgE (sIgE) ELISA** rely on the principle of mast-cell-bound IgE cross-linking. Serum **tryptase** is the gold-standard biomarker for diagnosing mast-cell-mediated anaphylaxis post-mortem or acutely.
- **Therapeutic Monoclonal Antibodies:**
  - **Omalizumab** — Anti-IgE mAb that binds circulating IgE, preventing mast cell sensitization. Used in severe asthma and chronic spontaneous urticaria.
  - **Dupilumab** — Anti-IL-4Rα mAb that blocks IL-4/IL-13 signaling, used in atopic dermatitis and asthma.
- **Engineering Parallel (Biomedical Engineering):** Modern allergen microarrays and biosensors use surface plasmon resonance (SPR) principles, analogous to receptor-ligand affinity measurements, to detect sIgE at concentrations of **< 0.35 kU/L**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — IgE Receptor Occupancy & Mast Cell Sensitization

The fraction of FcεRI receptors occupied by IgE on a mast cell determines its sensitization potential. We model receptor occupancy using the classic Langmuir adsorption isotherm.

### Step 1: Define the reversible binding equilibrium

$$
\text{IgE} + \text{Fc}\varepsilon\text{RI} \;\underset{k_d}{\overset{k_a}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; \text{IgE}\cdot\text{Fc}\varepsilon\text{RI}
$$

Where $k_a$ is the association rate constant ($M^{-1}s^{-1}$) and $k_d$ is the dissociation rate constant ($s^{-1}$).

### Step 2: Express the equilibrium dissociation constant

At equilibrium, by definition:

$$
K_d \;=\; \dfrac{[IgE][Fc\varepsilon RI]}{[IgE \cdot Fc\varepsilon RI]}
$$

Rearranging for the bound complex:

$$
[IgE \cdot Fc\varepsilon RI] \;=\; \dfrac{[IgE][Fc\varepsilon RI]}{K_d}
$$

### Step 3: Define the fraction of receptors occupied

Let $B_{max}$ be the total receptor concentration, and $B$ the bound receptor concentration. The free receptor concentration equals $B_{max} - B$. Substituting:

$$
B \;=\; \dfrac{[IgE](B_{max}-B)}{K_d}
$$

Solving for $B$:

$$
B \cdot K_d \;=\; [IgE] \cdot B_{max} - [IgE] \cdot B
$$

$$
B \cdot K_d + [IgE] \cdot B \;=\; [IgE] \cdot B_{max}
$$

$$
B \cdot (K_d + [IgE]) \;=\; [IgE] \cdot B_{max}
$$

### Step 4: The fractional occupancy equation

$$
\boxed{\;\theta \;=\; \dfrac{B}{B_{max}} \;=\; \dfrac{[IgE]}{[IgE] + K_d}\;}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical evaluation for typical atopic patient

For an atopic individual, assume serum total IgE $\approx 300\ \text{kU/L}$ (≈ $300 \times 2.4 = 720\ \text{ng/mL} = 7.2 \times 10^{-7}\ M$). The affinity $K_d \approx 10^{-10}\ M$. Therefore:

$$
\theta \;=\; \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{-7}}{7.2 \times 10^{-7} + 1.0 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
\theta \;=\; \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{-7}}{7.2001 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
\theta \;\approx\; 0.99986 \;\;(\approx 99.99\%\ \text{occupancy})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Because $K_d$ is extraordinarily small, mast cells become nearly **100% saturated** with IgE even at modest serum IgE concentrations. This explains why allergic individuals can react to minuscule allergen doses.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Allergens by Route of Exposure

| Allergen Source | Examples | Route of Entry | Typical Clinical Manifestation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inhalants (Aeroallergens)** | Pollen (ragweed, birch), dust mite (Der p 1), cockroach, animal dander (Fel d 1 from cats), mold spores | Inhalation | Allergic rhinitis, asthma |
| **Foods** | Peanut (Ara h 1), tree nuts, shellfish, milk (β-lactoglobulin), egg (ovalbumin), wheat, soy | Ingestion | Oral allergy syndrome, urticaria, food-induced anaphylaxis |
| **Injectants / Contact** | Bee/wasp venom (Api m 1), latex, drugs (penicillin), contrast media | Sting, IV, cutaneous | Urticaria, anaphylaxis |
| **Contactants** | Nickel, fragrances, poison ivy (Type IV mostly) | Skin contact | Contact dermatitis (usually Type IV) |

## 3.3 Derivation of Skin-Prick Test (SPT) Positivity Criterion

A positive skin-prick test result depends on the wheal diameter produced by allergen-induced mast cell degranulation in the dermis. Empirically:

$$
\text{Positive if:}\quad \text{Wheal diameter} \;\geq\; 3\ \text{mm}\ \text{vs. negative control}
$$

The wheal area is approximately circular:

$$
A_{wheal} \;\approx\; \pi r^2
$$

For a 3 mm diameter wheal:

$$
A_{wheal} \;=\; \pi \times (1.5)^2 \;\approx\; 7.07\ \text{mm}^2
$$

Thus the **clinical threshold is ≈ 7 mm²**. This geometric derivation justifies the standard SPT cut-off.

## 3.4 Pathway Comparison: Allergic Reaction vs. Anaphylaxis

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Local Allergic Reaction:} \quad & \text{Limited mast cell activation} \rightarrow \text{Local histamine release} \rightarrow \text{Sneezing, itching, hives} \\
\text{Systemic Anaphylaxis:} \quad & \text{Widespread mast cell activation} \rightarrow \text{Massive mediator release} \rightarrow \text{Bronchospasm + Hypotension + Laryngeal edema}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Phase Mechanism Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["First Exposure to Allergen"] --> B["Antigen Uptake by APC Dendritic Cell"]
    B --> C["Antigen Presentation on MHC Class II"]
    C --> D["Naive CD4+ T Cell Activation"]
    D --> E["Th2 Cell Differentiation"]
    E --> F["IL-4 IL-5 IL-13 Secretion"]
    F --> G["B Cell Class Switching to IgE"]
    G --> H["IgE Binds Fc epsilon RI on Mast Cell"]
    H --> I["Sensitized Mast Cell - Asymptomatic State"]

    J["Re-exposure to Same Allergen"] --> K["Allergen Cross-links Two IgE Molecules"]
    I --> K
    K --> L["Mast Cell Degranulation"]
    L --> M["Preformed Mediators Histamine Tryptase TNF"]
    L --> N["Newly Synthesized LTC4 PGD2 PAF"]
    M --> O["Early Phase Response 5 to 30 minutes"]
    N --> O
    O --> P["Vasodilation Bronchospasm Itching Urticaria"]
    O --> Q["Systemic Anaphylaxis if Widespread"]
    O --> R["Late Phase Response 2 to 48 hours"]
    R --> S["Eosinophil and Th2 Cell Infiltration"]
    S --> T["Chronic Inflammation and Tissue Remodeling"]
```

## 4.2 Anaphylaxis Management Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Suspected Anaphylaxis Onset"] --> B["Assess Airway Breathing Circulation"]
    B --> C{"Cardiopulmonary Arrest?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Begin CPR Immediately"]
    C -- "No" --> E["Administer IM Epinephrine 0.01 mg per kg"]
    E --> F["Position Patient Supine Legs Elevated"]
    F --> G["High Flow Oxygen via Mask"]
    G --> H{"Symptoms Persist after 5 to 15 min?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Repeat IM Epinephrine Dose"]
    H -- "No" --> J["Monitor for 4 to 24 hours Biphasic Risk"]
    I --> K{"Refractory Hypotension?"}
    K -- "Yes" --> L["IV Epinephrine Infusion"]
    K -- "No" --> M["Add H1 and H2 Antihistamines Corticosteroids Adjuncts"]
    L --> N["ICU Admission and Continuous Monitoring"]
    M --> N
    J --> N
```

## 4.3 IgE Structure & Receptor Binding Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["IgE Antibody Y shaped Monomer"] --> B["Two Antigen Binding Sites Fab Region"]
    A --> C["Fc epsilon Region Constant Domain"]
    C --> D["High Affinity Binding Site for Fc epsilon RI"]
    D --> E["Mast Cell or Basophil Surface Receptor"]
    E --> F["Alpha Chain binds IgE"]
    E --> G["Beta Chain Signal Amplification"]
    E --> H["Two Gamma Chains Disulfide linked ITAM Motif"]
    H --> I["Lyn and Syk Kinase Activation"]
    I --> J["Calcium Influx and Degranulation"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Allergen"] --> B["APC Dendritic Cell"]
    B --> C["Th2 Cell"]
    C -- IL-4 IL-13 --> D["B Cell Class Switch"]
    D --> E["IgE Secreting Plasma Cell"]
    E --> F["Circulating IgE"]
    F --> G["Mast Cell Sensitization"]
    C -- IL-5 --> H["Eosinophil Activation"]

```
