# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define immune complex hypersensitivity. List any two differences between soluble and insoluble immune complex-mediated reactions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Type III hypersensitivity is an immunopathological reaction caused by antigen-antibody complexes that activate complement and recruit neutrophils, leading to inflammatory tissue damage.

**Differences (1 mark each):**

| Feature | Soluble ICs | Insoluble ICs |
|---|---|---|
| Antigen:Ab ratio | Antigen excess | Equivalence |
| Location | Systemic (kidney, joints) | Local (injection site) |
| Onset | 7–14 days | 4–8 hours |
| Example | SLE, Serum sickness | Arthus reaction |

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the term "Zone of Equivalence" in the precipitin reaction. Why are complexes formed in slight antigen excess most pathogenic?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Zone of Equivalence (1.5 marks):**
The zone of equivalence is the antigen:antibody ratio at which the largest insoluble immune complexes (lattices) form, producing maximum precipitation. It corresponds to optimal cross-linking of multivalent antigens by bivalent antibodies.

**Why Antigen Excess is Pathogenic (1.5 marks):**
In slight antigen excess, complexes are small and soluble. They evade both the mononuclear phagocyte system (MPS) and glomerular filtration (since they are too large to filter but too small to be cleared by phagocytes), depositing in vessel walls — particularly fenestrated capillaries of the kidney glomerulus.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]

**(a) Describe in detail the pathogenesis of Type III hypersensitivity reaction with a neat diagram of complement activation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Type III hypersensitivity is mediated by antigen-antibody (immune) complexes that deposit in tissues, activate complement via the classical pathway, and recruit neutrophils, causing inflammation and tissue damage.

**Sequential Pathogenesis (5 marks):**

**1. Immune Complex Formation:**
When antigen meets specific IgG (subclasses 1, 3) or IgM antibody, immune complexes form. The size and solubility depend on the antigen-antibody ratio:
- Antibody excess → small, soluble
- Equivalence → large, insoluble lattices
- Antigen excess → small, soluble (most pathogenic clinically)

**2. Tissue Deposition:**
Circulating soluble complexes deposit in vessels with fenestrated endothelium:
- Renal glomeruli (most common)
- Joint synovia
- Skin blood vessels
- Choroid plexus
- Lung alveoli (in inhalation forms)

Local insoluble complexes deposit at the site of antigen entry (Arthus reaction).

**3. Complement Activation (Classical Pathway):**
$$\text{C1q} \rightarrow \text{C1r} \rightarrow \text{C1s} \rightarrow \text{C4} \rightarrow \text{C2} \rightarrow \text{C3} \rightarrow \text{C5}$$
- **C3a, C5a:** Anaphylatoxins causing mast cell degranulation, vasodilation, increased vascular permeability.
- **C3b:** Opsonizes immune complexes for phagocytosis.
- **C5a:** Potent neutrophil chemoattractant.

**4. Neutrophil Recruitment and Activation:**
Neutrophils bind ICs via **FcγR** (CD16, CD32, CD64) and **CR1** (CD35). This triggers:
- Phagocytosis attempt (often fails with surface-bound ICs)
- Degranulation releases lysosomal enzymes

**5. Tissue Damage:**
- **Proteolytic enzymes:** Elastase, collagenase, cathepsin G
- **Reactive oxygen species:** Superoxide anion, hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radical
- **Lipid mediators:** Leukotriene B4, prostaglandin E2

**Complement Activation Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Antigen-Antibody Complex → C1q binding → C1r/C1s activation
                                            ↓
              C4 → C4a + C4b ←——————— Activation cascade
              C2 → C2a + C2b
                     ↓
              C4b2a (C3 convertase)
                     ↓
              C3 → C3a (anaphylatoxin) + C3b (opsonin)
                     ↓
              C4b2a3b (C5 convertase)
                     ↓
              C5 → C5a (chemoattractant) + C5b
                     ↓
              C5b + C6-C9 → Membrane Attack Complex (MAC)
```

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The final result is vasculitis, glomerulonephritis, arthritis, or systemic inflammatory manifestations depending on the deposition site.

---

**(b) Compare and contrast Arthus reaction and Serum sickness. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Arthus Reaction | Serum Sickness |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of IC** | Insoluble (local) | Soluble (systemic) |
| **Location** | Site of antigen injection | Blood vessels, kidney, joints, skin |
| **Antigen source** | Exogenous (e.g., vaccine, insect bite) | Heterologous serum (e.g., anti-thymocyte globulin) |
| **Onset** | 4–8 hours | 7–14 days |
| **Size of IC** | Large lattice | Small (formed in antigen excess) |
| **Site of formation** | At injection site | In circulation |
| **Clearance** | Trapped locally | Evades MPS, deposits systemically |
| **Distribution** | Localized edema, hemorrhage, necrosis | Generalized vasculitis, fever, lymphadenopathy, arthralgia |
| **Complement involvement** | Local, intense | Systemic |
| **Symptoms** | Painful, indurated, erythematous swelling | Fever, urticaria, arthralgia, proteinuria |
| **Example** | Tetanus toxoid booster reaction | Anti-rabies serum sickness |
| **Mechanism** | Pre-existing IgG + injected Ag | Persistent Ag + rising Ab response |
| **Histology** | Neutrophilic infiltrate, fibrinoid necrosis, hemorrhage | Vasculitis with leukocytoclasis |

**Clinical Example (2 marks):**
- **Arthus:** A laboratory worker with prior immunization to horse serum develops painful, edematous, erythematous swelling at the site of subsequent intradermal horse serum injection within 4–8 hours.
- **Serum sickness:** A patient given anti-thymocyte globulin (rabbit) for transplant rejection develops fever, urticarial rash, arthralgia, and proteinuria 10 days later due to anti-rabbit IgG antibodies forming soluble ICs with rabbit IgG.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Alternative]

**(a) Explain the precipitin curve and discuss the factors that determine the pathogenicity of immune complexes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The Precipitin Curve (3 marks):**

The Heidelberger-Marrack curve plots antibody remaining in supernatant against antigen added. Three zones:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Zone 1 (Antibody excess / Prozone):} & \quad \text{Small soluble complexes} \\
\text{Zone 2 (Equivalence):} & \quad \text{Large insoluble lattice} \\
\text{Zone 3 (Antigen excess / Postzone):} & \quad \text{Small soluble complexes}
\end{aligned}$$

Mathematically, lattice size $L$ is:

$$L = f\left(\dfrac{[Ab]}{[Ag]}\right)$$

Maximum lattice occurs at:

$$\dfrac{dL}{d([Ag])} = 0 \implies \dfrac{[Ag]}{[Ab]} = \text{optimal ratio}$$

**Factors Determining Pathogenicity (4 marks):**

1. **Size of Complex:** Intermediate (≈ 19S–22S, ~1×10⁶ Da) — too large for glomerular filtration but too small for MPS clearance.

2. **Valency of Antigen:** Multivalent antigens (e.g., bacterial polysaccharides) form larger lattices → more pathogenic.

3. **Class and Subclass of Antibody:**
$$\text{Complement-fixing ability: IgM} > \text{IgG3} > \text{IgG1} > \text{IgG2} > \text{IgG4 (none)}$$

4. **Affinity/Avidity:** Low-affinity antibodies form smaller, dissociable complexes → persist longer → more pathogenic.

5. **Antigen Charge:** Cationic antigens bind the anionic glomerular basement membrane (GBM) preferentially.

6. **Haemodynamic Factors:** Turbulent flow at vessel bifurcations (e.g., glomerular capillaries) promotes deposition.

7. **MPS Function:** Defective FcγR expression (e.g., in SLE patients) impairs IC clearance.

8. **Complement Deficiency:** C1q, C2, C4 deficiencies → SLE-like syndrome.

---

**(b) Discuss the role of complement and neutrophils in Type III hypersensitivity-mediated tissue damage. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Complement (3.5 marks):**

The classical pathway is triggered when C1q binds to Fc regions of antigen-complexed IgG (≥2 Fc regions in proximity) or IgM (pentamer, exposes Fc upon antigen binding).

Cascade amplification:

$$\text{C1} \xrightarrow{} \text{C4b2a (C3 convertase)} \xrightarrow{} \text{C3 amplification} \xrightarrow{} \text{C5} \xrightarrow{} \text{MAC}$$

Three key effector functions:
1. **Anaphylatoxins (C3a, C5a):** Cause mast cell/basophil degranulation → histamine release → vasodilation, increased vascular permeability → IC deposition enhanced.
2. **C5a (chemoattractant):** Recruits neutrophils to the deposition site.
3. **C3b (opsonin):** Coats ICs for phagocytosis via CR1 (CD35).
4. **MAC (C5b-9):** Direct cell lysis (in ICs deposited on cell surfaces).

**Role of Neutrophils (3.5 marks):**

Once C5a is generated, neutrophils undergo:
1. **Chemotaxis** up the C5a gradient.
2. **Adhesion** to endothelium via LFA-1/ICAM-1.
3. **Migration** through vessel wall (diapedesis).
4. **Binding** to tissue-deposited ICs via FcγR and CR1.
5. **Degranulation** releasing:
   - **Lysosomal enzymes:** Elastase, collagenase, cathepsin G, lysozyme
   - **Reactive oxygen species:** Superoxide ($\text{O}_2^-$), hydrogen peroxide ($\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$), hydroxyl radical ($\text{OH}^\bullet$)
   - **Lipid mediators:** LTB4, PGE2

Resulting tissue damage includes:
- Endothelial cell injury → vasculitis
- GBM destruction → glomerulonephritis
- Synovial inflammation → arthritis
- Fibrinoid necrosis of vessel walls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Type III with Type II:** Type II is antibody-mediated cytotoxicity (ADCC, complement lysis of cells directly). Type III requires circulating immune complexes that deposit in tissues.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4–8 hour timing:** Many students wrongly describe Type III as "immediate." Always mention the lag period for neutrophil recruitment.
> 3. **Missing antigen excess rationale:** When asked why Type III occurs, students often fail to mention that the pathological state arises because of *defective clearance* of small complexes formed in antigen excess.
> 4. **Confusing Arthus with Type IV:** Arthus is antibody-mediated (Type III) — occurring 4–8 hours. Type IV is T-cell mediated — occurring 48–72 hours. Both can cause skin induration but mechanisms differ entirely.
> 5. **Skipping C3a/C5a roles:** Always mention both the anaphylatoxin (C3a/C5a) and the chemoattractant (C5a) functions separately.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Facts to Remember (Rapid Revision):**

- **Type III Hypersensitivity = Immune Complex Mediated**
- **Reaction time:** 4–8 hours (intermediate between Type I and Type IV)
- **Three zones of precipitin curve:** Antibody excess, Equivalence, Antigen excess
- **Pathogenic complexes form in slight antigen excess** → small, soluble, evade clearance
- **Antibody classes:** IgG (especially G1, G3) and IgM most common; IgA in some forms
- **Classical complement pathway:** C1q → C1r → C1s → C4 → C2 → C3 → C5 → C6–C9 (MAC)
- **Key complement fragments:**
  - **C3a, C5a** = anaphylatoxins (vasodilation)
  - **C5a** = potent neutrophil chemoattractant
  - **C3b** = opsonin
- **Effector cells:** Neutrophils (FcγR, CR1 binding)
- **Mediators of damage:** Lysosomal enzymes, ROS, lipid mediators (LTB4, PGE2)
- **Two main patterns:**
  - **Systemic (soluble ICs):** Serum sickness, SLE, RA, PSGN, PAN
  - **Local (insoluble ICs / Arthus):** Injection site reactions, farmer's lung, pigeon breeder's disease
- **Arthus Reaction:** Localized, 4–8 hours, edema + hemorrhage + necrosis, preformed IgG + injected antigen
- **Serum Sickness:** Systemic, 7–14 days, fever + urticaria + arthralgia + proteinuria, anti-serum antibodies + serum antigens
- **Deposition sites:** Glomeruli, joints, skin vessels, choroid plexus, lung alveoli
- **Histology:** Vasculitis with fibrinoid necrosis, neutrophilic infiltrate, immune complex deposits (detected by immunofluorescence)
- **Diagnostic markers:** Decreased serum C3/C4, circulating immune complexes, granular immunofluorescence pattern
- **Treatment:** Plasmapheresis (remove ICs), anti-inflammatory drugs, antigen avoidance, immunosuppression

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory aid:**
> *"Type III = IC + Complement (C3a/C5a) + Neutrophils (FcγR) + Tissue Damage (4–8 hrs) → Vasculitis/Glomerulonephritis"*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Type IV: Delayed Type (or) Cell-Mediated HS Reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Type IV: Delayed Type (or) Cell-Mediated HS Reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Type IV Hypersensitivity**, also known as **Delayed-Type Hypersensitivity (DTH)** or **Cell-Mediated Hypersensitivity (CMH)**, is an immune reaction in which tissue injury is mediated *solely* by antigen-specific effector **T lymphocytes** (T cells) rather than by antibodies. It is characteristically **delayed**, manifesting **48–72 hours** (or longer) after antigen re-exposure in a sensitized host. The reaction depends on the recognition of antigen presented in the context of MHC molecules on antigen-presenting cells (APCs) by sensitized T cells, which then secrete cytokines and recruit/activate inflammatory cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Type IV HS is the **only** hypersensitivity class in which **antibodies (Ig) play NO pathogenic role**. It is a *purely T-cell–driven* response. This is the single most repeated differentiating point in KTU ESE questions.

| Feature | Type I, II, III HS | **Type IV HS** |
|---|---|---|
| Mediator | IgE / IgG / Immune complexes | **Sensitized T lymphocytes** |
| Onset | Seconds–Hours | **48–72 hours (Delayed)** |
| Serum Transfer Possible | Yes | **No** (cells required) |
| Desensitization | Easy | **Difficult** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slow-Burn Sentry" Analogy

Imagine your body as a fortress. Type I HS is like a *sprinkler alarm* that floods the moment it senses smoke (immediate). Type IV HS, however, is like a **trained security guard** who must:
1. **First, be hired and trained** (the *sensitization* phase, taking 1–2 weeks).
2. **Recognize the intruder** on a second visit (the *elicitation* phase).
3. Then **physically fight** the intruder and call reinforcements (cytokines, macrophages).

The guard doesn't shoot bullets (antibodies); he grabs the intruder himself and alerts other guards. That's why the reaction is "delayed" — recruitment, recognition, and direct cellular combat take time.

### 1.3 Historical Context & Coombs-Gell Classification

Coombs and Gell (1963) classified four hypersensitivity types. Type IV was unique because it **could not be transferred by serum** (cell-free fluid), proving early on that the responsible factor was *cellular*, not humoral. The classical prototype is the **Tuberculin (Mantoux) Reaction** — when tuberculin (PPD) is injected intradermally into a person previously sensitized to *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, a firm, indurated (raised, hard) plaque appears at 48–72 hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "ACID" for HS types:**
> - **A**llergic / Anaphylactic → Type I
> - **C**ytotoxic → Type II
> - **I**mmune complex → Type III
> - **D**elayed → Type IV (cell-mediated)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two-Phase Mechanism of Type IV HS

The reaction proceeds in **two biologically distinct phases** — *Sensitization* and *Elicitation* — and KTU questions frequently test the cellular players at each stage.

#### **Phase 1 — Sensitization (Priming, Days 0–14)**

1. Antigen (e.g., mycobacterial protein, nickel ion, poison ivy catechol) crosses the **skin** or **mucosa** barrier.
2. **Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs)** — chiefly **dendritic cells (Langerhans cells in skin)** and macrophages — capture the antigen.
3. APCs process the antigen and present peptide fragments on **MHC class II** to naïve **CD4⁺ T helper cells** in the regional lymph node.
4. Under the influence of **IL-12** (from APC), naïve CD4⁺ T cells differentiate into **T_h1 effector cells**. A small pool of antigen-specific **memory T cells** is also generated.
5. **No effector function occurs yet** — the host is now "sensitized" but clinically asymptomatic.

> [!NOTE]
> For some intracellular pathogens (viruses, intracellular bacteria), antigen may also be presented on **MHC class I** to **CD8⁺ cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs)**, generating sensitized CTLs capable of directly lysing infected cells.

#### **Phase 2 — Elicitation (Re-exposure, 24–72 h)**

1. On re-exposure, APCs again process and present the antigen to **memory T_h1 cells**.
2. Memory T_h1 cells release a cascade of pro-inflammatory cytokines:
   - **IFN-γ** → activates macrophages (↑ phagocytosis, ↑ ROS, ↑ lysosomal enzymes)
   - **TNF-α + LT (lymphotoxin)** → local endothelial activation, ↑ vascular permeability, fibrin deposition
   - **IL-2** → autocrine T-cell proliferation
   - **Chemokines (CXCL9, CXCL10, CCL2)** → recruit monocytes/macrophages from blood
3. **CD8⁺ CTLs** (if primed) recognize antigen on MHC-I of target cells and induce **perforin/granzyme-mediated apoptosis** or **Fas–FasL apoptosis**.
4. Accumulation of macrophages + lymphocytes + edema + fibrin = visible **induration** (firm swelling) at 48–72 h.
5. In persistent antigen exposure, macrophages transform into **epithelioid cells** and fuse into **multinucleated giant cells**, producing **granulomas** — the hallmark of *chronic* DTH.

### 2.2 Cytokine "Master Switches"

| Cytokine | Source | Target | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IFN-γ** | T_h1, NK cells | Macrophage | Classical activation (M1), ↑MHC II, ↑iNOS, ↑ROS |
| **TNF-α** | Macrophage, T cells | Endothelium | ↑ Adhesion molecules (VCAM-1, ICAM-1), fibrin |
| **IL-12** | APCs (DC, Mφ) | Naïve CD4⁺ T | Drives T_h1 differentiation |
| **IL-2** | T_h1 cells | T cells | Autocrine proliferation |
| **IL-17** | T_h17 | Neutrophils | Recruits PMNs (Type IVd) |

### 2.3 Classification of Type IV HS — The **4 Subtypes (IVa–IVd)**

This is a **KTU high-yield topic**. Modern immunology subdivides Type IV by the dominant effector T-cell subset:

| Subtype | Effector Cell | Key Cytokines | Infiltrate | Prototype Disease |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IVa** | **T_h1** | IFN-γ, TNF | Macrophages | **Tuberculin test**, TB granuloma, Sarcoidosis |
| **IVb** | **T_h2** | IL-4, IL-5, IL-13 | Eosinophils | **Chronic asthma**, Atopic dermatitis (some forms) |
| **IVc** | **CD8⁺ CTL** | Perforin, Granzyme B, FasL | CTLs | **Contact dermatitis** (early), Viral hepatitis, Transplant rejection |
| **IVd** | **T_h17** | IL-17, IL-22 | Neutrophils | **Acute contact dermatitis**, Psoriasis, Behçet's disease |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quantitative Summary)

Because Type IV HS is a qualitative topic, the "formula sheet" is a **metrics & timing reference table**:

| Metric / Parameter | Standard Value / Range | KTU Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Reaction onset (typical) | **24–72 hours** | "Delayed" — hallmark |
| Peak induration reading | **48–72 hours** | Mantoux read at 72 h |
| Sensitization period (first contact → primed T cells) | **1–2 weeks (7–14 days)** | No skin reaction at first exposure |
| Serum transfer | **Not possible** | Only cell transfer (adoptive transfer) reproduces DTH |
| Key effector cells | CD4⁺ T_h1, CD8⁺ CTL, Macrophage | **No B cells, No Plasma cells, No Ig** |
| Master cytokine | **IFN-γ** | Activates macrophage; deficient mice fail DTH |
| Histological hallmark | **Granuloma** (chronic) | Epithelioid cells + Langhans giant cells |
| Tuberculin unit (TU) standard dose | 5 TU (0.1 mL of PPD-S) | WHO standard for Mantoux |
| Mantoux positive induration (≥15 mm in low-risk, ≥5 mm in HIV+) | **5–15 mm threshold** | Used as clinical rule |
| TCR co-receptor for APC presentation | **CD4** (MHC II) and **CD8** (MHC I) | Dual pathway for DTH |

### 2.5 Clinical Examples Mapping to Subtypes

- **Tuberculin (Mantoux) Test** → Type IVa (T_h1 + macrophage)
- **Granulomatous TB, Leprosy, Sarcoidosis, Crohn's Disease** → Type IVa (chronic T_h1 granuloma)
- **Nickel/Chromate Contact Dermatitis, Poison Ivy (Urushiol)** → Type IVc (early) + Type IVd (later neutrophil phase)
- **Acute Allergic Contact Dermatitis** → Type IVd (T_h17 + neutrophils)
- **Transplant Rejection** (acute cellular) → Type IVc (CD8⁺ CTL attacking graft)
- **Type 1 Diabetes, Multiple Sclerosis, Hashimoto's Thyroiditis** → Type IVa autoimmune
- **Granuloma formation around Schistosome eggs** → Type IVa (T_h1-mediated)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, step-by-step logical derivations** of the major Type IV HS concepts in exam-ready form.

### 3.1 Derivation of the "48–72 Hour" Timing

> **Why is the reaction delayed?** We trace the cellular chain:

**Step 1 — Antigen Re-entry (t = 0 h)**
Re-exposed antigen is captured by **Langerhans cells / dermal dendritic cells** in the epidermis. These cells must take up the antigen, process it in endolysosomes, and load peptide fragments onto **MHC class II** molecules.

**Step 2 — Migration to Lymph Node (t = 0–6 h)**
APCs migrate via afferent lymphatics to the **regional draining lymph node**, a process regulated by the chemokine receptor **CCR7** and its ligands CCL19/CCL21. This transit takes several hours.

**Step 3 — Re-encounter with Memory T_h1 (t = 6–24 h)**
Memory T_h1 cells specific for the antigen (generated during sensitization) recognize the peptide-MHC II complex via their **TCR + CD4 co-receptor**. The binding affinity is high (memory cells have lower activation thresholds than naïve cells).

**Step 4 — Effector Differentiation & Cytokine Burst (t = 24–48 h)**
Memory T cells re-differentiate rapidly into **effector T_h1 cells** and secrete cytokines:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory T}_{\text{h1}} + \text{APC} &\xrightarrow{\text{TCR-MHC II contact}} \text{Effector T}_{\text{h1}} \\
\text{Effector T}_{\text{h1}} &\rightarrow \text{IFN-}\gamma \;\;+\;\; \text{TNF-}\alpha \;\;+\;\; \text{IL-2} \;\;+\;\; \text{Chemokines}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Leukocyte Recruitment (t = 24–48 h)**
**TNF-α** and **IFN-γ** induce endothelial expression of adhesion molecules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Endothelium (resting)} &\xrightarrow{\text{TNF-}\alpha} \text{Endothelium expressing E-selectin, ICAM-1, VCAM-1} \\
\text{Blood monocytes} &\xrightarrow{\text{rolling, firm adhesion, transmigration}} \text{Tissue macrophages}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Macrophage Activation & Induration (t = 48–72 h)**
**IFN-γ** "classically activates" macrophages (M1 polarization). Activated macrophages:

- ↑ **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS)**: $\text{O}_2 + \text{NADPH oxidase} \rightarrow \text{O}_2^{\bullet -}$
- ↑ **iNOS pathway**: $\text{L-arginine} \xrightarrow{\text{iNOS}} \text{NO} + \text{L-citrulline}$
- ↑ **Phagolysosomal enzymes** (acid hydrolases)
- Secrete additional **TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6** (amplification)

**Step 7 — Clinical Induration (t = 72 h peak)**
Accumulation of macrophages + lymphocytes + fibrin-rich edema produces a **palpable, indurated plaque** — the measured endpoint of the Mantoux test.

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Delay Logic:**
> $$
> T_{\text{reaction}} = T_{\text{APC migration}} + T_{\text{memory activation}} + T_{\text{leukocyte recruitment}} \approx 48\text{–}72 \text{ h}
> $$
> This multi-step cellular cascade — not antibody binding — is the structural reason Type IV HS is **delayed**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Granuloma Formation (Chronic DTH)

When antigen is **not easily cleared** (e.g., mycobacteria, schistosome eggs, foreign bodies), macrophages undergo a morphological transformation:

**Step 1 — Persistence of IFN-γ Signal**
Continuous antigen presentation sustains T_h1 cells releasing IFN-γ.

**Step 2 — Macrophage Activation (M1)**
Macrophages become enlarged with abundant pink cytoplasm → **Epithelioid Cells**.

**Step 3 — Macrophage Fusion**
Multiple macrophages fuse under influence of **IFN-γ** and **IL-4** (T_h2) via **DC-STAMP** and other fusogenic proteins:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n \cdot \text{Macrophage} &\xrightarrow{\text{IFN-}\gamma \text{ + IL-4}} \text{Multinucleated Giant Cell} \\
\text{Types: } &\text{Langhans giant cells (peripheral nuclei, TB)} \\
&\text{Foreign body giant cells (random nuclei, sutures)} \\
&\text{Touton giant cells (lipid-laden, xanthomas)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Architectural Organization**
The mature **granuloma** has a central core of epithelioid cells ± giant cells ± necrotic debris (**caseous necrosis** in TB), surrounded by a rim of **CD4⁺ T_h1 cells** and outer **fibroblasts** depositing collagen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** Granuloma is the *histological signature* of chronic Type IVa HS. In its absence (e.g., HIV/AIDS patients with depleted CD4⁺ T cells), TB fails to form granulomas → **disseminated miliary TB**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Distinguishing the 4 Subtypes

| Feature | **IVa (T_h1)** | **IVb (T_h2)** | **IVc (CTL)** | **IVd (T_h17)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Master cytokine | IFN-γ | IL-4, IL-5, IL-13 | Perforin/Granzyme | IL-17, IL-22 |
| Dominant cell | Macrophage | Eosinophil | CTL (CD8⁺) | Neutrophil |
| Antigen presentation | MHC II → CD4 | MHC II → CD4 | **MHC I → CD8** | MHC II → CD4 |
| Histology | Granuloma | Eosinophil infiltrate | Lymphocytic infiltrate, apoptosis | Neutrophilic microabscess |
| Disease prototype | **TB**, Sarcoidosis | Chronic asthma | **Viral hepatitis**, Transplant rejection | **Psoriasis**, Acute contact dermatitis |
| KTU expected depth | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

---

### 3.4 Mantoux Test — Numerical Threshold Logic

The **Mantoux test** injects **5 Tuberculin Units (TU)** of Purified Protein Derivative (PPD-S) intradermally on the volar forearm. Induration (not erythema) is measured at 48–72 h.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Induration cutoff (mm)} &= f(\text{Risk category}) \\
\text{≥ 5 mm} &: \text{HIV+, recent TB contact, fibrotic CXR} \\
\text{≥ 10 mm} &: \text{Recent immigrants (<5y), IV drug users, lab personnel} \\
\text{≥ 15 mm} &: \text{No known risk factors (general population)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Booster Effect:** Repeated Mantoux tests in previously sensitized individuals may *boost* the response. The 2-step testing protocol (initial + booster 1–4 weeks later) is used in healthcare workers to establish a true baseline.

---

### 3.5 Pathogenesis of Contact Dermatitis (e.g., Poison Ivy)

**Step 1** — **Hapten Formation**: Urushiol (lipophilic catechol) penetrates skin and binds self-proteins → forms a complete antigen (hapten-carrier complex).

**Step 2** — **Sensitization**: Langerhans cells process and present to naïve T cells in the lymph node → T_h1, T_h17, and CD8⁺ CTL memory cells generated.

**Step 3** — **Re-exposure (Elicitation, 24–72 h)**:
- CD8⁺ CTLs (Type IVc) release **IFN-γ, TNF-α** → keratinocyte apoptosis → spongiosis.
- T_h17 cells (Type IVd) release **IL-17** → neutrophil recruitment → microabscesses.
- T_h1 cells (Type IVa) release **IFN-γ** → macrophage activation.

**Step 4** — **Clinical Lesion**: Erythema → vesicles → weeping → scaling. The classic *linear streaks* of poison ivy dermatitis reflect the path of plant contact.

---

### 3.6 Transplantation Rejection — Type IV HS in Action

| Type | Timing | Mechanism | Reversibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hyperacute** | Minutes | Preformed anti-donor antibodies (Type II) | Not reversible |
| **Acute** | Days–Months | **CD8⁺ CTL + CD4⁺ T_h1** (Type IV) attacking graft parenchyma + endothelium | Reversible with immunosuppression |
| **Chronic** | Months–Years | T-cell + antibody-mediated vasculopathy, fibrosis | Not reversible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key:** Acute cellular rejection = **Type IV** HS (CD8⁺ CTLs + CD4⁺ T_h1). Acute humoral rejection = **Type II** HS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Mechanism of Type IV HS (Two-Phase Cascade)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Antigen First Exposure] --> B[APC Uptake by Langerhans Cell]
    B --> C[Migration to Regional Lymph Node]
    C --> D[Presentation on MHC II to Naive CD4 T cell]
    D --> E[Sensitization Phase: 7 to 14 days]
    E --> F[Generation of Memory T h1 and CD8 CTL Pool]
    F --> G[Host is now Sensitized Asymptomatically]
    G --> H[Antigen Re exposure at 0 hour]
    H --> I[APC presents to Memory T h1]
    I --> J[Memory T h1 Activation within 6 to 24 hour]
    J --> K[Cytokine Burst: IFN gamma and TNF alpha and IL2]
    K --> L[Endothelial Activation VCAM1 ICAM1]
    L --> M[Monocyte Recruitment 24 to 48 hour]
    M --> N[Macrophage Activation by IFN gamma]
    N --> O[Induration Peak at 48 to 72 hour]
    O --> P{Antigen Cleared?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Resolution and Tissue Repair]
    P -- No --> R[Chronic Phase: Granuloma Formation]
    R --> S[Epithelioid Cells and Langhans Giant Cells]
    S --> T[Fibroblast Rim and Collagen Deposition]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Subtypes of Type IV HS (IVa–IVd)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Type IV HS] --> B[IVa T h1 driven]
    A --> C[IVb T h2 driven]
    A --> D[IVc CD8 CTL driven]
    A --> E[IVd T h17 driven]
    B --> B1[IFN gamma and TNF]
    B --> B2[Macrophage Activation]
    B --> B3[Prototype: Tuberculin Test and TB Granuloma]
    C --> C1[IL4 and IL5 and IL13]
    C --> C2[Eosinophil Recruitment]
    C --> C3[Prototype: Chronic Asthma]
    D --> D1[Perforin and Granzyme B and FasL]
    D --> D2[Direct Target Cell Apoptosis]
    D --> D3[Prototype: Viral Clearance and Acute Transplant Rejection]
    E --> E1[IL17 and IL22]
    E --> E2[Neutrophil Recruitment]
    E --> E3[Prototype: Psoriasis and Acute Contact Dermatitis]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Granuloma Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Persistent Antigen e.g. Mycobacterium] --> B[T h1 Cells Secrete IFN gamma]
    B --> C[Macrophage Activation M1 Polarization]
    C --> D[Epithelioid Cells Transformed Macrophages]
    D --> E[Macrophage Fusion via DC STAMP]
    E --> F[Langhans Giant Cells Peripheral Horseshoe Nuclei]
    D --> G[Central Caseous Necrosis in TB]
    F --> H[Outer Rim: CD4 T h1 Cells]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Fibroblast Layer Collagen Deposition]
    I --> J[Encapsulated Mature Granuloma]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Diagnostic Algorithm: Mantoux Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Receives 5 TU PPD Intradermal] --> B[Read Induration at 48 to 72 hour]
    B --> C{Induration Size in mm}
    C -- Greater Equal 5 mm --> D[Positive: HIV Positive or Recent TB Contact or Fibrotic CXR]
    C -- Greater Equal 10 mm --> E[Positive: Recent Immigrant or IV Drug User or Lab Worker or Child under 4]
    C -- Greater Equal 15 mm --> F[Positive: General Population No Risk Factors]
    C -- Less than cutoff --> G[Negative]
    D --> H[Investigate Active TB and Latent TB Therapy]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> I{Repeat Test in 1 to 4 weeks Booster Effect}
    I -- Converted --> H
    I -- Still Negative --> J[Consider Anergy or No TB Exposure]
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Cell Players vs. HS Type Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hypersensitivity Reactions] --> B[Type I IgE Mediated]
    A --> C[Type II IgG Cytotoxic]
    A --> D[Type III Immune Complex]
    A --> E[Type IV T Cell Mediated]
    B --> B1[Mast Cell Degranulation]
    B --> B2[Anaphylaxis and Allergy]
    C --> C1[Complement and ADCC]
    C --> C2[Hemolytic Disease and Goodpasture]
    D --> D1[Neutrophil Recruitment by Immune Complexes]
    D --> D2[SLE and Serum Sickness]
    E --> E1[CD4 T h1 and CD8 CTL and Macrophage]
    E --> E2[Tuberculin and Contact Dermatitis and Transplant Rejection]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*What is Type IV Hypersensitivity? Why is it called "delayed"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Type IV Hypersensitivity is a T-cell mediated immune reaction in which tissue injury is caused by antigen-sensitized T lymphocytes (CD4⁺ T_h1 and/or CD8⁺ CTLs) rather than by antibodies. **(1 mark)**

It is called "delayed" because the reaction manifests **48–72 hours** after antigen re-exposure in a sensitized host. **(1 mark)**

The delay is due to the time required for: (i) antigen processing by APCs, (ii) memory T-cell activation, (iii) cytokine secretion (IFN-γ, TNF-α), and (iv) recruitment and activation of macrophages at the site. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model QP]**
*List the four subtypes of Type IV HS with their dominant effector cells.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Type IVa** — T_h1 effector cells → macrophage activation (e.g., Tuberculin reaction, TB granuloma) **(0.75)**
2. **Type IVb** — T_h2 effector cells → eosinophil recruitment (e.g., chronic asthma) **(0.75)**
3. **Type IVc** — CD8⁺ Cytotoxic T Lymphocytes → target cell apoptosis via perforin/granzyme (e.g., viral hepatitis, transplant rejection) **(0.75)**
4. **Type IVd** — T_h17 effector cells → neutrophil recruitment (e.g., psoriasis, acute contact dermatitis) **(0.75)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Two completely independent alternatives, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **OPTION A (Set 1)**

**Q3A(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**
*Describe in detail the mechanism of Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction. Add a note on the cells involved.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Sensitization Phase (Priming)** **(3 marks)**
- On first exposure to antigen (e.g., tuberculin PPD), **Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs)** — Langerhans cells in skin, dendritic cells, macrophages — capture, process, and present the antigen on **MHC class II** to **naïve CD4⁺ T cells** in the regional lymph node.
- Under the influence of **IL-12** from APCs, naïve CD4⁺ T cells differentiate into **T_h1 effector cells** and a pool of **memory T cells** is generated.
- For intracellular antigens, CD8⁺ CTLs are also primed via MHC I cross-presentation.
- This phase takes **7–14 days**. The host is sensitized but clinically asymptomatic.

**2. Elicitation Phase (Effector)** **(3 marks)**
- On **re-exposure**, APCs again present antigen to **memory T_h1 cells**.
- Memory T_h1 cells are rapidly activated (within 6–12 hours) and secrete:
  - **IFN-γ** — activates macrophages (↑ ROS, ↑ iNOS, ↑ phagocytosis)
  - **TNF-α + Lymphotoxin** — activates local endothelium (↑ VCAM-1, ICAM-1, E-selectin)
  - **IL-2** — autocrine T-cell proliferation
  - **Chemokines (CXCL9, CXCL10, CCL2)** — recruit monocytes from blood
- Recruited monocytes differentiate into macrophages and are classically activated (M1) by IFN-γ.
- **CD8⁺ CTLs** (when primed) recognize antigen on MHC I of infected target cells and induce apoptosis via **perforin/granzyme** and **Fas-FasL** pathways.

**3. Clinical Manifestation & Cells Involved** **(1 mark)**
- Accumulation of macrophages + lymphocytes + fibrin = **induration** at 48–72 hours.
- Chronic DTH leads to **granuloma** formation (epithelioid cells + Langhans giant cells).
- **Cells involved**: CD4⁺ T_h1, CD8⁺ CTLs, macrophages, Langerhans cells, eosinophils (in IVb), neutrophils (in IVd).

---

**Q3A(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023, Adapted]**
*Explain the pathogenesis of Tuberculin (Mantoux) test and its interpretation.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Pathogenesis** **(4 marks)**
- A person previously infected with *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* harbours **memory T_h1 cells** specific for mycobacterial antigens (e.g., ESAT-6, CFP-10).
- Intradermal injection of **5 TU PPD (Purified Protein Derivative)** introduces antigen into the dermis.
- Dermal **Langerhans cells / dendritic cells** process PPD and present peptides on **MHC class II** to memory T_h1 cells.
- Memory T_h1 cells are rapidly activated and release **IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2, chemokines** within 24 hours.
- **IFN-γ** activates macrophages; **TNF-α** upregulates endothelial adhesion molecules; monocyte recruitment peaks at 48–72 hours.
- Histologically: perivascular cuffing of **CD4⁺ T cells** + **macrophages** + **fibrin deposition** → clinically palpable **induration**.
- A positive test indicates **Type IV HS** (cell-mediated immunity), *not* active disease per se.

**2. Interpretation** **(3 marks)**
- Measure **induration (not erythema)** in mm at 48–72 hours.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{≥ 5 mm} &: \text{HIV}^+, \text{ recent TB contact}, \text{ fibrotic CXR changes} \\
\text{≥ 10 mm} &: \text{Recent immigrants (<5y)}, \text{ IV drug users}, \text{ healthcare workers}, \text{ children <4y} \\
\text{≥ 15 mm} &: \text{Persons with no known risk factors}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Booster Effect**: A second test 1–4 weeks later may show a positive response (due to recall of memory), establishing true baseline in healthcare workers.
- **False negatives**: Anergy (HIV, malnutrition, sarcoidosis, miliary TB), recent live vaccination, technical error.
- **False positives**: Prior BCG vaccination, infection with non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM).

---

#### **OPTION B (Set 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

**Q3B(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*Classify Type IV Hypersensitivity into its four subtypes with examples. Differentiate them based on cytokines and effector cells.*

**Model Answer:**

Type IV HS is classified into four subtypes (IVa–IVd) based on the **dominant effector T-cell subset**, the **cytokines** they release, and the **inflammatory cells** they recruit. **(1 mark)**

**1. Type IVa — T_h1 driven (3 marks)**
- **Effector cells**: CD4⁺ T_h1 cells
- **Key cytokines**: **IFN-γ** (master), TNF-α, IL-2
- **Inflammatory cells recruited**: Macrophages (M1 polarized)
- **Mechanism**: IFN-γ classically activates macrophages → ↑ ROS, ↑ iNOS, ↑ phagolysosomal enzymes.
- **Prototypes**: **Tuberculin (Mantoux) test, TB granuloma, Sarcoidosis, Crohn's disease, Type 1 Diabetes (autoimmune)**.

**2. Type IVb — T_h2 driven (1 mark)**
- **Effector cells**: CD4⁺ T_h2 cells
- **Key cytokines**: **IL-4, IL-5, IL-13**
- **Inflammatory cells**: Eosinophils
- **Prototypes**: **Chronic asthma, atopic dermatitis (chronic phase)**.

**3. Type IVc — CD8⁺ CTL driven (1 mark)**
- **Effector cells**: CD8⁺ Cytotoxic T Lymphocytes
- **Key cytokines/Effectors**: **Perforin, Granzyme B, Fas–FasL**
- **Mechanism**: Direct lysis of target cells expressing antigen on MHC I.
- **Prototypes**: **Acute cellular transplant rejection, viral hepatitis, type IV contact dermatitis (early)**.

**4. Type IVd — T_h17 driven (1 mark)**
- **Effector cells**: CD4⁺ T_h17 cells
- **Key cytokines**: **IL-17, IL-22**
- **Inflammatory cells**: Neutrophils
- **Prototypes**: **Psoriasis, acute contact dermatitis, Behçet's disease**.

> *(Note: Allocate sub-marks as proportional to the marks-weightage indicated.)*

---

**Q3B(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Adapted]**
*Describe the pathogenesis of contact dermatitis (e.g., poison ivy). Why is it considered a Type IV reaction?*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Nature of the Antigen — Haptens** **(2 marks)**
- Poison ivy contains **urushiol**, a lipid-soluble catechol. It is a **hapten** — too small to be antigenic alone.
- Urushiol penetrates the skin and **covalently binds self-proteins** in the epidermis → forms a complete **hapten-carrier antigen**.

**2. Sensitization Phase** **(2 marks)**
- **Langerhans cells** in the epidermis capture the hapten-protein complex, migrate to the regional lymph node, and present on **MHC II**.
- Naïve CD4⁺ T cells differentiate into **T_h1, T_h17, and CD8⁺ CTL memory cells** (multi-subtype priming).
- Takes **10–14 days**.

**3. Elicitation Phase** **(2 marks)**
- On re-exposure, all three subsets are activated within **24–72 hours**:
  - **CD8⁺ CTLs (IVc)**: Release perforin/granzyme + IFN-γ → keratinocyte apoptosis → spongiosis.
  - **T_h17 (IVd)**: Release IL-17 → neutrophil recruitment → intraepidermal microabscesses.
  - **T_h1 (IVa)**: Release IFN-γ → macrophage activation.
- Histology: **spongiosis, lymphocytic infiltrate, eosinophils, neutrophils, epidermal vesicles**.

**4. Clinical Course** **(1 mark)**
- Erythema → vesicles → weeping → crusting → scaling (within 7–14 days).
- Classic **linear streaks** correspond to plant brushing.

**Why Type IV?**
- No antibody involvement; T cells are the sole effectors; reaction is delayed 24–72 h; cannot be transferred by serum; histology shows mononuclear cell infiltrate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing "IgE" or "antibody"** anywhere in a Type IV answer → automatic 0.5–1 mark cut per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing "induration" with "erythema"** in the Mantoux test. Only induration is measured.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the sensitization phase.** Type IV is *biphasic*. Skipping Phase 1 costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Writing "delayed due to antibody latency"** — antibodies do not mediate Type IV; the delay is purely cellular.
> 5. **Omitting IFN-γ as the master cytokine** — this is the single most-cited cytokine in valuation keys.
> 6. **Mixing up IVc (CD8) with IVa (CD4)** in transplant rejection questions.
> 7. **Not mentioning "granuloma"** when asked about chronic DTH → loss of 1–2 marks.
> 8. **Writing "Mantoux positive = active TB"** — it indicates *exposure/sensitization*, not active disease.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU-Focused)**

- ⭐ Type IV HS is the **only antibody-independent** hypersensitivity class.
- ⭐ Mediated by **T cells (CD4⁺ T_h1 + CD8⁺ CTLs) + macrophages**.
- ⭐ **Delayed onset: 48–72 hours** (the "D" in ACID classification).
- ⭐ **Two phases**: Sensitization (7–14 days) → Elicitation (24–72 h).
- ⭐ **Master cytokine: IFN-γ** (macrophage activator).
- ⭐ Other key cytokines: **TNF-α, IL-12, IL-2, IL-17**.
- ⭐ **Four subtypes (IVa–IVd)** based on effector T-cell subset (T_h1, T_h2, CD8⁺ CTL, T_h17).
- ⭐ **Mantoux Test** — prototype; induration ≥ 5/10/15 mm depending on risk.
- ⭐ **Granuloma** — histological hallmark of chronic Type IVa HS.
- ⭐ **Contact Dermatitis** (poison ivy) — hapten + multi-subtype (IVc + IVd).
- ⭐ **Transplant Rejection** — acute cellular = Type IVc (CD8⁺ CTL).
- ⭐ **Tuberculosis, Sarcoidosis, Crohn's, Leprosy, Type 1 DM, MS** = Type IVa examples.
- ⭐ **Psoriasis** = Type IVd (T_h17/IL-17).
- ⭐ **Chronic asthma** = Type IVb (T_h2/eosinophil).
- ⭐ **Cannot transfer by serum** — only by **adoptive cell transfer** (T cells).
- ⭐ **No plasma cells, no B cells, no immunoglobulins** involved.
- ⭐ **MHC restriction**: CD4 → MHC II; CD8 → MHC I (relevant for IVc).
- ⭐ **Coombs-Gell classification (1963)** originally had only one Type IV; the IVa–IVd sub-classification is from **Cher & Mosmann (1987)** onward, refined by **Kay** and others.
- ⭐ KTU-Favorite One-Liners for Answer Writing:
  - *"Type IV HS is a delayed, T-cell-mediated reaction without antibody participation."*
  - *"IFN-γ activates macrophages, the principal effector cells of chronic DTH."*
  - *"Granuloma formation reflects persistent antigen and ongoing T_h1 cytokine signaling."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tuberculin Reaction (Definition):**
> The **Tuberculin Reaction** is a classic example of **Type IV (Delayed-Type) Hypersensitivity (DTH)**, also known as **Cell-Mediated Hypersensitivity**. It is an *in vivo* immunological reaction elicited when an individual previously sensitized to antigens of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* is re-exposed to tuberculin (PPD – Purified Protein Derivative). The reaction manifests as a firm, indurated swelling at the injection site, peaking at **48–72 hours** post-injection, and is mediated primarily by **CD4⁺ Th1 lymphocytes** and activated **macrophages**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **"Memory Alarm Analogy" – How a Tuberculin Reaction Works:**
> Imagine your immune system is a **highly trained security guard** stationed in a large building. Once, a dangerous intruder (the TB bacterium) broke in. The guard fought it off, and more importantly, he **remembered the intruder's face** by keeping a photograph in the security office (memory T cells). 
>
> Weeks later, the guard is shown a small **photograph of the intruder** (the PPD injection). The guard doesn't see a real threat – just a picture – but his memory triggers him to **call in reinforcements**, blow whistles, and shut down the hallway. The hallway becomes swollen, red, and firm (the induration). This entire response takes a day or two to organize – hence the name *delayed* hypersensitivity. The size of the swelling tells us how strong the guard's memory is – i.e., how strongly sensitized the patient is.

### 1.3 Key Historical & Clinical Terms

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Tuberculin** | A glycerol extract of culture filtrate of *M. tuberculosis*, originally prepared by **Robert Koch (1890)**. |
| **PPD (Purified Protein Derivative)** | The standardized, purified form of tuberculin (PPD-S is the international standard). |
| **Mantoux Test** | The standard intradermal injection of **0.1 mL (5 TU) of PPD** on the volar surface of the forearm. |
| **Induration** | A palpable, raised, hardened area of swelling measured in **millimeters (mm)**. |
| **TU (Tuberculin Unit)** | Standardized biological potency unit of PPD. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Tuberculin Reaction is the **prototype example** of **Type IV Hypersensitivity** and is a high-yield topic in Module 3 of PEBBT631. It must be studied alongside Koch's phenomenon, the cells/cytokines involved, and the Mantoux test interpretation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Context – The Four Types of Hypersensitivity

The **Coombs and Gell Classification** divides hypersensitivity into four types. The Tuberculin Reaction belongs to **Type IV**, which is *cell-mediated* (no antibody involvement).

| Type | Mediator | Onset | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | IgE + Mast cells | Seconds–Minutes | Anaphylaxis, Hay fever |
| **Type II** | IgG/IgM (Cytotoxic) | Hours | Hemolytic transfusion reaction |
| **Type III** | Immune Complexes | Hours | Serum sickness, Arthus reaction |
| **Type IV** | T cells + Macrophages | **48–72 hours** | **Tuberculin reaction**, Contact dermatitis |

### 2.2 The Two Phases of the Tuberculin Reaction

The reaction occurs in two distinct, sequential phases:

#### **Phase 1 – Sensitization Phase (1–2 weeks after primary exposure)**
1. *M. tuberculosis* enters the body → engulfed by **Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs)**, particularly **dendritic cells** and **macrophages**.
2. APCs process the mycobacterial antigens and present peptide fragments on **MHC Class II** molecules to naïve **CD4⁺ T helper cells** in the regional lymph node.
3. In the presence of **IL-12** (secreted by APCs), naïve CD4⁺ T cells differentiate into **Th1 effector cells**.
4. These Th1 cells proliferate and form a pool of **memory Th1 cells** that circulate in the body for years (sometimes lifelong).

#### **Phase 2 – Effector / Elicitation Phase (48–72 hours after re-exposure / PPD injection)**
1. Intradermal injection of PPD is taken up by local APCs and presented on **MHC II**.
2. **Memory Th1 cells** recognize the antigen-MHC II complex (a process called **cognate recognition**).
3. Activated Th1 cells release a **cytokine storm**:
   - **IFN-γ** → activates macrophages (classical M1 activation)
   - **TNF-α** & **Lymphotoxin (TNF-β)** → local inflammation, endothelial activation
   - **IL-2** → autocrine T cell proliferation
   - **Chemokines (CXCL9, CXCL10, CCL2)** → recruit more monocytes/macrophages
4. Activated macrophages transform into **epithelioid cells** and may fuse to form **giant cells (Langhans giant cells)**.
5. The accumulated macrophages and T cells form a **granuloma** at the injection site → this is felt clinically as the **induration**.

### 2.3 Histopathological Hallmarks

> [!NOTE]
> **Histology of the Tuberculin Reaction Site (Biopsy):**
> - **Perivascular cuffing** of lymphocytes (predominantly CD4⁺ T cells) around post-capillary venules.
> - **Infiltrate of macrophages** in the dermis and subcutaneous tissue.
> - Edema and fibrin deposition causing the **induration**.
> - In severe reactions: **granuloma formation** with **epithelioid cells** and **Langhans giant cells**.
> - **Notably absent:** Eosinophils, basophils, and significant neutrophil infiltration (distinguishes from Types I and III).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cut-off Sheet

| Parameter | Standard Value / Formula | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Dose of PPD injected** | **0.1 mL containing 5 TU** | Standard international dose |
| **Reading time** | **48–72 hours** post-injection | Peak of DTH reaction |
| **What to measure** | **Induration (mm), NOT erythema** | Erythema alone is not diagnostic |
| **Positive cut-off (≥5 mm)** | HIV-positive, recent close contacts, fibrotic changes on CXR, immunosuppressed | High-risk groups |
| **Positive cut-off (≥10 mm)** | Recent immigrants (<5 yrs), IV drug users, lab personnel, residents of high-risk congregate settings, children <4 yrs, mycobacteriology lab personnel | Moderate-risk groups |
| **Positive cut-off (≥15 mm)** | Persons with no known risk factors | Low-risk groups |
| **Booster effect** | Repeat test after 1–2 weeks gives larger reaction | Recall of waned immunity |
| **Reaction size formula (qualitative)** | Induration size ∝ Intensity of cell-mediated immunity | Greater induration = stronger CMI |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Biomedical Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Beyond Medicine:** Although the Tuberculin Reaction is purely clinical, the principle of **delayed-type hypersensitivity** is exploited in modern biomedical engineering:
> - **Latex allergy patch testing** in healthcare workers (Type IV contact dermatitis from latex proteins).
> - **Drug hypersensitivity testing** for antibiotics, anti-epileptics.
> - **Biomaterial implant rejection studies** in tissue engineering – T-cell mediated responses to scaffold materials mimic the DTH pathway.
> - **Vaccine development** – DTH is used as a correlate of cell-mediated immunity (CMI) in TB and COVID-19 vaccine trials.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Tuberculin Reaction Mechanism

**Step 1 – Antigen Entry & Uptake (Day 0)**
$$
\text{Mycobacterium tuberculosis} \xrightarrow{\text{infection}} \text{APCs (Dendritic cells / Macrophages)}
$$

The bacilli are phagocytosed but survive intracellularly within phagosomes due to **cord factor (trehalose dimycolate)** and **sulfatides** that prevent phagolysosome fusion.

**Step 2 – Antigen Processing & Presentation**

$$
\text{Phagocytosed bacilli} \xrightarrow{\text{proteolysis}} \text{Peptide fragments (15–25 aa)}
$$
$$
\text{Peptide} + \text{MHC Class II} \xrightarrow{\text{vesicular trafficking}} \text{Plasma membrane of APC}
$$

**Step 3 – T Cell Activation & Differentiation (Day 7–14)**

$$
\text{Naive CD4}^+\text{ T cell} + \text{Antigen-MHC II} + \text{IL-12 (from APC)} \xrightarrow{\text{differentiation}} \text{Th1 effector cells}
$$

The **TCR-CD3 complex** binds the peptide-MHC II. Co-stimulatory signal **B7 (APC) ↔ CD28 (T cell)** is mandatory. Without it, **anergy** results.

**Step 4 – Memory Formation**

$$
\text{Th1 effector cells} \xrightarrow{\text{clonal expansion + IL-2 autocrine loop}} \text{Memory Th1 cells (CCR7}^+\text{, CD45RO}^+\text{)}
$$

These memory cells home to **peripheral tissues and lymph nodes** and can persist for decades.

**Step 5 – Re-exposure (Mantoux Test / PPD Injection)**

$$
\text{PPD (0.1 mL, 5 TU)} \xrightarrow{\text{intradermal injection}} \text{Local dermal APCs}
$$

**Step 6 – Cognate Recognition by Memory T Cells**

$$
\text{Memory Th1 (TCR)} + \text{APC (Peptide-MHC II)} \xrightarrow{\text{recognition}} \text{Th1 reactivation}
$$

**Step 7 – Cytokine Release (Effector Molecules)**

$$
\text{Activated Th1} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{IFN-}\gamma \\ \text{TNF-}\alpha \\ \text{Lymphotoxin (TNF-}\beta\text{)} \\ \text{IL-2} \\ \text{Chemokines (CXCL9, CXCL10, CCL2)} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 8 – Macrophage Activation & Recruitment**

$$
\text{IFN-}\gamma + \text{Macrophage} \rightarrow \text{Classically Activated Macrophage (M1)}
$$
$$
\text{M1 macrophages} \rightarrow \uparrow \text{ ROS, } \uparrow \text{ NO, } \uparrow \text{ MHC II, } \uparrow \text{ TNF-}\alpha
$$

**Step 9 – Granuloma Formation & Induration**

$$
\text{Macrophages} \xrightarrow{\text{TNF-}\alpha} \text{Epithelioid cells} \xrightarrow{\text{fusion}} \text{Langhans giant cells}
$$
$$
\text{Perivascular accumulation} \rightarrow \text{Palpable induration (measured in mm)}
$$

**Step 10 – Resolution**

After 48–72 hours, the reaction plateaus and gradually subsides over days to weeks as antigen is cleared and the immune response is downregulated by **Treg cells** and **IL-10**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Cells vs. Cytokines in Tuberculin Reaction

| Component | Cell Type / Molecule | Specific Role |
|---|---|---|
| **APCs** | Dendritic cells, Macrophages | Process & present TB antigens on MHC II |
| **Effector T cells** | **CD4⁺ Th1 cells** (primary) | Master regulators; release cytokines |
| **Effector T cells** | CD8⁺ T cells (secondary) | Kill infected cells; release IFN-γ |
| **Effector cells** | **Macrophages** | Form granuloma; cause induration |
| **Effector cells** | Neutrophils (minor, late) | Cleanup of debris |
| **Signature cytokine** | **IFN-γ** | Activates macrophages; most critical |
| **Pro-inflammatory** | **TNF-α** | Granuloma maintenance; inflammation |
| **T cell growth** | **IL-2** | Autocrine T cell proliferation |
| **T cell differentiation** | **IL-12** | Drives Th1 polarization |
| **Anti-inflammatory** | IL-10, TGF-β | Resolution phase |
| **Chemokines** | CXCL9, CXCL10, CCL2 | Recruit monocytes/T cells |
| **Surface marker** | **CD4, TCR, CD28, CCR5, CXCR3** | Characteristic of Th1 cells |

### 3.3 Decision Tree for Mantoux Test Interpretation

```
                        Read induration at 48–72 h
                                    |
                ---------------------------------------
                |                  |                  |
             < 5 mm            5–9 mm              10–14 mm            ≥ 15 mm
                |                  |                  |                  |
           NEGATIVE         Depends on           POSITIVE           POSITIVE
                             risk group          (in risk groups)   (any person)
                |                  |                                     |
        No infection /         Cut-offs:                                No risk
        Anergy / False neg     ≥ 5 mm → HIV+, contacts                  factor
                               ≥ 10 mm → Mod risk                      needed
                               ≥ 15 mm → Low risk
```

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanism Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid

    A["Mycobacterium tuberculosis enters body"] --> B["Uptake by APCs dendritic cells and macrophages"]
    B --> C["Antigen processing and presentation on MHC Class II"]
    C --> D["Naive CD4+ T cell recognition via TCR and CD28 co stimulation"]
    D --> E["IL 12 from APC drives Th1 differentiation"]
    E --> F["Th1 cells proliferate and form Memory Th1 cells"]
    F --> G["Sensitization complete patient now sensitized"]
    G --> H["Intradermal injection of PPD 0.1 mL 5 TU Mantoux test"]
    H --> I["Local APCs take up PPD and present on MHC II"]
    I --> J["Memory Th1 cells recognize antigen MHC II complex"]
    J --> K["Th1 cells release IFN gamma TNF alpha IL 2 and chemokines"]
    K --> L["IFN gamma activates macrophages to M1 phenotype"]
    L --> M["Macrophages accumulate and form epithelioid cells"]
    M --> N["Granuloma formation with Langhans giant cells"]
    N --> O["Palpable induration measured at 48 to 72 hours"]
    O --> P["Reaction resolves over days to weeks"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Define Tuberculin reaction. Mention the type of hypersensitivity it represents and the cells primarily involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Tuberculin reaction** is a localized, delayed inflammatory reaction that develops at the site of intradermal injection of tuberculin (PPD) in an individual previously sensitized to *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* antigens. (1 mark)

It is the **prototype example of Type IV (Delayed-Type / Cell-Mediated) Hypersensitivity**, mediated primarily by **CD4⁺ Th1 lymphocytes** and **activated macrophages**. (1 mark)

The reaction peaks at **48–72 hours** and is read by measuring the diameter of **induration** in millimeters. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**What is Mantoux test? How is the result interpreted?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Mantoux test** is a standardized intradermal test for detecting cell-mediated immunity to *M. tuberculosis*. It involves injecting **0.1 mL of PPD containing 5 TU** into the volar surface of the forearm. (1 mark)

The result is read at **48–72 hours** by measuring the **diameter of induration** (not erythema) in millimeters, transverse to the long axis of the arm. (1 mark)

Interpretation cut-offs: **≥5 mm positive** in HIV+/close contacts; **≥10 mm positive** in moderate-risk groups (immigrants, IV drug users, children <4 yrs); **≥15 mm positive** in low-risk individuals. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - Students often confuse **Type I (immediate, IgE)** with **Type IV (delayed, T-cell)** hypersensitivity – this alone costs **2–3 marks**.
> - Many describe the Mantoux test but **forget to mention induration vs. erythema** distinction.
> - In mechanism questions, the role of **IL-12 in Th1 differentiation** and **IFN-γ in macrophage activation** are *must-mention* points.
> - Always state the **time of reading (48–72 h)** – never say "immediate" or "after 24 h".
> - Drawing a **neat, labeled diagram** of the mechanism adds 1–2 marks in board exams.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Immunology Module 3]**

**(a) Explain the mechanism of Tuberculin reaction. Add a note on the cells and cytokines involved. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Tuberculin reaction is a Type IV (Delayed-Type) hypersensitivity reaction that develops in sensitized individuals upon intradermal injection of tuberculin (PPD). It is mediated by T cells and macrophages, peaking at 48–72 hours.

**Mechanism – Two Phases:**

**Phase I – Sensitization (1.5 marks):**
- *M. tuberculosis* bacilli enter the body and are phagocytosed by **APCs** (dendritic cells and macrophages).
- APCs process the bacterial antigens and present peptide fragments on **MHC Class II** molecules.
- In the regional lymph node, naïve CD4⁺ T cells recognize the antigen-MHC II complex via TCR.
- **IL-12** secreted by APCs drives differentiation into **Th1 effector cells**.
- Clonal expansion produces **memory Th1 cells** that persist in circulation for years.

**Phase II – Effector (Elicitation) Phase (2.5 marks):**
- Intradermal PPD injection is taken up by local APCs.
- **Memory Th1 cells** recognize the antigen and become reactivated.
- Activated Th1 cells release cytokines: **IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2, lymphotoxin, and chemokines**.
- **IFN-γ** is the key cytokine that activates macrophages (M1 polarization) → increased phagocytosis, ROS, NO production, MHC II upregulation.
- **TNF-α** causes local inflammation, endothelial activation, and recruitment of more monocytes.
- Macrophages transform into **epithelioid cells** and may fuse into **Langhans giant cells**, forming a **granuloma**.
- The granulomatous inflammation manifests clinically as **induration** (palpable swelling) at 48–72 hours.

**Cells Involved (1 mark):**
CD4⁺ Th1 cells (primary), CD8⁺ T cells, macrophages, dendritic cells (APCs), and occasional neutrophils.

**Cytokines Involved (1 mark):**
**IFN-γ** (master activator of macrophages), **TNF-α** (granuloma formation), **IL-12** (Th1 differentiation), **IL-2** (T cell proliferation), and chemokines (CXCL9, CXCL10, CCL2).

---

**(b) Describe Koch's phenomenon. What is its significance? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
**Koch's phenomenon** was first described by **Robert Koch in 1882**. It refers to the altered response of a guinea pig that has been previously infected with *M. tuberculosis* upon re-inoculation with the same organism.

**Observations in a Sensitized Guinea Pig (3 marks):**
1. **At the site of first (primary) inoculation:** A **nodule** develops at the injection site, which then **ulcerates** and heals slowly. The bacilli spread to regional lymph nodes (tubercles form).
2. **At the site of subsequent (secondary) inoculation:** A more **rapid, severe, and intense** reaction occurs at the site.
3. The second inoculation site **ulcerates quickly and heals faster**.
4. Importantly, the regional lymph nodes do **not** show tubercle formation on re-inoculation (immune containment at the local site).
5. This demonstrates that the **immune system has 'remembered' the bacillus** and mounts a faster, stronger local response – the basis of cell-mediated immunity.

**Significance (3 marks):**
1. **Foundation of DTH concept:** Established that immune responses can be **delayed** (not just immediate), leading to the understanding of Type IV hypersensitivity.
2. **Basis of Tuberculin test:** Koch's observation that re-exposure caused a local reaction led to the development of the **tuberculin test** by injecting bacterial extract (PPD) rather than live bacilli.
3. **Demonstration of cell-mediated immunity:** Provided early evidence that immunity to intracellular pathogens is **T cell-mediated**, not antibody-mediated.
4. **Vaccine development:** Paved the way for **BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin)** vaccine development.
5. **Diagnostic value:** The principle underlies the **Mantoux test** used to diagnose latent TB infection worldwide.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a) Discuss the cells and cytokines involved in Type IV hypersensitivity with specific reference to the Tuberculin reaction. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Type IV Hypersensitivity, also called **Delayed-Type Hypersensitivity (DTH)** or **Cell-Mediated Hypersensitivity**, is the only type of hypersensitivity that does NOT involve antibodies. It is mediated entirely by **T lymphocytes** and **macrophages**, with a reaction time of **48–72 hours**. The Tuberculin reaction is its **prototype example**.

**Cells Involved (3 marks):**

| Cell | Specific Role in Tuberculin Reaction |
|---|---|
| **Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs)** – Dendritic cells, Macrophages | Phagocytose mycobacterial antigens; process and present on **MHC II** |
| **CD4⁺ Th1 cells** (Primary effector) | Recognize antigen-MHC II; release cytokines; orchestrators |
| **CD8⁺ Cytotoxic T cells** (Secondary) | Kill infected cells; release IFN-γ |
| **Macrophages** | Accumulate at site, transform into epithelioid cells & Langhans giant cells |
| **Treg cells** | Limit reaction; secrete IL-10, TGF-β for resolution |
| **Neutrophils** | Minor; clean up cellular debris in late phase |

**Cytokines Involved (2 marks):**

| Cytokine | Source | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **IL-12** | APCs | Drives Th1 differentiation |
| **IFN-γ** | Th1 cells | Activates macrophages (M1 polarization) – **MOST CRITICAL** |
| **TNF-α & Lymphotoxin** | Th1 cells, macrophages | Granuloma formation, inflammation |
| **IL-2** | Th1 cells | Autocrine T cell proliferation |
| **Chemokines (CXCL9, CXCL10, CCL2)** | Th1 cells | Recruit monocytes & T cells to site |
| **IL-10, TGF-β** | Treg cells | Anti-inflammatory; resolve reaction |

**Why Type IV is "Delayed" (1 mark):**
The delay is due to the time required for **memory T cell recruitment, reactivation, cytokine production, and macrophage accumulation** – a process that takes 48–72 hours, unlike the immediate (Type I) or 2–24 hour (Type II/III) responses.

---

**(b) Write a note on the clinical applications and limitations of the Tuberculin test. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Clinical Applications (3.5 marks):**
1. **Diagnosis of Latent TB Infection (LTBI):** Identifies individuals with prior exposure to *M. tuberculosis* who do not have active disease.
2. **Epidemiological surveys:** Used to estimate the prevalence of TB infection in a population.
3. **Contact tracing:** Tests close contacts of active TB patients to identify recently infected individuals.
4. **Pre-employment / Pre-immigration screening:** Healthcare workers and immigrants from endemic areas are routinely screened.
5. **BCG vaccination effect evaluation:** Helps distinguish BCG-induced reactivity (wanes over years) from true infection.
6. **Pre-treatment baseline:** In patients starting anti-TNF therapy (e.g., infliximab), to rule out latent TB.
7. **Booster phenomenon:** Repeat test after 1–2 weeks boosts a waned response, useful in elderly patients.

**Limitations (3.5 marks):**
1. **False positives** in: BCG-vaccinated individuals, infection with non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM), or improper reading of erythema instead of induration.
2. **False negatives** in: HIV/AIDS, malnutrition, sarcoidosis, miliary TB, recent measles infection, immunosuppressants, severe TB, and advanced age (anergy).
3. **Cannot distinguish active vs. latent TB:** A positive test only indicates prior sensitization, not active disease.
4. **Subjective reading:** Inter-observer variation in measuring induration.
5. **Booster effect:** Repeated tests may show increased induration due to recall, mimicking new infection.
6. **No differentiation from BCG:** Cannot reliably distinguish true infection from prior BCG vaccination.
7. **Poor specificity in BCG-vaccinated populations** (e.g., India), limiting diagnostic utility – leading to use of **IGRA (Interferon-Gamma Release Assays)** as a superior alternative in such settings.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Tuberculin Reaction:**

- ✅ Tuberculin Reaction = **Type IV Hypersensitivity** (Delayed-Type, Cell-Mediated).
- ✅ Prototype example of **DTH** in immunology.
- ✅ Tuberculin = **PPD (Purified Protein Derivative)**, dose = **0.1 mL of 5 TU**, intradermal.
- ✅ Mantoux test read at **48–72 hours**; measure **induration (mm)**, NOT erythema.
- ✅ Cut-offs: **≥5 mm** (high risk), **≥10 mm** (moderate risk), **≥15 mm** (low risk).
- ✅ **Two phases:** Sensitization (1–2 weeks) and Effector (48–72 h).
- ✅ **Primary cells:** CD4⁺ **Th1** cells + **Macrophages**.
- ✅ **Key cytokines:** **IL-12** (Th1 differentiation), **IFN-γ** (macrophage activation – MOST IMPORTANT), **TNF-α** (granuloma), **IL-2** (T cell proliferation).
- ✅ **Histology:** Perivascular lymphocytes, macrophages, granuloma with **Langhans giant cells**.
- ✅ **Koch's phenomenon (1882):** Basis of the tuberculin test; rapid, intense local reaction on re-exposure.
- ✅ **No antibody involvement** – distinguishes from Types I, II, III.
- ✅ **Limitations:** False positives (BCG, NTM), false negatives (HIV, anergy), cannot differentiate active vs. latent TB.
- ✅ **Modern alternative:** **IGRA (Interferon-Gamma Release Assay)** – not affected by BCG.
- ✅ **Granuloma** is the histopathological hallmark.
- ✅ **Delayed onset** (48–72 h) is the defining clinical feature.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Autoimmunity: Introduction, Auto Recognition, Classes of Autoimmune Diseases (Hashimoto Disease, Thyrotoxicosis, Systemic Lupus Erythematosus, Rheumatoid Arthritis)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AUTOIMMUNITY** is a pathological condition in which the immune system mounts an adaptive immune response (humoral and/or cell-mediated) directed against the body's own self-antigens (autoantigens), resulting in structural and/or functional damage to tissues and organs.

The diseases arising from such responses are called **Autoimmune Diseases (AIDs)**. They arise when the fundamental principle of immunological **self-tolerance** fails, allowing autoreactive B lymphocytes and T lymphocytes to escape deletion and become activated against host tissues.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Autoimmunity vs. Autoimmune Disease**
> - **Autoimmunity** = the presence of an immune response against self (can be benign; e.g., natural autoantibodies in low titres).
> - **Autoimmune Disease** = clinical tissue damage/dysfunction caused by that response.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Friendly Fire" of the Immune System

Imagine the immune system as a **highly trained national army** whose only mission is to defend the country (the body) against invading enemies (pathogens).

- During training, soldiers (lymphocytes) undergo a strict **"boot camp"** in the thymus and bone marrow. Any soldier whose weapons are shaped to attack his own country's flag is **executed** (clonal deletion — central tolerance).
- A few rogue soldiers escape this screening. They are kept under lifelong **house arrest by a special police force** (regulatory T cells — peripheral tolerance).
- **Autoimmunity begins when this house arrest collapses**: rogue soldiers sneak out, multiply, and start attacking the country's own infrastructure — railways (joints), power plants (thyroid), water pipelines (kidneys), etc.

### 1.3 Auto Recognition — The Molecular Basis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AUTO RECOGNITION** is the ability of immune receptors (TCR/BCR) to bind self-antigens presented in the context of self-MHC molecules. It is the molecular event that precedes any autoimmune pathology.

For T cells:
- **CD4⁺ T cells** recognize self-peptides (9–15 aa) presented on **MHC class II (HLA-DR, -DP, -DQ)** molecules on APCs.
- **CD8⁺ T cells** recognize self-peptides on **MHC class I (HLA-A, -B, -C)** on all nucleated cells.

For B cells:
- **BCR** binds conformational epitopes directly; co-stimulation (typically from autoreactive CD4⁺ T cells) is required for full activation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Autoantigens** are not necessarily "foreign-looking". They are normal, unmodified self-proteins (e.g., TSH receptor, myelin basic protein, dsDNA, type II collagen) that the immune system erroneously treats as non-self.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Markers (Bold Highlights)

- **Prevalence of AIDs:** ~5–8% of the global population; ~80% of patients are women.
- **Female-to-Male Ratio:** for SLE ≈ 9:1; for Hashimoto's ≈ 7:1; for RA ≈ 3:1.
- **Autoantibody isotypes:** predominantly **IgG** (high-affinity, class-switched) — indicating T-cell dependent B-cell activation.
- **Standard Diagnostic Titres:** ANA ≥ 1:40, anti-dsDNA ≥ 30 IU/mL, RF ≥ 1:80, anti-TPO ≥ 100 IU/mL (kit-dependent cut-offs).
- **Predisposing HLA alleles:** HLA-DR3, DR4, DR5, B27 (varies by disease).
