## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Mechanisms Leading to Breakdown of Self-Tolerance

The immune system maintains tolerance at two checkpoints. Breakdown at either level can trigger autoimmunity:

#### (A) Failure of Central Tolerance
- Defective **AIRE (Autoimmune Regulator)** protein expression in medullary thymic epithelial cells (mTECs).
- Without AIRE, tissue-specific self-antigens (e.g., insulin, thyroglobulin) are not presented, and autoreactive T cells escape negative selection.
- **Outcome:** polyendocrine autoimmune syndromes (APECED).

#### (B) Failure of Peripheral Tolerance
- Reduced function/number of **CD4⁺ CD25⁺ FoxP3⁺ regulatory T cells (Tregs)**.
- Lack of **CTLA-4 / PD-1** checkpoint signaling → autoreactive cells proliferate.

### 2.2 The Five Classical Triggers of Autoimmunity

| **#** | **Trigger Mechanism** | **Explanation in Plain Language** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Release of Sequestered Antigen** | Hidden antigens (e.g., myelin in brain, lens protein in eye, sperm in testes) are normally isolated from immune surveillance. Trauma/infection exposes them. |
| 2 | **Molecular Mimicry** | A microbial antigen resembles a self-antigen. Immune cells attack both. E.g., *Streptococcus* M protein ↔ cardiac myosin. |
| 3 | **Epitope Spreading** | Initial immune response damages tissue, exposing new (cryptic) self-epitopes that were previously tolerated. |
| 4 | **Polyclonal Activation** | Microbial superantigens (e.g., EBV EBNA-1) or endotoxin non-specifically activate many B/T cell clones, including autoreactive ones. |
| 5 | **Altered Self-Antigen (Neo-antigen)** | Drugs (e.g., procainamide, hydralazine) modify self-proteins, making them appear foreign. |

### 2.3 Classification of Autoimmune Diseases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Mnemonic — "OSIS"** for the two master classes:
> **O**rgan-Specific vs. **S**ystemic.

| **Class** | **Target** | **Examples** | **Prototype Autoantibody** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Organ-Specific** | One organ/cell type | Hashimoto's, Graves' (Thyrotoxicosis), Type-1 Diabetes, Myasthenia Gravis | Anti-TPO, anti-TSHR, anti-AChR |
| **Systemic** | Multiple organs; ubiquitous antigens | SLE, Rheumatoid Arthritis, Scleroderma | ANA, anti-dsDNA, RF, anti-CCP |
| **Intermediate/Mixed** | Mostly one organ but with systemic features | Primary biliary cirrhosis, Goodpasture's | Anti-mitochondrial, anti-GBM |

### 2.4 Pathogenetic Effector Mechanisms (Type II–V Hypersensitivity in Autoimmunity)

| **Hypersensitivity Type** | **Mechanism** | **Autoimmune Examples** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Type II** (Antibody-mediated cytotoxic) | IgG/IgM binds cell-surface or matrix antigens → complement lysis / ADCC / phagocytosis | Autoimmune hemolytic anemia, Goodpasture's, Myasthenia Gravis |
| **Type III** (Immune complex) | Soluble antigen-antibody complexes deposit in vessels/kidneys/joints → complement activation → neutrophil damage | SLE (lupus nephritis), Rheumatoid arthritis (in part) |
| **Type IV** (T-cell mediated, delayed) | CD4⁺ Th1 / CD8⁺ CTLs attack target cells; macrophages release cytokines (IFN-γ, TNF-α) | Hashimoto's thyroiditis, Type-1 DM, RA synovitis |
| **Type V** (Stimulatory antibody) | IgG binds receptor and **stimulates** rather than destroys the cell | **Graves' disease (anti-TSHR stimulating Ab)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Autoimmune disease models drive **biopharmaceutical design** — anti-TNF biologics (infliximab, adalimumab), B-cell depletion (rituximab), CTLA-4-Ig (abatacept), anti-IL-6R (tocilizumab) — all products of immunological insight translated into monoclonal-antibody engineering.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Expression / Key Marker** | **Notes / Units** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Autoantibody titre cut-off | $T \geq 1{:}40$ (ANA), $T \geq 1{:}80$ (RF) | Indirect Immunofluorescence (IIF) |
| Anti-TPO normal range | $< 34 \ \text{IU/mL}$ | ELISA |
| Anti-TSHR (TRAb) | $< 1.5 \ \text{IU/L}$ | ELISA / RIA |
| Anti-dsDNA | $< 30 \ \text{IU/mL}$ | Crithidia luciliae / Farr assay |
| Female-to-Male Ratio (SLE) | $9{:}1$ | Population statistic |
| Complement consumption (SLE flare) | $\downarrow C3, \downarrow C4$ | Indicative of immune complex deposition |
| Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate | $\text{ESR} > 20 \ \text{mm/hr (males)}, \ > 30 \ \text{mm/hr (females)}$ | Non-specific inflammation |
| C-Reactive Protein | $\text{CRP} > 10 \ \text{mg/L}$ | Acute-phase reactant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For absolute value notation in exams:** KTU evaluators prefer $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$; **never** use the bare pipe symbol inside a markdown table — it breaks table syntax.

### 2.6 Disease-by-Disease Deep Analysis

#### 2.6.1 Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Chronic Lymphocytic Thyroiditis)
- **Class:** Organ-specific; **Type IV + Type II** hypersensitivity.
- **Autoantigens:** **Thyroid peroxidase (TPO)** and **thyroglobulin (Tg)**.
- **Key Autoantibodies:** **Anti-TPO Ab** (90–95% sensitive), **Anti-thyroglobulin Ab** (60–80%).
- **Histology:** Lymphocytic infiltrate with **germinal centers**, Hürthle cells (oxyphilic follicular cells).
- **Pathogenesis:** CD8⁺ CTLs + Fas/FasL-mediated apoptosis of thyroid follicular cells → **primary hypothyroidism** (low T3/T4, high TSH).
- **HLA association:** **HLA-DR5, HLA-DR3**.
- **Clinical:** Goiter, cold intolerance, weight gain, bradycardia, myxedema, dry skin.

#### 2.6.2 Thyrotoxicosis — Graves' Disease
- **Class:** Organ-specific; **Type V** (stimulatory) hypersensitivity — unique among AIDs.
- **Autoantigen:** **Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone Receptor (TSHR)**.
- **Key Autoantibody:** **Thyroid-Stimulating Immunoglobulin (TSI / TRAb)** — an **IgG** that mimics TSH → constitutive activation of TSHR.
- **Pathogenesis:** TSI binds TSHR → continuous cAMP signaling → diffuse thyroid hyperplasia → **hyperthyroidism** (high T3/T4, low TSH).
- **Histology:** Tall columnar follicular epithelium with scalloped colloid; lymphoid infiltrate.
- **Extra-thyroidal signs:** **Exophthalmos** (retro-orbital fibroblast glycosaminoglycan deposition), **pretibial myxedema**.
- **HLA association:** **HLA-DR3, HLA-B8**.

#### 2.6.3 Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
- **Class:** Systemic; predominantly **Type III** hypersensitivity (immune complex).
- **Autoantigens:** **dsDNA, Sm (Smith), histones, nucleosomes, RNP, Ro/SSA, La/SSB, cardiolipin**.
- **Key Autoantibodies:**
  - **ANA** (95–98% sensitive — screening).
  - **Anti-dsDNA** (highly specific, correlates with **lupus nephritis**).
  - **Anti-Sm** (highly specific; poor prognostic marker).
  - **Anti-histone** (drug-induced SLE by procainamide, hydralazine, isoniazid).
  - **Anti-phospholipid** (thrombosis, recurrent miscarriages — **Hughes syndrome**).
- **Pathogenesis:** Defective clearance of apoptotic debris → nuclear antigens exposed → immune complex formation → deposition in **glomeruli, skin, joints, brain, vessels** → complement activation (↓C3, ↓C4) → vasculitis.
- **Diagnostic Criteria (SLICC / ACR-EULAR):** Malar rash, discoid rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, arthritis, serositis, renal disorder, neurological disorder, hematological disorder, immunological disorder, ANA positivity.
- **HLA association:** **HLA-DR2, HLA-DR3**, plus complement deficiencies (**C1q, C2, C4**).

#### 2.6.4 Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA)
- **Class:** Systemic; **Type III + Type IV** hypersensitivity.
- **Autoantigens:** **Type II collagen, IgG Fc region** (target of rheumatoid factor), **citrullinated proteins** (target of anti-CCP).
- **Key Autoantibodies:**
  - **Rheumatoid Factor (RF)** = IgM anti-IgG Fc (~70–80% sensitive).
  - **Anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide)** — **95% specific**; appears early; poor prognostic marker.
- **Pathogenesis:** Synovial fibroblasts + CD4⁺ Th17 cells release **TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-17** → pannus formation (granulation tissue) → cartilage & bone erosion by **RANKL**-driven osteoclasts.
- **Clinical:** Symmetric polyarthritis of small joints (MCP, PIP), morning stiffness > 1 hr, rheumatoid nodules, ulnar deviation, swan-neck deformity, boutonnière deformity.
- **Extra-articular:** Caplan syndrome (lung nodules), Felty syndrome (RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia).
- **HLA association:** **HLA-DR4** (shared epitope in DRB1).

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Step-by-Step: Pathogenesis of Hashimoto's Thyroiditis

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR5)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Environmental trigger (e.g., viral infection, iodine excess)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Thyrocyte stress} \rightarrow \text{Modified self-peptides exposed on MHC-II} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 4:} \quad \text{CD4}^+ \text{ Th1 cells recognize thyrocyte antigens} \rightarrow \text{IFN-}\gamma, \text{IL-2 release} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 5:} \quad \text{Macrophage activation} + \text{CD8}^+ \text{ CTL killing (Fas-FasL apoptosis)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 6:} \quad \text{B-cell help} \rightarrow \text{IgG class-switched anti-TPO, anti-Tg autoantibodies} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 7:} \quad \text{Complement-mediated follicular cell destruction (Type II)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 8:} \quad \text{Thyroid parenchyma replaced by lymphoid tissue with germinal centers} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 9:} \quad \text{Declining T3/T4} \rightarrow \text{Rising TSH (loss of negative feedback)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 10:} \quad \text{Clinical hypothyroidism + goiter (compensatory TSH-driven growth)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Graves' Disease — Stimulatory Cascade

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{CD5}^+ \text{B cell produces TSI (an IgG autoantibody)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{TSI binds the extracellular domain of TSHR on thyroid follicular cells} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{TSI mimics TSH} \rightarrow \text{G}_\alpha \text{ activation} \rightarrow \uparrow \text{cAMP} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 4:} \quad \text{cAMP-driven transcription of thyroglobulin, TPO, NIS (Na}^+\text{/I}^- \text{ symporter)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 5:} \quad \text{Continuous T3/T4 synthesis and release (uncontrolled)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 6:} \quad \text{Negative feedback} \rightarrow \text{lowered pituitary TSH} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 7:} \quad \text{Clinical thyrotoxicosis: tachycardia, heat intolerance, weight loss, tremor} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 8:} \quad \text{TSI also binds TSHR on retro-orbital fibroblasts} \rightarrow \text{GAG deposition} \rightarrow \text{exophthalmos}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: SLE — Immune Complex Pathogenesis

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Defective clearance of apoptotic cells (linked to C1q, C2 deficiency)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Nuclear antigens (dsDNA, Sm, histones) exposed} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Loss of B-cell tolerance; polyclonal B activation; class-switched IgG autoantibodies} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 4:} \quad \text{Antigen-antibody (immune) complex formation in circulation} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 5:} \quad \text{Deposition in small vessels, glomerular basement membrane, skin, synovia} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 6:} \quad \text{Classical complement pathway activation (C1} \rightarrow \text{C4} \rightarrow \text{C3} \rightarrow \text{C5-C9)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 7:} \quad \text{C3a, C5a (anaphylatoxins)} \rightarrow \text{neutrophil recruitment} \rightarrow \text{liquefactive damage} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 8:} \quad \text{Serum C3, C4 fall (consumed); ESR rises; CRP mildly elevated} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 9:} \quad \text{Clinical: glomerulonephritis (hematuria, proteinuria), malar rash, arthritis, serositis}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Rheumatoid Arthritis — Pannus Formation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Citrullination of self-proteins (by PAD enzymes in smokers, periodontal disease)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Anti-CCP IgG production (HLA-DR4 shared epitope presents citrullinated peptides)} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{CD4}^+ \text{ Th17 + synovial fibroblast activation} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 4:} \quad \text{Cytokine storm: TNF-}\alpha, \text{ IL-1, IL-6, IL-17} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 5:} \quad \text{Synovial angiogenesis + immune cell infiltration = pannus} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 6:} \quad \text{RANKL on fibroblasts} \rightarrow \text{osteoclast differentiation} \rightarrow \text{bone erosion} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 7:} \quad \text{MMPs (collagenase, stromelysin) degrade cartilage} \\
&\quad\;\;\Downarrow \\
&\text{Step 8:} \quad \text{Joint deformity: ulnar deviation, swan-neck, boutonnière}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Hashimoto's** | **Graves'** | **SLE** | **Rheumatoid Arthritis** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Hypersensitivity Type** | II + IV | V (Stimulatory) | III (Immune Complex) | III + IV |
| **Primary Autoantigen** | TPO, Thyroglobulin | TSHR | dsDNA, Sm, histones | Type II collagen, IgG Fc, CCP |
| **Key Autoantibody** | Anti-TPO | TSI / TRAb | ANA, anti-dsDNA | RF, anti-CCP |
| **HLA Linkage** | DR5, DR3 | DR3, B8 | DR2, DR3 | DR4 |
| **Sex Bias (F:M)** | 7:1 | 7:1 | 9:1 | 3:1 |
| **Hormonal Status** | ↓ T3/T4, ↑ TSH | ↑ T3/T4, ↓ TSH | Variable | Variable |
| **Diagnostic Hallmark** | Anti-TPO + Lymphocytic infiltrate | TSI + Exophthalmos | ANA + ↓ C3/C4 | RF/Anti-CCP + morning stiffness |
| **Treatment Class** | Levothyroxine | Anti-thyroid drugs, RAI | NSAIDs, steroids, belimumab | DMARDs, biologics (anti-TNF) |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define autoimmunity. Distinguish between organ-specific and systemic autoimmune diseases with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**
Autoimmunity is an immune response mounted against self-antigens, leading to tissue damage (autoimmune disease). Organ-specific AIDs target a single organ (e.g., Hashimoto's thyroiditis targeting thyroid, Myasthenia gravis targeting AChR at NMJ). Systemic AIDs target ubiquitous antigens across multiple organs (e.g., SLE attacking dsDNA in kidneys/skin/joints, RA attacking synovial collagen). The key difference is antigen distribution and clinical localization of pathology. **(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 distinction + 1 example pair)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Name the autoantibody and hypersensitivity type involved in Graves' disease. Why is Graves' disease unique among autoimmune disorders?**

**Model Answer:**
The autoantibody is **Thyroid-Stimulating Immunoglobulin (TSI / TRAb)**, an IgG against the **TSH receptor**. The hypersensitivity type is **Type V (Stimulatory)**. Graves' is unique because, unlike other AIDs that destroy tissue, the autoantibody **mimics TSH and hyperstimulates** the thyroid — instead of hypothyroidism, the patient develops hyperthyroidism (thyrotoxicosis). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanisms by which self-tolerance may fail, leading to autoimmune diseases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-step):**

1. **Central tolerance failure** (2 marks)
   - Negative selection in thymus depends on **AIRE**-driven ectopic expression of tissue-specific antigens in mTECs.
   - Mutations in *AIRE* gene → APECED (polyendocrine autoimmune syndrome).
   - Autoreactive T cells escape to periphery.

2. **Peripheral tolerance failure** (2 marks)
   - Reduced **Treg (CD4⁺CD25⁺FoxP3⁺)** function.
   - Defective **CTLA-4 / PD-1 / Fas-FasL** checkpoint signaling.
   - Anergic cells become activated upon inflammation.

3. **Triggering mechanisms** (3 marks)
   - Molecular mimicry (e.g., rheumatic fever).
   - Sequestered antigen release (e.g., sympathetic ophthalmia).
   - Epitope spreading (MS, SLE).
   - Polyclonal B-cell activation (EBV).
   - Neo-antigen formation (drug-induced lupus).

**(b)** Classify autoimmune diseases. Describe the pathogenesis of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus with its diagnostic autoantibodies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (3 marks):**
- **Organ-specific** (e.g., Hashimoto's, Graves', Type-1 DM, MG).
- **Systemic** (e.g., SLE, RA, Scleroderma).
- **Intermediate/Mixed** (e.g., Primary biliary cirrhosis, Goodpasture's).

**SLE Pathogenesis (3 marks):**
Defective clearance of apoptotic debris → nuclear antigens exposed → loss of B-cell tolerance → IgG autoantibody production (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, anti-histone, anti-phospholipid) → circulating immune complexes → deposition in glomeruli, skin, joints, brain → classical complement activation (↓C3, ↓C4) → neutrophil-mediated tissue injury → lupus nephritis, malar rash, arthritis, serositis.

**Diagnostic Autoantibodies (1 mark):**
- **ANA** (screening, 95–98% sensitive).
- **Anti-dsDNA** (specific, correlates with renal disease).
- **Anti-Sm** (highly specific).
- **Anti-histone** (drug-induced SLE).
- **Anti-cardiolipin** (antiphospholipid syndrome).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast Hashimoto's thyroiditis and Graves' disease in terms of autoantibody, pathogenesis, and clinical outcome. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Tabular Comparison + Explanation):**

| **Feature** | **Hashimoto's** | **Graves'** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Autoantibody | Anti-TPO, Anti-Tg | TSI (anti-TSHR) |
| Hypersensitivity | Type II + IV | Type V (stimulatory) |
| Pathogenesis | Antibody + CTL destruction of follicular cells | Stimulatory Ab mimicking TSH |
| Hormones | ↓ T3/T4, ↑ TSH | ↑ T3/T4, ↓ TSH |
| Gland histology | Atrophy, lymphoid follicles, Hürthle cells | Diffuse hyperplasia, scalloped colloid |
| Clinical | Hypothyroidism, myxedema, goiter | Thyrotoxicosis, exophthalmos, pretibial myxedema |
| HLA | DR5, DR3 | DR3, B8 |
| Treatment | Levothyroxine | Anti-thyroid drugs (methimazole), RAI |

Both are organ-specific thyroid AIDs but **opposite in clinical outcome** — Hashimoto's destroys the gland (hypothyroidism), Graves' overstimulates it (hyperthyroidism). **(7 marks: 3 for comparison + 2 pathogenesis + 2 clinical correlation)**

**(b)** Describe the pathogenesis and key autoantibodies of Rheumatoid Arthritis. Add a note on the role of HLA-DR4. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Pathogenesis (4 marks):**
1. **Initiation:** Smoking / periodontal disease → PAD enzymes → **citrullination** of arginine residues on self-proteins (e.g., vimentin, fibrinogen).
2. **Immune recognition:** **HLA-DR4 "shared epitope"** presents citrullinated peptides → CD4⁺ T cell activation.
3. **B-cell help:** IgG **anti-CCP** produced; **RF (IgM anti-IgG Fc)** also generated → immune complexes.
4. **Synovitis:** Th17 + synovial fibroblast activation → cytokine storm (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-17).
5. **Pannus formation:** Angiogenesis + immune cell infiltrate → invasive granulation tissue.
6. **Joint destruction:** RANKL → osteoclast activation → bone erosion; MMPs → cartilage degradation.

**Key Autoantibodies (2 marks):**
- **Anti-CCP** (95% specific, early marker).
- **Rheumatoid Factor (RF)** (IgM anti-IgG; 70–80% sensitive).

**HLA-DR4 Role (1 mark):**
The "shared epitope" (QKRAA motif in DRβ chain) preferentially binds citrullinated peptides, breaking tolerance and conferring the strongest known genetic risk for RA (relative risk ~4–6×).

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing hypersensitivity types** — many students write "Type IV" for SLE. Correct answer is **Type III (immune complex)**; T cells are involved only secondarily.
> 2. **Mixing up Hashimoto's and Graves'** — examiners frequently set this as a direct 7-mark comparison; mixing the autoantibody (Anti-TPO vs. TSI) is an automatic **3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Omitting HLA associations** — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests HLA linkage (DR3, DR4, DR5). A "complete" answer without HLA loses at least **1 mark** per disease.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify autoantibody isotype (IgG vs. IgM)** — RF is IgM, anti-TPO is IgG, TSI is IgG. Skipping this loses a mark in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why is it autoimmune?" connection** — merely listing autoantibodies without explaining the **mechanism of tolerance breakdown** loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning anti-CCP** in RA — older answers often cite only RF. KTU 2024 explicitly tests anti-CCP due to its higher specificity.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autoimmunity — High-Yield Recap**

- **Definition:** Immune response against self-antigens → tissue damage.
- **Central vs. Peripheral Tolerance:** Thymus/bone marrow deletion vs. Treg/CTLA-4/PD-1 in periphery.
- **Five triggers:** Mimicry, sequestered antigen, epitope spreading, polyclonal activation, neo-antigen.
- **Two master classes:** **Organ-specific** (Hashimoto's, Graves') vs. **Systemic** (SLE, RA).

**Hashimoto's:** Anti-TPO + Anti-Tg; **Type II + IV**; ↓T3/T4; DR5/DR3; Hürthle cells.
**Graves':** TSI (anti-TSHR); **Type V**; ↑T3/T4; DR3/B8; exophthalmos.
**SLE:** ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm; **Type III** immune complex; ↓C3/C4; DR2/DR3; 9:1 F:M.
**RA:** RF, anti-CCP; **Type III + IV**; HLA-DR4 shared epitope; pannus; RANKL-driven bone erosion.

**Mnemonic for SLE autoantibodies = "DNA-SHARP":** **D**sDNA, **N**ucleosome, **A**NA, **S**m, **H**istone, **A**nti-phospholipid, **R**o/SSA, **P**rotein P.

**Mnemonic for RA: "FACT-CCP"** — **F**ibroblast, **A**nti-CCP, **C**ollagen II, **T**NF storm, **C**D4 Th17, **P**annus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transplantation: Terminology, Auto Graft, Isograft, Allograft, Xenograft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transplantation: Terminology, Auto Graft, Isograft, Allograft, Xenograft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Transplantation** is the medical, surgical, or experimental procedure of transferring living cells, tissues, or organs from a donor site to a recipient site within the same individual or between individuals, with the primary immunological challenge being the recognition and acceptance/rejection of the transplanted material by the host immune system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A successful transplant requires immunological compatibility between donor and recipient, primarily governed by the **Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC)** antigens. In humans, this is termed the **Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA)** system.

### 1.2 Foundational Terminology of Grafts

The classification of grafts is based on the **genetic relationship** between the donor and the recipient. This is one of the **highest-yield topics** for KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Rule:** The closer the genetic relationship between donor and recipient, the lower the likelihood of immunological rejection.

| Suffix Used | Etymology | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **-graft** | Old English *graeft* | A piece of living tissue transplanted |
| **Auto-** | Greek *autos* | Self |
| **Iso-** | Greek *isos* | Equal / Identical |
| **Allo-** | Greek *allos* | Other (same species, different genetics) |
| **Xeno-** | Greek *xenos* | Foreign / Stranger (different species) |

### 1.3 The Four Canonical Graft Types

#### 1.3.1 Autograft (Autologous Graft)
- **Definition:** Tissue transplanted from one site to another on the **same individual**.
- **Rejection Profile:** **No immunological rejection** (the host immune system recognizes the tissue as "self" due to identical MHC).
- **Examples:** Skin graft for burn patients, coronary artery bypass using the saphenous vein, bone marrow harvested and re-implanted in the same patient.

#### 1.3.2 Isograft (Syngraft / Isogeneic Graft)
- **Definition:** Tissue transplanted between two **genetically identical individuals** of the same species.
- **Rejection Profile:** **No immunological rejection** under normal circumstances (identical MHC haplotypes).
- **Examples:** Organ/tissue transplants between **monozygotic (identical) twins**, or between members of **inbred mouse strains** (e.g., BALB/c mice) used in laboratory research.

#### 1.3.3 Allograft (Homograft / Allogeneic Graft)
- **Definition:** Tissue transplanted between two **genetically non-identical individuals of the same species**.
- **Rejection Profile:** **Strong immunological rejection** likely unless immunosuppressive therapy is administered.
- **Examples:** Most human organ transplants — kidney, heart, liver, lung, and bone marrow transplants from HLA-matched (but not identical) donors.

#### 1.3.4 Xenograft (Heterograft / Xenogeneic Graft)
- **Definition:** Tissue transplanted between individuals of **different species**.
- **Rejection Profile:** **Very strong and rapid immunological rejection**; the foreign tissue is recognized aggressively by innate and adaptive immunity.
- **Examples:** Porcine (pig) heart valve replacements in humans, baboon-to-human bone marrow transplants attempted historically.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Neighborhood Security Analogy":** Imagine your immune system as a highly trained security team guarding your body (the neighborhood).
> 
> - **Autograft** = A resident moving from one room to another in their own house. Security recognizes them instantly — no alarm.
> - **Isograft** = An identical twin of the resident (same face, same ID card) moving in. Security has no way to tell them apart — no alarm.
> - **Allograft** = A cousin with a similar but distinctly different face moving in. Security gets suspicious — alarm rings; needs a special "visitor pass" (immunosuppressants).
> - **Xenograft** = A completely different species (say, a pet dog) trying to enter the house. Security panics — massive alarm, attack dogs released within minutes.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Quantitative Anchors

- **Standard HLA Matching Score:** In clinical kidney transplantation, a minimum of **6/6 HLA match** (HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-DR) is considered ideal.
- **Rejection Onset Window:** Hyperacute rejection begins within **minutes to hours**; acute rejection within **days to weeks**; chronic rejection occurs over **months to years**.
- **Clinical Graft Survival Benchmark:** A 1-year allograft survival rate of **>90%** for kidneys and **>85%** for hearts is the modern clinical standard under standard immunosuppression.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Genetic Basis of Graft Acceptance — The Histocompatibility Principle

The success or failure of any graft is governed by **histocompatibility antigens** — cell-surface glycoproteins encoded by a cluster of genes called the **Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC)**.

**In Humans:** The MHC is called the **HLA complex**, located on the short arm of **chromosome 6 (6p21.3)**. It is the **most polymorphic gene system in the human genome**, with hundreds of known alleles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law of Transplantation (Snell, 1948):** Grafts between individuals identical at the MHC locus are accepted; grafts between individuals differing at the MHC locus are rejected. The rate and intensity of rejection are proportional to the **degree of genetic disparity**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Why Each Graft Behaves Differently

The reasoning follows a strict logical chain:

- **Step 1 — Antigen Presentation:** Every nucleated cell in the body displays MHC class I molecules on its surface. These molecules present peptide fragments to **CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes**.
- **Step 2 — Self vs. Non-Self Recognition:** T cells undergo **thymic education** (positive and negative selection). T cells that recognize self-MHC + self-peptide too strongly are deleted. The surviving T cells recognize self-MHC + foreign-peptide.
- **Step 3 — Direct Allorecognition Pathway:** In an allograft, recipient T cells can directly recognize **donor MHC molecules** (intact, on the surface of donor APCs) as foreign — this is one of the strongest known immune responses.
- **Step 4 — Indirect Allorecognition Pathway:** Recipient APCs process donor MHC peptides and present them on recipient MHC class II to **CD4+ helper T cells**, sustaining chronic inflammation.
- **Step 5 — Effector Mechanisms:** CD8+ T cells kill graft cells via perforin/granzyme; CD4+ Th1 cells activate macrophages; B cells produce **anti-donor antibodies**; complement cascade destroys vasculature.

### 2.3 Application of Logic to Each Graft Type

| Graft Type | MHC Disparity | T-cell Activation | Antibody Response | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Autograft** | None (identical self) | None | None | **Permanent acceptance** |
| **Isograft** | None (identical twin/inbred) | None | None | **Permanent acceptance** |
| **Allograft** | Partial (same species, different alleles) | Strong direct + indirect allorecognition | Anti-HLA antibodies form | **Rejection** unless immunosuppressed |
| **Xenograft** | Massive (interspecies) | Hyperacute + acute + chronic | Preformed natural antibodies (e.g., anti-Gal) | **Hyperacute rejection within minutes** |

### 2.4 The Four Classical Laws of Transplantation (Foundational for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> These laws were experimentally derived by **George Snell** using congenic mouse strains (Nobel Prize, 1980). They are extremely high-yield for short-answer questions.

1. **Law 1:** Grafts transplanted between members of the same inbred strain are accepted.
2. **Law 2:** Grafts transplanted between members of different inbred strains are rejected.
3. **Law 3:** Grafts transplanted from a donor of inbred strain A into the F1 hybrid offspring (A × B) are accepted, but grafts from A into the F1's parent (B) are rejected.
4. **Law 4:** Grafts transplanted from a donor of inbred strain A into the F2 generation (F1 × F1) show a pattern of partial acceptance/rejection based on Mendelian segregation of histocompatibility genes.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Students:** Transplantation immunology is qualitative rather than quantitative, but the following metrics and ratios are frequently tested as short-answer numerical concepts.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **HLA Mismatch Score** | $M = 6 - N_{match}$ | Where $N_{match}$ is the number of matched HLA loci (out of 6 standard). Lower $M$ = better outcome. |
| **Rejection Risk Index (Conceptual)** | $R \propto D_{MHC}$ | Rejection risk $R$ is proportional to MHC disparity $D_{MHC}$. |
| **Graft Survival Half-life** | $t_{1/2}$ | Time for 50% of grafts to fail. Modern renal allografts: $t_{1/2} \approx 10\text{--}15$ years. |
| **Allele Frequency (Hardy-Weinberg)** | $p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$ | Used to estimate HLA allele homozygosity/heterozygosity in populations. |
| **Hyperacute Rejection Onset** | $t < 24\text{ hours}$ | Preformed antibodies mediate this. |
| **Acute Rejection Onset** | $t \approx 1\text{ week to 6 months}$ | T-cell mediated. |
| **Chronic Rejection Onset** | $t > 6\text{ months}$ | Both antibody and cellular; leads to fibrosis. |
| **Complement-dependent Cytotoxicity (CDC) Crossmatch** | $\text{Positive} \Rightarrow \text{Forbidden graft}$ | Detects preformed donor-specific antibodies. |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Clinical Utility

- **Tissue Engineering:** Autografts are the gold standard in reconstructive surgery (e.g., bone grafts from the iliac crest).
- **Xenotransplantation Engineering:** Genetically modified pigs (e.g., **GTKO pigs** — $\alpha$-1,3-galactosyltransferase gene knocked out) are being engineered to reduce hyperacute xenograft rejection.
- **3D Bioprinting:** Uses autologous cells to print patient-specific grafts, bypassing the rejection problem entirely.
- **Pharmacology:** Calcineurin inhibitors (Cyclosporine A, Tacrolimus) and antiproliferatives (Mycophenolate) are the clinical workhorses that enforce allograft acceptance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Immunological Basis of Graft Rejection Classification

The classification of graft rejection into hyperacute, acute, and chronic forms is derived from the **kinetics of the immune response** and the **effector mechanism dominant at each stage**.

#### 3.1.1 Derivation of Rejection Onset Time Equation

Let us define the rejection onset time $t_r$ as a function of the dominant effector mechanism:

$$t_r = f\left( E_{type}, A_{pre}, M_{match} \right)$$

Where:
- $E_{type}$ = Effector mechanism type (preformed antibody, primary T-cell response, chronic inflammation)
- $A_{pre}$ = Presence of preformed anti-donor antibodies (binary: 1 = present, 0 = absent)
- $M_{match}$ = HLA match score (0 to 6)

For **hyperacute rejection**, the dominant effector is preformed antibody:

$$t_{hyperacute} = \frac{V_{complement}}{R_{ab}} \cdot \frac{1}{A_{pre}}$$

Where:
- $V_{complement}$ = Velocity of complement activation (arbitrary units)
- $R_{ab}$ = Rate of antibody binding
- This yields $t_{hyperacute} < 24$ hours when $A_{pre} = 1$.

For **acute rejection**, T-cell clonal expansion dominates:

$$t_{acute} = t_{clonal} + t_{effector}$$

$$t_{clonal} = \frac{\ln(2)}{r_{prolif}}$$

Where:
- $r_{prolif}$ = Rate of T-cell proliferation per day (typically 0.5–1.0 doublings/day)
- $t_{effector}$ = Time for effector function (cytokine release, cytotoxicity) ≈ 2–4 days
- This gives $t_{acute} \approx 7$ to $180$ days.

For **chronic rejection**, the dominant process is fibrosis and vasculopathy:

$$\frac{dF}{dt} = k \cdot C(t) \cdot A(t)$$

Where:
- $F$ = Fibrotic tissue mass
- $C(t)$ = Chronic inflammation level
- $A(t)$ = Cumulative antibody deposition
- $k$ = Pro-fibrotic constant
- Integration over months to years yields progressive graft loss.

#### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example: HLA Match Score Calculation

A donor-recipient pair has the following HLA typing:

| Locus | Donor | Recipient |
|---|---|---|
| HLA-A | A*02:01 | A*02:01 |
| HLA-B | B*07:02 | B*44:02 |
| HLA-DR | DRB1*15:01 | DRB1*04:01 |

**Step 1:** Identify matches.
- HLA-A: Donor (A*02:01) = Recipient (A*02:01) → **Match (1)**
- HLA-B: Donor (B*07:02) ≠ Recipient (B*44:02) → **Mismatch**
- HLA-DR: Donor (DRB1*15:01) ≠ Recipient (DRB1*04:01) → **Mismatch**

**Step 2:** Count total matches.

$$N_{match} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Calculate mismatch score.

$$M = 6 - N_{match} = 6 - 1 = 5$$

**Step 4:** Interpret.
A mismatch score of 5 indicates **high rejection risk**; this pair would not be a good clinical match.

#### 3.1.3 Derivation of Hyperacute Rejection in Xenografts

In discordant xenografts (e.g., pig → human), humans possess **preformed natural antibodies (nAbs)** against the **$\alpha$-Gal epitope** (Gal$\alpha$1-3Gal$\beta$1-4GlcNAc-R) on pig cells.

The reaction sequence proceeds as:

$$\text{IgM/IgG}_{\text{anti-Gal}} + \text{Gal}_{\text{donor}} \rightarrow \text{Immune Complex}$$

$$\text{Immune Complex} \rightarrow \text{C1q activation} \rightarrow \text{Cascade}$$

$$\text{C3b/C5b-9} \rightarrow \text{Endothelial damage} \rightarrow \text{Thrombosis}$$

Time to vascular occlusion:

$$t_{occlusion} \approx \frac{D_{vessel}}{v_{throm}}$$

Where $D_{vessel}$ is vessel diameter and $v_{throm}$ is thrombosis propagation velocity, typically yielding $t_{occlusion}$ in the range of **minutes**.

#### 3.1.4 Exhaustive Matrix: Graft Acceptance Probability vs. Relationship

Let $P_{accept}$ be the probability of graft acceptance without immunosuppression.

$$P_{accept} = P(\text{no T-cell activation}) + P(\text{no antibody response})$$

For each graft type, this simplifies:

| Graft Type | T-cell Activation Probability | Antibody Response Probability | $P_{accept}$ (no drugs) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Autograft | $0$ | $0$ | $1.00$ |
| Isograft | $0$ | $0$ | $1.00$ |
| Allograft (HLA-matched) | $\approx 0.1$ | $\approx 0.2$ | $\approx 0.70$ |
| Allograft (HLA-mismatched) | $\approx 0.8$ | $\approx 0.6$ | $\approx 0.08$ |
| Xenograft (concordant) | $\approx 0.95$ | $\approx 0.99$ | $\approx 0.0005$ |
| Xenograft (discordant) | $\approx 0.99$ | $\approx 1.00$ (preformed) | $\approx 0.0000$ |

### 3.2 Analytical Insights — "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why is xenograft rejection faster than allograft?** Because humans have **evolutionarily conserved** natural IgM antibodies against carbohydrate epitopes common in lower mammals (like $\alpha$-Gal). These antibodies exist *before* any exposure — hence hyperacute.
- **Why are isografts accepted?** Because the MHC haplotype is **completely identical**; T-cell receptors in the recipient have been thymically selected to recognize exactly these MHC molecules as "self."
- **Why does chronic rejection still occur under immunosuppression?** Because immunosuppression primarily suppresses T-cell activation, but **antibody-mediated rejection (AMR)** and **non-immune mechanisms** (ischemia-reperfusion injury, drug toxicity) continue to damage the graft slowly.
- **Why is HLA-DR matching more critical than HLA-A/B?** Because HLA-DR is a **Class II MHC**, which directly activates **CD4+ helper T cells**, the master regulators of the entire adaptive immune response.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Diagram — The Four Graft Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[GRAFT TRANSPLANTATION] --> B[Genetic Relationship Basis]
    B --> C[Same Individual]
    B --> D[Different Individual Same Species]
    B --> E[Different Species]

    C --> C1[AUTOGRAFT]
    C1 --> C1a[Self to Self]
    C1 --> C1b[No Rejection]
    C1 --> C1c[Example: Skin Burn Graft]

    D --> D1[Genetically Identical]
    D --> D2[Genetically Different]
    D1 --> D1A[ISOGRAFT]
    D1A --> D1Aa[Syngraft]
    D1A --> D1Ab[Identical Twins / Inbred Strains]
    D1A --> D1Ac[No Rejection]
    D2 --> D2A[ALLOGRAFT]
    D2A --> D2Aa[Homograft]
    D2A --> D2Ab[Most Clinical Transplants]
    D2A --> D2Ac[Rejection Needs Immunosuppression]

    E --> E1[XENOGRAFT]
    E1 --> E1a[Heterograft]
    E1 --> E1b[Discordant Pig to Human]
    E1 --> E1c[Concordant Baboon to Human]
    E1 --> E1d[Hyperacute Rejection]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style D1A fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style D2A fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style E1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
```

### 4.2 Graft Rejection Pathway — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Donor Antigen Exposure] --> B[Antigen Presentation]
    B --> C[Direct Allorecognition]
    B --> D[Indirect Allorecognition]
    C --> E[CD8 T cell Activation]
    D --> F[CD4 T cell Activation]
    E --> G[Cytotoxic Killing]
    F --> H[B cell Activation]
    H --> I[Anti Donor Antibody Production]
    I --> J[Complement Activation]
    J --> K[Endothelial Damage]
    G --> K
    K --> L[Graft Rejection]

    M[Time Axis] --> N[t less than 24 hours]
    M --> O[t equals 1 week to 6 months]
    M --> P[t greater than 6 months]
    N --> N1[Hyperacute Rejection]
    O --> O1[Acute Rejection]
    P --> P1[Chronic Rejection]
    N1 --> L
    O1 --> L
    P1 --> L

    style A fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style L fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style N1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style O1 fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style P1 fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Rejection Type Decision Matrix (Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Graft Transplanted] --> Q1{Are Preformed Antibodies Present}
    Q1 -->|YES| HYP[Hyperacute Rejection within minutes]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{HLA Match Adequate}
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{T cell Response Mounted}
    Q2 -->|YES| ACCEPT[Long Term Acceptance]
    Q3 -->|YES| ACUTE[Acute Rejection 1 week to 6 months]
    Q3 -->|NO| Q4{Antibody Mediated Damage Over Time}
    Q4 -->|YES| CHRONIC[Chronic Rejection greater than 6 months]
    Q4 -->|NO| ACCEPT2[Tolerance Achieved]

    style HYP fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style ACUTE fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style CHRONIC fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style ACCEPT fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style ACCEPT2 fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Immunological Players Summary Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    IMM[Immune System Response to Graft] --> INN[Innate Immunity]
    IMM --> ADAP[Adaptive Immunity]

    INN --> INN1[Macrophages]
    INN --> INN2[NK Cells]
    INN --> INN3[Complement System]
    INN --> INN4[Dendritic Cells]

    ADAP --> ADAP1[CD4 T Helper cells]
    ADAP --> ADAP2[CD8 Cytotoxic T cells]
    ADAP --> ADAP3[B cells and Antibodies]
    ADAP --> ADAP4[Regulatory T cells]

    INN1 --> REJ[Graft Rejection]
    INN2 --> REJ
    INN3 --> REJ
    INN4 --> REJ
    ADAP1 --> REJ
    ADAP2 --> REJ
    ADAP3 --> REJ
    ADAP4 --> TOL[Immunological Tolerance]

    style IMM fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style REJ fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style TOL fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between autograft, isograft, allograft, and xenograft with one example each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Autograft:** Tissue transplanted from one site to another on the **same individual** (e.g., skin graft for burn injury).
- **Isograft (Syngraft):** Tissue transplanted between **genetically identical individuals** of the same species (e.g., kidney transplant between monozygotic twins).
- **Allograft (Homograft):** Tissue transplanted between **genetically non-identical individuals of the same species** (e.g., cadaveric kidney transplant between unrelated donors).
- **Xenograft (Heterograft):** Tissue transplanted between individuals of **different species** (e.g., pig heart valve replacement in human). *(3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** What are the four classical laws of transplantation as proposed by Snell?

**Model Answer:**
George Snell formulated the four laws of transplantation in 1948 using congenic mouse strains:
1. Grafts within the same inbred strain are accepted.
2. Grafts between different inbred strains are rejected.
3. F1 hybrid offspring accept grafts from either parent strain but reject grafts from F2 generations.
4. F2 generation grafts show Mendelian segregation of histocompatibility genes, producing partial acceptance/rejection patterns.
These laws established that graft rejection is governed by heritable genetic loci — the MHC. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Classify the different types of grafts based on genetic relationship between donor and recipient. Explain the immunological basis of acceptance for each type. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe in detail the mechanisms involved in hyperacute, acute, and chronic graft rejection. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) Classification of Grafts and Immunological Basis:**

Grafts are classified into four types based on genetic relationship:

**Autograft:** Transfer of tissue within the same individual (e.g., skin graft). Since the recipient's immune system recognizes the MHC molecules as identical self-antigens (educated during thymic selection), **no T-cell activation occurs** and **no antibody response is mounted**. Result: **Permanent acceptance without immunosuppression.**

**Isograft (Syngraft):** Transfer between genetically identical individuals (monozygotic twins or inbred mouse strains). MHC haplotypes are 100% identical, so the recipient T-cell repertoire does not detect any foreign antigens. Result: **Permanent acceptance without immunosuppression.**

**Allograft (Homograft):** Transfer between genetically non-identical members of the same species. The donor expresses different MHC alleles than the recipient. Recipient T cells recognize donor MHC directly via the **direct allorecognition pathway**, leading to strong CD8+ cytotoxic responses and CD4+ helper responses via the indirect pathway. **Rejection is the default outcome**; immunosuppression is mandatory.

**Xenograft (Heterograft):** Transfer between different species. The recipient has **preformed natural antibodies** (e.g., anti-Gal IgM in humans reacting to pig tissues) that activate complement immediately on contact. **Hyperacute rejection** occurs within minutes to hours.

The **Law of Transplantation** states that the rate and intensity of rejection are proportional to the MHC disparity between donor and recipient. *(7 marks)*

**(b) Mechanisms of Graft Rejection:**

Graft rejection is classified by **time of onset** and **dominant effector mechanism**:

**1. Hyperacute Rejection (within minutes to 24 hours):**
This is mediated by **preformed recipient antibodies** against donor antigens (e.g., anti-HLA antibodies from prior transfusions, pregnancies, or previous transplants; anti-Gal antibodies in xenografts). The mechanism involves:
- Antibody binding to donor vascular endothelium
- Activation of the **classical complement pathway** (C1q → C3 → C5b-9 MAC formation)
- Endothelial cell damage, platelet aggregation, and **diffuse intravascular thrombosis**
- The graft becomes mottled, cyanotic, and non-functional within minutes
- **Prevention:** Pre-transplant **crossmatch testing** to detect donor-specific antibodies

**2. Acute Rejection (1 week to 6 months):**
This is primarily **T-cell mediated** and has two subtypes:
- **Acute Cellular Rejection:** CD8+ T cells directly kill graft parenchymal cells; CD4+ Th1 cells recruit macrophages. Histology shows lymphocytic infiltrates.
- **Acute Humoral/Antibody-Mediated Rejection (AMR):** Recipient develops new anti-donor antibodies that bind graft endothelium, activating complement (C4d deposition is the diagnostic marker).
- **Treatment:** High-dose corticosteroids, anti-thymocyte globulin, plasmapheresis.

**3. Chronic Rejection (months to years):**
A slowly progressive form with both immune and non-immune components:
- **Immune mechanisms:** Repeated episodes of low-grade cellular and humoral attack; CD4+ Th1 → macrophage activation → cytokine release (TGF-β, PDGF)
- **Non-immune mechanisms:** Ischemia-reperfusion injury, drug toxicity (cyclosporine nephrotoxicity), hyperlipidemia
- **Pathology:** **Vascular fibrosis (graft vasculopathy)**, interstitial fibrosis, chronic inflammation
- **Outcome:** Progressive organ dysfunction; retransplantation is the only definitive therapy

The three types together demonstrate that graft rejection is a **spectrum of immune responses** whose kinetics depend on whether the response is pre-existing (antibody), primary (T-cell), or chronic (fibrotic). *(7 marks)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the genetic basis of graft rejection. Discuss the role of HLA matching in clinical transplantation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the mechanism of xenograft rejection with a focus on the hyperacute response, and discuss modern strategies (such as GTKO pigs) to overcome it. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — Question B

**(a) Genetic Basis and HLA Matching:**

The genetic basis of graft rejection lies in the **Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC)** — a cluster of genes encoding cell-surface glycoproteins that present peptide antigens to T cells. In humans, this is the **HLA complex** located on chromosome 6p21.3.

**Key Properties of HLA:**
- **Most polymorphic gene system** known in humans — over 25,000 HLA alleles have been identified.
- **Codominantly expressed** — both maternal and paternal alleles are expressed on every nucleated cell.
- **Class I (HLA-A, -B, -C):** Expressed on all nucleated cells; present endogenous peptides to CD8+ T cells.
- **Class II (HLA-DP, -DQ, -DR):** Expressed on professional APCs (dendritic cells, macrophages, B cells); present exogenous peptides to CD4+ T cells.

**Mechanism:** T-cell receptors recognize peptide-MHC complexes. When donor MHC molecules differ from recipient (even by a single amino acid), the recipient T cells can recognize them as foreign via the **direct allorecognition pathway** (donor APCs directly stimulating recipient T cells in lymphoid organs of the recipient).

**HLA Matching in Clinical Practice:**
For renal transplantation, the standard match score considers **6 antigens** (HLA-A × 2 alleles, HLA-B × 2 alleles, HLA-DR × 2 alleles). The hierarchy of importance:
- **HLA-DR matching is most critical** (Class II; activates CD4+ helper T cells, the master regulators).
- **HLA-B matching** is next most critical.
- **HLA-A matching** is least critical but still relevant.

A **6/6 match** (zero mismatch) gives the best long-term graft survival; a **0/6 match** (complete mismatch) carries the highest rejection risk. Modern immunosuppression has reduced the penalty for mismatch, but matched grafts still survive longer. *(7 marks)*

**(b) Xenograft Rejection and GTKO Pig Strategy:**

Xenografts face a **multi-layered immune barrier** that is even stronger than allograft rejection.

**Hyperacute Xenograft Rejection:**
Humans (and Old World monkeys) lack the **$\alpha$-1,3-galactosyltransferase enzyme** due to an inactivating mutation in the GGTA1 gene. Consequently, humans produce **natural IgM antibodies** against the **$\alpha$-Gal epitope (Gal$\alpha$1-3Gal$\beta$1-4GlcNAc-R)** which is abundantly expressed on pig cells. Upon transplantation:

- **Step 1:** Preformed anti-Gal IgM binds Gal epitopes on pig vascular endothelium
- **Step 2:** Classical complement pathway is activated (C1q → C4 → C3 → C5b-9 MAC)
- **Step 3:** Endothelial damage exposes subendothelial matrix → platelet aggregation
- **Step 4:** Intravascular thrombosis occludes the graft vasculature within minutes

**Other Xenograft Barriers (beyond hyperacute):**
- **Acute vascular (delayed) xenograft rejection:** Occurs within days; NK cells and macrophages infiltrate.
- **Cellular xenograft rejection:** T cells and B cells respond vigorously to xenogeneic MHC.
- **Physiological incompatibility:** Differences in complement regulatory proteins (e.g., pig CD46, CD55 may not adequately regulate human complement).

**Modern Strategy — GTKO Pigs:**
The most successful genetic engineering strategy has been the creation of **$\alpha$-1,3-galactosyltransferase knockout (GTKO) pigs** by somatic cell nuclear transfer and homologous recombination. By deleting the GGTA1 gene:
- Pig cells no longer express $\alpha$-Gal epitopes
- Hyperacute rejection is dramatically reduced
- Further "multitransgenic" pigs have been engineered expressing human complement regulatory proteins (CD46, CD55, CD59), anticoagulant proteins (thrombomodulin, CD39), and additional knockouts of other xenoantigens (Neu5Gc, SDa)

These advances are bringing **clinical xenotransplantation** closer to reality, with recent FDA-approved trials of genetically modified pig kidney and heart transplants in human patients (2024). *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing "isograft" when the question states "between members of the same species." Isograft specifically requires **genetically identical** individuals (e.g., identical twins) — not just same species.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing "allograft" terminology. Some textbooks use "homograft" interchangeably; KTU examiners accept both. Do not lose marks by arguing against accepted synonyms.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to mention the **time frame** for each rejection type. Examiners specifically allocate marks for distinguishing hyperacute (< 24 hours), acute (1 week–6 months), and chronic (> 6 months).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** For HLA matching questions, students often forget that **haplotype inheritance** is co-dominant. Always specify that both alleles at each locus matter.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In xenograft questions, students frequently omit the role of **preformed natural antibodies** and **complement** — these are the core mechanism of hyperacute rejection and MUST be mentioned.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

- **Four graft types:** Autograft, Isograft (Syngraft), Allograft (Homograft), Xenograft (Heterograft).
- **No rejection grafts:** Autograft and Isograft (no MHC disparity).
- **Rejection-prone grafts:** Allograft (clinical reality) and Xenograft (most aggressive).
- **Synonyms to remember:**
    - Isograft = Syngraft
    - Allograft = Homograft
    - Xenograft = Heterograft
- **HLA location:** Chromosome 6p21.3 in humans.
- **HLA hierarchy of importance in matching:** **DR > B > A**.
- **Rejection timing summary:** Hyperacute (< 24 h, preformed antibody) | Acute (1 week – 6 months, T cell) | Chronic (> 6 months, fibrosis).
- **Direct allorecognition:** Donor APCs → recipient T cells (strongest signal).
- **Indirect allorecognition:** Recipient APCs process donor antigens → present to CD4+ T cells.
- **Xenograft key molecule:** $\alpha$-Gal epitope; preformed human anti-Gal IgM causes hyperacute rejection.
- **GTKO pig:** Genetically engineered pig lacking $\alpha$-1,3-galactosyltransferase; reduces hyperacute xenograft rejection.
- **Snell's Laws:** Four classical laws of transplantation (1948).
- **Crossmatch test:** Mandatory pre-transplant test to detect preformed donor-specific antibodies.
- **C4d deposition:** Diagnostic marker of antibody-mediated rejection (AMR).
- **Calcineurin inhibitors:** Cyclosporine A, Tacrolimus — mainstay immunosuppressants blocking IL-2 transcription.
- **Anatomic site of graft destruction:** Primarily the **vasculature** (endothelium) — this is why hyperacute rejection manifests as vascular thrombosis.
- **GvHD (Graft-versus-Host Disease):** Especially relevant in bone marrow transplants — donor T cells attack recipient tissues (skin, gut, liver).
- **Privileged sites:** Anatomical locations where grafts may survive with reduced rejection (e.g., cornea, cartilage, testes — due to blood-tissue barriers and limited lymphatic drainage).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Immunological Basis of Transplantation Reactions (Role of T & B Cells)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Immunological Basis of Transplantation Reactions (Role of T & B Cells)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transplantation Immunology** is the branch of immunobiology that studies the immune responses elicited when living cells, tissues, or organs (collectively called **grafts** or **transplants**) are transferred from a donor to a recipient (host). The central phenomenon is **allograft rejection** — a destructive immune response mounted by the recipient's immune system against the foreign Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) antigens expressed on the donor cells.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, transplantation reactions are classified under **Type IV (cell-mediated) and Type II (antibody-mediated) hypersensitivity reactions**, governed primarily by the **Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA) system** located on chromosome 6.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The immune recognition of foreign grafts is fundamentally a problem of distinguishing *self* from *non-self* MHC molecules. Both **T lymphocytes** (cell-mediated arm) and **B lymphocytes** (humoral arm) play decisive, time-dependent roles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **high-security apartment complex** (your body) protected by an elite guard force (your immune system). Every resident carries a **unique photo ID card** (MHC/HLA molecules). 

- Now, suppose a new tenant moves in (a **transplant**). 
- The guards immediately check the new tenant's ID. 
- If the ID doesn't match the building's records, the guards (T cells & B cells) raise an alarm and either **physically evict** the intruder (CD8+ cytotoxic T cells) or **mark them for destruction with flyers** (antibodies from B cells).
- This "eviction process" is what we clinically call **graft rejection**.

The strength of the guard's response depends on **how different the IDs are** (degree of HLA mismatch) and **how alert the guards are** (immune status of the recipient).

### 1.3 Classification of Transplants (Grafts)

The KTU syllabus categorizes grafts based on the **genetic relationship** between donor and recipient:

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Canonical Graft Types (Must Memorize for KTU Exams):**

| Graft Type | Genetic Relationship | Example | Rejection Risk |
|------------|----------------------|---------|----------------|
| **Autograft** | Same individual (self) | Skin graft from thigh to face in burn patients | **None** |
| **Isograft (Syngeneic)** | Genetically identical individuals | Graft between monozygotic (identical) twins | **None** |
| **Allograft (Homograft)** | Same species, genetically different | Kidney transplant between unrelated humans | **Yes — requires immunosuppression** |
| **Xenograft (Heterograft)** | Different species | Pig heart valve → human recipient | **Very high — hyperacute rejection** |

### 1.4 The HLA System — The Molecular Trigger

The **Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA)** complex encodes the most polymorphic genes in the human genome. These are classified into:
- **Class I MHC:** HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C — expressed on **all nucleated cells**.
- **Class II MHC:** HLA-DR, HLA-DP, HLA-DQ — expressed on **Antigen Presenting Cells (APCs)** like dendritic cells, macrophages, B cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric for KTU:** HLA matching is graded by the number of mismatched alleles. **A 6/6 match** (HLA-A, -B, -DR all matched) is considered the gold standard for kidney transplantation. The half-life of graft survival drops **exponentially** with each additional mismatch.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pathways of Allograft Recognition

The recipient's T cells recognize donor MHC antigens via **two principal pathways**, and a third supplementary pathway:

#### A. Direct Pathway of Allorecognition
- Recipient **CD8+ T cells** directly recognize **intact donor MHC Class I** molecules on the surface of donor APCs that have migrated from the graft (called "passenger leukocytes").
- This pathway is the **dominant mechanism in acute rejection** and is responsible for the high frequency of alloreactive T cells (≈ 1–10% of total T cells, compared to <0.01% for conventional antigens).

#### B. Indirect Pathway of Allorecognition
- Donor MHC antigens are **shed**, taken up, processed, and presented by **recipient APCs** on **recipient MHC Class II** molecules.
- This activates **recipient CD4+ T helper cells**, which then orchestrate a broader immune response (B cell help, macrophage activation, cytokine release).
- This pathway is **dominant in chronic rejection** and is analogous to a normal immune response to an exogenous antigen.

#### C. Semi-Direct Pathway
- Recipient APCs acquire **intact donor MHC molecules** through cell-cell contact or exosome transfer and present them directly to T cells.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Mechanism of Acute Cellular Rejection

The cellular cascade proceeds in **five sequential steps**:

1. **Antigen Recognition:** Recipient T cells encounter donor MHC via direct or indirect pathway → **Signal 1** (TCR-MHC engagement) is delivered.
2. **Costimulation:** Donor APCs express B7-1/B7-2, which bind CD28 on T cells → **Signal 2** (costimulation) is delivered. *Absence of Signal 2 leads to T cell anergy.*
3. **Clonal Expansion:** Activated T cells proliferate (IL-2 driven autocrine signaling) and differentiate into effector cells.
4. **Effector Phase:** 
   - **CD8+ T cells** migrate to the graft and kill donor cells via **perforin/granzyme pathway** and **Fas-FasL interactions**.
   - **CD4+ T cells** (Th1) release **IFN-γ** and **TNF-α**, activating macrophages.
5. **Graft Destruction:** Necrosis of graft tissue, vascular damage, and infiltration by lymphocytes.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Mechanism of Antibody-Mediated (Humoral) Rejection

1. **Sensitization:** Recipient B cells encounter donor MHC antigens (especially on endothelial cells of graft vasculature).
2. **T Cell Help:** Activated CD4+ T helper cells (Th2) provide cognate help to B cells via CD40L-CD40 interaction and cytokines (IL-4, IL-5, IL-6).
3. **Class Switching & Affinity Maturation:** B cells in germinal centers produce **high-affinity IgG antibodies** (especially **anti-HLA Class I antibodies**).
4. **Effector Mechanisms:** Antibodies bind graft endothelium → activate **complement cascade (C3b, C5b-C9 MAC)** → recruit neutrophils → cause vasculitis and thrombosis.

### 2.4 Clinical Classification of Rejection Reactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Table — Must memorize timing and mechanism:**

| Rejection Type | Onset | Primary Mechanism | Histology | Treatment |
|----------------|-------|-------------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Hyperacute** | Minutes to hours (intraoperative) | Pre-existing recipient antibodies + complement (Type II HS) | Fibrinoid necrosis, thrombosis, neutrophil infiltration | Graft removal (irreversible) |
| **Acute Cellular** | Days to months | CD8+ T cell mediated (Type IV HS) | Lymphocytic infiltrate, tubulitis | Pulse steroids, anti-thymocyte globulin |
| **Acute Humoral** | Days to months | Anti-donor antibodies + complement | C4d deposition in peritubular capillaries | Plasmapheresis, IVIG, Rituximab |
| **Chronic** | Months to years | Both cellular + humoral; indirect allorecognition | Vascular fibrosis, intimal thickening, atrophy | Supportive; retransplant |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Transplantation Kinetics

While transplantation immunology is qualitative, the following quantitative relationships are tested in KTU numericals:

| Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units / Interpretation |
|---------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Alloreactive T cell frequency** | $\approx 1\text{--}10\%$ of total T cells | Compare with $0.01\%$ for nominal antigens → $1000\times$ stronger |
| **HLA mismatch effect on survival** | $\Delta t_{1/2} = -k \cdot M$ | $M$ = number of mismatches; $k$ = decay constant (kidney ≈ 2.5 years/mismatch) |
| **Graft survival half-life** | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential decay model for chronic rejection |
| **Mixed Lymphocyte Reaction (MLR) stimulation index** | $SI = \dfrac{cpm_{\text{test}}}{cpm_{\text{control}}}$ | $SI > 3$ indicates significant alloreactivity |
| **Complement-dependent cytotoxicity (CDC) crossmatch** | \% Cell Death $= \dfrac{\text{Test lysis} - \text{Spontaneous}}{\text{Maximum} - \text{Spontaneous}} \times 100$ | $>20\%$ is considered positive (contraindication to transplant) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** Just as a power grid must synchronize **frequency and phase** before interconnecting two networks, donor and recipient HLA must "synchronize" to prevent the immune system from rejecting the graft. The tighter the synchronization, the longer the system runs stably.

### 2.6 Graft-Versus-Host Disease (GVHD) — A Special Case

**GVHD** occurs primarily in **bone marrow / hematopoietic stem cell transplants** where immunocompetent donor T cells are transferred into an **immunocompromised recipient**. The donor T cells recognize host tissues as foreign.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for GVHD Target Organs (3):** "**S**kin, **L**iver, **G**ut" (SLG).

The three requirements for GVHD (Billingham's Postulates):
1. The graft must contain **immunologically competent cells** (T cells).
2. The recipient must express **tissue antigens foreign to the donor**.
3. The recipient must be **unable to mount an effective immune response** against the graft.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Mechanistic Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Role of T Cells vs B Cells in Rejection

| Parameter | T Lymphocytes (Cellular Arm) | B Lymphocytes (Humoral Arm) |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Primary Effector** | CD4+ Th1, CD8+ Cytotoxic T cells | Plasma cells secreting IgG |
| **Antigen Recognized** | Processed peptides on MHC | Native conformational epitopes |
| **Recognition Pathway** | Direct (acute) & Indirect (chronic) | Mostly indirect (donor MHC shed → opsonized) |
| **Rejection Type Driven** | Acute cellular + chronic | Hyperacute, acute humoral, chronic |
| **Effector Molecules** | Perforin, granzymes, IFN-γ, TNF-α | Antibodies, complement (C3b, MAC) |
| **Kinetics** | Days to weeks (clonal expansion needed) | Minutes (preformed) to weeks (de novo) |
| **Histologic Hallmark** | Lymphocytic infiltration, tubulitis | C4d deposition, vasculitis |
| **Detected by** | MLR (Mixed Lymphocyte Reaction) | Crossmatch assays (CDC, Flow, Virtual) |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Allorecognition Probability

Although qualitative, KTU examiners sometimes ask students to estimate the **probability of finding an HLA-matched donor** in a population.

> **Step 1:** Define allele frequencies. Let the frequency of HLA-A*02:01 in a population be $p_1 = 0.25$ and HLA-A*24:02 be $p_2 = 0.20$.

> **Step 2:** By Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, the probability that a random individual expresses **homozygously** for HLA-A*02:01 is:

$$P(\text{A*02:01 / A*02:01}) = p_1^2 = (0.25)^2 = 0.0625$$

> **Step 3:** The probability of being **heterozygous** for these two alleles is:

$$P(\text{A*02:01 / A*24:02}) = 2 \cdot p_1 \cdot p_2 = 2 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.20 = 0.10$$

> **Step 4:** Summing the desired genotypes (assuming the recipient is heterozygous A*02:01/A*24:02):

$$P(\text{match at HLA-A}) = p_1^2 + 2p_1 p_2 = 0.0625 + 0.10 = 0.1625$$

> **Step 5:** Assuming independence between loci, and a similar match probability of $0.16$ at each of the 3 critical loci (HLA-A, -B, -DR), the probability of a **6/6 match** is:

$$P(\text{6/6 match}) = (0.16)^3 = 0.004096 \approx 0.41\%$$

> **Step 6:** Interpretation: In a pool of $N$ potential donors, the expected number of fully HLA-matched donors is:

$$E(\text{matches}) = N \times P(\text{6/6 match}) = 0.0041 \cdot N$$

For example, to find one 6/6 match, you need at minimum:

$$N = \dfrac{1}{0.0041} \approx 244 \text{ donors}$$

> **Step 7:** This is the mathematical basis for maintaining **large national bone marrow registries** (e.g., DKMS, NMDP) containing millions of donors.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Alloreactive T Cell Precursor Frequency

> **Step 1:** Normal antigen-specific T cell frequency ≈ 1 in $10^5$ to $10^6$ cells.

> **Step 2:** Alloreactive T cell frequency ≈ 1 in $10^2$ to $10^3$ cells.

> **Step 3:** Ratio of alloreactive to nominal reactive:

$$R = \dfrac{1/10^2}{1/10^5} = 10^3 = 1000\text{-fold higher}$$

> **Step 4:** This massive precursor frequency explains why **immunosuppression is mandatory** in clinical transplantation — the immune system has a pre-existing "standing army" ready to attack the graft.

### 3.4 Mechanistic Flow of Acute Humoral Rejection

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Donor endothelial cells express foreign HLA Class I} \\
&\quad \downarrow \text{(exposed to recipient circulation)} \\
&\text{Recipient B cell receptor (BCR) binds donor HLA} \\
&\quad \downarrow \text{(Signal 1: antigen)} \\
&\text{CD4+ Th cell provides cognate help via CD40L–CD40} \\
&\quad \downarrow \text{(Signal 2: costimulation)} \\
&\text{B cell class-switches to IgG anti-HLA antibody} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\text{Antibody binds graft endothelium} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\text{Complement activation (Classical Pathway: C1q → C4 → C3 → MAC)} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\text{MAC forms pores in endothelium → cell lysis} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\text{C3a, C5a recruit neutrophils → vasculitis} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\text{Platelet activation, thrombosis, fibrinoid necrosis} \\
&\quad \downarrow \\
&\textbf{Graft Loss}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pathways of Allograft Rejection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Donor Organ Transplanted into Recipient] --> B[Foreign HLA Antigens Detected]
    B --> C{Pathway of Recognition}
    C -->|Direct| D1[Recipient CD8+ T cells recognize Intact Donor MHC I on Passenger APCs]
    C -->|Indirect| D2[Recipient APCs process shed Donor MHC, present on Recipient MHC II]
    C -->|Semi-Direct| D3[Recipient APCs acquire intact Donor MHC via cell contact]
    D1 --> E1[Acute Cellular Rejection]
    D2 --> E2[Chronic Rejection / CD4+ Help]
    D3 --> E3[Mixed Response]
    E1 --> F1[CD8+ T cell killing via Perforin and Granzyme]
    E1 --> F2[CD4+ Th1 release IFN gamma and TNF alpha]
    E2 --> G1[B Cell Activation and Class Switching]
    G1 --> G2[Anti-HLA IgG Antibodies Produced]
    E3 --> F1
    E3 --> G2
    F1 --> H[Graft Tissue Necrosis]
    F2 --> H
    G2 --> I[Complement Activation C1q to C3b to MAC]
    I --> J[Endothelial Cell Lysis and Vasculitis]
    J --> K[Graft Failure]
    H --> K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Types of Rejection by Time & Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Acute_Reactions [Time-Based Rejection Classification]
        A1[Hyperacute: Minutes to Hours] --> A2[Preformed Anti-HLA Antibodies + Complement]
        A2 --> A3[Graft Thrombosis and Loss]
    end
    subgraph Delayed_Reactions [Subacute and Chronic]
        B1[Acute Cellular: Days to Months] --> B2[CD8+ and CD4+ T cell infiltration]
        B2 --> B3[Reversible with Immunosuppression]
        C1[Acute Humoral: Days to Months] --> C2[De Novo Anti-HLA Antibodies + C4d deposit]
        C2 --> C3[Requires Plasmapheresis]
        D1[Chronic: Months to Years] --> D2[Indirect Allorecognition dominant]
        D2 --> D3[Vascular Fibrosis and Atrophy]
    end
    subgraph GVHD [Special Case]
        E1[Graft Versus Host Disease] --> E2[Donor T cells attack Host Skin Liver Gut]
        E2 --> E3[SLG Triad]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Functional Block Architecture: Pre-Transplant Crossmatching Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StepA[Step 1: Recipient Serum + Donor Lymphocytes] --> StepB{Step 2: Complement Dependent Cytotoxicity CDC Crossmatch}
    StepB -->|Positive| Out1[STOP: Contraindication to Transplant]
    StepB -->|Negative| StepC[Step 3: Flow Cytometry Crossmatch for IgG]
    StepC -->|Positive| Out1
    StepC -->|Negative| StepD[Step 4: Virtual Crossmatch using Single Antigen Bead Assay SAB]
    StepD -->|Donor Specific Antibody DSA present| Out1
    StepD -->|No DSA| StepE[Step 5: Proceed to Transplant with Immunosuppression Protocol]
    StepE --> StepF[Step 6: Post Transplant Monitoring of DSA and Graft Function]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparative Map: T Cell vs B Cell Roles

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TCells [T Lymphocyte Roles]
        T1[CD8+ Cytotoxic T cells: Direct killing of donor cells]
        T2[CD4+ Th1 cells: Macrophage activation via IFN gamma]
        T3[CD4+ Th17 cells: Neutrophil recruitment in acute rejection]
        T4[Treg cells: Immunological tolerance when dominant]
    end
    subgraph BCells [B Lymphocyte Roles]
        B1[Plasma cells: Secrete anti-HLA IgG antibodies]
        B2[Memory B cells: Enable accelerated secondary rejection]
        B3[B regulatory cells Bregs: Suppress immunity via IL-10]
    end
    T1 --> Reject[Graft Rejection]
    T2 --> Reject
    T3 --> Reject
    T4 --> Accept[Allograft Acceptance / Tolerance]
    B1 --> Reject
    B2 --> Reject
    B3 --> Accept
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The immune response to a transplant can be modeled as a **dual-input control system** — T cells constitute the **fast feedback loop** (direct killing), while B cells form the **adaptive feedforward loop** (antibody-mediated delayed but stronger response). Achieving tolerance is essentially the problem of **stabilizing a multi-input, non-linear dynamic system** at a new equilibrium.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between the direct and indirect pathways of allorecognition. Which pathway is dominant in chronic rejection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Direct pathway:** Recipient T cells recognize **intact donor MHC molecules** on the surface of donor APCs (passenger leukocytes). Dominant in **acute rejection**. (1.5 marks)
- **Indirect pathway:** Donor MHC antigens are **processed** by recipient APCs and presented as peptides on **recipient MHC Class II** to recipient CD4+ T cells. Dominant in **chronic rejection**. (1.5 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*List the four types of grafts with one example each. Which type has the highest risk of hyperacute rejection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Autograft** — Skin graft from thigh to face. (0.75 marks)
2. **Isograft** — Kidney transplant between identical twins. (0.75 marks)
3. **Allograft** — Kidney transplant between unrelated humans. (0.75 marks)
4. **Xenograft** — Pig heart valve to human. (0.75 marks)
- **Highest hyperacute rejection risk → Xenograft** (due to preformed natural antibodies against α-Gal epitope). (overlapping within the 4th point above)

---

### PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Cellular Mechanisms in Rejection*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **mechanism of acute cellular rejection** mediated by T lymphocytes, highlighting the roles of **direct and indirect allorecognition** and the **dual signal hypothesis**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **pathogenesis of Graft-Versus-Host Disease (GVHD)**. List the three Billingham's postulates and the three target organs. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

> **Step 1 (1 mark): Introduction.** Acute cellular rejection is a **Type IV (delayed-type) hypersensitivity reaction** occurring within days to months post-transplant. The central players are **CD8+ cytotoxic T cells** and **CD4+ helper T cells** of the recipient.

> **Step 2 (1.5 marks): Direct Pathway.** Recipient T cells encounter **intact donor MHC molecules** on donor APCs (passenger leukocytes) that have migrated out of the graft. CD8+ T cells recognize donor MHC I + peptide; CD4+ T cells recognize donor MHC II + peptide. This is the **dominant mechanism in the early post-transplant period** and explains the **1–10% alloreactive T cell frequency**.

> **Step 3 (1.5 marks): Indirect Pathway.** Donor MHC antigens are **shed**, internalized, processed by recipient APCs, and presented as peptide fragments on **recipient MHC Class II**. This activates **CD4+ T cells** and is the **dominant mechanism in chronic rejection**.

> **Step 4 (1.5 marks): Dual Signal Hypothesis.** 
> - **Signal 1:** TCR–MHC-peptide engagement (antigen-specific).
> - **Signal 2:** Costimulation via **B7 (CD80/86) on APC** binding to **CD28 on T cell**. 
> - **Absence of Signal 2** → T cell **anergy** (peripheral tolerance mechanism exploited by CTLA-4-Ig / Abatacept).

> **Step 5 (1.5 marks): Effector Mechanisms.** Activated T cells proliferate (IL-2 autocrine), differentiate, and migrate to the graft. **CD8+ T cells** kill donor cells via **perforin/granzyme** and **Fas-FasL** pathways. **CD4+ Th1 cells** release **IFN-γ and TNF-α**, activating macrophages that cause DTH-like damage.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

> **Step 1 (1 mark): Definition.** GVHD is an immune response where **immunocompetent donor T cells** in the transplanted graft recognize the **recipient's tissues as foreign** and mount an attack.

> **Step 2 (2 marks): Billingham's Three Postulates:**
> 1. The graft must contain **immunologically competent donor T cells**.
> 2. The recipient must express **tissue antigens (HLA) foreign to the donor**.
> 3. The recipient must be **immunocompromised** (unable to reject the graft).

> **Step 3 (2 marks): Pathogenesis.** Donor T cells in the bone marrow graft recognize host HLA → activate → proliferate → attack host tissues. Three sequential phases:
> - **Phase 1 (Afferent):** Tissue damage from conditioning regimen (chemo/radiation) releases **LPS, TNF-α**, activates host APCs.
> - **Phase 2 (Efferent):** Donor T cells proliferate and differentiate into **Th1 and CTL effectors**; release **IFN-γ, IL-1, TNF-α**.
> - **Phase 3 (Effector):** Cytokine-mediated cytotoxicity + direct T cell killing of host epithelial cells.

> **Step 4 (2 marks): Target Organs (SLG):**
> - **Skin:** Maculopapular rash, erythema, epidermal necrolysis.
> - **Liver:** Cholestasis, jaundice, elevated bilirubin.
> - **Gut (Gastrointestinal):** Diarrhea, abdominal pain, GI bleeding.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Humoral Mechanisms & Graft Types*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Classify the **four types of transplantation grafts** with suitable examples. Explain the **genetic basis** of graft acceptance and rejection with reference to the **HLA system**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **mechanism of hyperacute and chronic rejection**. Add a note on the **role of immunosuppressive therapy** in transplant survival. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

> **Step 1 (2 marks): Four Graft Types** *(tabulated as in Section 1.3 of these notes)*.

> **Step 2 (2 marks): Genetic Basis — HLA System.** HLA genes are located on the **short arm of chromosome 6 (6p21.3)**. They are the **most polymorphic genes in the human genome**, with hundreds of alleles at each locus. Each individual expresses a unique set, and unless donor and recipient are genetically identical (autograft/isograft), some HLA mismatch is inevitable.

> **Step 3 (1.5 marks): Rules of Graft Acceptance (Medawar's Laws of Transplantation):**
> - Grafts between inbred strains of animals are **accepted** (genetic identity).
> - Grafts between different strains are **rejected** (genetic disparity).
> - Rejection is **immunologically specific** (a graft from strain A primes the animal to reject strain A but not strain B grafts faster — **second-set rejection**).
> - Rejection is **systemic** (lymphocytes from a sensitized animal can transfer rejection to a naïve animal — **adoptive transfer**).

> **Step 4 (1.5 marks): HLA Matching in Clinical Practice.**
