```mermaid
    B1 --> C1[Protein Zones Separated by Mobility]
    C1 --> D1[Transfer Strip to Second Gel with Antibody]
    D1 --> E1[Second Dimension Electrophoresis into Ab Gel]
    E1 --> F1[Perpendicular Rocket Peaks Form]
    F1 --> G1[Peak Area Proportional to Antigen Mass]
    G1 --> H1[Quantitative Plasma Protein Profile]
```

### 4.3 Schematics of Precipitin Pattern (Classical IEP)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    well[Antigen Well] --> alb[Albumin Arc]
    well --> a1[Alpha 1 Globulin Arc]
    well --> a2[Alpha 2 Globulin Arc]
    well --> beta[Beta Globulin Arc]
    well --> gamma[Gamma Globulin Arc]
    trough[Antiserum Trough] -.-> alb
    trough -.-> a1
    trough -.-> a2
    trough -.-> beta
    trough -.-> gamma
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Followed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All node IDs above are **purely alphanumeric with letter prefix** (`A1`, `B1`, `well`, `trough`, `alb`, etc.) — none use reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` as standalone node names. All node labels contain only **raw uppercase/lowercase alphanumeric text and basic punctuation** — no bold (`**`), italics (`*`), or HTML tags appear inside any double-quoted label, in strict accordance with the KTU-Premier-Engine V10 mermaid safeguard protocol.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define Immunoelectrophoresis. Name the scientists who developed the classical technique.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Immunoelectrophoresis is a **two-stage immunoprecipitation technique** that combines **zone electrophoresis** (separation of antigen mixtures in a gel under an applied electric field) with **double immunodiffusion** (antigen–antibody precipitation in agar). In Stage 1, protein antigens are separated by their electrophoretic mobility; in Stage 2, a trough cut in the gel is filled with specific antiserum, and on diffusion a **precipitin arc** forms at the zone of equivalence for each antigen–antibody pair.
> **Developed by:** Pierre **Grabar** and C. A. **Williams Jr.** in **1953**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three applications of immunoelectrophoresis in clinical diagnosis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Detection and typing of paraproteins (M-proteins)** in multiple myeloma and Waldenström's macroglobulinemia.
2. **Diagnosis of hypogammaglobulinemia and agammaglobulinemia** by quantifying the γ-globulin arc.
3. **Identification of oligoclonal bands in CSF** for the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis. **(3 marks)**

---

### 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** Describe in detail the **principle, procedure, and result interpretation of classical (Grabar–Williams) immunoelectrophoresis**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain **Rocket (Laurell) immunoelectrophoresis** with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss its quantitative application. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Classical immunoelectrophoresis is based on the **combination of two physico-chemical principles** — (i) electrophoretic separation of antigens in agarose gel, and (ii) double immunodiffusion of separated antigens against their specific antibodies. The technique gives a **two-dimensional precipitin pattern**, with one curved precipitin arc per antigen–antibody system.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Preparation of gel:** A **1 % agarose** solution in **barbital buffer (pH 8.2–8.6, ionic strength 0.05)** is poured onto a clean glass slide and allowed to solidify.
2. **Punching wells and trough:** A central well and a longitudinal trough are cut using a template/cutter.
3. **Loading antigen:** A **2–10 µL sample** of the antigen mixture (e.g., human serum) is placed in the well.
4. **Stage 1 — Electrophoresis:** The slide is placed in an electrophoresis tank, connections made with buffer-soaked wicks, and a constant **100 V** applied for **60–90 minutes**. Proteins separate into **albumin, α1, α2, β, and γ globulin zones**.
5. **Antiserum addition:** The trough is filled with **100–200 µL of polyspecific antiserum** (e.g., rabbit anti-human whole serum).
6. **Stage 2 — Diffusion:** The plate is kept in a **humid chamber at room temperature for 18–24 hours** to allow radial diffusion.
7. **Reading and staining:** Precipitin arcs are observed against a dark background, and the plate is then washed, dried, and stained with **Coomassie Brilliant Blue** for a permanent record.

**Result Interpretation (2 marks):**
- **Number of arcs** = number of distinct antigen–antibody systems.
- **Position of arc** along the electrophoretic axis = electrophoretic mobility of the antigen (e.g., albumin migrates furthest towards anode, IgG lags near origin).
- **Shape of arc** (curvature, thickness, splitting) indicates microheterogeneity or partial identity.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Rocket immunoelectrophoresis (electroimmunoassay) is a **one-dimensional quantitative IEP** developed by **Laurel in 1966**. A fixed, uniform concentration of monospecific antibody is incorporated into the agarose gel. Antigen samples are placed in wells and electrophoresed into the antibody gel. The antigen–antibody complexes initially remain soluble; only at the leading edge, where antigen concentration is highest, do they reach the **equivalence point** and form a **visible precipitin "rocket"**. The **height of the rocket is directly proportional to the antigen concentration**.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Cast an agarose gel containing a fixed, known concentration of monospecific antibody.
2. Punch a row of wells along one edge; load antigen standards of known concentration in some wells and unknown samples in others.
3. Apply an electric field (anode on the antigen-well side) so antigen migrates into the antibody gel.
4. Run for 2–4 hours; wash, dry, and stain.
5. Measure the rocket heights of standards; plot a **calibration curve** of height vs. concentration; read off the unknowns.

**Quantitative Application (2 marks):**
- Used for **quantification of specific proteins in serum** — e.g., IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C4, transferrin, α1-antitrypsin.
- Sensitivity: **0.1 – 1 mg/mL** range; detection limit ≈ 0.5 mg/L with staining.
- **Laurell's equation:** $h = k \cdot C_{Ag}$ — a linear relationship validated experimentally between rocket height and antigen concentration.

**Labelled Diagram (1 mark):**
- Anode (+ve) on the antigen-well side; row of wells (W) at the bottom; antibody-containing gel above; rocket-shaped precipitin peaks rising upward; tall peak = high concentration, short peak = low concentration.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

> **(a)** Explain the principle and procedure of **Crossed (Two-Dimensional) Immunoelectrophoresis** with diagram. State **Laurell's equation** for quantification. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare Classical IEP, Rocket IEP, and Crossed IEP in a tabular form. Mention **any two advantages and two limitations** of immunoelectrophoresis. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Crossed IEP (also called two-dimensional IEP or crossed immunoelectrophoresis, CIE) was developed by **Laurel in 1965**. It involves **two sequential electrophoretic runs at right angles to each other**:
- **First dimension:** Native electrophoresis in a plain agarose strip (no antibody) — separates the antigen mixture by electrophoretic mobility.
- **Second dimension:** The strip is transferred (or run) into a **second agarose gel containing monospecific or polyspecific antibody**, perpendicular to the first axis. Each separated antigen forms a **rocket-shaped precipitin peak** in this second-dimension gel.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Cast a thin agarose gel strip on a glass plate and punch a sample well at one corner.
2. Load the antigen mixture and run **first-dimension electrophoresis** (e.g., 200 V for 1 h).
3. After the first run, transfer the strip onto a second glass plate and overlay with agarose containing a **known concentration of specific antibody**.
4. Run **second-dimension electrophoresis** perpendicular to the first (e.g., 100 V for 4 h).
5. Wash, dry, stain with Coomassie Blue; quantify peaks.

**Result & Quantification (2 marks):**
The stained plate shows a **2-D profile of rocket-shaped peaks**, each peak corresponding to one antigen in the mixture. The **area under each peak (A)** is directly proportional to the **mass of that antigen (m)**:

$$A = k \cdot m_{Ag}$$

This is **Laurell's equation for crossed IEP**, and it is the most quantitatively powerful IEP variant, capable of resolving and quantifying 30–50 proteins in human plasma simultaneously.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Classical IEP | Rocket IEP | Crossed IEP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Developed by | Grabar & Williams (1953) | Laurell (1966) | Laurell (1965) |
| Antibody in gel | No (in trough) | Yes (uniform) | Yes (2nd-dim gel) |
| Dimensions | 1-D sep + 1-D diffusion | 1-D | 2-D |
| Output | Curved arcs | Rocket peaks | 2-D peak profile |
| Quantitation | Semi-quantitative | Quantitative (height) | Highly quantitative (area) |
| Sample capacity | Multi-analyte | Single analyte | Multi-analyte |
| Time | 18–24 h | 2–4 h | 4–8 h |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |

**Two Advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Simultaneous separation and identification** of multiple antigens in one run.
2. **High specificity** due to antigen–antibody reaction; **high resolution** due to electrophoretic separation.

**Two Limitations (1 mark):**
1. **Low sensitivity** compared to modern ELISA/CLIA; detection limit ≈ mg/L range.
2. **Time-consuming** (18–24 h diffusion for classical IEP); requires expert interpretation of arcs.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IEP Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Rocket IEP" with "Crossed IEP":** Rocket = **one-dimensional**, single antibody in gel; Crossed = **two-dimensional**, sequential runs. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you mix these up.
> 2. **Forgetting units of mobility:** Always write **cm²·V⁻¹·s⁻¹** or **m²·V⁻¹·s⁻¹** — answers without units lose ½ mark.
> 3. **Omitting the pH of the buffer:** Always state **pH 8.2–8.6** when describing barbital buffer — this is above the pI of most serum proteins, ensuring they migrate towards the anode.
> 4. **Writing "Grabar" instead of "Grabar and Williams":** Classical IEP was developed by **two** scientists — both names are required for full credit.
> 5. **Drawing the trough *across* the wells instead of *parallel* to the electrophoretic axis:** The trough in classical IEP is cut **parallel** to the migration axis so that each separated zone diffuses into the antibody line — perpendicular orientation is incorrect.
> 6. **Stating that IEP is "highly sensitive":** It is **not** highly sensitive (mg/L range) — for high-sensitivity applications, modern ELISA/chemiluminescence is preferred. Do not over-claim.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Full name:** Immunoelectrophoresis (IEP); classical form = **Grabar–Williams IEP (1953)**.
- ✅ **Two stages:** (1) **Zone electrophoresis** (separation by charge/size) + (2) **Immunodiffusion/precipitation** (identification by antigen–antibody reaction).
- ✅ **Gel:** Agarose (0.8–1.5 %); **Buffer:** Barbital, pH 8.2–8.6, I = 0.02–0.05.
- ✅ **Antigen migrates towards anode (+)** at pH 8.6 because net charge is negative.
- ✅ **Key formula (most asked):** $\mu = \dfrac{d \cdot L}{V \cdot t}$ with units **cm²·V⁻¹·s⁻¹**.
- ✅ **Precipitin arc** appears at the **zone of equivalence** (Heidelberger–Kendall lattice).
- ✅ **Rocket IEP (Laurell, 1966):** antibody in gel; **rocket height ∝ antigen concentration** ($h = k \cdot C_{Ag}$).
- ✅ **Crossed IEP (Laurell, 1965):** 2-D; **peak area ∝ antigen mass** ($A = k \cdot m_{Ag}$).
- ✅ **Diffusion time:** 18–24 h in **humid chamber**; **Stain:** Coomassie Brilliant Blue R-250 / Amido Black 10B.
- ✅ **Applications:** Multiple myeloma typing, hypogammaglobulinemia diagnosis, CSF oligoclonal banding, vaccine QC, forensic species identification.
- ✅ **Sensitivity:** mg/L range; **not as sensitive** as ELISA/CLIA but **more resolving** for protein mixtures.
- ✅ **Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse rocket (1-D) with crossed (2-D); do **not** omit units in the mobility formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Western Blot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Western Blot # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Western Blot (Immunoblotting)** is an analytical immunoassay technique used for the specific detection, identification, and semi-quantitative estimation of target proteins in a complex biological sample. The technique combines the high-resolution size-separation power of **SDS-Polyacrylamide Gel Electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE)** with the high specificity of **antigen-antibody interaction**, followed by a chemiluminescent, fluorescent, or colorimetric detection step. The term **"Western"** blot is a play on nomenclature — it was named by mirroring **Edwin Southern's "Southern Blot"** (1975) for DNA. Subsequent analogous techniques were called **Northern blot (RNA)**, **Western blot (protein)**, **Southwestern blot (DNA-protein)**, and **Far-Western blot (protein-protein)**, all named based on geographic direction rather than their discoverers. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Wanted Poster" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of Western Blot as a "Wanted Poster" screening process at a busy airport:** | Real-World Analogy | Western Blot Equivalent | |---|---| | A huge crowd of passengers at the airport | Crude protein lysate containing thousands of proteins | | Seating passengers based on age (height) on the same bench | SDS-PAGE separation based on molecular weight | | Transferring the seated passengers to a separate room via photocopy | Electro-transfer (electroblotting) onto nitrocellulose/PVDF membrane | | Hiding empty seats (to stop random people filling them) | Blocking with 5% non-fat milk or BSA | | A detective with a specific photo of the criminal (Primary Detective) | Primary antibody (recognizes target protein) | | A second detective carrying a flashlight to identify the first detective (Secondary Detective) | HRP/AP-conjugated secondary antibody | | Photograph of the suspect glowing in the dark | Chemiluminescent/ECL detection signal | ## 1.3 Key Vocabulary for KTU 2024 Evaluation > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Terminology Checklist (High-Yield for 2-3 Mark Questions):** > - **SDS-PAGE** — Sodium Dodecyl Sulphate Polyacrylamide Gel Electrophoresis. > - **PVDF / Nitrocellulose Membrane** — Solid phase for protein immobilization. > - **Blocking Buffer** — Prevents non-specific antibody binding. > - **Primary Antibody (1° Ab)** — Target-specific, raised against the antigen. > - **Secondary Antibody (2° Ab)** — Conjugated (HRP/AP) antibody against the Fc region of the 1° Ab. > - **BCA / Bradford Assay** — Quantification of total protein *before* loading. > - **ECL (Enhanced Chemiluminescence)** — Most common detection method. > - **Transfer Buffer (Towbin Buffer)** — Tris-Glycine buffer used during electroblotting. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants - **Molecular weight of SDS micelle-binding contribution per amino acid residue:** ~1.4 kDa uniform mass-to-charge ratio. - **Standard SDS-PAGE running gel percentage for general proteins:** **10% acrylamide** (resolves 20–80 kDa). - **Standard protein loading quantity:** **20–50 µg total protein per lane**. - **Typical transfer time:** **60 minutes at 100 V** (semi-dry) or **overnight at 30 V** (wet transfer). - **Blocking standard:** **5% (w/v) non-fat dried milk in TBST**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Biochemical Principles The technique rests on **three independent biophysical principles** working in sequence: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Principle 1 — Uniform Negative Charge via SDS Denaturation:** > SDS is an anionic detergent. It binds to proteins at a constant ratio of ~1.4 g SDS per 1 g protein, masking intrinsic charges and conferring a uniform negative charge-to-mass ratio. This means proteins migrate purely according to their **molecular size (length)**, not their native charge. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Principle 2 — Size-Based Sieving in a Polyacrylamide Mesh:** > The polymerized acrylamide-bisacrylamide matrix forms a controlled-pore gel. Smaller proteins navigate the pores faster; larger proteins are retarded — achieving **size-based fractionation** with near-log-linear resolution in the appropriate kDa range. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Principle 3 — Immunological Specificity for Detection:** > Even after separation, thousands of irrelevant proteins are present. Detection relies on the **lock-and-key specificity** of antigen-antibody binding, allowing unambiguous identification of the single target. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Sample Lysis & Quantification** - Cells/tissues lysed using RIPA buffer (Radio-Immunoprecipitation Assay Buffer). - Total protein concentration determined using **Bradford (Coomassie dye-binding, A595)** or **BCA (bicinchoninic acid, A562)** assays. - Equal protein loading (typically **20–50 µg**) is **critical** to prevent artifactual band intensity differences. 2. **SDS-PAGE Separation** - Samples boiled in **Laemmli buffer** (containing SDS, β-mercaptoethanol, glycerol, bromophenol blue) at **95 °C for 5 minutes** to ensure complete denaturation and disulfide bond reduction. - Loaded onto a discontinuous gel: **stacking gel (5% acrylamide, pH 6.8)** + **resolving gel (8–15% acrylamide, pH 8.8)**. - Run at constant voltage: **80 V through stacking gel → 120 V through resolving gel**. 3. **Electro-Transfer (Blotting)** - Gel and membrane sandwiched in **transfer cassette** (filter paper → gel → membrane → filter paper), avoiding trapped air bubbles. - **Wet Transfer (Towbin, 1979):** 100 V for 1 hour, or 30 V overnight, in Tris-Glycine transfer buffer. - **Semi-Dry Transfer:** Faster (15–60 min), uses stacked buffer-soaked filter papers. 4. **Blocking** - Membrane incubated in **5% non-fat milk in TBST** (Tris-Buffered Saline + 0.1% Tween-20) for **1 hour at room temperature**. - Phospho-proteins require **5% BSA** (milk casein contains phospho-proteins that cause background). 5. **Primary Antibody (1° Ab) Incubation** - Diluted in blocking buffer (typical range 1:500 – 1:5000). - Incubated **overnight at 4 °C** (preferred for specificity) or **1–2 hours at RT**. - Washed **3 × 5 min with TBST**. 6. **Secondary Antibody (2° Ab) Incubation** - Conjugated to **Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP)** or **Alkaline Phosphatase (AP)**. - Typical dilution 1:2000 – 1:10,000 in blocking buffer. - Incubated **1 hour at room temperature**, then washed **3 × 5 min with TBST**. 7. **Detection & Imaging** - **ECL Substrate** (luminol + H₂O₂ + enhancer) added → HRP catalyzes oxidation → emits light. - Signal captured on **X-ray film** (chemiluminescent) or **CCD camera/imager**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Formulas Required for KTU 2024 Calculations** | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Variable Definitions & Units | |---|---|---| | Relative Mobility ($R_f$) | $R_f = \dfrac{\text{Distance migrated by protein}}{\text{Distance migrated by dye front}}$ | Dimensionless ratio; used to plot standard curves | | Protein Mass-to-Charge Ratio (SDS-bound) | $m/z = \text{constant} \approx \dfrac{1.4 \text{ kDa}}{\text{residue}}$ | Uniform after SDS coating | | Acrylamide % Selection vs. MW | $\%T \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{M_r}}$ | Higher % → resolves smaller proteins | | Bradford Standard Curve | $A_{595} = m \cdot [\text{Protein}] + c$ | Linear range 1–10 µg/mL BSA | | Beer-Lambert (Quantitation) | $A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$ | For BCA / Bradford absorbance | | Band Density (Semi-Quantitation) | $\text{Normalized Expression} = \dfrac{\text{Target Band Density}}{\text{Loading Control Density}}$ | Compared across lanes | | ECL Reaction (Steady-State Light) | $\text{Luminol} \xrightarrow{\text{HRP}/\text{H}_2\text{O}_2} \text{3-Aminophthalate} + h\nu$ | Emits light at ~428 nm | > [!NOTE] > **Acrylamide Percentage Selection Guide (Frequently Asked):** > - **8% gel** → resolves **80–200 kDa** proteins. > - **10% gel** → resolves **20–80 kDa** (most common). > - **12% gel** → resolves **12–60 kDa**. > - **15% gel** → resolves **10–40 kDa** (small peptides). ## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does an engineering biotechnologist care about Western Blot?** > - **Diagnostic Medicine:** Confirmatory test for **HIV (anti-gp120/p24 antibodies)**, **Lyme disease (Borrelia antigens)**, **Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy (prion protein PrPˢᶜ)**. > - **Pharmaceutical QC:** Validates successful expression of recombinant therapeutic proteins (insulin, monoclonal antibodies, erythropoietin). > - **Vaccine Development:** Verifies antigen identity and purity in vaccine formulations. > - **Disease Mechanism Research:** Detects overexpression or absence of tumor suppressors (e.g., p53, BRCA1). > - **Post-Translational Modification (PTM) Studies:** Specialized antibodies detect phosphorylation, ubiquitination, glycosylation. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Calculation of Protein Molecular Weight Using $R_f$ Standards > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (Standard KTU Numerical Type):** > A Western Blot is performed on a protein sample. The bromophenol blue dye front traveled **8.0 cm**, the marker (BSA, 66 kDa) traveled **3.6 cm**, ovalbumin (45 kDa) traveled **5.0 cm**, and lysozyme (14 kDa) traveled **7.2 cm**. A mystery band traveled **4.2 cm**. Calculate the apparent molecular weight of the mystery protein. ### Step 1 — Compute $R_f$ for all standards and the unknown The relative mobility $R_f$ is defined as: $$ R_f = \frac{\text{Distance migrated by band}}{\text{Distance migrated by tracking dye (Bromophenol Blue)}} $$ Apply this to every standard band: $$ R_{f,\text{BSA}} = \frac{3.6 \text{ cm}}{8.0 \text{ cm}} = 0.450 $$ $$ R_{f,\text{Ovalbumin}} = \frac{5.0 \text{ cm}}{8.0 \text{ cm}} = 0.625 $$ $$ R_{f,\text{Lysozyme}} = \frac{7.2 \text{ cm}}{8.0 \text{ cm}} = 0.900 $$ $$ R_{f,\text{Unknown}} = \frac{4.2 \text{ cm}}{8.0 \text{ cm}} = 0.525 $$ ### Step 2 — Construct the Semi-Log Standard Curve The relationship between $R_f$ and $\log_{10}(\text{MW})$ is **linear** within the resolving range of the gel. Therefore: $$ \log_{10}(M_r) = a \cdot R_f + b $$ We compute $\log_{10}(\text{MW})$ for each standard: $$ \log_{10}(66\,000) = 4.8195 $$ $$ \log_{10}(45\,000) = 4.6532 $$ $$ \log_{10}(14\,000) = 4.1461 $$ ### Step 3 — Solve Linear Regression for Coefficients $(a, b)$ Using the two-point extremes (BSA and Lysozyme) for a quick linear fit: $$ \text{Slope } a = \frac{4.1461 - 4.8195}{0.900 - 0.450} = \frac{-0.6734}{0.450} = -1.4964 $$ $$ \text{Intercept } b = 4.8195 - (-1.4964)(0.450) = 4.8195 + 0.6734 = 5.4929 $$ Thus the standard curve equation becomes: $$ \log_{10}(M_r) = -1.4964 \cdot R_f + 5.4929 $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute the Unknown $R_f$ $$ \log_{10}(M_{r,\text{unknown}}) = -1.4964 \times 0.525 + 5.4929 $$ $$ \log_{10}(M_{r,\text{unknown}}) = -0.7856 + 5.4929 = 4.7073 $$ ### Step 5 — Take Antilog to Recover Apparent Molecular Weight $$ M_{r,\text{unknown}} = 10^{4.7073} \text{ Da} $$ $$ M_{r,\text{unknown}} = 5.097 \times 10^{4} \text{ Da} \approx 51 \text{ kDa} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** The unknown protein has an apparent molecular weight of **≈ 51 kDa**. In a real Western Blot, this calculated mass would be cross-referenced with the antibody specification sheet to confirm the target identity. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Antibody Selection Logic > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Asked: "Why are two antibodies used instead of one?"** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Specificity Gain} &= \text{Specificity of } 1°\text{Ab} \times \text{Specificity of } 2°\text{Ab} \\ &\approx 0.95 \times 0.99 \approx 0.94 \text{ (signal)} \\ \text{Background (non-specific)} &\propto 1 \text{ (single Ab)} \rightarrow 0.05 \text{ (two-step)} \\ \text{Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)} &= \frac{\text{True Signal}}{\text{Background}} \end{aligned} $$ The **two-antibody (sandwich) system** amplifies signal **exponentially** because each 1° Ab can bind **multiple** 2° Ab molecules (HRP-conjugated), giving **signal amplification of ~10² to 10³ fold**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Blocking Agent | Target Detected | Recommended Blocker | Rationale | |---|---|---| | Non-phosphorylated protein | **5% non-fat milk in TBST** | Cheap, effective, low background | | Phosphorylated protein (p-Tyr, p-Ser, p-Thr) | **5% BSA in TBST** | Milk casein is phospho-protein → competes with target | | Biotinylated protein | **5% BSA only** | Milk contains biotin → blocks streptavidin-HRP | | Glycoprotein (lectin detection) | **5% BSA** | Avoids glycoprotein contamination | | General purpose | **5% fish gelatin** | Low cross-reactivity, expensive | ## 3.4 Troubleshooting Matrix (High-Yield KTU Scenario Questions) | Observation | Probable Cause | Remedy | |---|---|---| | No bands, white membrane | Insufficient protein / no transfer | Ponceau S stain to verify transfer | | Multiple non-specific bands | Insufficient blocking / Ab concentration too high | Increase blocking time, dilute Ab | | High background (uniform) | Insufficient washing | Increase TBST wash duration | | Patchy/speckled background | Membrane dried out / contaminated buffers | Keep membrane wet, use fresh buffers | | Band at wrong molecular weight | Proteolytic degradation / post-translational modification | Add protease inhibitors (PMSF, leupeptin) | | Smiley-shaped bands | Excessive voltage / overheating | Reduce voltage, run at 4 °C | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Full Western Blot Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sample Lysis
RIPA Buffer] --> B[Protein Quantification
Bradford or BCA Assay] B --> C[Sample Denaturation
Laemmli Buffer at 95C] C --> D[SDS-PAGE Electrophoresis
Stacking + Resolving Gel] D --> E[Electro-Transfer
Gel to Nitrocellulose or PVDF] E --> F[Ponceau S Stain
Transfer Verification] F --> G[Blocking Step
5% Milk in TBST] G --> H[Primary Antibody
Overnight at 4C] H --> I[TBST Wash 3x5 min] I --> J[Secondary Antibody
HRP or AP Conjugated] J --> K[TBST Wash 3x5 min] K --> L[ECL Substrate Addition
Chemiluminescent Detection] L --> M[Signal Capture
X-ray Film or CCD Imager] M --> N[Image Analysis
Band Densitometry Software] subgraph stage1[Stage 1 - Size Separation] A B C D end subgraph stage2[Stage 2 - Solid Phase Transfer] E F end subgraph stage3[Stage 3 - Immunodetection] G H I J K end subgraph stage4[Stage 4 - Visualization] L M N end ``` ## 4.2 Block Architecture — Antibody Sandwich Amplification Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Target Protein
Immobilized on Membrane] --> B[Primary Antibody Binds
Specific Epitope Recognition] B --> C[Wash Removes Excess 1 Degree Ab] C --> D[Secondary Antibody HRP Conjugated
Binds Fc Region of 1 Degree Ab] D --> E[Wash Removes Excess 2 Degree Ab] E --> F[ECL Substrate Luminol plus H2O2] F --> G[HRP Catalyzes Oxidation] G --> H[Light Emission at 428 nm] H --> I[Signal Captured on Detector] ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Decision Map — Detection Chemistries ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Detection Method] --> B{Sensitivity Required?} B -- High --> C[Chemiluminescence ECL] B -- Medium --> D[Colorimetric DAB or BCIP NBT] B -- Multiplex --> E[Fluorescence with LI COR or Typhoon] C --> F[Substrate Luminol plus P Coumaric Acid] D --> G[HRP plus DAB Brown color or AP plus NBT BCIP Purple] E --> H[IR Dye 680 or 800 Conjugates] F --> I[Detect on X ray Film or CCD] G --> J[Visible Light Imaging] H --> K[Near Infrared Imager] ``` ## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Transfer Sandwich Assembly ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph cassette[Transfer Cassette Cathode to Anode Orientation] A1[Sponge Pad] --> A2[Filter Paper 3 Sheets] A2 --> A3[SDS-PAGE Gel] A3 --> A4[Nitrocellulose or PVDF Membrane] A4 --> A5[Filter Paper 3 Sheets] A5 --> A6[Sponge Pad] end A6 --> Z[Anode plus Direction of Protein Migration toward Positive Electrode] A1 --> Y[Cathode Negative Electrode where Gel Originates] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Critical Engineering Insight:** Proteins are **negatively charged** after SDS binding and therefore migrate from the **cathode (–)** toward the **anode (+)** during transfer. PVDF membranes must be **pre-activated in 100% methanol** for 30 seconds, while nitrocellulose membranes require only buffer equilibration. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1, 3 Marks]** > **Q1. Define Western Blotting. Mention the role of SDS and β-mercaptoethanol in sample preparation.** **Model Answer:** Western Blotting (or immunoblotting) is an analytical technique used for the specific detection of a target protein from a complex mixture using **SDS-PAGE** followed by **antibody-based detection**. - **Role of SDS (Sodium Dodecyl Sulphate):** It is an anionic detergent that **denatures proteins**, unfolds their tertiary structure, and binds in a constant ratio (~1.4 g SDS per 1 g protein), conferring a **uniform negative charge-to-mass ratio**. This ensures that proteins migrate during electrophoresis **based purely on molecular size**, not on intrinsic charge. - **Role of β-Mercaptoethanol:** It is a **reducing agent** that cleaves **disulfide (–S–S–) bonds** between cysteine residues, ensuring complete linearization of the polypeptide and preventing reformation of tertiary/quaternary structures that could alter migration. > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2, 3 Marks]** > **Q2. What is the function of the secondary antibody in Western Blotting? Why is HRP preferred?** **Model Answer:** The **secondary antibody** is conjugated to a **reporter enzyme (HRP or AP)** and binds specifically to the **Fc region (constant region) of the primary antibody**. It serves two purposes: 1. **Signal amplification** — multiple secondary antibodies can bind to a single primary antibody, multiplying the signal. 2. **Detection** — the conjugated enzyme (HRP) catalyzes the conversion of chemiluminescent substrate (luminol) into a measurable light signal. **HRP is preferred** because of its **high turnover number** (rapid substrate conversion), **small size** (better tissue penetration), **high stability**, and compatibility with **enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL)** systems which offer femtogram-level sensitivity. ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks Total]** > **Question A:** > **(a)** [7 Marks] Describe in detail the principle and stepwise procedure of Western Blotting. Add a note on the role of blocking and the choice of detection system. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat labelled diagram, explain the working of SDS-PAGE. Discuss how the percentage of acrylamide affects resolution of proteins. ### Model Answer — Question A(a) The **principle of Western Blotting** relies on three sequential biophysical events: 1. **Size-based separation** of proteins using SDS-PAGE. 2. **Electro-transfer** of separated proteins onto a solid membrane (nitrocellulose or PVDF). 3. **Immunological detection** using specific antibodies conjugated to reporter enzymes. **Stepwise Procedure:** **Step 1 — Sample Preparation:** Cells/tissues are lysed using RIPA buffer. Protease and phosphatase inhibitors (PMSF, leupeptin, Na₃VO₄) are added to prevent degradation. Total protein is quantified using **Bradford or BCA assay**, and **20–50 µg of protein** is loaded per lane. **Step 2 — SDS-PAGE:** The sample is mixed with **Laemmli buffer** (containing SDS, β-mercaptoethanol, bromophenol blue tracking dye, glycerol) and **boiled at 95 °C for 5 minutes** to ensure denaturation. Samples are loaded into wells of a **discontinuous polyacrylamide gel** consisting of a **stacking gel (5%, pH 6.8)** for sample concentration and a **resolving gel (8–15%, pH 8.8)** for size separation. Electrophoresis is performed at **80 V (stacking)** and **120 V (resolving)** in **Tris-Glycine-SDS running buffer**. **Step 3 — Transfer:** Separated proteins are transferred from the gel onto a **nitrocellulose (0.45 µm pore) or PVDF membrane** using either **wet transfer (Towbin, 100 V, 1 hour)** or **semi-dry transfer (15–60 minutes)** in Tris-Glycine transfer buffer containing methanol. **Step 4 — Verification of Transfer:** The membrane is stained with **Ponceau S** (a reversible red dye) to confirm successful and uniform protein transfer before proceeding. **Step 5 — Blocking:** The membrane is incubated in **5% (w/v) non-fat dry milk in TBST** for 1 hour at room temperature. This step **saturates the remaining protein-binding sites** on the membrane, preventing non-specific antibody binding and reducing background. **Step 6 — Primary Antibody Incubation:** The membrane is incubated with a **target-specific primary antibody** (typically diluted 1:1000 in blocking buffer) **overnight at 4 °C** to maximize binding specificity. Subsequently washed **3 × 5 min with TBST**. **Step 7 — Secondary Antibody Incubation:** An **HRP-conjugated secondary antibody** (anti-species IgG, dilution 1:5000) is added for 1 hour at room temperature, then washed thoroughly. **Step 8 — Detection:** **ECL substrate** (luminol + H₂O₂ + enhancer) is added. HRP catalyzes the oxidation of luminol, producing **light at ~428 nm**, which is captured on **X-ray film** or a **CCD imager**. **Choice of Detection System:** - **Chemiluminescence (ECL):** Highest sensitivity, femtogram range. - **Colorimetric (DAB, BCIP/NBT):** Visible bands, no special equipment, lower sensitivity. - **Fluorescence (IR dyes):** Multiplexing, quantitative, no substrate needed. ### Model Answer — Question A(b) **SDS-PAGE Working Principle:** **Discontinuous Gel Setup:** The system uses **two gels of different pore sizes and pH**: - **Stacking Gel (5% acrylamide, pH 6.8):** Large pores → all proteins enter the gel and **stack into a tight, thin band** at the stacking/resolving interface. This is governed by **moving boundary electrophoresis** and the **isotachophoresis** phenomenon, exploiting the differential mobility of leading (Cl⁻) and trailing (glycinate) ions. - **Resolving Gel (8–15% acrylamide, pH 8.8):** Smaller pores → proteins separate based on **molecular size**, with SDS imparting uniform charge. Smaller proteins migrate faster; larger proteins are retarded. **Effect of Acrylamide Percentage on Resolution:** The acrylamide percentage (\%T) determines the **pore size** of the gel matrix. The relationship is: $$ \text{Pore Size} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\%T}} $$ | Acrylamide % | Optimal MW Range | Application | |---|---|---| | 8% | 80–200 kDa | Large proteins, immunoglobulins | | 10% | 20–80 kDa | General purpose, most common | | 12% | 12–60 kDa | Medium proteins, receptor subunits | | 15% | 10–40 kDa | Small proteins, peptides, hormones | - **Lower % gels** have **larger pores** → better for resolving **larger proteins**, but offer poor resolution for small proteins. - **Higher % gels** have **smaller pores** → better for resolving **small proteins**, but large proteins may be excluded or co-migrate at the top. **Resolving Range Rule:** The optimal separation occurs for proteins between **0.1 × and 0.9 × of the total gel length traveled by tracking dye**, so gel percentage and run time must be matched to the target protein's expected molecular weight. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks Total — Alternative Choice]** > **Question B:** > **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the components of transfer buffer used in Western blotting. Discuss wet vs semi-dry transfer methods with advantages of each. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] What are the different types of blotting techniques? Compare Southern, Northern, and Western blotting. ### Model Answer — Question B(a) **Transfer Buffer (Towbin Buffer) Components:** The classical **Towbin transfer buffer** (1979) consists of: | Component | Concentration | Function | |---|---|---| | Tris-HCl | 25 mM, pH 8.3 | Maintains stable pH, provides buffering | | Glycine | 192 mM | Carries electrical current, migrates with proteins | | SDS | 0.1% (optional) | Maintains protein solubility, improves elution of large proteins | | Methanol | 20% (v/v) | Removes SDS from proteins (improves binding to NC), prevents gel swelling | | Distilled Water | to volume | Solvent | **Methanol** is critical because it **strips SDS from proteins**, restoring their partial hydrophobicity, which improves binding to nitrocellulose. However, methanol can also **shrink gels** and reduce transfer efficiency of very large proteins (>100 kDa), in which case SDS concentration is increased and methanol is reduced. **Comparison of Transfer Methods:** | Parameter | Wet Transfer (Towbin) | Semi-Dry Transfer | |---|---|---| | Buffer volume | ~1–2 liters | ~10–50 mL | | Duration | 1–2 hours (or overnight) | 15–60 minutes | | Voltage | 100 V (constant) | 10–25 V | | Cooling required | Yes (ice pack or 4 °C cold room) | No (lower voltage, less heat) | | Protein size efficiency | Excellent for all sizes | Best for small to medium proteins | | Cost | Higher (large buffer) | Lower | | Setup complexity | High (tank assembly) | Low (stacked layers) | | Heat generation | High | Low | **Advantages of Wet Transfer:** Better for **large proteins (>100 kDa)**, more uniform transfer, more reproducible, lower background. **Advantages of Semi-Dry Transfer:** Faster, uses less buffer, ideal for routine small-to-medium protein analysis, no cooling required. ### Model Answer — Question B(b) **Types of Blotting Techniques:** Blotting refers to the transfer of nucleic acids or proteins from a gel matrix onto a solid membrane support, followed by detection using labeled probes. Different blots target different biomolecules: **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Southern Blot | Northern Blot | Western Blot | |---|---|---|---| | **Target molecule** | DNA | RNA | Protein | | **Named after** | Edwin Southern (1975) | Directional analogy | Directional analogy | | **Separation method** | Agarose gel electrophoresis | Agarose gel (denaturing) | SDS-PAGE | | **Solid support** | Nitrocellulose / Nylon | Nitrocellulose / Nylon | Nitrocellulose / PVDF | | **Probe/Detection** | Labeled DNA/RNA probe (complementary sequence) | Labeled cDNA/RNA probe (complementary) | Antibody (immunological) | | **Label type** | Radioactive (³²P) or fluorescent | Radioactive (³²P) or fluorescent | Enzymatic (HRP/AP) or fluorescent | | **Specificity basis** | Base-pair complementarity | Base-pair complementarity | Antigen-antibody interaction | | **Transfer method** | Capillary transfer | Capillary or electrophoretic | Electro-transfer (always) | | **Denaturation step** | NaOH (DNA) | Not required (RNA already single-stranded) | SDS + β-mercaptoethanol (protein) | | **Application** | RFLP analysis, gene detection, forensics, mutation screening | Gene expression analysis, mRNA size determination, splice variants | Protein detection, PTM analysis, disease diagnostics, vaccine validation | > **Other Specialized Blots:** **Southwestern** (DNA-binding protein detection using labeled DNA probe), **Far-Western** (protein-protein interaction using labeled protein probe), **Reverse Northern** (RNA on membrane probed with labeled DNA), **Eastern blot** (lipid detection). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Western Blot Questions:** > > 1. **DO NOT confuse Ponceau S with Coomassie Blue.** Ponceau S is a **reversible** stain (used *before* blocking) to verify transfer. Coomassie stains the **gel** (post-electrophoresis) to verify the loading pattern. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Distinguish between PVDF and Nitrocellulose:** PVDF has **higher protein binding capacity (~150–200 µg/cm²)** vs nitrocellulose (~80–100 µg/cm²) and is more mechanically robust, BUT requires **methanol pre-activation**. State both these points for full marks. > > 3. **Always mention "overnight at 4 °C"** for primary antibody incubation in procedure-based questions. Writing only "1 hour at room temperature" is acceptable but the cold overnight protocol is the **gold standard** and earns examiner goodwill. > > 4. **Don't write "primary antibody gives the signal."** The primary antibody only **binds** the target. The **HRP-conjugated secondary antibody produces the signal** via ECL substrate oxidation. This conceptual slip is heavily penalized. > > 5. **Blocking is NOT optional.** If a question asks "Why is BSA preferred over milk for phospho-protein detection?", answer: **"Milk casein is a phospho-protein, which competes with the phospho-target for antibody binding, causing reduced signal and high background."** Do not give a vague "milk is bad" answer. > > 6. **For numerical Rf problems, ALWAYS show 4–5 significant figures** in intermediate steps and the final molecular weight in **kDa**, not Da, to match board expected answer format. > > 7. **PVDF vs. Nitrocellulose binding chemistry is often asked:** NC binds proteins via **hydrophobic + ionic interactions**; PVDF binds via **hydrophobic interactions only** (hence methanol pre-wetting for proper hydrophobic exposure). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Western Blot (PEBBT631, Module 4)** - **Western Blot = Immunoblotting = SDS-PAGE + Electro-transfer + Antibody detection.** - **Three Core Principles:** (1) SDS gives uniform negative charge, (2) Polyacrylamide gel sieves by size, (3) Antibodies provide specific detection. - **SDS binds at ~1.4 g per 1 g protein**, conferring uniform charge:mass ratio. - **β-Mercaptoethanol** reduces disulfide bonds → linearized polypeptides. - **Sample Prep:** Lyse with RIPA + protease inhibitors → quantify with Bradford/BCA → boil with Laemmli buffer at 95 °C × 5 min. - **Loading Standard:** 20–50 µg total protein per lane. - **Discontinuous Gel:** 5% stacking (pH 6.8) + 8–15% resolving (pH 8.8). - **Voltage Protocol:** 80 V (stacking) → 120 V (resolving); run until dye front exits gel. - **Transfer Membranes:** Nitrocellulose (0.45 µm) OR PVDF (pre-activate in 100% methanol). - **Transfer Buffers:** Towbin buffer = 25 mM Tris + 192 mM glycine ± 0.1% SDS + 20% methanol. - **Transfer Methods:** Wet (1 h, 100 V, cooled) vs. Semi-dry (15–60 min, low voltage, no cooling). - **Ponceau S Stain:** Reversible red stain → verifies transfer success → must be washed off before blocking. - **Blocking Standard:** 5% non-fat milk in TBST for 1 h at RT (use 5% BSA for phospho-proteins). - **Primary Antibody (1° Ab):** Specific to target → typically 1:1000 dilution → overnight at 4 °C → wash 3 × 5 min TBST. - **Secondary Antibody (2° Ab):** HRP or AP conjugated → 1:5000 → 1 h at RT → wash 3 × 5 min TBST. - **Detection Chemistries:** ECL (chemiluminescence, most sensitive) | DAB (HRP colorimetric) | BCIP/NBT (AP colorimetric) | IR fluorescence (quantitative multiplex). - **HRP Substrate Reaction:** Luminol + H₂O₂ → HRP → 3-Aminophthalate + Light (428 nm). - **Loading Controls:** Housekeeping proteins — β-actin (~42 kDa), GAPDH (~37 kDa), α-tubulin (~50 kDa). - **Molecular Weight Marker:** Pre-stained protein ladder run in parallel lane. - **Apparent MW Calculation:** Plot $R_f$ vs $\log_{10}(\text{MW})$ of standards → determine unknown by interpolation. - **Acrylamide Selection:** 8% for 80–200 kDa, 10% for 20–80 kDa, 12% for 12–60 kDa, 15% for 10–40 kDa. - **Family of Blots:** Southern (DNA) | Northern (RNA) | Western (Protein) | Southwestern (DNA-binding protein) | Far-Western (Protein-Protein). - **Critical Applications:** HIV confirmatory test, recombinant protein QC, vaccine antigen validation, tumor suppressor detection (p53, BRCA1), PTM studies (phosphorylation, ubiquitination). - **Troubleshooting Quick-Reference:** - No band → No transfer / no Ab / no protein - Multiple bands → Insufficient blocking / non-specific Ab - High background → Insufficient washing - Wrong size → Proteolysis / PTMs - Smile bands → Excessive voltage / overheating - **Examiner's Mantra:** "Mention the controls, blocking, antibody species matching, and ECL substrate — these four are non-negotiable for full marks in any KTU Western Blot essay question." ---

topics.title: ELISA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT631 - IMMUNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ELISA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **ELISA (Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay)** is a highly sensitive, plate-based **immunoassay** technique used to detect and quantify the presence of a specific **antigen** (or **antibody**) in a biological sample. It relies on the high specificity of antibody–antigen interaction and an **enzyme-conjugated** detection system that produces a quantifiable **chromogenic**, **fluorogenic**, or **chemiluminescent** signal proportional to the target analyte concentration.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for Module 4 (Diagnostic Immunology), ELISA is classified as a **solid-phase heterogeneous immunoassay** because:
- One reactant is immobilized on a **solid phase** (typically a 96-well **polystyrene microtiter plate**).
- Separation of bound from free fractions is achieved through **sequential washing steps**.
- A **chromogenic enzyme-substrate reaction** provides the analytical readout.

### Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Working volume per well** | **50 – 200 µL** | Standard 96-well format |
| **Coating buffer pH** | **9.6 (carbonate buffer)** | Maximizes passive adsorption |
| **Washing buffer** | **PBS-T (0.05% Tween-20)** | Reduces non-specific binding |
| **Detection wavelength (HRP/TMB)** | **450 nm** (after stop) or **650 nm** (before stop) | Quantitative absorbance readout |
| **Lower detection limit** | **pg – ng/mL** range | ~1000× more sensitive than RIA |
| **Dynamic range** | **3 – 4 log units** | Broad working concentration span |
| **Assay time** | **2 – 18 hours** | Depending on protocol type |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Fishing Net"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Fishing with a Tagged Hook**
> 
> Imagine you want to find out if a particular fish species (the **antigen**) is present in a vast lake (the **patient serum**). 
> 
> 1. You coat the inside of a **bucket** (the **microplate well**) with a **bait** that only that fish species will bite — this is the **capture antibody**.
> 2. You pour the lake water into the bucket. Only the target fish gets stuck.
> 3. You wash away all the other water and debris.
> 4. Now you add a second **hook with a glowing tag** (the **enzyme-conjugated detection antibody**).
> 5. When you add a **chemical** (the **substrate**), the tag lights up — and the **brightness** tells you **how many fish** were caught.
> 
> This is ELISA in plain English: **a highly specific, enzyme-amplified, quantitative detection system**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Examiners frequently ask — *"Why is ELISA more sensitive than precipitation or agglutination tests?"* The answer lies in the **enzyme amplification step**: a single enzyme molecule (e.g., HRP) can turn over **10³ – 10⁶ substrate molecules per second**, dramatically amplifying the signal from even a few antigen molecules.

---

## 1.3 Historical & Clinical Context

ELISA was developed in **1971 by Engvall and Perlmann** (Sweden) and independently by **Van Weemen and Schuurs** (Netherlands) as a safer, non-radioactive alternative to **Radioimmunoassay (RIA)**. 

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ELISA replaced RIA:**
> - **No radioactive waste** — enzymes are stable and non-hazardous.
> - **Longer reagent shelf-life** — conjugates stable for 6–12 months vs. days for radioisotopes.
> - **Cheaper instrumentation** — a basic microplate reader vs. gamma counter.
> - **Higher throughput** — 96 wells per plate, easily automated.

### Clinical Applications (KTU Module 4 — Diagnostic Immunology)

- **Infectious disease diagnosis**: HIV, Hepatitis B/C, Dengue, COVID-19 serology
- **Autoimmune disease**: Anti-dsDNA (SLE), ANA
- **Endocrinology**: TSH, hCG (pregnancy test), insulin
- **Allergy diagnostics**: Specific IgE
- **Oncology**: Tumor markers (PSA, CEA)
- **Food industry**: Allergen detection
- **Research**: Cytokine quantification (IL-6, TNF-α)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Principle — Antibody-Antigen Specificity + Enzymatic Amplification

ELISA is built on **two pillars**:

1. **Immunological specificity** → governed by the **lock-and-key complementarity** between the **paratope** (antibody binding site) and the **epitope** (antigenic determinant).
2. **Enzymatic amplification** → the **turnover number (kcat)** of the reporter enzyme converts a small number of binding events into a large, measurable color change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal Generation Equation (Conceptual):**
> 
> $$\text{Signal Intensity} \;\propto\; \underbrace{[\text{Analyte}]}_{\text{Bound fraction}} \;\times\; \underbrace{k_{cat}}_{\text{Enzyme turnover}} \;\times\; \underbrace{t}_{\text{Incubation time}}$$

---

## 2.2 Operational Workflow — Generalized Steps

The standard ELISA protocol follows a strict sequence. **Skipping or reordering steps leads to high background or loss of signal.**

### Step 1 — **Coating (Adsorption)**
- The **capture reagent** (antigen or antibody) is dissolved in **coating buffer (0.05 M carbonate-bicarbonate, pH 9.6)**.
- Pipetted into polystyrene wells (typically **100 µL/well**).
- Incubated **2 h at 37°C** or **overnight at 4°C**.
- Mechanism: **Passive hydrophobic adsorption** — proteins bind to the hydrophobic polystyrene surface via Van der Waals forces.

### Step 2 — **Washing**
- **3× washes** with **PBS-T** (Phosphate Buffered Saline + 0.05% Tween-20).
- Removes unbound capture reagent.
- Tween-20 is a **non-ionic detergent** that reduces surface tension and prevents non-specific adsorption.

### Step 3 — **Blocking**
- Add **1–5% BSA** (Bovine Serum Albumin) or **5% non-fat dry milk** in PBS.
- Incubate **1 h at 37°C**.
- **Purpose**: Saturate any remaining hydrophobic binding sites on the plate surface to prevent subsequent non-specific binding of sample proteins or detection antibodies.
- **Critical**: Inadequate blocking → high background; over-blocking → reduced sensitivity.

### Step 4 — **Sample Incubation**
- Add **serum, plasma, urine, cell culture supernatant**, etc.
- Target analyte binds to the immobilized capture reagent.
- Incubate **1–2 h at 37°C** or **overnight at 4°C** for low-abundance targets.

### Step 5 — **Washing (again)**
- 3–5 washes with PBS-T to remove unbound sample components.

### Step 6 — **Detection Antibody Incubation**
- Add **enzyme-conjugated detection antibody** (HRP- or AP-labeled).
- Binds to the captured analyte, forming the **"sandwich" or "bridge" complex**.
- Incubate **1 h at 37°C**.

### Step 7 — **Final Wash**
- 3–5 washes — **this is the most critical wash** to remove unbound conjugate (otherwise high background).

### Step 8 — **Substrate Addition**
- Add **chromogenic substrate** (e.g., **TMB** for HRP, or **p-NPP** for AP).
- Incubate in the **dark** for **10–30 minutes** at room temperature.
- A **colored product** forms in proportion to the amount of bound enzyme.

### Step 9 — **Stop Reaction**
- Add **stop solution**:
  - For HRP/TMB: **2 M H₂SO₄** → yellow color
  - For AP/p-NPP: **3 M NaOH** → deep yellow
- **Stop solution** denatures the enzyme, **fixing the absorbance** for stable reading.

### Step 10 — **Readout**
- Measure **absorbance (OD)** at the appropriate wavelength using a **microplate reader (ELISA reader)**.
- Quantify analyte concentration by **interpolation from a standard curve**.

---

## 2.3 The Four Major ELISA Formats

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Topic.** Examiners **frequently** ask students to **compare** or **draw schematic diagrams** of the four ELISA types. Memorize these thoroughly.

### Type 1: **Direct ELISA**

- **Antigen is coated** on the plate.
- **Single enzyme-labeled primary antibody** binds directly to the antigen.
- **Fastest** protocol (fewest steps).
- **Drawback**: Lower sensitivity, requires labeling of every primary antibody.

$$\text{Plate} — \text{Ag} — \text{Ab}_{enzyme} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Substrate} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Color}$$

### Type 2: **Indirect ELISA**

- **Antigen is coated** on the plate.
- **Unlabeled primary antibody** binds the antigen.
- **Enzyme-labeled secondary antibody** (e.g., anti-IgG-HRP) binds the primary.
- **Advantage**: Signal **amplification** (multiple secondary antibodies bind each primary) → **higher sensitivity**.
- **Disadvantage**: Possible cross-reactivity of secondary antibody.

$$\text{Plate} — \text{Ag} — \text{Ab}_1 — \text{Ab}_{2,\text{enzyme}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Substrate} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Color}$$

### Type 3: **Sandwich ELISA** (most widely used clinically)

- **Capture antibody is coated** on the plate.
- **Antigen** in sample is captured.
- **Enzyme-labeled detection antibody** binds a *different epitope* on the antigen.
- **Used for**: Complex, multivalent antigens (cytokines, pathogen proteins, hormones).
- **Requires**: Two non-overlapping **monoclonal antibodies** recognizing different epitopes.

$$\text{Plate} — \text{Ab}_{\text{capture}} — \text{Ag} — \text{Ab}_{\text{detect,enzyme}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Substrate} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Color}$$

### Type 4: **Competitive (Inhibition) ELISA**

- Used for **small molecules** (haptens) with only **one epitope** — they cannot be "sandwiched".
- **Sample antigen** competes with **reference antigen** (coated on plate) for binding to a **fixed amount of labeled antibody**.
- **More antigen in sample → LESS color** (inverse relationship).
- **Used for**: Drug monitoring, steroid hormones, small toxins.

$$\text{Plate} — \text{Ag}_{\text{ref}} + \text{Ab}_{\text{enzyme}} + \text{Ag}_{\text{sample}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Inverse color}$$

### Comparison Table (KTU Hot Topic)

| Feature | Direct | Indirect | Sandwich | Competitive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coated reagent** | Antigen | Antigen | Capture Ab | Reference Ag |
| **Detection Ab** | Labeled primary | Labeled secondary | Labeled detection Ab | Labeled Ab |
| **Sensitivity** | Low | High | **Highest** | Moderate |
| **Specificity** | High | Moderate | **Highest** | High |
| **Signal vs. [Analyte]** | Direct | Direct | Direct | **Inverse** |
| **Cross-reactivity risk** | Low | Higher | Low | Low |
| **Best for** | Pure antigens | Antibody detection | Complex antigens | Small molecules/haptens |
| **Number of steps** | Fewest | Many | Moderate | Moderate |

---

## 2.4 Enzymes & Substrates Used in ELISA

| Enzyme | Full Name | Substrate | Chromogen | Read at (nm) | Stop Solution |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HRP** | Horseradish Peroxidase | H₂O₂ + **TMB** | Blue → **Yellow** (after stop) | **450 / 650** | **2 M H₂SO₄** |
| **HRP** | Horseradish Peroxidase | H₂O₂ + OPD | Orange | **492** | 2 M H₂SO₄ |
| **HRP** | Horseradish Peroxidase | H₂O₂ + ABTS | Green | **414** | 1% SDS |
| **AP** | Alkaline Phosphatase | **p-NPP** | Yellow | **405** | 3 M NaOH |
| **β-Gal** | β-Galactosidase | ONPG | Yellow | **420** | Na₂CO₃ |

> [!TIP]
> **Why HRP is most common:** Small size (44 kDa), high kcat (~10³ s⁻¹), stable conjugates, cheap substrates, and high turnover with TMB.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — KTU frequently tests quantitative interpretation.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$$ | **Beer-Lambert Law** — absorbance ∝ concentration |
| 2 | $$\text{LOD} = \text{Mean}_{\text{blank}} + 3 \times \text{SD}_{\text{blank}}$$ | **Limit of Detection** — IUPAC definition |
| 3 | $$\text{LOQ} = \text{Mean}_{\text{blank}} + 10 \times \text{SD}_{\text{blank}}$$ | **Limit of Quantification** |
| 4 | $$\text{Cutoff} = \text{Mean}_{\text{neg}} + n \times \text{SD}_{\text{neg}}$$ | **Diagnostic cutoff** (n = 2 to 3 SDs) |
| 5 | $$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ | True positive rate |
| 6 | $$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$ | True negative rate |
| 7 | $$\text{PPV} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$ | Positive Predictive Value |
| 8 | $$\text{NPV} = \frac{TN}{TN + FN}$$ | Negative Predictive Value |
| 9 | $$\text{CV\%} = \frac{\text{SD}}{\text{Mean}} \times 100$$ | Coefficient of Variation (precision check) |
| 10 | $$\text{\%CV}_{\text{intra}} < 10\%,\;\; \text{\%CV}_{\text{inter}} < 15\%$$ | **Acceptable assay precision** |
| 11 | $$\text{Recovery\%} = \frac{\text{Measured}}{\text{Expected}} \times 100$$ | **Accuracy** check (acceptable 80–120%) |
| 12 | $$\text{\% Inhibition} = 1 - \frac{A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{control}}} \times 100$$ | Used in **competitive ELISA** |
| 13 | $$\text{Titer} = \text{Highest dilution giving } A > \text{Cutoff}$$ | Endpoint dilution (antibody ELISA) |
| 14 | $$k_{cat}^{\text{HRP}} \approx 1000\;\text{s}^{-1}$$ | HRP turnover number — **signal amplifier** |
| 15 | $$A_{450} - A_{650} = A_{\text{corrected}}$$ | **Reference subtraction** (corrects plate imperfections) |

> **Notation note:** $A$ = absorbance (OD), $c$ = concentration (M or ng/mL), $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient (M⁻¹·cm⁻¹), $\ell$ = path length (cm), TP/FP/TN/FN = true/false positive/negative, SD = standard deviation, CV = coefficient of variation.

| **Clinical Diagnostics** | HIV, HBV, HCV, Dengue, COVID-19 antibody detection |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Foundation — From Absorbance to Concentration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Goal:** Show how a raw ELISA readout (Optical Density) is converted into a clinically meaningful analyte concentration through the **standard curve method**.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Apply the Beer-Lambert Law

The Beer-Lambert Law (1852) states that **absorbance is directly proportional to the concentration of an absorbing species**:

$$A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$$

Where:
- $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless, often called **OD — Optical Density**)
- $\varepsilon$ = **molar extinction (absorptivity) coefficient**, in **M⁻¹·cm⁻¹**
- $c$ = molar concentration, in **mol/L (M)**
- $\ell$ = optical path length, in **cm**

In an ELISA well, the chromogenic product (e.g., oxidized TMB) absorbs light at **450 nm**. The microplate reader measures transmittance $T$ and converts to absorbance:

$$A = -\log_{10}(T) = -\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

> **Conversion logic:** $I_0$ = incident light intensity (100%), $I$ = transmitted light intensity. As more chromogen forms, $I$ decreases → $A$ increases.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Establish the Direct Proportionality

In ELISA, the chromogen concentration is governed by the **amount of bound enzyme**, which in turn equals the **amount of bound detection antibody**, which equals the **amount of bound antigen (analyte)**:

$$[\text{Chromogen}] \;\propto\; [\text{Enzyme bound}] \;\propto\; [\text{Analyte bound}]$$

Therefore, by Beer-Lambert:

$$A_{450} \;\propto\; c_{\text{analyte}}$$

The proportionality constant is empirically determined using a **standard curve**.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Generate the Standard Curve

- Prepare a **serial dilution** of a **standard of known concentration** (e.g., 10, 5, 2.5, 1.25, 0.625, 0 ng/mL).
- Run the ELISA on each standard (in duplicate or triplicate).
- Plot **A₄₅₀ (y-axis)** vs. **log [analyte] (x-axis)** → **4-parameter logistic (4PL) regression**:

$$A = A_{\min} + \frac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{1 + \left(\frac{c}{EC_{50}}\right)^{h}}$$

Where:
- $A_{\min}$ = background absorbance (minimum asymptote)
- $A_{\max}$ = maximum absorbance (plateau)
- $EC_{50}$ = concentration giving **half-maximal response** (a measure of **assay sensitivity**)
- $h$ = **Hill slope** (steepness of the curve)

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Interpolate Sample Concentration

For an unknown sample with measured absorbance $A_{\text{sample}}$:

$$c_{\text{sample}} = EC_{50} \times \left(\frac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\text{sample}} - A_{\min}} - 1\right)^{\!1/h}$$

Or, in the **linear working range** (typically between $A_{\min} + 2\,SD$ and $A_{\max} - 2\,SD$), a simple linear regression suffices:

$$A = m \cdot c + b \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; c_{\text{sample}} = \frac{A_{\text{sample}} - b}{m}$$

Where $m$ = slope (sensitivity) and $b$ = y-intercept.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Apply Quality Control Checks

> [!TIP]
> **Every ELISA plate MUST include these QC elements:**

| Control | Purpose | Acceptance Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **Blank** (no analyte) | Background check | A < 0.100 |
| **Negative control** | Specificity check | A < Cutoff |
| **Positive control** | Sensitivity check | A > Cutoff |
| **Standard curve** | Quantification | R² > 0.99 |
| **Spike-and-recovery** | Accuracy | 80–120% |
| **Duplicate CV%** | Precision | Intra < 10%, Inter < 15% |

### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A sandwich ELISA for **IL-6** gives the following standard curve data:
> 
> | [IL-6] (pg/mL) | A₄₅₀ |
> |---|---|
> | 500 | 2.450 |
> | 250 | 1.890 |
> | 125 | 1.020 |
> | 62.5 | 0.580 |
> | 31.25 | 0.310 |
> | 0 | 0.085 |
> 
> A patient sample gave A₄₅₀ = **0.875**. Estimate the IL-6 concentration using a 4PL fit with $EC_{50} = 130$ pg/mL, $A_{\max} = 2.5$, $A_{\min} = 0.08$, $h = 1.0$.

**Step A:** Substitute into the 4PL equation:

$$A = 0.08 + \frac{2.5 - 0.08}{1 + (c/130)^1} = 0.08 + \frac{2.42}{1 + c/130}$$

**Step B:** Solve for $c$ when $A = 0.875$:

$$0.875 = 0.08 + \frac{2.42}{1 + c/130}$$

$$0.795 = \frac{2.42}{1 + c/130}$$

$$1 + \frac{c}{130} = \frac{2.42}{0.795} = 3.044$$

$$\frac{c}{130} = 2.044$$

$$c = 130 \times 2.044 \approx 265.7 \;\text{pg/mL}$$

**Step C:** Verify using a simpler linear regression on the central points (log-linear in that range):

Using points 125 pg/mL (A=1.020) and 250 pg/mL (A=1.890):
- Slope $m = (1.890 - 1.020)/(250 - 125) = 0.00696$ per pg/mL
- Intercept $b = 1.020 - 0.00696 \times 125 = 0.150$

Then: $c = (0.875 - 0.150)/0.00696 \approx 104.2$ pg/mL

> **Discrepancy explanation:** The 4PL fit accounts for the **sigmoidal shape** at low and high concentrations, while the linear fit is only valid in the **central working range**. The "true" answer lies between 100 and 270 pg/mL, demonstrating why **curve fitting matters** for accurate quantification.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Diagnostic Test Performance

### 3.2.1 Derivation of Diagnostic Sensitivity and Specificity

A 2×2 contingency table from a clinical study of 1000 patients (500 disease-positive, 500 disease-negative) tested by ELISA:

| | Disease + | Disease − |
|---|---|---|
| **Test +** | TP = 480 | FP = 20 |
| **Test −** | FN = 20 | TN = 480 |
| **Total** | 500 | 500 |

**Sensitivity** (true positive rate):

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{480}{500} = 96\%$$

**Specificity** (true negative rate):

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{480}{500} = 96\%$$

**Positive Predictive Value (PPV)** — probability that a positive test means the disease is truly present:

$$PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{480}{500} = 96\%$$

**Negative Predictive Value (NPV)**:

$$NPV = \frac{TN}{TN + FN} = \frac{480}{500} = 96\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trap Question:** A student might answer "Sensitivity = 96% means 4% false positives." **This is WRONG.** Sensitivity is the false **negative** rate complement. 1 − Sensitivity = 4% = FN rate.

### 3.2.2 Effect of Cutoff Threshold on Sensitivity/Specificity (ROC Analysis)

Moving the **cutoff** absorbance changes sensitivity and specificity in **opposite directions**:

| Cutoff OD | Sensitivity | Specificity | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low (0.2)** | High (99%) | Low (70%) | **Screening** (catch all positives) |
| **Medium (0.5)** | 96% | 96% | **General diagnostics** |
| **High (1.0)** | Low (80%) | High (99%) | **Confirmatory testing** |

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** plots Sensitivity (y) vs. (1 − Specificity) (x). The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** quantifies overall diagnostic accuracy:
- AUC = 1.0 → **Perfect test**
- AUC = 0.5 → **Random (useless) test**
- **Acceptable ELISA** → AUC > 0.90

---

## 3.3 Derivations of Key Validation Parameters

### 3.3.1 Limit of Detection (LOD)

LOD is defined as the lowest analyte concentration distinguishable from blank with statistical confidence (IUPAC):

$$LOD = \bar{x}_{\text{blank}} + 3 \cdot \sigma_{\text{blank}}$$

Where:
- $\bar{x}_{\text{blank}}$ = mean absorbance of **20+ blank wells**
- $\sigma_{\text{blank}}$ = standard deviation of blank

**Example:** If 20 blank wells give mean $A = 0.082$ and $SD = 0.012$:

$$LOD_{A} = 0.082 + 3(0.012) = 0.118$$

The corresponding **concentration LOD** is found by plugging $A = 0.118$ into the standard curve equation.

### 3.3.2 Precision — Intra-assay and Inter-assay CV%

**Intra-assay CV%** (within one plate, replicates of same sample):

$$CV_{\text{intra}} = \frac{\sqrt{\text{Mean of variances of each sample}}}{\text{Mean of sample means}} \times 100$$

**Inter-assay CV%** (across multiple plates/days):

$$CV_{\text{inter}} = \frac{SD_{\text{between runs}}}{\text{Grand mean}} \times 100$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **accuracy** (how close to the true value, measured by % recovery) with **precision** (how reproducible, measured by CV%). A test can be **precise but inaccurate** (systematic bias) or **accurate but imprecise** (random error).

### 3.3.3 % Recovery (Accuracy)

$$Recovery\% = \frac{c_{\text{measured}}}{c_{\text{spiked}}} \times 100$$

**Acceptable range: 80–120%** per FDA and ICH guidelines.

### 3.3.4 Linearity of Dilution (Parallelism)

A valid ELISA should show **linearity** when sample is serially diluted — i.e., the **% CV of corrected concentrations** should be < 20% across dilutions. This confirms absence of matrix interference (Hook effect, etc.).

---

## 3.4 The **Hook Effect** (Prozone Phenomenon) — Critical KTU Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** In **sandwich ELISA**, when **antigen concentration is extremely high**, the **signal paradoxically decreases** because:
> 1. Excess antigen saturates the **capture antibody** AND the **detection antibody** **separately**.
> 2. No "sandwich" can form because each antibody binds to a **different antigen molecule**.
> 3. Result: A **false-negative** despite high antigen.

**Mathematical model:**

$$\text{Signal}(c) = \frac{A_{\max}}{1 + \frac{c}{K_d} + \frac{c^2}{K_1 K_2}}$$

Where $K_1$, $K_2$ are dissociation constants for capture and detection antibodies. At very high $c$, the $c^2/K_1 K_2$ term dominates → signal drops.

**Clinical relevance:** Failure to recognize the **hook effect** in **hCG ELISA** for **gestational trophoblastic disease** or **testicular cancer** can lead to **missed diagnoses** of very high hCG levels.

> **Mitigation:** Always run **serial dilutions** of unknown samples; if the calculated concentration **decreases** with dilution, suspect the hook effect.

---

## 3.5 Complete Worked Example — Indirect ELISA for Anti-HIV Antibody Detection

> **Scenario:** A diagnostic lab performs indirect ELISA to screen 5 patient samples for anti-HIV antibodies. The cutoff is set as **mean(negative controls) + 2 SD = 0.250**.
> 
> | Sample | A₄₅₀ | Interpretation |
> |---|---|---|
> | NC1 | 0.085 | Negative control |
> | NC2 | 0.092 | Negative control |
> | NC3 | 0.078 | Negative control |
> | **Mean NC** | **0.085** | — |
> | **SD NC** | **0.007** | — |
> | **Cutoff** | **0.085 + 2(0.007) = 0.099** | Adjusted cutoff |
> | PC (positive control) | 1.450 | Pass |
> | Patient 1 | 0.082 | **Negative** |
> | Patient 2 | 1.250 | **Positive** (reactive) |
> | Patient 3 | 0.105 | **Borderline** — repeat in duplicate |
> | Patient 4 | 0.075 | **Negative** |
> | Patient 5 | 0.880 | **Positive** |

> **Note:** In real HIV testing, any **reactive** sample must be **re-tested in duplicate**. If repeatedly reactive → **confirmatory Western Blot** is performed (the **3-tier algorithm** recommended by WHO/CDC).

```
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
```
